> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Materials of Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Materials of Construction** in food processing equipment refer to the carefully selected engineering substances (metals, alloys, polymers, ceramics, and composites) used to fabricate vessels, piping, conveyors, heat exchangers, mixers, and storage tanks that come into direct or indirect contact with food products. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCFTT601, this topic establishes the foundational criteria for material selection based on **mechanical strength**, **chemical inertness**, **corrosion resistance**, **thermal conductivity**, **sanitary finish**, and **regulatory compliance** with food safety standards (e.g., FSSAI, USFDA, EU 1935/2004, 3-A Sanitary Standards).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Selection of materials is governed by the *trio of food safety* — (1) non-toxicity, (2) non-reactivity with the food matrix, and (3) cleanability. Failure in any one of the three renders the material unfit for food contact surfaces.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are choosing a material to build a **water bottle** for a child. You would never pick a rusted iron can (reactive), a lead cup (toxic), or a porous clay pot that traps bacteria (uncleanable). You would pick **food-grade stainless steel or BPA-free polymer** — durable, non-leaching, and easy to wash.

Now scale this thinking up to industrial food processing:

- A **milk pasteurizer** handles acidic, hot liquid → needs an alloy that resists **crevice corrosion** and **pitting**.
- A **tomato sauce cooker** handles low-pH (pH ≈ 4.0–4.5), high-temperature, chloride-rich food → needs **SS 316L** with **Mo ≥ 2%** to resist pitting.
- A **grain silo** handles dry, abrasive bulk solids → needs **mild steel with food-grade epoxy lining** for cost-effective structural strength.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Material Selection Triad:**
> 1. **Functional Performance** — Does it withstand the process conditions (T, P, pH, abrasion)?
> 2. **Food Safety Compliance** — Is it non-toxic and non-absorptive?
> 3. **Economical Fabricability** — Can it be welded, polished, and maintained at reasonable cost?

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standardized benchmarks govern food-grade material selection:

- **Surface Roughness (Ra):** For sanitary food contact surfaces, the average roughness must be **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm (32 μin)** for polished surfaces; for ultra-sanitary pharmaceutical-grade, **Ra ≤ 0.4 μm (16 μin)**.
- **Chloride (Cl⁻) Threshold for Pitting:** SS 304 tolerates **Cl⁻ < 200 ppm**; SS 316L tolerates **Cl⁻ < 1000 ppm** without pitting at ambient temperature.
- **Maximum Allowable Iron Pickup (Stainless Steel):** **≤ 4 μg/ft²** of surface area (FTI/ASTM A967 passivation criteria).
- **Operating Temperature Range:** SS 304 up to **870 °C** (intermittent); SS 316 up to **925 °C**; food-grade silicone rubber **-60 °C to +230 °C**.
- **Hardness (Brinell):** SS 304 = **123 HB**, SS 316 = **149 HB**, SS 316L = **140 HB** (annealed).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Materials Used in Food Processing Equipment

Materials of construction are broadly classified into **four families**, each occupying a specific design niche:

### A. Ferrous Metals and Alloys

1. **Carbon Steel (CS)** — Low cost, high mechanical strength. Used for structural frames, non-contact supports, and dry bulk storage silos. *Limitation:* Rusts in moist/wet service, so always food-grade epoxy coated or galvanized.
2. **Galvanized Iron (GI)** — Carbon steel hot-dip coated with zinc. Limited to **dry, non-acidic, ambient-temperature** service. Zinc coating thickness typically **60–120 μm**.
3. **Cast Iron** — Used for slow-speed grinders, crushers, and mortars. High compressive strength, brittle in tension. Must be food-grade enamel lined.
4. **Stainless Steel (SS)** — The **workhorse** of food processing. Iron-based alloy with **Cr ≥ 10.5%** that forms a passive Cr₂O₃ layer.

### B. Non-Ferrous Metals and Alloys

1. **Aluminium and its alloys** (e.g., AA 1100, AA 3003) — Lightweight, high thermal conductivity (~**205 W/m·K**). Used for cooking kettles, baking trays. *Limitation:* reacts with acidic and alkaline foods; must be anodized to ≥ **25 μm** for food contact.
2. **Copper (Cu)** — Excellent thermal conductivity (~**385 W/m·K**), traditionally used in brewing kettles and confectionery. *Limitation:* catalyzes oxidative rancidity in fats; in many jurisdictions, copper is restricted to **non-direct-contact** surfaces.
3. **Tin (Sn)** — Used as a coating on steel (tinplate) for cans. Coating mass typically **2.8–11.2 g/m²**.

### C. Non-Metallic Materials

1. **Polymers (Food-Grade Plastics)**
   - **HDPE (High-Density Polyethylene):** Tanks, piping, milk crates. Temp range: **-50 °C to +80 °C**.
   - **PP (Polypropylene):** Autoclavable containers, bottle caps. Temp: **-20 °C to +135 °C**.
   - **PTFE (Teflon):** Non-stick coatings for baking and sealing. Temp: **-200 °C to +260 °C**.
   - **EPDM, Silicone, Nitrile Rubber:** Gaskets and seals.
2. **Glass and Glass-Lined Steel** — Chemically inert, used for reactors and storage of high-purity flavors.
3. **Ceramics** — Acid-resistant tiles, grinding media (e.g., alumina, zirconia).

### D. Composite and Hybrid Materials

- **FRP (Fibre-Reinforced Plastic)** — Glass-fibre + polyester/vinylester resin. Used for large chemical-resistant tanks.
- **Rubber-Lined Steel** — For abrasive slurry handling.

## 2.2 Stainless Steel — The Flagship Material

### 2.2.1 The Chromium Passive Layer Mechanism

Stainless steel resists corrosion because of a self-healing, nano-scale **chromium oxide (Cr₂O₃)** layer that forms spontaneously when Cr ≥ 10.5% reacts with atmospheric oxygen:

$$4 \text{Cr} + 3 \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2 \text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$$

This passive film is **~2–3 nm thick**, transparent, and self-repairs in oxygenated environments. If mechanically damaged (scratched), it re-forms within **microseconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** The passive layer is **destroyed** in low-oxygen environments (crevices, under gaskets) and by chlorides. This is why **316L with 2–3% Mo** is preferred for chloride-rich foods (tomato sauce, salted cheese whey, brines).

### 2.2.2 AISI/SAE Stainless Steel Grades — Food Industry Standard

| Grade | Composition (wt%) | Key Alloying Addition | Typical Food Application |
|-------|-------------------|-----------------------|--------------------------|
| **SS 304** | 18 Cr, 8 Ni | — | General food contact, milk tanks, cutlery |
| **SS 304L** | 18 Cr, 8 Ni, low C (≤0.03%) | Low Carbon | Welded vessels, brewery tanks |
| **SS 316** | 16 Cr, 10 Ni, 2 Mo | Mo | Sauces, brines, pharmaceuticals |
| **SS 316L** | 16 Cr, 10 Ni, 2–3 Mo, low C | Mo + Low Carbon | Sanitary welded piping, CIP systems |
| **SS 430** | 16–18 Cr (ferritic, no Ni) | — | Decorative panels, economical non-corrosive surfaces |
| **SS 2205 (Duplex)** | 22 Cr, 5 Ni, 3 Mo | Duplex structure | High-stress, chloride-rich process tanks |

### 2.2.3 Mechanical and Physical Properties (Annealed Condition)

| Property | SS 304 | SS 316 | SS 316L |
|----------|--------|--------|---------|
| Density (kg/m³) | 8000 | 8000 | 8000 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 515 | 515 | 485 |
| Yield Strength (MPa) | 205 | 205 | 170 |
| Elongation (%) | 40 | 40 | 40 |
| Hardness (HB) | 123 | 149 | 140 |
| Thermal Conductivity (W/m·K) | 16.2 | 15.9 | 15.9 |
| CTE (×10⁻⁶/°C) | 17.3 | 16.0 | 16.0 |
| Max Service Temp (°C) | 870 | 925 | 925 |

## 2.3 Selection Criteria — Engineering Decision Matrix

The following **eight criteria** are evaluated sequentially when selecting a material for a food processing equipment component:

1. **Chemical compatibility** with food (pH, salts, sugars, oils, enzymes).
2. **Operating temperature and pressure** envelope.
3. **Mechanical load** (static, dynamic, fatigue, impact).
4. **Corrosion type** expected (uniform, pitting, crevice, galvanic, SCC).
5. **Surface finish requirement** (Ra value, electropolishing, passivation).
6. **Fabrication process** (welding, bending, machining, deep drawing).
7. **Cleaning and sterilization compatibility** (CIP, SIP, autoclave).
8. **Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)** — material + fabrication + maintenance + replacement.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|--------------------|---------------|---------------|
| Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number (PREN) | $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$ | Higher PREN ⇒ better pitting resistance. SS 316 ⇒ PREN ≈ 25 |
| Hoop Stress (Thin-Walled Cylinder) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ | Pa; for pressure vessel design |
| Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{pD}{4t}$ | Pa |
| Required Wall Thickness | $t = \dfrac{pD}{2SE - 1.2p} + \text{corrosion allowance}$ | E = joint efficiency, S = allowable stress |
| Allowable Stress (ASME BPVC) | $S = \dfrac{\sigma_{UTS}}{n}$ where $n \geq 4$ for SS | MPa |
| Corrosion Allowance (typical) | $CA = CR \times DL$ | CR = corrosion rate (mm/yr), DL = design life (yr) |
| Passivation Film Thickness | ~2–3 nm Cr₂O₃ | Self-healing in O₂ presence |
| Sanitary Surface Finish | Ra ≤ 0.8 μm | 3-A / EHEDG standard |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** PREN is the single most important number an examiner expects when justifying a stainless steel choice for chloride-rich or acidic foods. Always compute it.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Wall Thickness for a Stainless Steel Pressure Vessel

A cylindrical milk storage tank operates at an internal design pressure **p = 0.3 MPa**, with internal diameter **D = 1500 mm**. The material is SS 316L with ultimate tensile strength (UTS) **σᵤ = 485 MPa** and joint efficiency **E = 0.85**. Design life **DL = 15 years**, corrosion rate **CR = 0.05 mm/yr**. Compute the required wall thickness.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation (ASME BPVC Section VIII, Div 1):**

For a thin-walled cylinder, the hoop stress governs:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t} \leq SE$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange for t (without corrosion allowance):**

$$t = \frac{pD}{2SE - 1.2p}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{(0.3 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}) \times (1.5 \text{ m})}{2 \times (485 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} / 4) \times 0.85 - 1.2 \times (0.3 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa})}
\end{aligned}
$$

First, compute the allowable stress:

$$S = \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{n} = \frac{485 \text{ MPa}}{4} = 121.25 \text{ MPa}$$

Then substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{(0.3 \times 10^6)(1.5)}{2 \times (121.25 \times 10^6)(0.85) - 1.2 \times (0.3 \times 10^6)} \\[6pt]
t &= \frac{450{,}000}{206{,}125{,}000 - 360{,}000} \\[6pt]
t &= \frac{450{,}000}{205{,}765{,}000} \\[6pt]
t &= 2.187 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \\[6pt]
t &= 2.187 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add corrosion allowance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CA &= CR \times DL = 0.05 \text{ mm/yr} \times 15 \text{ yr} = 0.75 \text{ mm} \\[6pt]
t_{total} &= t + CA = 2.187 + 0.75 = 2.937 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Round up to standard plate thickness:**

$$t_{selected} = 3.0 \text{ mm (next standard gauge)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For this milk tank, a **3 mm SS 316L** shell is sufficient. If chloride exposure were higher (e.g., brine tank), one would either increase thickness or upgrade to **Duplex 2205** with PREN ≈ 35.

## 3.2 Comparative Analysis: Why SS 316L Over SS 304 for Tomato Sauce?

Compute the PREN for both grades (assume SS 304: 18 Cr, 0 Mo, 0 N; SS 316L: 16 Cr, 2.5 Mo, 0.05 N):

**For SS 304:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PREN}_{304} &= 18 + 3.3 \times 0 + 16 \times 0 \\
&= 18
\end{aligned}
$$

**For SS 316L:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PREN}_{316L} &= 16 + 3.3 \times 2.5 + 16 \times 0.05 \\
&= 16 + 8.25 + 0.80 \\
&= 25.05
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision:** Tomato sauce at pH ≈ 4.2 and Cl⁻ from salt addition ≈ 1500 ppm exceeds the safe working envelope of SS 304 (PREN 18). **SS 316L (PREN ≈ 25)** is selected.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Material vs. Food Compatibility

| Food Product | pH | Cl⁻ (ppm) | Temp (°C) | Recommended Material | Rejected Material |
|--------------|-----|-----------|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Fresh Milk | 6.7 | 100 | 5–80 | SS 304, SS 316L | Carbon steel, Copper |
| Tomato Sauce | 4.2 | 1500 | 90–100 | SS 316L, Duplex 2205 | SS 304, Aluminium |
| Fruit Juice (citrus) | 2.5–3.5 | 50 | 5–20 | SS 316L, Glass-lined steel | Carbon steel, PP |
| Beer | 4.0–4.5 | 200 | 0–90 | SS 304L (welded), SS 316L | Aluminium (flavour issues) |
| Edible Oil | — | 0 | 60–180 | SS 304, Carbon Steel (epoxy-lined) | Copper (catalyzes oxidation) |
| Salted Brine | 6.5 | 250,000 | 25 | Duplex 2205, Titanium | SS 304 (severe pitting) |
| Dry Grain | — | — | Ambient | Galvanized Steel, SS 304 | Cast Iron |
| Sugar Syrup | 5–7 | 50 | 100–130 | SS 304, SS 316L | Aluminium |

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow: Material Selection Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **KTU Module 1 material selection logic** from process specification to final validated selection:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Food Product and Process Parameters] --> B[Identify pH, Temperature, Cl Concentration, Abrasiveness]
    B --> C{Surface Contact Direct or Indirect}
    C -->|Direct| D[Apply Food Contact Safety Regulations: FSSAI USFDA EU1935]
    C -->|Indirect| E[Apply Structural and Mechanical Criteria Only]
    D --> F[Compute PREN and Corrosion Allowance]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Expected Corrosion Mode}
    G -->|Uniform| H[Carbon Steel with Food Grade Epoxy Lining]
    G -->|Pitting| I[SS 316L PREN greater than 25]
    G -->|Crevice| J[SS 316L plus Electropolished Surface Ra below 0.8 micrometer]
    G -->|Galvanic| K[Isolate Dissimilar Metals with Dielectric Unions]
    G -->|Stress Corrosion Cracking| L[Duplex 2205 or SS 316L with Stress Relief]
    H --> M[Evaluate Mechanical Load and Fatigue]
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N{Meets Strength Safety Factor greater than 4}
    N -->|No| O[Increase Thickness or Upgrade to Higher Alloy]
    N -->|Yes| P[Validate CIP SIP Compatibility]
    O --> M
    P --> Q{Compatible with Cleaning Chemicals NaOH HNO3}
    Q -->|No| R[Switch to Polymer Lined or Glass Lined Steel]
    Q -->|Yes| S[Final Cost Benefit Analysis]
    R --> P
    S --> T[Material Selection Approved Documented with PREN Ra and Cost Data]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Stainless Steel Passivation and Pitting Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O2[Atmospheric Oxygen O2] --> CR[Chromium in Alloy greater than 10.5 percent]
    CR --> PASS[Passive Cr2O3 Layer 2 to 3 nm]
    PASS --> SAFE[Corrosion Resistant Surface]
    CL[Chloride Ions Cl-] --> ATTACK[Breaks Down Passive Layer Locally]
    ATTACK --> PIT[Pitting Corrosion Nucleates]
    PIT --> FAIL[Alloy Failure at Localized Sites]
    MO[Molybdenum 2 to 3 percent] --> STAB[Stabilizes Cr2O3 Against Cl- Attack]
    STAB --> SAFE
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Galvanic Series and Dissimilar Metal Isolation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ANODE[Anodic End Less Noble Corrodes First] --> ZN[Zinc Magnesium Aluminium]
    ZN --> CS[Carbon Steel Cast Iron]
    CS --> SS304[Stainless Steel 304 Active State]
    SS304 --> SS304P[Stainless Steel 304 Passive State]
    SS304P --> SS316[Stainless Steel 316 Passive State]
    SS316 --> CU[Copper Brass Bronze]
    CU --> NICU[Nickel Copper Alloys]
    NICU --> TI[Titanium Graphite Cathodic End Most Noble]
    TI --> ISOLATE[Isolate Anode and Cathode with Dielectric Union or Polymer Gasket to Prevent Galvanic Corrosion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Galvanic Series:** When two dissimilar metals are coupled in a conductive food (e.g., salty brine), the **less noble** metal (top of series) corrodes preferentially. To avoid this, food piping systems always couple SS 316 to SS 316 — never SS 316 to mild steel.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Material of Construction" in the context of food processing equipment. State any three selection criteria.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PCFTT601, Q1a]**

**Model Answer:**
Materials of construction refer to the engineering substances used to fabricate equipment and machinery that come in contact with food during processing, storage, or packaging. Selection is governed by three principal criteria:

1. **Chemical inertness and food safety** — non-toxicity and non-reactivity with the food matrix (pH, salts, lipids).
2. **Mechanical and thermal adequacy** — sufficient tensile strength, yield strength, and thermal endurance for the operating envelope.
3. **Cleanability and sanitary finish** — surface roughness Ra ≤ 0.8 μm and compatibility with CIP (Clean-In-Place) chemicals.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently list *cost* as a primary selection criterion. **Cost is tertiary**, never primary, for food contact surfaces. Safety is non-negotiable.

---

### Question 2: What is PREN? Why is it critical in selecting stainless steels for chloride-rich foods?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCFTT601, Q1b]**

**Model Answer:**
**PREN (Pitting Resistance Equivalent Number)** is a dimensionless empirical index that quantifies an alloy's resistance to pitting corrosion in chloride-bearing environments.

$$\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$$

**Significance in chloride-rich foods:**
- Chloride ions (Cl⁻) penetrate and locally destroy the passive Cr₂O₃ layer, initiating **pits**.
- Higher PREN ⇒ higher critical pitting temperature ⇒ safer operation.
- For tomato sauce (Cl⁻ ≈ 1500 ppm, pH ≈ 4.2), **PREN ≥ 24** is mandatory ⇒ SS 316L is selected (PREN ≈ 25); SS 304 with PREN ≈ 18 will pit.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): 

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, PCFTT601, Module 1, Q2]**

(a) Classify the materials of construction used in food processing equipment with suitable examples for each class. **(7 Marks)**

(b) Discuss in detail the composition, properties, and food-grade applications of SS 304 and SS 316L. Justify why SS 316L is preferred over SS 304 for chloride-rich acidic foods. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Classification of Materials:**

| Class | Examples | Typical Food Use |
|-------|----------|------------------|
| Ferrous | Carbon steel, Cast iron, Galvanized iron, Stainless steel | Silos, structural frames, tanks |
| Non-ferrous | Aluminium, Copper, Tin | Cooking vessels, tinplate cans |
| Polymers | HDPE, PP, PTFE, EPDM | Pipes, seals, gaskets |
| Ceramics & Glass | Glass-lined steel, porcelain tiles | Acid storage, grinding media |
| Composites | FRP, Rubber-lined steel | Corrosion-resistant tanks |

**(b) SS 304 vs SS 316L — Detailed Comparison:**

**SS 304** (Austenitic, 18 Cr / 8 Ni):
- Tensile strength 515 MPa, density 8000 kg/m³, max service temp 870 °C.
- Applications: milk storage, brewery fermentation vessels, food-grade piping for low-chloride service.
- Limitation: PREN = 18, vulnerable to pitting in Cl⁻ > 200 ppm.

**SS 316L** (Austenitic, 16 Cr / 10 Ni / 2–3 Mo, low C ≤ 0.03%):
- Tensile strength 485 MPa, density 8000 kg/m³, max service temp 925 °C.
- **Molybdenum addition (2–3%)** stabilizes the passive layer against chloride attack.
- PREN = 16 + 3.3(2.5) + 16(0.05) ≈ **25**.
- Applications: tomato sauce cookers, brine tanks, pharmaceutical CIP loops, welded sanitary piping.

**Justification for SS 316L preference:**
For chloride-rich acidic foods (e.g., tomato sauce pH 4.2, Cl⁻ ≈ 1500 ppm), the chloride ions locally penetrate the Cr₂O₃ passive film of SS 304 (PREN 18), initiating **pitting corrosion** at temperatures as low as 30 °C. SS 316L's molybdenum (PREN 25) raises the **critical pitting temperature** above the operating range, ensuring food safety and equipment longevity. Additionally, the **low carbon (≤0.03%)** of 316L prevents **intergranular corrosion** in the heat-affected zone after welding.

---

### Question B (Choice 2):

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, PCFTT601, Module 1, Q3]**

(a) Explain the mechanism of passivation in stainless steel with a suitable reaction. Why is the Cr content kept ≥ 10.5%? **(7 Marks)**

(b) A stainless steel pressure vessel for fruit juice storage has design pressure 0.4 MPa, diameter 1.2 m, material SS 316L with UTS = 485 MPa, joint efficiency 0.85, design life 12 years, and corrosion rate 0.04 mm/yr. Determine the required wall thickness as per ASME BPVC. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Passivation Mechanism:**

Stainless steel resists corrosion due to a self-healing, transparent **chromium oxide (Cr₂O₃)** passive film that forms spontaneously on the surface when chromium content is ≥ 10.5%. The governing reaction is:

$$4\text{Cr} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$$

**Why Cr ≥ 10.5%:**
- Empirically established as the threshold at which a continuous, defect-free passive film covers the entire alloy surface.
- Below 10.5% Cr, the passive layer is discontinuous and porous → iron underneath rusts.
- The film is **self-repairing** in oxygenated environments; if scratched, it reforms within microseconds.
- This film is the basis for all stainless steel grades used in food processing.

**(b) Wall Thickness Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Allowable stress (with safety factor n = 4):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= \frac{\sigma_{UTS}}{n} = \frac{485}{4} = 121.25 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Hoop stress equation (ASME BPVC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{pD}{2SE - 1.2p}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting values (p = 0.4 × 10⁶ Pa, D = 1.2 m):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{(0.4 \times 10^6)(1.2)}{2 \times (121.25 \times 10^6)(0.85) - 1.2 \times (0.4 \times 10^6)} \\[6pt]
t &= \frac{480{,}000}{206{,}125{,}000 - 480{,}000} \\[6pt]
t &= \frac{480{,}000}{205{,}645{,}000} \\[6pt]
t &= 2.334 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.334 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Corrosion allowance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CA &= CR \times DL = 0.04 \times 12 = 0.48 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Total required thickness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{total} &= 2.334 + 0.48 = 2.814 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Standard plate selection:**

$$t_{selected} = 3.0 \text{ mm (SS 316L standard plate)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors (Valuation Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor n = 4** — losing 2 marks; examiners expect S = σᵤ/4 for SS 316L.
> 2. **Ignoring the 1.2p term** in the denominator — only acceptable if p is negligible (< 10% of 2SE).
> 3. **Forgetting corrosion allowance** — losing 1 mark; examiners explicitly test this.
> 4. **Not rounding up to standard gauge** — final answer must be a commercially available thickness.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 — Materials of Construction: Key Takeaways**

- ☐ **Food safety triad** — non-toxicity, non-reactivity, cleanability.
- ☐ **Five material families** — ferrous, non-ferrous, polymers, ceramics, composites.
- ☐ **Stainless steel is the industry workhorse** — Cr ≥ 10.5% enables Cr₂O₃ passive layer.
- ☐ **SS 304** — 18 Cr / 8 Ni; PREN ≈ 18; for general food contact, low-chloride service.
- ☐ **SS 316L** — 16 Cr / 10 Ni / 2–3 Mo, low C; PREN ≈ 25; for chloride/acidic foods.
- ☐ **Duplex 2205** — for severe chloride and stress corrosion environments.
- ☐ **PREN formula** — $\text{PREN} = \%\text{Cr} + 3.3 \times \%\text{Mo} + 16 \times \%\text{N}$.
- ☐ **Sanitary surface finish** — Ra ≤ 0.8 μm (3-A / EHEDG standard).
- ☐ **Hoop stress** — $\sigma_h = pD / 2t$; **Longitudinal** — $\sigma_L = pD / 4t$.
- ☐ **Wall thickness (ASME BPVC)** — $t = pD / (2SE - 1.2p) + CA$.
- ☐ **Corrosion allowance** — $CA = CR \times DL$ (mm/yr × years).
- ☐ **Galvanic series rule** — couple like with like; isolate with dielectric unions.
- ☐ **Molybdenum stabilizes passive film** against chloride-induced pitting.
- ☐ **Low carbon (≤ 0.03%) grades (304L, 316L)** prevent intergranular corrosion after welding.
- ☐ **Aluminium and copper** — limited use; aluminum for thermal conductivity, copper restricted.
- ☐ **Polymers (HDPE, PP, PTFE, EPDM)** — temperature-specific applications for gaskets and pipes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
