topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure Vessel - Design of Cylindrical and Spherical Shell Subject to Internal and External Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure Vessel:** A container designed to hold gases or liquids at a pressure substantially different (higher or lower) from the ambient atmospheric pressure. These vessels are critical components in food processing plants, dairy industries, breweries, autoclaves, pasteurizers, and storage tanks.

**Cylindrical Shell:** A pressure vessel whose principal load-bearing geometry is a right circular cylinder, closed at both ends by hemispherical, ellipsoidal, or torispherical heads.

**Spherical Shell:** A pressure vessel whose geometry is a complete or partial sphere — offering the most uniform stress distribution and the highest strength-to-weight ratio.

In KTU food processing equipment design, the cylindrical shell is the **workhorse geometry** because it is **easy to fabricate, transport, and integrate with heads and nozzles**, while spherical shells are used where **high pressure and minimum material consumption** are priorities (e.g., LPG storage, dairy silos).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Balloon Analogy:** Imagine blowing up a long cylindrical balloon. You will notice two distinct things:
> 1. The rubber stretches **more around the circumference** (it tries to split lengthwise) — this represents the **hoop (circumferential) stress**, $\sigma_h$.
> 2. The rubber also stretches **along the length**, but less — this represents the **longitudinal (axial) stress**, $\sigma_l$.
> 3. A spherical balloon of the same diameter and pressure stretches **equally in all directions**, so the wall stress is **half** that of a cylinder.
> 4. If you suck air out (vacuum/external pressure), the same formulas apply but with opposite sign — the vessel tries to **collapse inward**.

### 1.3 Design Philosophy

In the **KTU 2024 scheme**, pressure vessel design follows the **strength-of-materials approach** combined with **code-based safety factors**. The vessel must withstand:

- **Internal Pressure** ($P_i$) — most common case (steam boilers, autoclaves, dairy processing tanks).
- **External Pressure** ($P_e$) — vacuum vessels, condensers, refrigerated storage.

To ensure safety, designers use the **maximum allowable working pressure** ($P_{design}$), apply a **corrosion allowance** ($C_A$), account for **joint efficiency** ($\eta$), and use a **factor of safety** ($n$) on the ultimate tensile strength.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Pressure Vessel Design:** *The wall must be thick enough to keep the calculated stress BELOW the allowable stress of the material at the design temperature, for the entire design life of the vessel.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Assumptions (Thin-Walled Vessel Theory)

For a vessel to be analyzed using simple formulas, it must satisfy the **thin-walled assumption**:

$$\frac{t}{D} \le \frac{1}{10} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{t}{r} \le \frac{1}{20}$$

Where:
- $t$ = wall thickness
- $D$ = inner diameter
- $r$ = inner radius

> [!NOTE]
> When this condition is violated, the vessel is **thick-walled** and must be analyzed using **Lamé's equations**.

### 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

#### **A. Hoop Stress (Circumferential Stress) — Cylindrical Shell**

**Step 1:** Imagine cutting the cylinder longitudinally along its axis into two halves.

**Step 2:** The internal pressure pushes the two halves apart. Consider the projected area:
$$A_{projected} = D \cdot L$$

**Step 3:** The internal pressure force pushing the halves apart:
$$F_{pressure} = P_i \cdot D \cdot L$$

**Step 4:** This force is resisted by the **two walls** (top and bottom of the cut), each with cross-sectional area $t \cdot L$.

**Step 5:** Equilibrium gives the hoop stress:
$$\sigma_h = \frac{P_i \cdot D}{2t}$$

#### **B. Longitudinal Stress (Axial Stress) — Cylindrical Shell**

**Step 1:** Imagine cutting the cylinder perpendicular to its axis (along a diametral plane).

**Step 2:** The internal pressure pushes on the end cap of area $\frac{\pi D^2}{4}$.

**Step 3:** Pressure force on the cap:
$$F_{pressure} = P_i \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 4:** This force is resisted by the **circumferential wall** of area $\pi D \cdot t$.

**Step 5:** Equilibrium gives the longitudinal stress:
$$\sigma_l = \frac{P_i \cdot D}{4t}$$

#### **C. Spherical Shell Stress**

**Step 1:** Cut the sphere along any great circle (diameter plane).

**Step 2:** Projected area = $\pi r^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$.

**Step 3:** Resisting area = $\pi D \cdot t$.

**Step 4:** Equilibrium gives:
$$\sigma_s = \frac{P_i \cdot D}{4t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** For the **same pressure and diameter**, a spherical shell carries only **half the stress** of a cylindrical shell, meaning it requires only **half the wall thickness**. This is why pressure cookers, LPG tanks, and milk storage spheres are spherical.

### 2.3 Maximum Shear Stress Theory — Failure Criterion

The **maximum shear stress** in a thin cylindrical shell acts on a plane at **45°** to the axis:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \frac{P_i D}{8t}$$

### 2.4 Design Equations (Including Safety Factors)

The **required wall thickness** for a cylindrical shell under internal pressure is:

$$t = \frac{P_i \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta - P_i} + C_A$$

The **required wall thickness** for a spherical shell under internal pressure is:

$$t = \frac{P_i \cdot D}{4 \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta - P_i} + C_A$$

Where:
- $P_i$ = internal design pressure (**MPa**)
- $D$ = inner diameter (**mm**)
- $\sigma_{allow}$ = allowable stress = $\sigma_{uts}/n$ or $\sigma_{y}/n$ (**MPa**)
- $\eta$ = joint efficiency (**0 to 1**)
- $C_A$ = corrosion allowance (**mm**)

### 2.5 Cylindrical Shell Under External Pressure

For **external pressure**, the vessel can fail by **elastic instability (buckling/collapse)** rather than yielding. The critical buckling pressure (for a long, perfect cylinder) is:

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 \cdot E}{1 - \mu^2} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^3$$

Where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus of the material
- $\mu$ = Poisson's ratio
- $t$ = wall thickness
- $D$ = outer diameter

For **short cylinders** with length $L$, a correction factor is used. In code-based design (ASME BPVC Section VIII), **charts** are used to determine allowable external pressure.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P_i D}{2t}$ | Hoop stress in thin cylinder | MPa |
| 2 | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P_i D}{4t}$ | Longitudinal stress in thin cylinder | MPa |
| 3 | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{P_i D}{4t}$ | Stress in thin sphere | MPa |
| 4 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{P_i D}{8t}$ | Maximum shear stress in cylinder | MPa |
| 5 | $t = \dfrac{P_i D}{2 \sigma_{allow} \eta - P_i} + C_A$ | Cylinder thickness (internal) | mm |
| 6 | $t = \dfrac{P_i D}{4 \sigma_{allow} \eta - P_i} + C_A$ | Sphere thickness (internal) | mm |
| 7 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\mu^2}\left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3$ | Critical buckling pressure (long cylinder) | MPa |
| 8 | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{uts}}{n}$ or $\dfrac{\sigma_y}{n}$ | Allowable stress | MPa |
| 9 | $P_{design} = P_{working} + P_{static}$ | Design pressure | MPa |
| 10 | $\dfrac{t}{D} \le 0.1$ | Thin-wall validity criterion | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** In numerical problems, always include **corrosion allowance** and **joint efficiency** in the final expression, even if the problem does not mention them. Examiners award marks for "completeness of design assumptions."

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food processing tanks** (milk, juice, wine) — vertical cylindrical vessels with conical or flat heads.
- **Autoclaves & retorts** — horizontal cylinders, often under 1.5–3 bar steam pressure.
- **Storage silos** — vertical cylinders for grains/flour (may be under slight vacuum during discharge).
- **Vacuum pans** (sugar industry) — external pressure design governs.
- **Refrigerated LPG bullets** — spherical ends, cylindrical body, vacuum insulated.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Shell

**Starting Setup:** Consider a thin cylindrical pressure vessel of inner diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, and length $L$, subjected to an internal gauge pressure $P_i$.

**Step 1 — Section the Cylinder:** Imagine a longitudinal (axial) cut that splits the cylinder into two identical halves.

**Step 2 — Identify Forces:** The internal pressure acts radially outward on every part of the inner wall. After sectioning, the net force tending to separate the two halves is the integral of $P_i$ over the **projected area** (the rectangle of length $L$ and width $D$).

**Step 3 — Pressure Force:**
$$F_P = P_i \times (\text{projected area}) = P_i \times D \times L$$

**Step 4 — Resisting Force:** The two cut walls each have a cross-sectional area of $t \times L$. Both walls contribute to resisting the separation.

**Step 5 — Resisting Force from Wall Stress:**
$$F_R = \sigma_h \times (\text{resisting area}) = \sigma_h \times 2 \times t \times L$$

**Step 6 — Apply Equilibrium** ($F_P = F_R$):
$$P_i \cdot D \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$$

**Step 7 — Cancel Common Terms** ($L \neq 0$):
$$P_i \cdot D = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t$$

**Step 8 — Solve for Hoop Stress:**
$$\boxed{\sigma_h = \frac{P_i D}{2 t}}$$

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Longitudinal Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Shell

**Starting Setup:** Same vessel, now section it transversely (perpendicular to the axis) by an imaginary plane cutting the cylinder into two pieces.

**Step 1 — Identify Forces:** The internal pressure acts on the circular end area of one piece.

**Step 2 — End Cap Area:**
$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure Force on the End Cap:**
$$F_P = P_i \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Resisting Area:** The wall of the cylinder, cut along the circular circumference, has an area:
$$A_{wall} = \pi D \times t$$

**Step 5 — Resisting Force from Wall Stress:**
$$F_R = \sigma_l \times \pi D \times t$$

**Step 6 — Apply Equilibrium** ($F_P = F_R$):
$$P_i \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma_l \cdot \pi D \cdot t$$

**Step 7 — Cancel Common Factors** ($\pi D \neq 0$):
$$\frac{P_i D}{4} = \sigma_l \cdot t$$

**Step 8 — Solve for Longitudinal Stress:**
$$\boxed{\sigma_l = \frac{P_i D}{4 t}}$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Stress in a Thin Spherical Shell

**Starting Setup:** Consider a thin spherical shell of inner diameter $D$ and wall thickness $t$ subjected to internal pressure $P_i$.

**Step 1 — Section the Sphere:** Cut the sphere along any plane through its center (a great circle). This separates it into two identical hemispherical halves.

**Step 2 — Identify the Projected Area:** The projected (silhouette) area of the hemisphere on the cut plane is a circle of diameter $D$:
$$A_{proj} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Pressure Force:**
$$F_P = P_i \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Resisting Area:** The cut wall is a circular ring of circumference $\pi D$ and thickness $t$:
$$A_{wall} = \pi D \times t$$

**Step 5 — Resisting Force:**
$$F_R = \sigma_s \times \pi D \times t$$

**Step 6 — Apply Equilibrium:**
$$P_i \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma_s \cdot \pi D \cdot t$$

**Step 7 — Simplify:**
$$\sigma_s = \frac{P_i D}{4 t}$$

**Step 8 — Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\sigma_s = \frac{P_i D}{4 t}}$$

### 3.4 Comparative Stress Matrix — Cylinder vs Sphere

| Pressure Component | Cylindrical Shell | Spherical Shell | Ratio (Cyl : Sphere) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hoop/Circumferential | $\dfrac{P_i D}{2t}$ | $\dfrac{P_i D}{4t}$ | $2 : 1$ |
| Longitudinal/Axial | $\dfrac{P_i D}{4t}$ | (Same as hoop) | $1 : 1$ |
| Maximum Shear | $\dfrac{P_i D}{8t}$ | $0$ (biaxial equal) | — |
| Principal Stress Ratio $\sigma_h : \sigma_l$ | $2 : 1$ | $1 : 1$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the cylinder's hoop stress DOUBLE its longitudinal stress?** Because the same internal pressure has to push the cylinder apart over **two diameters of material** in the hoop direction, but only **one diameter of material** in the axial direction.

### 3.5 Derivation — Critical External Pressure for a Long Thin Cylinder

For a long, perfect, thin cylinder under uniform external pressure, classical elastic buckling theory (Bresse, 1859) gives the critical pressure:

**Step 1 — Start with the Differential Equation of Buckled Equilibrium:**
For an infinitely long cylinder of radius $R$ and thickness $t$, the radial deflection $w$ satisfies:

$$D_{flex} \cdot \nabla^8 w + \frac{E \cdot t}{R^2} \cdot \frac{\partial^4 w}{\partial x^4} + P_{cr} \cdot \nabla^4 \left(\nabla^2 w\right) = 0$$

Where $D_{flex} = \dfrac{E t^3}{12(1-\mu^2)}$ is the flexural rigidity.

**Step 2 — Assume a Sinusoidal Buckle Pattern:**
$$w = w_0 \sin\left(\frac{m \pi x}{L}\right) \sin\left(\frac{n y}{R}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Critical Load** (classical result by Timoshenko):
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 \cdot E}{1 - \mu^2} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^3$$

**Step 4 — Practical Modification (Mises, 1913):** For real cylinders with imperfections, a knockdown factor of $\approx 0.75$ is applied:
$$P_{cr, real} \approx 0.75 \times \frac{2 \cdot E}{1 - \mu^2} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for KTU Board Exam:** When asked about external pressure failure, the examiner expects **two** failure modes: (1) yielding (same formula as internal but with negative sign) and (2) elastic buckling (collapse). Always mention BOTH.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A cylindrical pressure vessel in a dairy pasteurizer has an inner diameter of $1000 \text{ mm}$ and a wall thickness of $10 \text{ mm}$. The internal design pressure is $1.5 \text{ MPa}$. Calculate:
(a) Hoop stress
(b) Longitudinal stress
(c) Maximum shear stress

**Solution:**

**(a) Hoop Stress:**
$$\sigma_h = \frac{P_i D}{2 t} = \frac{1.5 \times 1000}{2 \times 10} = \frac{1500}{20} = 75 \text{ MPa}$$

**(b) Longitudinal Stress:**
$$\sigma_l = \frac{P_i D}{4 t} = \frac{1.5 \times 1000}{4 \times 10} = \frac{1500}{40} = 37.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**(c) Maximum Shear Stress:**
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \frac{75 - 37.5}{2} = 18.75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Check Thin-Wall Validity:**
$$\frac{t}{D} = \frac{10}{1000} = 0.01 \le 0.1 \;\; \checkmark \text{ (Thin-wall assumption valid)}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pressure Vessel Stress System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Internal Pressure Pi] --> B[Force on Projected Area]
    A --> C[Force on End Cap]
    B --> D[Hoop Stress σh = Pi D / 2t]
    C --> E[Longitudinal Stress σl = Pi D / 4t]
    D --> F[Maximum Shear Stress τmax]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Failure Mode?}
    G -- Yielding --> H[Increase Thickness or σallow]
    G -- Buckling --> I[Stiffener Rings Required]
    D --> J[Spherical Shell]
    J --> K[σs = Pi D / 4t - Half of Cylinder]
    H --> L[Final Design]
    I --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Apply Corrosion Allowance CA]
    L --> N[Apply Joint Efficiency η]
    M --> O[Verified Safe Design]
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Cylindrical Shell — Stress Distribution Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Internal Pressure Pi] --> B[Projected Rectangular Area D×L]
    A --> C[Circular End Area πD²/4]
    B --> D[2 Cut Walls of t×L]
    C --> E[Ring Wall of πD×t]
    D --> F[Hoop Stress σh = PiD/2t]
    E --> G[Longitudinal Stress σl = PiD/4t]
    F --> H[Hoop Stress is HIGHER]
    G --> I[Axial Stress is LOWER]
    H --> J[Critical for Design]
    I --> J
```

### 4.3 Design Decision Matrix — Cylindrical vs Spherical Shell

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Pressure Vessel Design] --> Q1{Diameter fixed?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Pressure > 10 MPa?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Sphere[Choose SPHERICAL Shell]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Transportation needed?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Cyl[Choose CYLINDRICAL Shell]
    Q3 -- No --> Sphere
    Q1 -- No --> Q4{Material cost critical?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Sphere
    Q4 -- No --> Cyl
    Cyl --> Step1[Add Heads: Hemispherical or Torispherical]
    Sphere --> Step2[Add Nozzles and Manways]
    Step1 --> Final[Verify σh, σl ≤ σallow]
    Step2 --> Final
```

### 4.4 Schematic Representation of Stress States

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CYL[Cylindrical Shell - Biaxial Stress State]
        C1[Hoop Stress σh = Pi D / 2t - Circumferential]
        C2[Longitudinal Stress σl = Pi D / 4t - Axial]
        C3[Radial Stress σr ≈ 0 - Neglected in thin wall]
    end
    subgraph SPH[Spherical Shell - Equal Biaxial Stress State]
        S1[Meridional Stress σm = Pi D / 4t]
        S2[Hoop Stress σh = Pi D / 4t]
        S3[σm = σh - Uniform biaxial]
    end
    CYL --> C4[σh is Maximum Principal Stress]
    SPH --> S4[No Preferred Failure Direction]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway from the Diagrams:** A spherical shell has **no preferred failure direction** because both principal stresses are equal. This is why spherical pressure vessels are inherently safer and require less material for the same pressure rating.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**Define hoop stress and longitudinal stress in a thin cylindrical pressure vessel. Write the expressions for both.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Hoop Stress:** The circumferential (tangential) stress developed in the wall of a cylindrical pressure vessel due to internal pressure. It acts along the circumference of the shell.
> $$\sigma_h = \frac{P_i D}{2t}$$
>
> **Longitudinal Stress:** The axial stress developed in the wall of a cylindrical pressure vessel due to internal pressure acting on the end caps. It acts along the length of the shell.
> $$\sigma_l = \frac{P_i D}{4t}$$
>
> The hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress in a thin cylindrical shell.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for definition each + 1 mark for correct expression)*

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**Why is a spherical pressure vessel preferred over a cylindrical one for high-pressure applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A spherical pressure vessel is preferred because:
1. **Lower stress:** For the same diameter and pressure, the wall stress in a sphere ($\sigma_s = P_i D / 4t$) is **half** that in a cylinder ($\sigma_h = P_i D / 2t$).
2. **Material saving:** A sphere requires **half the wall thickness** for the same allowable stress, reducing material cost.
3. **No preferred failure direction:** Both principal stresses are equal in a sphere (equal biaxial state), giving it **inherently uniform strength** in all directions.
4. **Larger volume per unit surface area:** A sphere encloses the maximum volume for a given surface area, further reducing material consumption.

*(Allocation: 1.5 marks for stress comparison + 1.5 marks for material & structural advantages)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Part B question in the End Semester Examination (ESE) provides an **internal choice** between two questions of equal marks. You must answer either **Question A or Question B**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Cylindrical Shell Design**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

A cylindrical pressure vessel in a food processing plant is used for steam sterilization at $200 \text{ °C}$. The vessel has an **inner diameter of $1.2 \text{ m}$**, and is fabricated from **SA-516 Grade 70 carbon steel** (ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{uts} = 485 \text{ MPa}$, yield strength $\sigma_y = 260 \text{ MPa}$). The **maximum working pressure is $2.0 \text{ MPa}$** (gauge). The longitudinal joint is **double-welded** with full radiography ($\eta = 1.0$), and the circumferential joint is **single-welded** with spot radiography ($\eta = 0.85$). A **corrosion allowance of $2 \text{ mm}$** is required.

**(a)** Calculate the **required wall thickness** for the cylindrical shell using both hoop and longitudinal stress considerations. State which one governs. (7 marks)

**(b)** If the vessel is to operate under an **external pressure of $0.05 \text{ MPa}$**, check whether the calculated thickness is safe against elastic buckling. Given: $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, $\mu = 0.30$. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution — Question A**

###### **Part (a) — Thickness Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Calculate Design Pressure** (1 mark)
$$P_{design} = P_{working} + \text{static head} \approx 2.0 \text{ MPa}$$
*(For simplicity in this problem, we use design pressure = working pressure, but examiners appreciate students mentioning the addition of static head.)*

**Step 2: Select Allowable Stress** (1 mark)
For carbon steel at elevated temperature, design codes use the lower of:
$$\sigma_{allow} = \min\left(\frac{\sigma_{uts}}{n_1}, \frac{\sigma_y}{n_2}\right)$$

Using $n_1 = 4$ and $n_2 = 1.6$ (typical for pressure vessels):
$$\sigma_{allow,uts} = \frac{485}{4} = 121.25 \text{ MPa}$$
$$\sigma_{allow,yield} = \frac{260}{1.6} = 162.5 \text{ MPa}$$

Take the lower (governing) value:
$$\sigma_{allow} = 121.25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Apply Governing Formula (Hoop Stress Governs)** (2 marks)

For cylindrical shells, hoop stress is the governing criterion. The required thickness for hoop stress is:
$$t_{hoop} = \frac{P_i \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta - P_i} + C_A$$

**Step 4: Substitute Values** (1 mark)
$$t_{hoop} = \frac{2.0 \times 1200}{2 \times 121.25 \times 1.0 - 2.0} + 2$$

$$t_{hoop} = \frac{2400}{242.5 - 2.0} + 2 = \frac{2400}{240.5} + 2$$

$$t_{hoop} = 9.98 + 2 = 11.98 \approx 12 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Check with Longitudinal Stress** (1 mark)
$$t_{long} = \frac{P_i \cdot D}{4 \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta - P_i} + C_A$$
$$t_{long} = \frac{2.0 \times 1200}{4 \times 121.25 \times 1.0 - 2.0} + 2 = \frac{2400}{485 - 2.0} + 2$$
$$t_{long} = 4.97 + 2 = 6.97 \approx 7 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion** (1 mark)
Since $t_{hoop} > t_{long}$, the **hoop stress governs the design**.
$$\boxed{t_{required} = 12 \text{ mm (including corrosion allowance)}}$$

###### **Part (b) — External Pressure Buckling Check (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Calculate Critical Buckling Pressure** (3 marks)

Using Timoshenko's formula for a long thin cylinder:
$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 E}{1 - \mu^2} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^3$$

Given: $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200{,}000 \text{ MPa}$, $\mu = 0.30$, $t = 12 \text{ mm}$, $D = 1200 \text{ mm}$ (assuming $D \approx D_{inner}$ for thin wall).

$$P_{cr} = \frac{2 \times 200{,}000}{1 - (0.30)^2} \cdot \left(\frac{12}{1200}\right)^3$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{400{,}000}{1 - 0.09} \cdot (0.01)^3$$

$$P_{cr} = \frac{400{,}000}{0.91} \times 10^{-6}$$

$$P_{cr} = 439{,}560.4 \times 10^{-6} = 0.4396 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Apply Knockdown Factor for Real Cylinders** (1 mark)
$$P_{cr, real} = 0.75 \times P_{cr} = 0.75 \times 0.4396 = 0.3297 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Apply Safety Factor** (1 mark)
Using $n = 4$ (typical for buckling):
$$P_{cr, allowable} = \frac{P_{cr, real}}{n} = \frac{0.3297}{4} = 0.0824 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Compare with Applied External Pressure** (1 mark)
Applied external pressure: $P_e = 0.05 \text{ MPa}$

Since $P_e = 0.05 \text{ MPa} < P_{cr, allowable} = 0.0824 \text{ MPa}$,

$$\boxed{\text{The vessel is SAFE against elastic buckling.}}$$

**Step 5: Check Thin-Wall Validity** (1 mark)
$$\frac{t}{D} = \frac{12}{1200} = 0.01 \le 0.1 \;\; \checkmark$$
The thin-wall assumption is valid.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Spherical Shell Design (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

A **spherical pressure vessel** is used in a dairy plant to store pasteurized milk at a temperature of $80 \text{ °C}$. The vessel has an **inner diameter of $2.0 \text{ m}$** and is made of **stainless steel AISI 304** ($\sigma_{uts} = 515 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_y = 205 \text{ MPa}$, $E = 193 \text{ GPa}$, $\mu = 0.29$). The **maximum internal pressure is $0.8 \text{ MPa}$** (gauge). The joint is **double-welded** ($\eta = 1.0$). Corrosion allowance = $1.5 \text{ mm}$.

**(a)** Calculate the **required wall thickness** of the spherical shell. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the **material volume** required for this spherical vessel with that of a cylindrical vessel of the same diameter, length, pressure, and allowable stress. (7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution — Question B**

###### **Part (a) — Spherical Shell Thickness (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Design Pressure** (1 mark)
$$P_{design} = 0.8 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Allowable Stress** (1 mark)
$$\sigma_{allow} = \min\left(\frac{515}{4}, \frac{205}{1.6}\right) = \min(128.75, 128.13) = 128.13 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Apply Spherical Shell Formula** (2 marks)
$$t = \frac{P_i \cdot D}{4 \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot \eta - P_i} + C_A$$

**Step 4: Substitute** (1 mark)
$$t = \frac{0.8 \times 2000}{4 \times 128.13 \times 1.0 - 0.8} + 1.5$$
$$t = \frac{1600}{512.52 - 0.8} + 1.5 = \frac{1600}{511.72} + 1.5$$
$$t = 3.127 + 1.5 = 4.627 \approx 5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Verify Thin-Wall Assumption** (1 mark)
$$\frac{t}{D} = \frac{5}{2000} = 0.0025 \le 0.1 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 6: Result** (1 mark)
$$\boxed{t_{required} = 5 \text{ mm (for spherical shell)}}$$

For comparison, a **cylindrical vessel** of the same diameter and pressure would require:
$$t_{cyl} = \frac{P_i D}{2 \sigma_{allow} \eta - P_i} + C_A = \frac{0.8 \times 2000}{2 \times 128.13 - 0.8} + 1.5 = 6.27 + 1.5 \approx 7.8 \text{ mm}$$

###### **Part (b) — Material Volume Comparison (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Spherical Surface Area** (1 mark)
$$A_{sphere} = \pi D^2 = \pi \times (2.0)^2 = 12.566 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Cylindrical Surface Area** (Assuming length = diameter for fair comparison) (2 marks)
$$A_{cyl} = \pi D L + 2 \times \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \pi D^2 + \frac{\pi D^2}{2} = \frac{3 \pi D^2}{2}$$

$$A_{cyl} = \frac{3 \pi \times (2.0)^2}{2} = \frac{3 \pi \times 4}{2} = 18.85 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Volume of Material — Spherical Vessel** (1 mark)
$$V_{sphere} = A_{sphere} \times t_{sphere} = 12.566 \times 0.005 = 0.0628 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4: Volume of Material — Cylindrical Vessel** (1 mark)
$$V_{cyl} = A_{cyl} \times t_{cyl} = 18.85 \times 0.0078 = 0.1470 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5: Comparison** (1 mark)
$$\frac{V_{sphere}}{V_{cyl}} = \frac{0.0628}{0.1470} = 0.427$$

$$\boxed{\text{The spherical vessel requires } \approx 57.3\% \text{ LESS material.}}$$

**Step 6: Conclusion** (1 mark)
> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** Despite higher fabrication complexity, spherical vessels offer **substantial material savings** for the same pressure capacity and storage volume. This is why large-scale milk storage tanks in modern dairies often use **spherical or near-spherical geometries**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the corrosion allowance** — Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if $C_A$ is omitted from the thickness formula.
> 2. **Confusing hoop and longitudinal formulas** — Always remember: $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$ for thin cylinders. Many students invert these.
> 3. **Using the wrong joint efficiency** — Read the problem carefully. Longitudinal joints have **higher** $\eta$ than circumferential joints.
> 4. **Not checking the thin-wall assumption** — If $t/D > 0.1$, the simple formulas are **invalid** and Lamé's equations must be used.
> 5. **Forgetting units** — Always convert $D$ to **mm** if $P$ is in **MPa** to get $t$ in **mm**.
> 6. **External pressure: only yielding analysis** — Always check **buckling** as well; it usually governs for thin vessels.
> 7. **Missing 45° shear plane** — Maximum shear stress in a cylinder is at 45° to the axis. Stating this is mandatory.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pressure Vessel Design (Cylindrical & Spherical Shells)**

- ✅ **Thin-wall criterion:** $t/D \le 0.1$ must be verified first.
- ✅ **Cylinder — Hoop stress:** $\sigma_h = P_i D / (2t)$ — **governs design**.
- ✅ **Cylinder — Longitudinal stress:** $\sigma_l = P_i D / (4t)$ — **half** of hoop.
- ✅ **Cylinder — Maximum shear stress:** $\tau_{max} = P_i D / (8t)$ — acts on **45° plane**.
- ✅ **Sphere — Stress (uniform biaxial):** $\sigma_s = P_i D / (4t)$ — **half** of cylinder's hoop stress.
- ✅ **Design thickness (cylinder):** $t = P_i D / (2\sigma_{allow} \eta - P_i) + C_A$.
- ✅ **Design thickness (sphere):** $t = P_i D / (4\sigma_{allow} \eta - P_i) + C_A$.
- ✅ **Allowable stress:** Use **lower** of $\sigma_{uts}/4$ and $\sigma_y/1.6$ (ASME-style).
- ✅ **Joint efficiency $\eta$:** Double-welded full radiography = 1.0; spot radiography = 0.85.
- ✅ **Corrosion allowance $C_A$:** Typically **1.5 to 3 mm**; always added at the end.
- ✅ **External pressure failure:** Two modes — **yielding** AND **buckling**. Critical buckling pressure for long cylinder: $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\mu^2}(t/D)^3$.
- ✅ **Spheres save material:** ~50% less wall thickness and ~40% less material volume than cylinders of same capacity.
- ✅ **Always state units** and verify **thin-wall validity** at the end of every numerical answer.
- ✅ **Real-world food processing applications:** Pasteurizers, autoclaves, retorts, milk storage silos, vacuum pans, LPG bullets.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Silo Design - Rankine Theory - Airy Equation - Janssen Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Silo Design - Rankine Theory - Airy Equation - Janssen Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — Silo Design: Rankine, Airy, and Janssen Theories

## 1.1 Core Definitions and Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Silo (Deep Bin) vs. Bin (Shallow Bin)**
> In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **silo** is a *deep* storage container in which the stored bulk solid (grain, flour, sugar, salt, plastic pellets) interacts with the walls so strongly that the **vertical pressure does NOT increase linearly with depth** — it asymptotically approaches a maximum. A **shallow bin** (or overhead hopper) is one in which the granular mass is not constrained laterally enough for the wall friction to relieve vertical load, so vertical pressure grows **linearly** as $\gamma h$.

### 1.1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definitions

- **Bulk Solid**: A granular, free-flowing, or cohesive assembly of discrete particles (e.g., wheat, maize, rice, soybean, sugar, milk powder) characterized by **bulk density** $\gamma$ (kN/m³ or kg/m³), **angle of internal friction** $\phi$ (deg), and **angle of wall friction** $\phi_w$ (deg).
- **Hopper**: The converging (pyramidal or conical) bottom section of a bin used to channel the bulk solid toward a discharge outlet.
- **Silage / Silo**: In food processing plants, a *silo* is the tall, vertical, axisymmetric storage vessel used to hold raw grains or intermediate powders, typically with a flat bottom or a hopper bottom.
- **Rankine Active State**: The stress condition inside a granular mass that is *allowed to relax laterally* (e.g., a bin wall moves slightly outward). The horizontal stress becomes a minimum fraction of the vertical stress.
- **Rankine Passive State**: The stress condition when the granular mass is *compressed laterally* (e.g., a wall pushes inward). The horizontal stress becomes a maximum multiple of the vertical stress.
- **Airy Equation (1885)**: An approximate analytical expression for **lateral wall pressure in shallow bins**, treating the bulk solid as a Coulomb-type continuum where vertical pressure = $\gamma h$ (no arching/bridging effect).
- **Janssen Equation (1895)**: The governing differential equation for **vertical and lateral pressures in deep bins/silos**, accounting for **wall friction** that carries a portion of the overburden weight, preventing the vertical stress from growing indefinitely with depth.

### 1.1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Coffee Silo" Analogy**
> Imagine a tall transparent cylinder filled with ground coffee.
> - If the cylinder is **short and squat (shallow bin)**, when you pour coffee in, the bottom feels almost the full weight of the column above it. The vertical pressure grows as $\gamma h$ — this is the **Airy** world.
> - If the cylinder is **tall and slender (deep bin / silo)**, the side walls are rough enough that they **grip the coffee** and *carry part of the weight*. The bottom does NOT feel the full hydrostatic weight; instead, the vertical pressure saturates to a limiting value $p_\infty$. This load transfer to the walls is the **Janssen** phenomenon.
> - The **Rankine coefficient** $K_a$ is simply the "ratio" telling you how much horizontal push the coffee exerts on the walls given the vertical pressure. For loose dry grains, $K_a$ is small (around 0.3–0.4), so lateral loads are manageable.

### 1.1.3 Typical Food-Grain Design Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> Standard food-grain values used in KTU board problems (textbook ranges, conservative design values):
>
> - **Wheat**: $\gamma \approx 7.8$–$8.0$ kN/m³, $\phi \approx 25°$–$30°$, $\mu_w = \tan\phi_w \approx 0.30$–$0.45$
> - **Rice (paddy)**: $\gamma \approx 5.8$ kN/m³, $\phi \approx 30°$–$35°$
> - **Maize (corn)**: $\gamma \approx 7.5$ kN/m³, $\phi \approx 28°$–$32°$
> - **Soybean**: $\gamma \approx 7.2$ kN/m³, $\phi \approx 25°$–$30°$
> - **Granulated sugar**: $\gamma \approx 8.0$–$8.5$ kN/m³, $\phi \approx 35°$–$40°$

The **transition depth** that separates Airy (shallow) behavior from Janssen (deep) behavior is the **characteristic depth**:

$$h_c = \frac{R}{\mu_w K_a}$$

where $R = A/U$ is the **hydraulic radius** (cross-sectional area divided by wetted perimeter).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rankine Theory: Active and Passive States

Rankine (1857) considered a semi-infinite granular mass with a horizontal free surface and no wall adhesion. At any depth $h$, the vertical stress is $\sigma_v = \gamma h$. The maximum and minimum possible horizontal stresses (Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope) are:

$$K_a = \frac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right) \quad \text{(active)}$$

$$K_p = \frac{1+\sin\phi}{1-\sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right) \quad \text{(passive)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule of Thumb**: For a *discharging* silo, designers usually use the **active** state ($K_a$) because the bulk solid flows and the wall moves outward microscopically. For a *filling* silo, some codes (Eurocode 3 / IS 9178) use $K_a$ on the wall and the lower-bound switch value $K = 1.0$ at the bottom (switch pressure). For a first course in food equipment, **always use $K = K_a$** unless stated otherwise.

### 2.1.1 Why Rankine Coefficients Matter in Silo Design

- The lateral wall pressure $p_h = K_a \sigma_v$ governs the **hoop stress in the steel shell** (cylindrical silos).
- The vertical wall friction $w = \mu_w p_h$ governs the **downward load on the silo foundation** (which is therefore *less* than the full weight of contents — exactly the Janssen effect).
- $K_p$ is used in **retaining wall** analysis or wedge-pushing problems, but is *not* a typical silo-side coefficient in food bin design.

## 2.2 Airy Equation — Lateral Pressure in Shallow Bins

Airy (1885) assumed the bulk solid behaves like a Coulomb continuum with vertical pressure $\sigma_v = \gamma h$ (no arching). The lateral pressure on a vertical wall is then:

$$p_h = K_a\,\gamma h$$

This is essentially a **linear hydrostatic** variation with depth, similar in form to a fluid, but scaled down by $K_a < 1$. The **vertical wall friction** per unit perimeter is:

$$w = \mu_w\,p_h = \mu_w K_a\,\gamma h$$

Airy's equation is **valid when** $h < h_c = R/(\mu_w K_a)$, i.e., when the bin is short and wide (typical for overhead hoppers and small day-bins in flour mills).

## 2.3 Janssen Equation — Lateral and Vertical Pressure in Deep Bins/Silos

Janssen (1895) recognized that in a *deep* bin, the walls carry part of the weight via friction, so $\sigma_v$ does NOT grow indefinitely. The governing ODE for vertical pressure is:

$$\frac{d\sigma_v}{dh} + \frac{K_a\,\mu_w}{R}\,\sigma_v = \gamma$$

whose solution is the **Janssen vertical-pressure profile**:

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_v(h) = \frac{\gamma R}{K_a \mu_w}\!\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{K_a \mu_w\,h}{R}\right)\right]\;}$$

The **lateral pressure** is then $p_h = K_a \sigma_v$, and the **limiting (asymptotic) pressures** as $h \to \infty$ are:

$$\sigma_{v,\infty} = \frac{\gamma R}{K_a \mu_w}, \qquad p_{h,\infty} = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu_w}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Physical Insight (KTU favourite)**
> Notice that $p_{h,\infty}$ depends on $\mu_w$ but **NOT** on $K_a$. The wall is gripped by friction, and the *maximum* lateral thrust is set by the wall-friction angle alone. Doubling the internal friction angle (smaller $K_a$) reduces $\sigma_{v,\infty}$ proportionally because the wall is *asked* to take more load.

## 2.4 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols and Units**: $\gamma$ in kN/m³, $h$ in m, $R$ in m, $\mu_w$ dimensionless, $\phi$ in degrees, $\sigma_v, p_h$ in kN/m² (or kPa).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rankine active coefficient | $K_a = (1-\sin\phi)/(1+\sin\phi)$ | Any bulk solid |
| 2 | Rankine passive coefficient | $K_p = (1+\sin\phi)/(1-\sin\phi) = 1/K_a$ | Retaining / pushing |
| 3 | Airy lateral pressure | $p_h = K_a\,\gamma h$ | Shallow bin, $h < h_c$ |
| 4 | Airy wall friction / unit perimeter | $w = \mu_w K_a \gamma h$ | Shallow bin |
| 5 | Janssen vertical pressure | $\sigma_v = (\gamma R / K_a \mu_w)[1-\exp(-K_a\mu_w h / R)]$ | Deep bin, $h > h_c$ |
| 6 | Janssen lateral pressure | $p_h = K_a \sigma_v$ | Deep bin |
| 7 | Limiting vertical pressure | $\sigma_{v,\infty} = \gamma R / (K_a \mu_w)$ | $h \to \infty$ |
| 8 | Limiting lateral pressure | $p_{h,\infty} = \gamma R / \mu_w$ | $h \to \infty$ |
| 9 | Characteristic depth | $h_c = R / (\mu_w K_a)$ | Shallow/deep transition |
| 10 | Hydraulic radius (circle D) | $R = D/4$ | Cylindrical silo |
| 11 | Hydraulic radius (square a) | $R = a/4$ | Square bin |
| 12 | Hoop stress in cylindrical shell | $f_t = p_h D / (2 t)$ | Cylindrical silo wall |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Food Engineering

- **Flour mills and rice mills**: Janssen analysis is used to size the steel bins in the cleaning, conditioning, and storage sections; without it, designers would massively over-thick the walls near the bottom.
- **Hopper design**: The pressure profile feeds into the **Walters walker** or **Jenike flow** analysis that determines hopper angle and outlet size to ensure **mass flow** (no arching, no rat-holing).
- **Cost reduction**: Accurate Janssen analysis has been shown to reduce silo wall steel weight by **30–50%** compared with a naive Airy/linear calculation, especially for tall slender silos of diameter-to-height ratio $D/H < 1$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are written line-by-line. **No step is skipped.** Every algebraic transition is justified by a one-line comment.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rankine Active Pressure Coefficient

Consider a semi-infinite granular mass with a horizontal free surface. At depth $h$, the vertical stress is hydrostatic:

$$\sigma_v = \gamma h$$

The Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope gives a relationship between the major and minor principal stresses:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_3\,\tan^2\!\left(45° + \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right) + 2c\,\tan\!\left(45° + \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

For a cohesionless bulk solid (cohesion $c = 0$, valid for free-flowing grains in most KTU problems):

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_3\,\tan^2\!\left(45° + \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

In the **active state**, the granular mass extends horizontally and the vertical stress is the **major principal stress**: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_v$. The horizontal stress $\sigma_3 = p_h$ is the **minor principal stress**. Substituting:

$$\sigma_v = p_h\,\tan^2\!\left(45° + \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

Rearranging for $p_h$:

$$p_h = \sigma_v\,\tan^2\!\left(45° - \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

Defining the Rankine active coefficient:

$$\boxed{\;K_a = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi}\;}$$

This is obtained by the trigonometric identity $\tan^2(45°-\phi/2) = (1-\sin\phi)/(1+\sin\phi)$, applied using $\tan(45°-\phi/2) = (1-\sin\phi)/\cos\phi$ and squaring.

A similar argument with $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ interchanged gives $K_p = 1/K_a$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Janssen Equation

### 3.2.1 Geometry and Sign Convention

Consider a vertical-walled cylindrical silo of **cross-sectional area** $A$ (m²) and **wetted perimeter** $U$ (m). The **hydraulic radius** is $R = A/U$. For a circular section of diameter $D$:

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}, \qquad U = \pi D, \qquad R = \frac{A}{U} = \frac{D}{4}$$

Take $h$ as depth measured **downward from the free surface** of the bulk solid. Let $\sigma_v(h)$ be the mean vertical stress on a horizontal plane, $p_h(h)$ the lateral wall pressure, and $w(h)$ the upward wall friction stress acting on the bulk solid.

### 3.2.2 Force Balance on an Element of Thickness dh

Consider a thin horizontal slice of the bulk solid between depths $h$ and $h+dh$. Forces acting on it (in the vertical direction) are:

1. **Weight of the slice**: $\mathrm{d}W = \gamma\,A\,\mathrm{d}h$ (downward).
2. **Vertical pressure on the top face**: $\sigma_v(h)\,A$ (downward).
3. **Vertical pressure on the bottom face**: $-\sigma_v(h+\mathrm{d}h)\,A$ (upward reaction on the slice).
4. **Friction from the wall on the slice**: $w \cdot U \cdot \mathrm{d}h$ (upward, because the slice tends to settle and the wall grips it).

Equilibrium (sum of vertical forces = 0):

$$\sigma_v(h)\,A - \sigma_v(h+\mathrm{d}h)\,A + \gamma A\,\mathrm{d}h - w\,U\,\mathrm{d}h = 0$$

Divide by $A\,\mathrm{d}h$:

$$-\frac{\mathrm{d}\sigma_v}{\mathrm{d}h} + \gamma - \frac{w\,U}{A} = 0$$

Note that $U/A = 1/R$, so:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}\sigma_v}{\mathrm{d}h} = \gamma - \frac{w}{R}$$

### 3.2.3 Constitutive Link Between $w$, $p_h$, and $\sigma_v$

The wall friction is assumed proportional to the lateral wall pressure:

$$w = \mu_w\,p_h, \qquad \mu_w = \tan\phi_w$$

The lateral pressure is assumed to be a fixed fraction (Rankine active state) of the vertical stress:

$$p_h = K_a\,\sigma_v$$

Substituting both:

$$w = \mu_w K_a\,\sigma_v$$

### 3.2.4 The Governing First-Order Linear ODE

Insert $w = \mu_w K_a \sigma_v$ into the equilibrium equation:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}\sigma_v}{\mathrm{d}h} = \gamma - \frac{\mu_w K_a}{R}\,\sigma_v$$

Rearranged into the standard linear-ODE form:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\mathrm{d}\sigma_v}{\mathrm{d}h} + \frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}\,\sigma_v = \gamma\;}$$

### 3.2.5 Solution of the ODE

The integrating factor is:

$$\mathrm{IF}(h) = \exp\!\left(\int \frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}\,\mathrm{d}h\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}\,h\right)$$

Multiply the ODE by the IF:

$$\exp\!\left(\frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}h\right)\frac{\mathrm{d}\sigma_v}{\mathrm{d}h} + \frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}\exp\!\left(\frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}h\right)\sigma_v = \gamma\exp\!\left(\frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}h\right)$$

The left-hand side is now an exact derivative:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}h}\!\left[\sigma_v\exp\!\left(\frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}h\right)\right] = \gamma\exp\!\left(\frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}h\right)$$

Integrate both sides with respect to $h$:

$$\sigma_v\exp\!\left(\frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}h\right) = \gamma\cdot\frac{R}{K_a \mu_w}\exp\!\left(\frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}h\right) + C_1$$

Solve for $\sigma_v$:

$$\sigma_v(h) = \frac{\gamma R}{K_a \mu_w} + C_1\exp\!\left(-\frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}h\right)$$

### 3.2.6 Boundary Condition

At the free surface ($h = 0$), if there is no surcharge, $\sigma_v(0) = 0$. Applying this:

$$0 = \frac{\gamma R}{K_a \mu_w} + C_1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad C_1 = -\frac{\gamma R}{K_a \mu_w}$$

### 3.2.7 Final Janssen Solution

$$\boxed{\;\sigma_v(h) = \frac{\gamma R}{K_a \mu_w}\!\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}h\right)\right]\;}$$

The lateral wall pressure is then $p_h(h) = K_a \sigma_v(h)$:

$$\boxed{\;p_h(h) = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu_w}\!\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{K_a \mu_w}{R}h\right)\right]\;}$$

### 3.2.8 Limiting (Asymptotic) Behaviour

As $h \to \infty$, the exponential term vanishes:

$$\sigma_{v,\infty} = \frac{\gamma R}{K_a \mu_w}, \qquad p_{h,\infty} = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu_w}$$

This is the **maximum vertical and lateral stress** the silo will ever experience, no matter how tall you build it. The fraction of "full hydrostatic" pressure actually realized at depth $h$ is:

$$\frac{\sigma_v(h)}{\gamma h} = \frac{R}{K_a \mu_w h}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{K_a \mu_w h}{R}\right)\right]$$

## 3.3 Sample Numerical Problem (Worked)

> **Problem**: A cylindrical silo of diameter $D = 4$ m stores wheat with $\gamma = 8.0$ kN/m³, $\phi = 30°$, $\mu_w = 0.40$. Find: (i) $K_a$, (ii) $h_c$, (iii) $\sigma_v$ and $p_h$ at $h = 10$ m, (iv) limiting pressures.

**Solution**:

(i) Rankine active coefficient:

$$K_a = \frac{1-\sin 30°}{1+\sin 30°} = \frac{1-0.5}{1+0.5} = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

(ii) Hydraulic radius and characteristic depth:

$$R = \frac{D}{4} = \frac{4}{4} = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

$$h_c = \frac{R}{\mu_w K_a} = \frac{1.0}{0.40 \times 0.333} = \frac{1.0}{0.1333} = 7.5 \text{ m}$$

So at $h = 10$ m $> h_c$, we are in the **deep (Janssen) regime** — Janssen's equation is mandatory.

(iii) Janssen vertical pressure at $h = 10$ m:

$$\sigma_v = \frac{8.0 \times 1.0}{0.333 \times 0.40}\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{0.333 \times 0.40}{1.0}\times 10\right)\right]$$

$$\sigma_v = \frac{8.0}{0.1333}\left[1 - e^{-1.333}\right] = 60.0 \times \left[1 - 0.2636\right] = 60.0 \times 0.7364$$

$$\sigma_v \approx 44.2 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

Lateral pressure:

$$p_h = K_a \sigma_v = 0.333 \times 44.2 \approx 14.7 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

For comparison, the **Airy (linear)** answer would be $p_h^{\text{Airy}} = K_a \gamma h = 0.333 \times 8.0 \times 10 = 26.7$ kN/m² — Janssen gives **45% less lateral load** than the naive Airy estimate.

(iv) Limiting pressures:

$$\sigma_{v,\infty} = \frac{\gamma R}{K_a \mu_w} = \frac{8.0 \times 1.0}{0.1333} = 60.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$p_{h,\infty} = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu_w} = \frac{8.0 \times 1.0}{0.40} = 20.0 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

So even a *hypothetically infinite* silo would only need to be designed for $p_{h,\infty} = 20$ kN/m² on the wall.

## 3.4 Comparison Table: Airy vs. Janssen

| Depth regime | Vertical stress $\sigma_v$ | Lateral stress $p_h$ | Wall friction $w$ | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $h \ll h_c$ (shallow, Airy) | $\gamma h$ (linear) | $K_a \gamma h$ | $\mu_w K_a \gamma h$ | Overhead bins, day bins |
| $h \gg h_c$ (deep, Janssen) | $(\gamma R/K_a\mu_w)[1-e^{-K_a\mu_w h/R}]$ | $K_a \sigma_v$ | $\mu_w K_a \sigma_v$ | Tall silos, storage tanks |
| $h \to \infty$ (limit) | $\gamma R/(K_a\mu_w)$ | $\gamma R/\mu_w$ | $\gamma R/R = \gamma$ | Asymptote |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Matrix — Why the Wall Friction Equals $\gamma$ at $h\to\infty$**: Substituting $w = \mu_w p_h$ and $p_h = \gamma R/\mu_w$ gives $w = \gamma$, which is a remarkable result: in a *very deep* silo, the **upward friction on any horizontal slice equals the full weight of the slice per unit area**, so the bottom of the silo carries essentially the weight of the bottom-most slice only — not the entire column. This is the practical reason tall silos are feasible at all.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Silo Design Methodology — Block Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the KTU 2024 Scheme silo-design workflow as a block-level functional architecture, starting from bulk-solid property input and ending at the wall thickness specification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Granular Food Material Selection] --> B[Measure Properties: gamma, phi, mu_w]
    B --> C[Choose Silo Geometry: Diameter D, Height H]
    C --> D[Compute Hydraulic Radius R = A / U]
    D --> E[Compute Rankine Ka = 1 minus sin phi over 1 plus sin phi]
    E --> F[Compute Characteristic Depth hc = R divided by mu_w times Ka]
    F --> G{Is H greater than hc?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Janssen Regime: Use Janssen Equation]
    G -- No --> I[Airy Regime: Use Linear p_h = Ka gamma h]
    H --> J[Compute sigma_v and p_h vs depth]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Wall Friction w = mu_w times p_h]
    K --> L[Compute Hoop Stress ft = p_h times D over 2t]
    L --> M[Iterate t to satisfy allowable stress]
    M --> N[Design Hopper and Discharge Outlet]
    N --> O[End: Final Silo Wall Thickness and Drawings]
```

## 4.2 Pressure Distribution Comparison — Airy vs. Janssen

The next diagram compares the *physical* vertical stress distribution in a shallow vs. deep bin, drawn as a block-level comparison (since a true stress profile is a 1D plot and not a Mermaid graph).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SHALLOW [Shallow Bin - Airy Regime - h less than hc]
        A1[Free Surface sigma_v = 0] --> A2[Depth h: sigma_v grows LINEARLY as gamma h]
        A2 --> A3[Bottom: sigma_v_max = gamma H FULL weight of column]
    end
    subgraph DEEP [Deep Silo - Janssen Regime - h greater than hc]
        B1[Free Surface sigma_v = 0] --> B2[Depth h_c: stress reaches 63 percent of limit]
        B2 --> B3[Depth greater than 3h_c: stress SATURATES at sigma_v_infinity = gamma R over Ka mu_w]
    end
    SHALLOW -- Compare at H identical --> DEEP
```

## 4.3 Free-Body of a Silo Slice

The classical free-body diagram used in the Janssen derivation, rendered as a Mermaid block diagram (rather than a hand-drawn sketch, which cannot be done in Mermaid).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top[Top face: sigma_v at depth h times Area A acting DOWN] --> Slice[Horizontal Slice of thickness dh at depth h]
    Bot[Bottom face: sigma_v at depth h plus dh times Area A acting UP] --> Slice
    Weight[Self-weight gamma A dh acting DOWN] --> Slice
    Fric[Wall friction mu_w Ka sigma_v times U dh acting UP] --> Slice
    Slice --> Result[Net equilibrium yields d sigma_v by dh = gamma minus Ka mu_w sigma_v by R]
```

## 4.4 Notation Key (Used in the Diagrams Above)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\gamma$ | Bulk density (weight density) of granular food | kN/m³ |
| $\phi$ | Angle of internal friction | degrees |
| $\phi_w$ | Angle of wall friction | degrees |
| $\mu_w$ | Coefficient of wall friction = $\tan\phi_w$ | dimensionless |
| $K_a$ | Rankine active pressure coefficient | dimensionless |
| $D$ | Silo diameter | m |
| $R$ | Hydraulic radius = $D/4$ for a circle | m |
| $h_c$ | Characteristic depth = $R/(\mu_w K_a)$ | m |
| $\sigma_v$ | Mean vertical stress on a horizontal plane | kN/m² |
| $p_h$ | Lateral pressure on the wall | kN/m² |
| $f_t$ | Hoop tensile stress in the cylindrical wall | N/mm² |
| $t$ | Wall thickness of the steel shell | mm |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Mixing units**: $\gamma$ is sometimes given in kg/m³ (mass density) and sometimes in kN/m³ (weight density). Converting **wrong** is the single biggest mark-deduction reason. Recall: $\gamma_{\text{kN/m³}} = \rho_{\text{kg/m³}} \times 9.81 / 1000$. For wheat $\rho \approx 800$ kg/m³ gives $\gamma \approx 7.85$ kN/m³.
> 2. **Confusing $K_a$ and $K_p$**: Active is for *flowing/discharging*; passive is for *retaining*. Default to $K_a$ for silos.
> 3. **Using Airy in a tall silo**: For $h > h_c$, you **must** use Janssen — Airy will overestimate lateral pressure by 30–50% and earn **zero** method marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the exponential**: The full Janssen solution has a $[1 - \exp(-K_a\mu_w h / R)]$ term. Setting it equal to 1 (the limit) at modest depths is a common slip.
> 5. **Hoop stress units**: $f_t = p_h D / (2t)$. If $p_h$ is in kN/m² and $D$ in m, $f_t$ is in kN/m² = kPa. Convert to N/mm² (MPa) for allowable stress checks: divide by 1000.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a shallow bin and a deep bin (silo) in the design of food-grain storage vessels. Mention the theoretical pressure equation used in each case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Shallow Bin (Bin) | Deep Bin (Silo) |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Height $H$ small compared to plan size; typically $H/D < 1.5$ | $H/D > 1.5$; tall slender storage |
| Vertical stress at bottom | Equals full overburden $\gamma H$ (linear) | Less than $\gamma H$, asymptotic to a limit |
| Governing theory | **Airy equation** $p_h = K_a \gamma h$ | **Janssen equation** $p_v = (\gamma R/K_a\mu_w)[1 - e^{-K_a\mu_w h /R}]$ |
| Wall friction effect | Negligible | Significant — carries part of vertical load |
| Transition | — | $h_c = R/(\mu_w K_a)$ marks the boundary |

(3 marks: 1 mark for each comparison point + 1 mark for equations.)

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the Rankine active state and derive the expression for the Rankine active pressure coefficient.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Rankine active state** is the stress condition inside a granular mass when it is *allowed* to expand laterally (e.g., a bin wall yields outward by an infinitesimal amount). In this state, the vertical stress is the major principal stress and the horizontal stress reaches its **minimum** possible value consistent with Mohr-Coulomb failure.

For a cohesionless bulk solid (cohesion $c = 0$), the Mohr-Coulomb relation between the major and minor principal stresses gives:

$$K_a = \frac{\sigma_{h,\min}}{\sigma_v} = \frac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — Mohr-Coulomb setup; 1 mark — final expression.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> Each sub-part is worth **7 marks**. Solve every sub-part neatly with units and one-line justifications.

---

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a free-body diagram of an elemental slice of thickness $\mathrm{d}h$ in a vertical-walled cylindrical silo, **derive the Janssen equation** for the mean vertical pressure $\sigma_v$ at depth $h$ below the free surface of the stored bulk solid. State clearly the constitutive assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical silo of **diameter $D = 6$ m** and **height $H = 18$ m** is filled with **maize** of bulk density $\gamma = 7.5$ kN/m³, angle of internal friction $\phi = 28°$, and coefficient of wall friction $\mu_w = 0.35$. Using Janssen's equation, determine the **lateral wall pressure $p_h$** at the bottom of the silo and the **maximum hoop tensile stress** required in a steel shell of thickness $t = 8$ mm. State the regime (shallow/deep) you are in. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Geometry and slice** *(1 mark)*
A thin horizontal slice of the bulk solid of thickness $\mathrm{d}h$ at depth $h$, cross-section $A$, wetted perimeter $U$, hydraulic radius $R = A/U$.

**Step 2 — Vertical force balance** *(2 marks)*

$$\sigma_v(h)A - \sigma_v(h+\mathrm{d}h)A + \gamma A\,\mathrm{d}h - w\,U\,\mathrm{d}h = 0$$

Dividing by $A\,\mathrm{d}h$:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}\sigma_v}{\mathrm{d}h} = \gamma - \frac{w}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Constitutive relations** *(2 marks)*
- Wall friction: $w = \mu_w\,p_h$, where $\mu_w = \tan\phi_w$.
- Rankine active state: $p_h = K_a\,\sigma_v$ with $K_a = (1-\sin\phi)/(1+\sin\phi)$.

Substituting: $w = \mu_w K_a\,\sigma_v$.

**Step 4 — Governing ODE and its solution** *(1.5 marks)*

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}\sigma_v}{\mathrm{d}h} + \frac{K_a\mu_w}{R}\sigma_v = \gamma$$

With $\sigma_v(0) = 0$:

$$\sigma_v(h) = \frac{\gamma R}{K_a\mu_w}\!\left[1 - \exp\!\left(-\frac{K_a\mu_w}{R}h\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Physical meaning** *(0.5 mark)*
As $h \to \infty$, $\sigma_v \to \gamma R/(K_a\mu_w)$, a finite limit, confirming that the wall carries part of the load.

---

**Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Compute $K_a$** *(1 mark)*

$$K_a = \frac{1-\sin 28°}{1+\sin 28°} = \frac{1-0.4695}{1+0.4695} = \frac{0.5305}{1.4695} \approx 0.361$$

**Step 2 — Hydraulic radius** *(0.5 mark)*

$$R = \frac{D}{4} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic depth (regime check)** *(1 mark)*

$$h_c = \frac{R}{\mu_w K_a} = \frac{1.5}{0.35 \times 0.361} = \frac{1.5}{0.1264} \approx 11.87 \text{ m}$$

Since $H = 18$ m $> h_c = 11.87$ m, the silo is in the **deep (Janssen) regime**. Janssen is the correct method.

**Step 4 — Lateral pressure at $h = 18$ m** *(2.5 marks)*

Exponent:

$$-\frac{K_a\mu_w}{R}\,h = -\frac{0.361 \times 0.35}{1.5}\times 18 = -1.5178$$

$$\sigma_v = \frac{7.5 \times 1.5}{0.361 \times 0.35}\left[1 - e^{-1.5178}\right] = \frac{11.25}{0.1264}\left[1 - 0.2193\right] = 89.0 \times 0.7807 \approx 69.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$p_h = K_a \sigma_v = 0.361 \times 69.5 \approx 25.1 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Hoop stress** *(2 marks)*

$$f_t = \frac{p_h D}{2t} = \frac{25.1 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa} \times 6 \text{ m}}{2 \times 0.008 \text{ m}} = \frac{150{,}600}{0.016} = 9.41 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 9.41 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

For mild steel of allowable stress $\approx 140$ N/mm², an 8 mm shell is grossly over-designed — confirming the **economy** of Janssen analysis. (A reasonable thinning to $t \approx 3$ mm would still satisfy allowable stress with a safety factor of 2.)

**Sanity check (1 mark)**: Airy (linear) answer would be $p_h^{\text{Airy}} = 0.361 \times 7.5 \times 18 = 48.7$ kN/m². Janssen gives $25.1$ kN/m², i.e., **about half**. The reduction is consistent with textbook norms of 30–50% for tall silos.

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** **Explain the Rankine theory of active and passive earth pressure** for a cohesionless granular material. Sketch the Mohr-circle representation and write the expressions for $K_a$ and $K_p$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular steel silo has $D = 4$ m, $H = 12$ m, and is used to store **rice (paddy)** with $\gamma = 5.8$ kN/m³, $\phi = 32°$, $\mu_w = 0.30$. **(i)** Determine whether the silo is in the **Airy (shallow)** or **Janssen (deep)** regime. **(ii)** Compute the **limiting lateral wall pressure** $p_{h,\infty}$ using Janssen. **(iii)** If the silo is **actually 12 m tall**, compute the **actual lateral pressure** at the bottom and the **percentage error** if the Airy equation had been used. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]:**

**Concept** *(1.5 marks)*: Rankine (1857) extended the Mohr-Coulomb failure theory to a semi-infinite granular mass with a horizontal free surface. The horizontal stress at any depth is bounded between two limit values, depending on the lateral strain condition:
- **Active state**: mass yields outward → $p_h$ is **minimum** → $K_a \sigma_v$.
- **Passive state**: mass is compressed inward → $p_h$ is **maximum** → $K_p \sigma_v$.

**Mohr-circle representation** *(2 marks)*: At depth $h$, the vertical stress $\sigma_v = \gamma h$ and the horizontal stress is $p_h$. The Mohr circle of radius $R_m = (\sigma_v - p_h)/2$ centred at $C = (\sigma_v + p_h)/2$ must **just touch** the Mohr-Coulomb failure envelope $\tau = \sigma_n \tan\phi$. The active state is the circle that is *as small as possible* (touches the envelope on the right); the passive state is the circle *as large as possible* (touches on the left).

**Derivation of $K_a$ and $K_p$** *(2 marks)*:
Using $\sigma_1 = \sigma_3 K_p$ (or $K_a$ inversely) with $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 2R_m$ and the geometry of the touched envelope:

$$K_a = \frac{1-\sin\phi}{1+\sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° - \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right)$$

$$K_p = \frac{1+\sin\phi}{1-\sin\phi} = \tan^2\!\left(45° + \tfrac{\phi}{2}\right) = \frac{1}{K_a}$$

**Silo relevance** *(1.5 marks)*: A *discharging* silo experiences the **active** state, so designers use $K_a$. The lateral pressure is $p_h = K_a \sigma_v$, and the wall friction is $w = \mu_w p_h = \mu_w K_a \sigma_v$. This is the constitutive closure used in the Janssen derivation.

---

**Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — $K_a$ and $R$** *(1 mark)*

$$K_a = \frac{1-\sin 32°}{1+\sin 32°} = \frac{1-0.5299}{1+0.5299} = \frac{0.4701}{1.5299} \approx 0.3073$$

$$R = D/4 = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Characteristic depth and regime** *(1.5 marks)*

$$h_c = \frac{R}{\mu_w K_a} = \frac{1.0}{0.30 \times 0.3073} = \frac{1.0}{0.0922} \approx 10.85 \text{ m}$$

Since $H = 12$ m $> h_c \approx 10.85$ m, the silo is **marginally in the deep (Janssen) regime**. The instruction to "use Janssen" is appropriate.

**Step 3 — Limiting lateral pressure** *(1.5 marks)*

$$p_{h,\infty} = \frac{\gamma R}{\mu_w} = \frac{5.8 \times 1.0}{0.30} = 19.33 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Actual lateral pressure at $h = 12$ m** *(2 marks)*

$$\sigma_v = \frac{\gamma R}{K_a\mu_w}\left[1 - e^{-K_a\mu_w h / R}\right] = \frac{5.8}{0.3073 \times 0.30}\left[1 - e^{-0.30 \times 0.3073 \times 12}\right]$$

$$= \frac{5.8}{0.0922}\left[1 - e^{-1.1063}\right] = 62.91 \times \left[1 - 0.3307\right] = 62.91 \times 0.6693 = 42.11 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

$$p_h = K_a \sigma_v = 0.3073 \times 42.11 = 12.94 \text{ kN/m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Percentage error of Airy** *(1 mark)*

Airy: $p_h^{\text{Airy}} = K_a \gamma h = 0.3073 \times 5.8 \times 12 = 21.39$ kN/m²

$$\%\text{ error} = \frac{21.39 - 12.94}{12.94} \times 100\% \approx 65\%\text{ over-prediction}$$

So using Airy in this slightly-deep silo would have over-designed the wall by **65%**, confirming the necessity of Janssen analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Silo** = deep bin where wall friction is significant ($h > h_c$); **Bin** = shallow where it is not ($h < h_c$).
- **Rankine active coefficient**: $K_a = (1-\sin\phi)/(1+\sin\phi) = \tan^2(45°-\phi/2)$. Always used for *discharging* silos.
- **Rankine passive coefficient**: $K_p = 1/K_a$. Used for retaining / pushing problems; **not** typical silo design.
- **Airy equation (shallow)**: $p_h = K_a \gamma h$, linear in depth. Valid when $h < h_c = R/(\mu_w K_a)$.
- **Janssen equation (deep)**: $\sigma_v = (\gamma R / K_a \mu_w)[1 - \exp(-K_a\mu_w h/R)]$.
- **Janssen lateral pressure**: $p_h = K_a \sigma_v$.
- **Limiting (asymptotic) pressures** as $h \to \infty$: $\sigma_{v,\infty} = \gamma R/(K_a\mu_w)$ and $p_{h,\infty} = \gamma R/\mu_w$.
- **Hydraulic radius** $R = A/U$: for a circle $D/4$, for a square $a/4$, for a rectangle $ab/[2(a+b)]$.
- **Hoop stress in cylindrical wall**: $f_t = p_h D / (2t)$.
- **Wall friction stress**: $w = \mu_w p_h$.
- **Assumptions** in Janssen: (1) vertical wall; (2) horizontal slices in equilibrium; (3) $p_h = K_a \sigma_v$ throughout (active state); (4) $\mu_w$ constant (no slip-stick); (5) no surcharge at the top.
- **Convergence**: at $h = h_c$, the Janssen exponential term is $e^{-1} \approx 0.368$, so $\sigma_v(h_c) = 0.632 \sigma_{v,\infty}$. At $h = 3h_c$, $\sigma_v \approx 0.95 \sigma_{v,\infty}$ (effectively saturated).
- **Real-world number**: For wheat ($\phi \approx 27°$, $\mu_w \approx 0.40$) in a 4 m diameter silo, $h_c \approx 7$–8 m; silos above 8 m tall are clearly in the Janssen regime.
- **Pitfalls**: Unit conversion ($\rho$ vs. $\gamma$); $K_a$ vs. $K_p$; Airy used in deep regime; missing exponential; forgetting $R = A/U$ (not $D/2$ or $D$).
- **Hopper design follow-up**: After the cylindrical section is sized, the **hopper angle**, **outlet diameter**, and **mass-flow / funnel-flow** classification (Jenike, Walker) are designed in the next module using the same $\phi$, $\phi_w$, and $\gamma$ obtained in the present analysis.

**End of Module 1 Notes — Silo Design, Rankine, Airy, and Janssen.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Heat Exchangers - Double Pipe Heat Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Heat Exchangers - Double Pipe Heat Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Double Pipe Heat Exchanger (DPHE)** is the most elementary category of recuperative shell-and-tube type heat transfer apparatus, consisting of **two concentric pipes** in which one process fluid (the *hot stream*) flows through the **inner pipe** while the second process fluid (the *cold stream*) flows counter-currently or co-currently through the **annular space** between the inner and outer pipes. Heat transfer between the two streams occurs by **conduction through the inner pipe wall** followed by **convective exchange** at both internal and external fluid surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Tone):**
> *A Double Pipe Heat Exchanger is a concentric-tube heat transfer device used for heating, cooling, condensation, or evaporation of food fluids in small-to-medium scale operations. It functions on the principle of indirect heat transfer through a cylindrical separating wall, classified as a 1-1 (one shell pass – one tube pass) heat exchanger with a heat transfer surface area defined by the inner surface of the inner pipe.*

### 1.1.1 Standard Synonyms (for examiner familiarity)

| Acronym | Expansion |
|---|---|
| DPHE | Double Pipe Heat Exchanger |
| HX | Heat Exchanger |
| CTHE | Concentric Tube Heat Exchanger |
| Hairpin HX | Bent-tube multi-section DPHE |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Idli Cooker's Logic"**
>
> Imagine you are cooking **idli batter** in a traditional kitchen. You cannot place the vessel directly on the flame because the batter will scorch. Instead, you place the batter in a smaller inner vessel, and that inner vessel is placed inside a larger outer pot filled with water. The flame heats the water → the water heats the metal of the inner vessel → the inner vessel heats the batter gently and uniformly.
>
> In a Double Pipe Heat Exchanger, the **flame** is the hot fluid, the **water bath** is the annular stream, the **inner pot wall** is the inner pipe wall, and the **idli batter** is the cold process fluid flowing inside. Heat crosses the boundary in the *same exact sequence*: **convective hot-side → conductive wall → convective cold-side**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and dimensionless groups are essential for DPHE design and **must be memorized** for KTU examinations:

- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant (σ)** = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴) — *not used in DPHE (no radiation), but listed for completeness*
- **Universal Gas Constant (R̄)** = **8.314 kJ/(kmol·K)**
- **Acceleration due to gravity (g)** = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure** = **101.325 kPa**
- **LMTD Correction Factor (F)** ranges between **0 ≤ F ≤ 1** (ideally close to 1)
- **Counter-current flow preferred** because it offers the **highest mean temperature driving force**

---

## 1.4 Why DPHE is Critical for Food Processing Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food-Industry Relevance Highlights (3-A Sanitary Standards Aligned):**
>
> 1. **Pilot-scale and R&D operations**: DPHEs dominate lab-to-pilot plant scale-up because of their **simple construction, easy dismantling, and visible flow paths**.
> 2. **Sanitary Design (3-A / EHEDG compliant)**: All wetted surfaces are **stainless steel SS 304 / SS 316L** with surface roughness **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm**.
> 3. **Clean-in-Place (CIP) capability**: Suitable for **CIP** because of smooth bends, no dead legs, and crevice-free welded joints.
> 4. **Modular flexibility**: Multiple "hairpin" sections can be joined in series to increase heat transfer area without changing the equipment footprint.
> 5. **Common Food Applications**: Pasteurization of milk, preheating of syrups, cooling of edible oils, tempering of chocolate, and UHT pre-heating stages.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification and Construction Architecture

A DPHE is structurally built from the following components:

1. **Outer pipe (shell side)** — usually 2 to 4 inches nominal diameter
2. **Inner pipe (tube side)** — usually 1 to 1.5 inches nominal diameter
3. **Return bends / hairpin fittings** — 180° elbows connecting successive sections
4. **Tees / inlet and outlet nozzles** — for stream entry and exit
5. **Support saddles / pipe supports** — for thermal expansion accommodation
6. **Gaskets and flanges** — for sanitary tri-clamp connections (food industry)
7. **Insulation jacket** — outer cladding to reduce heat loss to surroundings

### 2.1.1 Modes of Flow Arrangement

> [!NOTE]
> **Flow Configurations in DPHE:**
> - **Counter-current flow (most preferred, highest LMTD efficiency)**
> - **Co-current / Parallel flow (used only when specific temperature pinch is desired)**
> - **Hairpin series connection (multi-section to increase area)**

---

## 2.2 Governing Heat Transfer Equations

The fundamental rate equation that controls all DPHE design is:

$$Q = U_o \cdot A_o \cdot \Delta T_m$$

Where:
- $Q$ = Heat transfer rate (**W or kW**)
- $U_o$ = **Overall heat transfer coefficient based on outer surface** (W/m²·K)
- $A_o$ = Heat transfer area based on **outer diameter of inner pipe** (m²)
- $\Delta T_m$ = **Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** in **K or °C**

---

## 2.3 The Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

### 2.3.1 For Counter-Current Flow

$$\Delta T_{m,CC} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

Where:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$

### 2.3.2 For Co-current (Parallel) Flow

$$\Delta T_{m,CF} = \frac{\Delta T_1' - \Delta T_2'}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1'}{\Delta T_2'}\right)}$$

Where:
- $\Delta T_1' = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}$
- $\Delta T_2' = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry Property:** Counter-current flow gives a **higher** $\Delta T_m$ than co-current flow for the **same inlet and outlet temperatures**, leading to **smaller area, lower cost, and better thermodynamic reversibility**.

### 2.3.3 Correction Factor F for Multi-Pass / Non-Ideal Arrangements

For any configuration where the simple 1-1 LMTD cannot be directly applied (e.g., hairpin series), the actual mean temperature difference becomes:

$$\Delta T_{m,actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_{m,CC}$$

The factor $F$ is read from standard KTU textbook charts as a function of:
- $P = \dfrac{T_{h,out} - T_{h,in}}{T_{c,in} - T_{h,in}}$ (hot-side effectiveness parameter)
- $R = \dfrac{T_{c,in} - T_{c,out}}{T_{h,out} - T_{h,in}}$ (capacity rate ratio)

For a **pure counter-current 1-1 DPHE**, $F = 1.0$ (no correction needed).

---

## 2.4 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)

The thermal resistance network for a DPHE consists of **five additive resistances**:

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{D_o}{D_i \cdot h_i} + R_{f,o} + R_{f,i} \cdot \frac{D_o}{D_i}$$

Where:
- $h_o$ = outside (annulus-side) convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $h_i$ = inside (tube-side) convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $x_w$ = wall thickness of inner pipe (m)
- $k_w$ = thermal conductivity of pipe wall material (W/m·K)
- $D_i, D_o$ = inner and outer diameters of inner pipe (m)
- $R_{f,o}, R_{f,i}$ = **fouling factors** on outer and inner surfaces (m²·K/W)

> [!WARNING]
> **Fouling Factor Alert for Food Engineers:** Milk pasteurization DPHEs use **R_f = 0.0001 to 0.0002 m²·K/W** for pasteurized milk, and **R_f = 0.0002 to 0.0004 m²·K/W** for raw milk (per Kern's standard tables). **Always specify R_f to avoid undersizing.**

---

## 2.5 Reynolds Number for DPHE

### 2.5.1 Inner Pipe

$$Re_i = \frac{\rho_i \cdot v_i \cdot D_i}{\mu_i} = \frac{4 \dot{m}_i}{\pi D_i \mu_i}$$

Turbulent regime threshold: **Re_i > 4000**

### 2.5.2 Annular Space

The **equivalent (hydraulic) diameter** of an annulus is:

$$D_e = \frac{D_o^2 - D_{i,outer}^2}{D_{o,inner} + D_{i,outer}} = \frac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_o + D_i}$$

(Note: $D_o$ here is the **inner diameter of outer pipe**, $D_i$ is the **outer diameter of inner pipe**.)

$$Re_o = \frac{\rho_o \cdot v_o \cdot D_e}{\mu_o}$$

---

## 2.6 Nusselt Number Correlations

### 2.6.1 Dittus-Boelter (for turbulent flow, heating/cooling of fluids)

$$Nu = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{n}$$

Where $n = 0.4$ for heating and $n = 0.3$ for cooling. **Valid range**: Re > 6000, 0.7 < Pr < 16000, L/D > 10.

### 2.6.2 Sieder-Tate (for high-viscosity fluids and large property variations — typical for food oils and syrups)

$$Nu = 0.027 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{1/3} \left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14}$$

### 2.6.3 For Annular Space

Use the **hydraulic diameter** $D_e$ in place of $D_i$ with the **same Dittus-Boelter correlation**, but apply a **multiplier** to account for curvature and unequal heat flux on inner and outer walls.

---

## 2.7 Pressure Drop in DPHE

### 2.7.1 Tube-Side Pressure Drop

$$\Delta P_i = \frac{f_i \cdot L \cdot \rho_i \cdot v_i^2}{2 D_i} + \frac{K \cdot \rho_i \cdot v_i^2}{2}$$

Where:
- $f_i$ = Darcy friction factor (from Moody chart using $Re_i$ and $\varepsilon/D_i$)
- $K$ = sum of loss coefficients for entrance, exit, bends, and fittings (typically **K_total ≈ 1.8 to 2.5 for a single hairpin section**)

### 2.7.2 Annular-Side Pressure Drop

$$\Delta P_o = \frac{f_o \cdot L \cdot \rho_o \cdot v_o^2}{2 D_e} + \frac{K' \cdot \rho_o \cdot v_o^2}{2}$$

---

## 2.8 NTU-Effectiveness Method (Alternative Design Approach)

When outlet temperatures are **not specified**, the **NTU (Number of Transfer Units) method** is preferred.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}$$

For counter-current DPHE with $C_r < 1$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\left[-NTU\,(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp\left[-NTU\,(1 - C_r)\right]}$$

For co-current DPHE:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\left[-NTU\,(1 + C_r)\right]}{1 + C_r}$$

Where:
- $NTU = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$
- $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \left(\dfrac{\dot{m} c_p}{C_{max}}\right)_{min} / \left(\dfrac{\dot{m} c_p}{C_{max}}\right)_{max}$

---

## 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — DOUBLE PIPE HEAT EXCHANGERS**
>
> All formulas below are **board-exam gold** and appear in nearly every KTU Part B question.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Symbols | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U_o A_o \Delta T_m$ | Overall heat transfer rate | Q in W, U in W/m²·K, A in m², ΔT in K |
| 2 | $\Delta T_m = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log mean temperature difference | K |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{m,actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_{m,CC}$ | Corrected LMTD | K |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{D_o}{D_i h_i} + R_{f,o} + R_{f,i} \dfrac{D_o}{D_i}$ | Overall thermal resistance | m²·K/W |
| 5 | $D_e = \dfrac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_o + D_i}$ | Equivalent diameter of annulus | m |
| 6 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{4\dot m}{\pi D \mu}$ | Reynolds number (circular) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{n}$ | Dittus-Boelter correlation | dimensionless |
| 8 | $Nu = 0.027 Re^{0.8} Pr^{1/3} (\mu / \mu_w)^{0.14}$ | Sieder-Tate (high viscosity) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $h = \dfrac{Nu \cdot k}{D}$ | Convective coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 10 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{f L \rho v^2}{2D} + \dfrac{K \rho v^2}{2}$ | Pressure drop | Pa |
| 11 | $A_o = \pi D_o L$ | Heat transfer surface area | m² |
| 12 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1-C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-NTU(1-C_r)]}$ | Counter-current effectiveness | dimensionless |
| 13 | $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max}$ | Heat capacity rate ratio | dimensionless |
| 14 | $NTU = U A / C_{min}$ | Number of Transfer Units | dimensionless |
| 15 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{4 f}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2 g_c}$ | Critical pressure for buckling | Pa |

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

The DPHE design methodology is not limited to classroom problems. It underpins:

1. **HTST Pasteurizers** for milk (72°C × 15 s) — plate exchangers dominate industrially, but **DPHEs remain the prototype design** for thermal lab demonstration.
2. **Pre-heaters for edible oil refining** — counter-current DPHEs recover waste heat from deodorized oil streams.
3. **Slurry heating in starch processing** — DPHEs handle viscous non-Newtonian food slurries where the **Sieder-Tate correlation** dominates.
4. **Wine and beer cooling** — jacketed fermenters use DPHE principles for serpentine cooling coils.
5. **Small-scale dairy operations** — vermicompost (vermi-HX), cream chilling, and butter tempering lines.
