# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

### 3.1.1 Physical Setup

Consider a differential element of length $dL$ along a counter-current double pipe heat exchanger. Let:
- Hot fluid enters at $T_{h,i}$ and leaves at $T_{h,o}$
- Cold fluid enters at $T_{c,i}$ and leaves at $T_{c,o}$

In the differential element, the hot fluid temperature drops by $dT_h$ and the cold fluid temperature rises by $dT_c$.

### 3.1.2 Energy Balance Across the Differential Element

The heat transferred through the differential area $dA$ equals the heat lost by hot fluid and gained by cold fluid:

$$dQ = -(\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}) \, dT_h = (\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}) \, dT_c = U \, dA \, \Delta T$$

Where $\Delta T$ is the **local temperature difference** between hot and cold fluids at position $x$.

### 3.1.3 Expressing dT_h and dT_c in Terms of dΔT

Let $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$. Differentiating across the differential element:

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} - \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}$$

$$d(\Delta T) = -dQ \left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right)$$

$$dQ = \frac{-d(\Delta T)}{\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}}$$

### 3.1.4 Combining with Heat Transfer Equation

From $dQ = U \, dA \, \Delta T$:

$$U \, dA \, \Delta T = \frac{-d(\Delta T)}{\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}}$$

$$U \left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right) dA = \frac{-d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T}$$

### 3.1.5 Integration Across the Entire Length

Integrating from inlet ($A = 0$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_2$) to outlet ($A = A$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_1$):

$$U A \left(\frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}\right) = \int_{\Delta T_2}^{\Delta T_1} \frac{-d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = \ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right)$$

From the total energy balance:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$$

So:

$$\frac{Q}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} = T_{h,i} - T_{h,o} = \Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{Q}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} = T_{c,o} - T_{c,i} = \Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$$

Therefore:

$$U A \left(\frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q} + \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right)$$

$$U A \cdot \frac{2(\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{Q} = \ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right)$$

Rearranging to solve for $Q$:

$$Q = U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

### 3.1.6 Final Result

$$\boxed{Q = U_o A_o \Delta T_m \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta T_m = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}}$$

The term $\Delta T_m$ is called the **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**, and the derivation above proves rigorously why a logarithmic mean (rather than arithmetic mean) is the correct thermodynamic average temperature driving force for a linear heat transfer process.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — LMTD vs Arithmetic Mean for ΔT₁/ΔT₂ Ratios

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose:** This matrix teaches the student how significantly LMTD deviates from the arithmetic mean, especially when the temperature difference ratio $\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2$ exceeds 2.0.

| $\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2$ Ratio | $\Delta T_1$ (K) | $\Delta T_2$ (K) | Arithmetic Mean (K) | LMTD (K) | % Deviation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 50 | 50 | 50.0 | 50.0 | 0.0% |
| 1.5 | 60 | 40 | 50.0 | 49.5 | 1.0% |
| 2.0 | 80 | 40 | 60.0 | 57.7 | 3.8% |
| 2.5 | 100 | 40 | 70.0 | 65.6 | 6.3% |
| 3.0 | 120 | 40 | 80.0 | 72.8 | 9.0% |
| 5.0 | 200 | 40 | 120.0 | 102.6 | 14.5% |
| 10.0 | 400 | 40 | 220.0 | 161.4 | 26.6% |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often substitute the **arithmetic mean** of temperatures into $Q = U A \Delta T$. This produces **gross over-design** when $\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2 > 2$, leading to unnecessarily expensive equipment. Always use LMTD unless explicitly told otherwise.

---

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]`

A counter-current double pipe heat exchanger is used to cool **hot edible oil** flowing through the inner pipe from **150°C to 80°C** at a mass flow rate of **0.5 kg/s**. Cooling water enters the annulus at **25°C** and leaves at **55°C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient based on the outer surface of the inner pipe is **U_o = 350 W/m²·K**. The inner pipe has **D_o = 0.042 m** (42 mm OD) and is to be built from stainless steel SS 316.

**Required:**
1. Heat duty $Q$
2. Mass flow rate of cooling water
3. Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)
4. Required heat transfer area $A_o$
5. Required length of inner pipe $L$

Given:
- Specific heat of oil: $c_{p,h}$ = 2.1 kJ/(kg·K)
- Specific heat of water: $c_{p,c}$ = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K)

---

### 3.3.2 Step 1 — Calculate Heat Duty Q

Energy balance on the hot oil stream:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o})$$

$$Q = 0.5 \times 2.1 \times (150 - 80)$$

$$Q = 0.5 \times 2.1 \times 70$$

$$Q = 73.5 \text{ kW} = 73500 \text{ W}$$

> **Heat duty Q = 73.5 kW**

---

### 3.3.3 Step 2 — Calculate Cooling Water Mass Flow Rate

Energy balance on the cold water stream:

$$\dot{m}_c = \frac{Q}{c_{p,c}(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})}$$

$$\dot{m}_c = \frac{73500}{4180 \times (55 - 25)}$$

$$\dot{m}_c = \frac{73500}{4180 \times 30}$$

$$\dot{m}_c = \frac{73500}{125400}$$

$$\dot{m}_c = 0.586 \text{ kg/s}$$

> **Cooling water flow rate = 0.586 kg/s**

---

### 3.3.4 Step 3 — Calculate the LMTD (Counter-Current)

For counter-current flow:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 150 - 55 = 95 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 80 - 25 = 55 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \frac{95 - 55}{\ln(95/55)}$$

$$\ln(95/55) = \ln(1.7273) = 0.5469$$

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{40}{0.5469}$$

$$\Delta T_m = 73.13 \text{ K}$$

> **LMTD = 73.13 K**

---

### 3.3.5 Step 4 — Calculate Required Heat Transfer Area

Rearranging the fundamental rate equation:

$$A_o = \frac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_m}$$

$$A_o = \frac{73500}{350 \times 73.13}$$

$$A_o = \frac{73500}{25595.5}$$

$$A_o = 2.872 \text{ m}^2$$

> **Required Area A_o = 2.872 m²**

---

### 3.3.6 Step 5 — Calculate Required Length of Inner Pipe

Using $A_o = \pi D_o L$:

$$L = \frac{A_o}{\pi D_o} = \frac{2.872}{\pi \times 0.042}$$

$$L = \frac{2.872}{0.13195}$$

$$L = 21.77 \text{ m}$$

> **Required Length L = 21.77 m**

Since 21.77 m is quite long for a single pipe run, the designer would split this into **3 hairpin sections of approximately 7.26 m each**, joined with 180° return bends.

---

### 3.3.7 Verification and Sanity Check

- Capacity rate ratio: $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \dfrac{0.5 \times 2.1}{0.586 \times 4.18} = \dfrac{1.05}{2.449} = 0.429$
- Heat capacity of cold side is larger → $C_{min}$ is the hot side (oil).
- Check effectiveness: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{73.5}{1.05 \times (150-25)} = \dfrac{73.5}{131.25} = 0.560 = 56\%$
- This is a reasonable effectiveness for a single hairpin; multi-section design can push it higher.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — DPHE Design Workflow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** illustrates the iterative design decision workflow for a Double Pipe Heat Exchanger used in food processing. This is the **designer's mental algorithm** from problem statement to commissioning.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Process Requirements] --> B[Identify Hot Stream and Cold Stream]
    B --> C[Collect Thermophysical Properties]
    C --> D[Calculate Heat Duty Q from Energy Balance]
    D --> E[Compute LMTD]
    E --> F{Is F Correction Required?}
    F -->|No CC 1-1| G[Use LMTD Directly]
    F -->|Yes Multi-pass| H[Compute P and R and Lookup F]
    H --> I[Apply F Factor to LMTD]
    G --> J[Assume Initial U Value from Past Data]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Compute Required Area A]
    K --> L[Select Standard Pipe Diameters]
    L --> M[Compute Re for Both Streams]
    M --> N{Re Greater than 4000?}
    N -->|No| O[Apply Laminar Correlation]
    N -->|Yes| P[Apply Dittus Boelter]
    O --> Q[Compute h and Recompute U]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R{Does U Match Assumption within 10 Percent?}
    R -->|No| J
    R -->|Yes| S[Compute Pressure Drop Both Sides]
    S --> T{Drop within Allowable Limit?}
    T -->|No| U[Revise Pipe Diameter or Length]
    U --> M
    T -->|Yes| V[Select Sanitary Fittings and Materials]
    V --> W[Check 3A and EHEDG Compliance]
    W --> X[Finalize Drawing and P and ID]
    X --> Y[End: Issue for Fabrication]
```

---

## 4.4 Process Logic and Decision Rationale

The DPHE designer moves through **four distinct design loops**:

1. **Thermal Loop (Heat Duty, LMTD, Area)** — establishes the size envelope.
2. **Hydraulic Loop (Reynolds, Nusselt, Pressure Drop)** — ensures fluid mechanical feasibility.
3. **Mechanical Loop (Wall thickness, Supports, Expansion)** — establishes structural integrity.
4. **Sanitary Loop (3-A, CIP, Surface Finish)** — ensures food-grade compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway from Schematics:** The DPHE design is fundamentally **iterative** in steps M to U of the workflow. The student must iterate at least **2-3 times** between assumed U, computed Re/Nu/h, and recomputed U until convergence within **±10%**. This is the most frequent examiner trap.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient" in the context of a double pipe heat exchanger. State the standard equation used for its calculation based on the outer surface area.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** in a double pipe heat exchanger is defined as the **reciprocal of the total thermal resistance per unit area** offered by the inner convective film, the pipe wall, the outer convective film, and the fouling layers on either side of the separating wall, all referenced to a chosen surface (typically the outer surface of the inner pipe).

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{D_o}{D_i \cdot h_i} + R_{f,o} + R_{f,i} \cdot \frac{D_o}{D_i}$$

where $h_o$ and $h_i$ are the convective coefficients (W/m²·K), $x_w$ is the wall thickness (m), $k_w$ is the wall thermal conductivity (W/m·K), and $R_f$ terms represent fouling resistances (m²·K/W). The units of U are **W/(m²·K)**.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for explaining each term or units.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**What is the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)? Why is counter-current flow preferred over co-current flow in a double pipe heat exchanger?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **LMTD** is the **logarithmic mean** of the temperature differences at the two ends of a heat exchanger, given by:

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

**Counter-current flow is preferred because:**
1. It produces a **higher LMTD** for the same inlet and outlet temperatures, resulting in a **smaller required heat transfer area** and lower cost.
2. The **cold fluid can be heated above the outlet temperature of the hot fluid** at the exit (no pinch point), enabling better energy utilization.
3. The temperature profile is **more uniform**, reducing thermal stresses on the pipe wall.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for LMTD formula, 2 marks for the three reasons.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Rule:** Students must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Both questions cover the same module learning outcomes but are constructed independently.

---

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**A double pipe heat exchanger is to be designed to cool hot sugarcane juice from 95°C to 45°C using chilled water available at 10°C. The chilled water flow rate is 1.2 kg/s and its outlet temperature must not exceed 35°C. Assume an overall heat transfer coefficient of 800 W/m²·K based on the outer diameter of the inner pipe, which is 38 mm. Specific heats: $c_{p,juice}$ = 3.6 kJ/(kg·K), $c_{p,water}$ = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K).**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Calculate:
1. Heat duty of the exchanger.
2. Required mass flow rate of juice.
3. Log Mean Temperature Difference (counter-current arrangement).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Calculate:
1. Required heat transfer area and the corresponding length of inner pipe.
2. Discuss the choice of counter-current versus co-current arrangement if the juice flow rate is doubled.

---

#### **Part (a) Model Solution**

**Step 1 — Heat duty Q from chilled water side:**

$$Q = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}(T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}) = 1.2 \times 4.18 \times (35 - 10)$$

$$Q = 1.2 \times 4.18 \times 25 = 125.4 \text{ kW} = 125400 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate of juice:**

$$\dot{m}_h = \frac{Q}{c_{p,h}(T_{h,i} - T_{h,o})} = \frac{125400}{3.6 \times (95 - 45)}$$

$$\dot{m}_h = \frac{125400}{3.6 \times 50} = \frac{125400}{180} = 696.67 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — LMTD (counter-current):**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 95 - 35 = 60 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 45 - 10 = 35 \text{ K}$$

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{60 - 35}{\ln(60/35)} = \frac{25}{\ln(1.7143)} = \frac{25}{0.5390}$$

$$\Delta T_m = 46.38 \text{ K}$$

---

#### **Part (b) Model Solution**

**Step 1 — Area and Length:**

$$A_o = \frac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_m} = \frac{125400}{800 \times 46.38} = \frac{125400}{37104} = 3.379 \text{ m}^2$$

$$L = \frac{A_o}{\pi D_o} = \frac{3.379}{\pi \times 0.038} = \frac{3.379}{0.1194} = 28.30 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Effect of doubling juice flow:**

If $\dot{m}_h$ doubles to **1393.34 kg/s**, then:
- New $T_{h,o}$ (from energy balance): $T_{h,o} = 95 - \dfrac{125400}{1393.34 \times 3600} = 95 - 25.0 = 70 \text{ °C}$
- The hot fluid outlet temperature rises from **45°C to 70°C**, indicating **incomplete cooling**.

**Recommendation:**
- Counter-current remains **superior** because the cold water can still reach 35°C.
- However, the design must either:
  - **Increase the length** to provide more area, or
  - **Reduce chilled water inlet temperature** to widen $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$.
- Co-current flow would be **strictly worse** because the maximum possible cold outlet temperature is fixed by the hot outlet temperature, creating a temperature pinch.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**A counter-current double pipe heat exchanger uses condensing steam at 130°C in the annulus to heat milk flowing in the inner pipe from 20°C to 75°C. The milk mass flow rate is 0.8 kg/s. The inner pipe has an outer diameter of 33 mm with a 2 mm wall thickness and is made of stainless steel ($k_w$ = 16 W/m·K). The inner convective heat transfer coefficient is estimated as 1800 W/m²·K, and the steam-side coefficient is 8500 W/m²·K. Assume fouling factors of 0.0002 m²·K/W on both sides. Specific heat of milk = 3.9 kJ/(kg·K).**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

Calculate:
1. Heat duty required.
2. Overall heat transfer coefficient $U_o$.
3. The necessary length of the inner pipe if the LMTD is 45 K.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Explain:
1. The role of the fouling factor in the design, and how it would change for unhomogenized vs. homogenized milk.
2. State and justify **two material-selection criteria** for a DPHE used in a dairy plant (3-A sanitary standard).

---

#### **Part (a) Model Solution**

**Step 1 — Heat duty Q:**

$$Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,o} - T_{h,i}) = 0.8 \times 3.9 \times (75 - 20)$$

$$Q = 0.8 \times 3.9 \times 55 = 171.6 \text{ kW} = 171600 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Overall heat transfer coefficient:**

Given $D_o = 33$ mm = 0.033 m, $D_i = D_o - 2 x_w = 0.033 - 2(0.002) = 0.029$ m.

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{D_o}{D_i \cdot h_i} + R_{f,o} + R_{f,i} \cdot \frac{D_o}{D_i}$$

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = \frac{1}{8500} + \frac{0.002}{16} + \frac{0.033}{0.029 \times 1800} + 0.0002 + 0.0002 \times \frac{0.033}{0.029}$$

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = 1.176 \times 10^{-4} + 1.25 \times 10^{-4} + 6.323 \times 10^{-4} + 2.0 \times 10^{-4} + 2.276 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{1}{U_o} = 13.025 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2\text{K/W}$$

$$U_o = \frac{1}{0.0013025} = 767.8 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$$

**Step 3 — Length:**

$$A_o = \frac{Q}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_m} = \frac{171600}{767.8 \times 45} = \frac{171600}{34551} = 4.967 \text{ m}^2$$

$$L = \frac{A_o}{\pi D_o} = \frac{4.967}{\pi \times 0.033} = \frac{4.967}{0.10367} = 47.91 \text{ m}$$

This requires multiple hairpin sections, typically **3 sections of 16 m each**.

---

#### **Part (b) Model Solution**

**1. Role of fouling factor:**

The fouling factor $R_f$ accounts for the **additional thermal resistance** caused by deposits (milk proteins, minerals like calcium phosphate, fat films, bacteria) that accumulate on the heat transfer surfaces during operation. It is **conservatively added** to ensure the exchanger is not undersized when the surfaces become dirty over time.

- **Unhomogenized milk:** Has larger fat globules, which tend to coalesce and form **thicker fouling layers** on the wall. Hence, $R_f$ is **higher** (≈ 0.0004 m²·K/W).
- **Homogenized milk:** Fat globules are broken to < 2 µm and remain dispersed, reducing deposit formation. Hence, $R_f$ is **lower** (≈ 0.0002 m²·K/W).

**2. Two material selection criteria for a dairy DPHE:**

- **Criterion A — Corrosion Resistance:** Use **SS 316L** (not SS 304) because of its superior resistance to chloride-induced pitting and corrosion from cleaning chemicals (e.g., nitric acid, sodium hydroxide used in CIP). The "L" denotes low carbon content, which prevents sensitization and intergranular corrosion at weld joints.

- **Criterion B — Surface Finish and Cleanability:** The inner surface must be **electropolished to Ra ≤ 0.8 μm** (per 3-A Standard 09-07 for sanitary tubing). This ultra-smooth finish:
  - Reduces bacterial adhesion and biofilm formation.
  - Improves cleanability during CIP cycles.
  - Reduces fouling tendency (which directly affects the assumed $R_f$).

*(Examiner's note: Full 7 marks require both numerical accuracy in part (a) and conceptual justification in part (b). Students who simply state "SS is used because it doesn't rust" lose 2-3 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kJ to W:** A Q in kJ/s mistakenly used as W inflates the area by **1000×**. Always recheck units — this is the **#1 mistake** in KTU DPHE problems.
> 2. **Using arithmetic mean instead of LMTD:** Leads to area overestimation of **up to 27%** when $\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2 \geq 10$. Examiners award **0 marks** if no logarithmic form appears.
> 3. **Mixing up $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$** in counter-current vs co-current: Use the **correct stream pair** at each end. The first subscript of $\Delta T$ is always the **hot fluid** at that end.
> 4. **Forgetting to apply $D_o/D_i$ ratio** when computing $U_o$: this conversion factor is mandatory when reporting $U$ on the outer surface but resistances are inside.
> 5. **Ignoring fouling factors** entirely: A design with $R_f = 0$ will pass the KTU heat balance but will **fail in field operation** within weeks. Examiners specifically test this awareness.
> 6. **Not mentioning sanitary design** in food applications: KTU food-processing examiners deduct marks when the answer treats the problem as pure chemical engineering without food-grade considerations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — Double Pipe Heat Exchanger Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Revision)**

### Core Conceptual Points
- ☐ DPHE consists of **two concentric pipes** — hot fluid in inner, cold fluid in annulus.
- ☐ Counter-current flow is **always preferred** over co-current.
- ☐ Hairpin multi-section arrangement is used to **scale up area** without long single pipes.
- ☐ 3-A sanitary standards and CIP compatibility are **mandatory** for food applications.

### Key Formulas to Memorize (in priority order)
- ☐ **Heat rate equation**: $Q = U_o A_o \Delta T_m$ *(the most tested formula)*
- ☐ **LMTD**: $\Delta T_m = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$
- ☐ **LMTD correction**: $\Delta T_{m,actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_{m,CC}$
- ☐ **Equivalent diameter**: $D_e = \dfrac{D_o^2 - D_i^2}{D_o + D_i}$
- ☐ **Overall resistance**: $\dfrac{1}{U_o} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{D_o}{D_i h_i} + R_{f,o} + R_{f,i} \dfrac{D_o}{D_i}$
- ☐ **Dittus-Boelter**: $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^n$
- ☐ **Sieder-Tate**: $Nu = 0.027 Re^{0.8} Pr^{1/3} (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$
- ☐ **Counter-current NTU effectiveness**: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-NTU(1-C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-NTU(1-C_r)]}$

### Critical Numerical Constants
- ☐ $c_{p,water} = 4.18 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- ☐ $c_{p,milk} = 3.9 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- ☐ $k_w (SS316) = 16 \text{ W/m·K}$
- ☐ Standard $R_f$ for pasteurized milk = **0.0002 m²·K/W**

### Common Design Pitfalls
- ☐ Do **not** use arithmetic mean temperature difference.
- ☐ Do **not** skip fouling factors.
- ☐ Do **not** ignore $D_o/D_i$ ratio correction.
- ☐ Do **not** forget **unit conversion** (kW ↔ W).
- ☐ Do **not** recommend SS304 for chloride-rich food streams — specify SS316L.

### Examiner-Critical Words to Use
- ☐ "Counter-current flow maximizes LMTD."
- ☐ "Fouling factor accounts for in-service deposits."
- ☐ "Sanitary design complies with 3-A Standard."
- ☐ "CIP capability ensures hygienic operation."
- ☐ "Electropolishing reduces surface roughness to Ra ≤ 0.8 μm."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plate Heat Exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plate Heat Exchanger

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE):** A compact, highly efficient thermal device that transfers heat between two fluid streams using a stack of thin, corrugated metal plates sealed by gaskets and held together within a rigid frame. Each plate creates a dedicated flow channel, and the alternating arrangement of hot and cold channels permits indirect heat transfer through the plate walls without mixing the two fluids.

In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme Food Process Engineering terminology, a PHE is classified as a **recuperative, indirect-contact, compact heat exchanger** belonging to the family of surface-type exchangers. The **standard thermal metric** governing its sizing is the *overall heat transfer coefficient* (**U**), typically ranging between **3000–7000 W/m²·K** for water–water service, which is significantly higher than shell-and-tube exchangers (typically 1000–3000 W/m²·K).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Staircase of Heat"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Apartment Building:** Imagine a tall apartment building where one side is the hot fluid (sweaty afternoon summer wing) and the other side is the cold fluid (air-conditioned winter wing). Instead of one giant wall separating them, there are dozens of thin floors (plates), each floor carrying a small load of heat. Because there are so many floors, the heat is exchanged gradually and efficiently, just like how a multi-story building has many elevators instead of one massive freight lift. The corridor gaskets between floors (plate gaskets) prevent the two sides from mixing. That is precisely what a Plate Heat Exchanger does — it multiplies the available transfer surface by stacking many thin plates into a compact volume.

## 1.3 Functional Classification

| Parameter | Classification Detail |
|---|---|
| Heat Transfer Mode | Indirect (surface type) |
| Flow Geometry | Counter-flow / Cross-flow (chevron-induced) |
| Compactness | **Compact heat exchanger** (β > 700 m²/m³) |
| Construction Type | Gasketed, Welded, Brazed, Spiral |
| Operating Regime | Steady-state, continuous duty |
| Food Industry Category | **Pasteurizer / Heater / Cooler / Regenerator** |

## 1.4 Why Plate Heat Exchangers Dominate Food Processing

> [!NOTE]
> Food processing demands **gentle, rapid, and hygienic** thermal treatment to preserve nutrients, flavor, color, and shelf life. The PHE satisfies these constraints because it has **high turbulence at low pressure drop**, short residence time, and is **CIP (Clean-In-Place) friendly** — making it the gold standard for milk pasteurization, juice heating, and dairy processing lines.

**Key physical benchmarks** cited in KTU module standards:
- **Plate thickness:** 0.5 – 1.0 mm (typically **stainless steel AISI 304 / 316** for food contact)
- **Channel spacing:** 1.5 – 5.0 mm
- **Chevron corrugation depth:** 3 – 5 mm
- **Maximum operating pressure:** up to **25 bar (gasketed)** and **40 bar (welded)**
- **Maximum operating temperature:** up to **180 °C (gasketed)** and **350 °C (welded)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Stepwise Logic

A Plate Heat Exchanger performs heat transfer through the following orchestrated sequence:

1. **Frame Compression:** The carrying frame compresses the plate pack via tightening bolts, ensuring gaskets seal each inter-plate passage.
2. **Fluid Distribution:** Hot fluid enters through one corner port and is distributed uniformly across every alternate channel via the **header port** and **distribution area**.
3. **Chevron-Induced Turbulence:** As the fluid travels across the plate, the chevron corrugations generate a sinusoidal flow path. This induces **intermittent separation and reattachment**, drastically increasing the convective heat transfer coefficient.
4. **Heat Conduction Through Plate Wall:** The temperature gradient across the thin stainless steel plate drives conductive heat flux perpendicular to the plate surface.
5. **Counter-Stream Discharge:** Cold fluid enters from the opposite corner, absorbs heat across the plate wall, and exits at the elevated temperature.
6. **Phase Transition Possibility:** In dairy and juice plants, the PHE may operate as an evaporator (shell side condensing steam vs. cold milk) or a regenerator (pasteurized milk preheats incoming raw milk).

## 2.2 The "Why & How" Behind the Design Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why thin plates?** Because the conductive resistance scales linearly with thickness (Fourier's Law: $q = kA\,\frac{\Delta T}{t}$). Halving the plate thickness doubles the conductive flux for a given temperature difference.
>
> **Why corrugate?** A flat plate gives laminar flow with low h. The chevron pattern forces the fluid along a tortuous path, raising the Reynolds number and effective $h$ by a factor of **3 to 5×**.
>
> **Why gaskets?** They channel flow into discrete passages while permitting disassembly for manual cleaning in food plants that run multiple product runs daily.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the core of all PHE numerical problems in KTU University Exams.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F$ | Fundamental design equation (with LMTD correction factor F) | Q in W, U in W/m²·K, A in m² |
| 2 | $Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | Energy balance on hot/cold streams | W |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-Mean Temperature Difference | K |
| 4 | $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_h} + \frac{t_p}{k_p} + R_{f,h} + R_{f,c} + \frac{1}{h_c}$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient (resistance in series) | m²·K/W |
| 5 | $D_e = \frac{2 \cdot b}{\phi}$ | Equivalent hydraulic diameter of a plate channel | m |
| 6 | $Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D_e}{\mu} = \frac{G \cdot D_e}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number in plate channel (mass velocity $G = \rho v$) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Nu = a \cdot Re^b \cdot Pr^{1/3} \left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14}$ | Nusselt number correlation (plate geometry dependent) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\Delta P = \frac{4 \cdot f \cdot L \cdot G^2}{2 \cdot \rho \cdot D_e} \cdot N_p$ | Channel pressure drop (with $N_p$ = number of passes) | Pa |
| 9 | $f = a \cdot Re^{-b}$ | Friction factor (plate-specific) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $P = N \cdot A_p \cdot \eta_{area}$ | Total effective heat transfer area | m² |
| 11 | $N + 1 = 2 \cdot (\text{no. of hot passes}) + 2 \cdot (\text{no. of cold passes})$ | Plate-count vs. pass relationship | integer |
| 12 | $A_{channel} = B \cdot b$ | Single channel cross-sectional area (B = plate width, b = gap) | m² |
| 13 | $\dot{V} = v \cdot A_{channel}$ | Volumetric flow rate per channel | m³/s |
| 14 | $\epsilon = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\mathrm{NTU}(1 - C_r)\right]$ | Effectiveness for single-pass cross-flow (mixed/unmixed) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $\mathrm{NTU} = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$ | Number of Transfer Units | dimensionless |

**Boundary / Limiting Conditions:**
- $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max}$ (heat capacity ratio), bounded between **0 and 1**.
- Plate pitch $b$: typically **2.0 – 4.0 mm** for liquid service, **5.0 – 10 mm** for viscous or two-phase flow.
- Chevron angle $\beta$: typically **30°** (low $\beta$ → low pressure drop, soft duty) to **60°** (high $\beta$ → high heat transfer, high pressure drop, heavy duty).
- Surface enlargement factor $\phi$ (ratio of developed area to projected area): typically **1.15 – 1.30**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Plate Heat Exchangers form the **thermal heart** of nearly every continuous food processing line:

- **Dairy Industry:** High-Temperature Short-Time (HTST) pasteurization of milk at **72 °C for 15 seconds**, plus the regenerative section reusing the outgoing pasteurized milk to preheat incoming raw milk from **4 °C → 60 °C**, saving **≈ 85% of the heating steam**.
- **Fruit Juice Processing:** Apple juice heating from **20 °C → 95 °C** for enzyme deactivation (pectin methyl esterase) before concentration.
- **Beer & Wine:** Wort cooling from boiling to fermentation temperature (**≈ 20 °C**) using a two-stage PHE.
- **Edible Oils:** Pre-heating crude palm oil before refining and deodorization.
- **Tomato Processing:** Pasteurization and hot-break treatment in ketchup and sauce lines.

The PHE's ability to handle **sensitive, viscous, particulate-laden** fluids makes it irreplaceable where shell-and-tube units would foul or fail to meet hygienic standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Point — Fourier's Law of Conduction:** Consider a differential elemental area $dA$ of one plate. The hot fluid at bulk temperature $T_h$ loses heat by convection to the plate surface, heat conducts through the plate thickness, and then convects from the opposite plate face to the cold fluid.

**Step 1:** Convective heat flux from hot fluid to plate surface (hot side):

$$
q'' = h_h \, (T_h - T_{s,h})
$$

where $T_{s,h}$ is the plate surface temperature on the hot side.

**Step 2:** Conductive heat flux through the plate wall of thickness $t_p$ and thermal conductivity $k_p$:

$$
q'' = \frac{k_p}{t_p} \, (T_{s,h} - T_{s,c})
$$

**Step 3:** Convective heat flux from cold plate surface to cold fluid:

$$
q'' = h_c \, (T_{s,c} - T_c)
$$

**Step 4:** To eliminate the unknown surface temperatures, equate all three expressions by rearranging each:

$$
\frac{q''}{h_h} = T_h - T_{s,h}
$$
$$
\frac{q'' \cdot t_p}{k_p} = T_{s,h} - T_{s,c}
$$
$$
\frac{q''}{h_c} = T_{s,c} - T_c
$$

**Step 5:** Add all three equations — the surface temperatures cancel as a telescoping series:

$$
q'' \left( \frac{1}{h_h} + \frac{t_p}{k_p} + \frac{1}{h_c} \right) = T_h - T_c
$$

**Step 6:** Define $U$ such that $q'' = U (T_h - T_c)$:

$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_h} + \frac{t_p}{k_p} + \frac{1}{h_c}
$$

**Step 7 (Final, with fouling):** Adding fouling resistances $R_{f,h}$ and $R_{f,c}$ (units: m²·K/W) to represent deposited milk-stone, protein scale, or burnt-on food residue:

$$
\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_h} + \frac{t_p}{k_p} + R_{f,h} + R_{f,c} + \frac{1}{h_c}
$$

**Practical food-industry fouling values:**
- Milk service: $R_f \approx 0.0001$ to $0.0003$ m²·K/W
- Juice service: $R_f \approx 0.0002$ to $0.0005$ m²·K/W
- Beer/wort service: $R_f \approx 0.0001$ m²·K/W

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Justification:** In a counter-flow PHE, the temperature difference between hot and cold streams is *not* constant along the flow path. We need an *effective mean* that preserves the true integrated driving force.

**Step 1:** For a differential area $dA$, the local heat transfer rate is:

$$
dQ = U \, (T_h - T_c) \, dA
$$

**Step 2:** Energy balance on hot fluid over the differential length:

$$
dQ = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \, dT_h
$$

Hence,

$$
dT_h = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}}
$$

**Step 3:** Energy balance on cold fluid:

$$
dQ = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \, dT_c
$$

Hence,

$$
dT_c = \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}
$$

**Step 4:** Differentiating the temperature difference $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$:

$$
d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c = -dQ \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} \right)
$$

**Step 5:** Define a constant $K = \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} \right)$ for counter-flow. Rearranging:

$$
dQ = -\frac{d(\Delta T)}{K}
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back into $dQ = U \, \Delta T \, dA$:

$$
-\frac{d(\Delta T)}{K} = U \, \Delta T \, dA
$$

$$
\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -U \cdot K \cdot dA
$$

**Step 7:** Integrate from inlet (where $\Delta T = \Delta T_1$) to outlet (where $\Delta T = \Delta T_2$):

$$
\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = -UK \int_0^{A} dA
$$

$$
\ln \left( \frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1} \right) = -U \cdot K \cdot A
$$

**Step 8:** Rearrange to isolate $Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$. Noting that $K = \frac{T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{Q} + \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}}{Q}$, and defining $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$:

$$
Q = U \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}
$$

This confirms the LMTD identity. For multi-pass or cross-flow PHEs, the correction factor $F$ (function of $P$ and $R$ parameters from the standard heat-exchanger charts) must be applied, giving $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROBLEM:** A plate heat exchanger is used to pasteurize milk in a dairy plant. Cold raw milk enters at **4 °C** and is heated to **72 °C** using hot water entering at **90 °C** and leaving at **75 °C**. The mass flow rate of milk is **5,000 kg/h** with $c_p = 3.9$ kJ/kg·K. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 4500 W/m²·K**. Determine:
> (i) The required heat transfer area.
> (ii) The hot water mass flow rate if its $c_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K.

**Solution:**

**Step (i-a):** Calculate the heat duty:

$$
Q = \dot{m}_c \cdot c_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = \frac{5000}{3600} \times 3900 \times (72 - 4)
$$

$$
Q = 1.3889 \, \text{kg/s} \times 3900 \, \text{J/kg·K} \times 68 \, \text{K}
$$

$$
Q = 368{,}333 \, \text{W} \approx 368.33 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step (i-b):** Identify $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ (assuming counter-flow):

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 90 - 72 = 18 \, \text{K}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 75 - 4 = 71 \, \text{K}
$$

**Step (i-c):** Compute LMTD:

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{71 - 18}{\ln(71 / 18)} = \frac{53}{\ln(3.944)} = \frac{53}{1.3729} \approx 38.6 \, \text{K}
$$

**Step (i-d):** Apply correction factor $F \approx 1.0$ for nearly counter-current 1-shell / 1-pass PHE:

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F} = \frac{368{,}333}{4500 \times 38.6 \times 1.0}
$$

$$
A = \frac{368{,}333}{173{,}700} \approx 2.12 \, \text{m}^2
$$

**Step (ii):** Hot water mass flow rate from energy balance:

$$
\dot{m}_h = \frac{Q}{c_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})} = \frac{368{,}333}{4180 \times (90 - 75)}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_h = \frac{368{,}333}{62{,}700} \approx 5.87 \, \text{kg/s} \approx 21{,}140 \, \text{kg/h}
$$

**Final Answer:** $A \approx 2.12 \, \text{m}^2$, $\dot{m}_h \approx 21{,}140 \, \text{kg/h}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting PHE Plate Geometry

| Food Product | Recommended $\beta$ | Recommended $b$ | Fouling Allowance | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Raw milk | 45° | 2.5 mm | Medium | Avoid dead zones; CIP-compatible |
| Cream (high fat) | 30° | 3.0 mm | High | Lower velocity prevents fat damage |
| Fruit juice (clear) | 45° – 60° | 2.5 mm | Low – Medium | High turbulence for thin films |
| Tomato paste (viscous) | 30° | 4.0 mm | High | Wide gap to prevent plugging |
| Beer / wort | 45° | 2.5 mm | Low | Sterile design, polished surfaces |
| Edible oil | 30° | 3.0 mm | Low – Medium | Slow flow to avoid shear damage |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Plate Heat Exchanger

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the **flow, thermal, and mechanical interactions** within a gasketed PHE in a manner suitable for direct KTU Board examination sketches.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Hot Fluid Inlet Header] --> B[Distribution Channel Block A]
    B --> C[Hot Side Plate Channel with Chevron Angle Beta]
    C --> D[Stainless Steel Plate Wall with Thickness tp and Conductivity kp]
    D --> E[Cold Side Plate Channel with Chevron Angle Beta]
    E --> F[Collection Channel Block B]
    F --> G[Cold Fluid Outlet Header]

    H[Cold Fluid Inlet Header] --> I[Distribution Channel Block C]
    I --> E
    F --> J[Hot Fluid Outlet Header]

    K[Fixed Frame Plate] --> C
    K --> E
    L[Movable Pressure Plate] --> C
    L --> E
    M[Tightening Bolt Assembly] --> L

    N[Gasket Ring Hot Channel] -.Seals.-> C
    O[Gasket Ring Cold Channel] -.Seals.-> E

    P[Heat Flux Q from Hot to Cold] -.Conduction.-> D
    QNTU[NTU Effectiveness Epsilon] -.Performance.-> C
    PDROP[Channel Pressure Drop Delta P] -.Hydraulic.-> C
```

## 4.2 Schematic — Multi-Pass Plate Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FrameBlock["Carrying Frame Assembly"]
        F1[Fixed Plate End]
        F2[Movable Pressure Plate]
        F3[Tie Bolts]
    end

    subgraph PlatePack["Plate Pack Alternating Hot and Cold Passes"]
        P1[Plate 1 Hot]
        P2[Plate 2 Cold]
        P3[Plate 3 Hot]
        P4[Plate 4 Cold]
        P5[Plate N Cold]
    end

    subgraph Porting["Corner Porting System"]
        H1[Hot Inlet Top Left]
        H2[Hot Outlet Bottom Right]
        C1[Cold Inlet Bottom Left]
        C2[Cold Outlet Top Right]
    end

    H1 --> P1
    P1 --> P3
    P3 --> H2
    C1 --> P2
    P2 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> C2

    F3 -.Compresses.-> P1
    F3 -.Compresses.-> P5
```

## 4.3 Annotated PHE Component Layout (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start([Begin PHE Operation]) --> Inlet1{Hot Fluid Inlet T_h_in}
    Inlet1 --> Dist[Distribution Area Across Plate Pack]
    Dist --> HeatFlow[Chevron Induced Turbulent Flow Across Plate Surface]
    HeatFlow --> Transfer[Conductive Heat Transfer Through Plate Wall t_p]
    Transfer --> ColdFlow[Turbulent Flow on Cold Side h_c]
    ColdFlow --> Collect[Collection Area Cold Fluid]
    Collect --> Out1([Cold Fluid Outlet T_c_out])

    Inlet2{Cold Fluid Inlet T_c_in} --> Dist
    Dist --> HeatFlow
    HeatFlow --> Transfer
    Transfer --> ColdFlow
    ColdFlow --> Collect

    Collect --> Out2([Hot Fluid Outlet T_h_out])

    Transfer -.Thermal Resistance.-> CalcU[Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient U]
    HeatFlow -.Pressure Drop.-> CalcDP[Delta P Channel]

    CalcDP --> Perf
    Perf --> End([PHE Operation Stable])
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: Define a Plate Heat Exchanger and list any four advantages it offers over a conventional shell-and-tube exchanger in food processing applications. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE) is a compact, surface-type heat transfer device consisting of a series of thin, corrugated metal plates clamped together within a frame, with hot and cold fluids flowing in alternate channels separated by the plate walls.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **High heat transfer coefficient (3000–7000 W/m²·K)** due to chevron-induced turbulence — much higher than shell-and-tube units (≈ 1000–3000 W/m²·K).
2. **Compactness:** Large surface area packed in small volume (β > 700 m²/m³).
3. **Easy to dismantle and clean** manually or via CIP — critical for multi-product food plants.
4. **Flexible capacity expansion:** Plates can be added or removed to scale capacity without changing the frame.
5. **Low fouling tendency** at high turbulence, plus **lower hold-up volume** which is important for hygienic and sanitary service.

---

### Question 2: Explain the significance of the chevron angle ($\beta$) in plate heat exchanger design. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

> The chevron angle $\beta$ is the inclination of the corrugations embossed on the heat-transfer plates, measured relative to the horizontal flow direction. It is the **primary geometric parameter** that controls the trade-off between heat transfer and pressure drop in a PHE.
>
> - **Low $\beta$ (≈ 30°, "soft" plates):** Produces lower turbulence, lower $h$, and lower pressure drop. Used for **low-viscosity liquids** and where pumping cost must be minimized.
> - **High $\beta$ (≈ 60°, "hard" plates):** Produces intense turbulence, high $h$, but high $\Delta P$. Used for **viscous fluids** like cream, syrups, and tomato paste where thermal performance dominates.
> - **Intermediate $\beta$ (≈ 45°):** Most common general-purpose compromise.
>
> Selecting $\beta$ correctly is essential because it directly determines the Nusselt number correlation $Nu = a \cdot Re^b \cdot Pr^{1/3}$ and the friction factor $f = c \cdot Re^{-d}$ used in PHE design.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Examination Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students most commonly lose marks in PHE problems by (a) forgetting to convert mass flow from kg/hr to kg/s, (b) failing to apply the LMTD correction factor $F$ for multi-pass arrangements, and (c) confusing the directionality of $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ (always subtract across the same fluid pair at the same end of the exchanger). Always **carry units through every step** and **box your final answer**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

In a dairy processing plant, a plate heat exchanger is used as a **regenerator** in an HTST milk pasteurization line. The incoming raw milk at **4 °C** is preheated by the outgoing pasteurized milk (which enters the regenerator at **72 °C** and leaves at **15 °C**). The milk mass flow rate is **10,000 kg/h** with $c_p = 3.9$ kJ/kg·K. The overall heat transfer coefficient for the regenerator is **U = 5200 W/m²·K**.

**Part (a)** [7 Marks]: Calculate the heat duty and the temperature of the raw milk leaving the regenerator.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks]: Determine the total plate heat transfer area required if the LMTD correction factor $F = 0.97$ (for the multi-pass configuration).

---

**Part (a) — Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{10{,}000}{3600} = 2.778 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply energy balance on the hot (pasteurized) milk stream to find the heat transferred:

$$
Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}) = 2.778 \times 3900 \times (72 - 15)
$$

$$
Q = 2.778 \times 3900 \times 57 = 617{,}300 \, \text{W} \approx 617.3 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply energy balance on the cold (raw) milk to find its outlet temperature:

$$
Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})
$$

$$
617{,}300 = 2.778 \times 3900 \times (T_{c,out} - 4)
$$

$$
T_{c,out} - 4 = \frac{617{,}300}{10{,}834.2} = 56.97
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{c,out} = 60.97 \, ^\circ\text{C} \approx 61 \, ^\circ\text{C}}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute LMTD. For counter-flow orientation (typical for regenerator):

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 72 - 61 = 11 \, \text{K}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 15 - 4 = 11 \, \text{K}
$$

Since $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, the LMTD reduces to:

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2 = 11 \, \text{K}
$$

(When $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, $\Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1$ because $\lim_{x \to 1} \frac{x - 1}{\ln x} = 1$.)

**Step 2:** Apply the heat transfer equation with correction factor:

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F} = \frac{617{,}300}{5200 \times 11 \times 0.97}
$$

$$
A = \frac{617{,}300}{55{,}484} \approx 11.13 \, \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 11.13 \, \text{m}^2}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

A plate heat exchanger is being designed to heat **tomato pulp** (an intermediate food product in ketchup manufacturing) from **25 °C to 85 °C** using steam condensing at **110 °C** on the other side of the plate. The tomato pulp flow rate is **8,000 kg/h**, with $c_p = 3.95$ kJ/kg·K. The overall heat transfer coefficient estimated is **U = 2500 W/m²·K** (lower because of pulp viscosity).

**Part (a)** [7 Marks]: Compute the heat duty and the LMTD for the system.

**Part (b)** [7 Marks]: Calculate the required plate heat transfer area assuming $F = 1.0$ for a true counter-flow single-pass configuration. Comment on the design implication of the lower $U$ for tomato pulp versus milk.

---

**Part (a) — Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Convert mass flow rate:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{8{,}000}{3600} = 2.222 \, \text{kg/s}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute heat duty:

$$
Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) = 2.222 \times 3950 \times (85 - 25)
$$

$$
Q = 2.222 \times 3950 \times 60 = 526{,}632 \, \text{W} \approx 526.6 \, \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3:** Identify the temperature differences at each end (treating steam at constant 110 °C since condensation is isothermal at the saturation temperature):

$$
\Delta T_1 = T_{steam} - T_{pulp,out} = 110 - 85 = 25 \, \text{K}
$$

$$
\Delta T_2 = T_{steam} - T_{pulp,in} = 110 - 25 = 85 \, \text{K}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute LMTD:

$$
\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{85 - 25}{\ln(85 / 25)} = \frac{60}{\ln(3.4)} = \frac{60}{1.2238} \approx 49.02 \, \text{K}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q \approx 526.6 \, \text{kW}, \quad \Delta T_{lm} \approx 49.02 \, \text{K}}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply heat transfer equation:

$$
A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F} = \frac{526{,}632}{2500 \times 49.02 \times 1.0}
$$

$$
A = \frac{526{,}632}{122{,}550} \approx 4.30 \, \text{m}^2
$$

$$
\boxed{A \approx 4.30 \, \text{m}^2}
$$

**Step 2: Design Comment on Low U:**

> The overall heat transfer coefficient for tomato pulp ($U \approx 2500$ W/m²·K) is **roughly half** that of milk ($U \approx 5000$ W/m²·K) because tomato pulp is more **viscous**, contains **particulates** (cellular debris, seeds, skin fragments), and has a higher tendency to **foul the plate surfaces** with burnt-on solids. Consequently, the designer must:
> - Use plates with **wider channel gaps (3.0–4.0 mm)** to prevent plugging.
> - Use a **lower chevron angle (≈ 30°)** to reduce pressure drop and avoid pumping damage to the particulate structure.
> - Specify **higher fouling allowance** (≈ 0.0005 m²·K/W) in the design.
> - Schedule **more frequent CIP cycles** to maintain performance.
>
> The lower $U$ is the physical penalty for processing more demanding food matrices, and the design must compensate through enlarged plate area, careful selection of plate geometry, and rigorous cleaning protocols.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Plate Heat Exchanger (KTU Module 2)**

- **PHE Definition:** Compact heat exchanger using stacked thin corrugated plates held in a frame; fluids flow in alternate channels separated by the plate wall.
- **Plate thickness:** 0.5 – 1.0 mm; **material:** SS AISI 304 / 316 for food contact.
- **Channel gap (b):** 1.5 – 5.0 mm.
- **Chevron angle $\beta$:** 30° (soft, low ΔP) → 45° (general) → 60° (hard, high h).
- **Plate enlargement factor $\phi$:** 1.15 – 1.30.
- **U range:** **3000 – 7000 W/m²·K** (water–water); **2000 – 4000 W/m²·K** (viscous food).
- **Master equation:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} \cdot F$.
- **LMTD formula:** $\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$.
- **Overall heat transfer resistance:** $\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_h} + \frac{t_p}{k_p} + R_{f,h} + R_{f,c} + \frac{1}{h_c}$.
- **Equivalent diameter:** $D_e = \frac{2b}{\phi}$.
- **Pressure drop:** $\Delta P = \frac{4 f L G^2}{2 \rho D_e} \cdot N_p$ — keep within pump budget (typically < 50–80 kPa).
- **Correction factor F:** For multi-pass and cross-flow, $F < 1$; always apply for accurate sizing.
- **Plate count vs. passes:** $N + 1 = 2 \times (\text{hot passes} + \text{cold passes})$ for a balanced arrangement.
- **CIP compatibility:** Specify gasket material (EPDM, NBR) rated for cleaning chemicals (NaOH, HNO₃) and product temperatures.
- **Food industry applications:** HTST pasteurization, regeneration, juice heating, beer wort cooling, edible oil preheating.
- **Design priority hierarchy:** Hygienic design (3-A / EHEDG) → Heat transfer → Pressure drop → Mechanical integrity.
- **Common exam pitfalls:** Unit conversion (kg/h → kg/s); correct $\Delta T_1$/$\Delta T_2$ assignment; using the right $F$; not forgetting fouling resistance.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: SHELL AND TUBE HEAT EXCHANGERS — CORE CONCEPTUAL FRAMEWORK

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** A **Shell and Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** is a class of recuperative, indirect-contact, pressure-bound, continuous-flow heat transfer apparatus in which one fluid flows through a bundle of parallel cylindrical tubes enclosed within a larger pressure vessel (the *shell*), while a second fluid flows over the outside of the tubes within the shell space. Sensible and/or latent heat transfer between the two streams occurs through the tube wall, the inside and outside of which are coated with predictable thermal resistances including convective films, wall conduction, and fouling layers.

The governing thermal duty equation (integral to every KTU problem) is:

$$
Q \;=\; U \, A \, \Delta T_m
$$

where **Q** is the heat duty in **W**, **U** is the **overall heat transfer coefficient in W/(m²·K)**, **A** is the effective heat transfer area in **m²**, and **ΔTₘ** is the **log-mean temperature difference (LMTD) in K or °C**.

The product fluid is normally contained inside the tubes (the *tube-side*), and the service fluid (heating or cooling medium) circulates on the shell side. The choice of which fluid goes where is dictated by **corrosion potential, fouling tendency, pressure, toxicity, and CIP (Clean-In-Place) accessibility** — a recurring KTU examiner focus in food-plant design.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Coiled Garden Hose Inside a Bucket"

Imagine a high-pressure garden hose carrying hot broth, coiled multiple times inside a sealed metal bucket through which chilled water is recirculated. The hot broth loses heat as it races through the narrow hose walls, the bucket water warms up, and the two streams **never physically mix** — they exchange heat *only* through the metal hose wall.

This analogy maps directly onto a real STHE:

| Real Component | Garden-Hose Analogy | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Tubes | The garden hose | Carries tube-side fluid |
| Shell | The sealed bucket | Houses shell-side fluid |
| Tube sheet | The hose fittings on the bucket wall | Seals tubes to shell |
| Baffles | Internal cross-bars in the bucket | Force shell fluid to flow across tubes |
| Channel head | The hose inlet/outlet cap | Distributes tube-side flow |

## 1.3 Why Shell-and-Tube Geometry Dominates Food Processing Plants

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU Cares:** Food plants (dairy, juice, brewing, edible-oil, starch) almost universally adopt STHEs because they (a) tolerate high **pressure (up to ~100 bar)** with thin-walled tubing, (b) scale to **multi-megawatt duties**, (c) tolerate **thermal expansion** via floating-head designs, and (d) can be **mechanically cleaned (CIP)** by circulating detergent through both sides.

The food industry additionally favours stainless-steel construction (typically **AISI 304 / AISI 316L**), tube-side product flow for hygienic containment, and sanitary tri-clamp connections — all of which are routinely tested as **Part A (3-mark)** questions.

## 1.4 Classification Overview (TEMA Framework)

The **Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association (TEMA)** has standardised shell-and-tube geometry into letter-coded configurations. The three most relevant to KTU problems are:

| TEMA Class | Name | Salient Feature | Typical Food-Plant Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BEM** | Fixed Tubesheet, Bonnet Channel | Cheapest; no expansion joint | Pasteuriser pre-heaters |
| **AEM** | Channel-and-Switch, Removable Bundle | Floating-head, full bundle withdrawal | Dairy regenerators |
| **CFU** | Pull-Through Floating Head | Full bundle removal; largest expansion allowance | Steriliser (UHT) duty |

The letter triplet has a fixed meaning: **First letter = Front-end stationary head type**, **Second letter = Shell type**, **Third letter = Rear-end head type**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# MODULE 2: SHELL AND TUBE HEAT EXCHANGERS — DEEP THEORY & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Principal Components and Their Engineering Roles

A STHE is built up from several discrete mechanical sub-assemblies. Each contributes a defined thermal, hydraulic, or mechanical function, and every KTU 14-mark problem statement almost always lists one or more for design.

### 2.1.1 The Shell
The shell is the **outer pressure vessel** that confines the shell-side fluid. In dairy and beverage service, shells are typically fabricated from **AISI 304 stainless steel** of thickness determined by ASME Section VIII pressure-vessel code:

$$
t_s \;=\; \frac{P \, R}{S \, E \;-\; 0.6 \, P} \;+\; C
$$

where **P** = design pressure, **R** = shell inner radius, **S** = allowable stress, **E** = joint efficiency, and **C** = corrosion allowance (**~1.5 mm** for food-grade service).

### 2.1.2 The Tube Bundle
Composed of **straight tubes** (BEM/AEM) or **U-bent tubes** (CFU-style), commonly of **outer diameter 19.05 mm (¾") or 25.4 mm (1")**, with wall gauges from **1.2 mm to 2.0 mm BWG (Birmingham Wire Gauge)**. The tube pitch (centre-to-centre spacing) is critical:

$$
\text{Pitch ratio } P_t / D_o \;=\; 1.25 \;\; (\text{standard}) \quad \text{or} \quad 1.5 \;\; (\text{CIP-friendly})
$$

A **pitch ratio < 1.25** causes cleaning difficulties; values **> 1.5** increase shell diameter unnecessarily. The **triangular pitch** maximises tube count, while the **square pitch** (rotated 45°) aids mechanical cleaning — KTU board favourite.

### 2.1.3 Baffles
Longitudinal or transverse plates that **force the shell-side fluid to flow perpendicular to the tube bank**, increasing velocity, turbulence, and the shell-side heat transfer coefficient. Baffle cut (the chord cut to allow fluid to bypass the shell wall) is set between **20 % and 45 %** of the shell inside diameter; **25 %** is the textbook optimum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Baffles Matter for the Designer:**
> 1. Increase shell-side $h_o$ via cross-flow turbulence.
> 2. Support tubes against vibration-induced fatigue failure (a common KTU pitfall in 14-mark questions).
> 3. Create pressure drop — must be checked against allowable $\Delta P$.

### 2.1.4 Tube Sheets, Channels, and Heads
- **Tube sheets** are thick rolled plates into which the tubes are expanded (roller-expanded joint), welded, or explosively bonded.
- **Channels** (front and rear) direct tube-side flow via pass-divider plates.
- **Passes** (1, 2, 4, 6 …) reverse flow direction inside the tube bank; an **Nₜ-pass** exchanger has the tube bundle divided into **Nₜ** parallel-flow groups.

### 2.1.5 Tie Rods, Spacers, Sealing Strips
Mechanical ancillaries that hold baffles in rigid alignment and prevent **bypass streaming** of shell fluid around the bundle.

## 2.2 Modes of Heat Transfer Inside an STHE

The total thermal resistance is built from **five** additive terms, written in analogy to electrical series resistance:

$$
\frac{1}{U} \;=\; \frac{1}{h_i} \;+\; R_{f,i} \;+\; \frac{\ln(D_o / D_i)}{2 \pi k_w L} \;+\; R_{f,o} \;+\; \frac{D_i}{D_o \, h_o}
$$

> **Reading the equation (from tube-side fluid to shell-side fluid):**
> 1. **Inside convective film resistance** $1/h_i$ — tube-side fluid pumping energy converted to convective heat flux.
> 2. **Tube-side fouling resistance** $R_{f,i}$ — burnt milk solids, protein deposit, scale.
> 3. **Cylindrical wall conduction** $\ln(D_o/D_i)/(2\pi k_w L)$ — the only term requiring logarithmic geometry.
> 4. **Shell-side fouling resistance** $R_{f,o}$ — cooling-water biofilm, mineral scale.
> 5. **Outside convective film resistance scaled by area ratio** $D_i/(D_o \, h_o)$ — accounts for different inner and outer heat-transfer areas.

For thin tubes and moderate fouling, the simplified form is acceptable:

$$
\frac{1}{U} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{h_i} \;+\; R_{f,i} \;+\; \frac{x_w}{k_w} \;+\; R_{f,o} \;+\; \frac{1}{h_o}
$$

## 2.3 Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

The arithmetic mean ΔT is **only valid for constant specific heats and equal mass-flow × cp products**. Real exchangers deviate, and the correct mean driving force is:

$$
\Delta T_{LM} \;=\; \frac{\Delta T_1 \;-\; \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the **terminal temperature differences** (hot-end and cold-end). The LMTD applies strictly to a **single-pass, counter-current, true counterflow** exchanger. For multi-pass shells the **Correction Factor F** must be applied:

$$
\Delta T_m \;=\; F \cdot \Delta T_{LM}
$$

**P → NT1 → NC1 Parameterisation:** KTU examiners routinely give students a parameter pair **P** and **R**:

$$
P \;=\; \frac{t_2 - t_1}{T_1 - t_1}, \qquad R \;=\; \frac{T_1 - T_2}{t_2 - t_1}
$$

These two dimensionless numbers define the operating "box" on the standard **F-chart**, from which **F** is read off.

## 2.4 Heat-Transfer Coefficient Correlations

### 2.4.1 Tube Side — Sieder–Tate Equation (Laminar, Re < 2100)

$$
Nu_D \;=\; 1.86 \, (Re \, Pr)^{1/3} \left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14} \left(\frac{L}{D}\right)^{-1/3}
$$

### 2.4.2 Tube Side — Dittus–Boelter (Turbulent, Re > 10 000)

$$
Nu_D \;=\; 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{n}
$$

with **n = 0.4 for heating** and **n = 0.3 for cooling** of the tube-side fluid.

### 2.4.3 Shell Side — Kern's Correlation

$$
Nu_D \;=\; 0.36 \, Re_D^{0.55} \, Pr^{0.33} \left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14}
$$

Kern uses the **equivalent shell diameter** $D_e$ for the cross-flow section, defined for square pitch as $D_e = 4(P_t^2 - \pi D_o^2/4)/(\pi D_o)$.

## 2.5 Pressure-Drop Design Checks

Each side must satisfy:

$$
\Delta P_{side} \;\leq\; \Delta P_{allow,side}
$$

with $\Delta P_{allow} \approx$ **10–20 % of system pump head** as a rule of thumb, and an absolute cap of ~**0.7 bar** on the shell side for food duty to avoid pump cavitation.

### Tube-Side

$$
\Delta P_t \;=\; \frac{f_t \, L \, N_p \, G_t^2}{2 \, \rho \, D_i} \;+\; 4 \cdot \frac{G_t^2}{2\rho} \left(\frac{N_p}{N_{t,p}}\right)
$$

### Shell-Side (Kern)

$$
\Delta P_s \;=\; \frac{f_s \, (D_s)(N_b + 1) \, G_s^2}{2 \, \rho \, D_e}
$$

where $N_b$ = number of baffles.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Cheat Sheet — keep the following compact table mentally ready before the exam.** When transcribing into markdown tables, avoid the literal `|` character inside cells.

| # | Equation | Use / Physical Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Energy balance on a single stream | W, kg/s, J/kg·K, K |
| 2 | $Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_m$ | Thermal duty via overall ΔT | W, W/m²·K, m², K |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{LM} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$ | True counterflow mean ΔT | K |
| 4 | $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | Multi-pass correction (F ≤ 0.9 typically) | K |
| 5 | $P = (t_2 - t_1)/(T_1 - t_1)$ | Chart-axis parameter | dimensionless |
| 6 | $R = (T_1 - T_2)/(t_2 - t_1)$ | Chart-axis parameter | dimensionless |
| 7 | $1/U = 1/h_i + R_{f,i} + \ln(D_o/D_i)/(2\pi k_w L) + R_{f,o} + D_i/(D_o h_o)$ | Overall-resistance series sum | m²·K/W |
| 8 | $Nu_D = 0.023 \, Re^{0.8} \, Pr^{n}$ | Dittus–Boelter, tube-side turbulent | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Nu_D = 0.36 \, Re^{0.55} \, Pr^{0.33} (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$ | Kern, shell-side | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta P_t = f_t L N_p G_t^2 / (2 \rho D_i) + 4 N_p G_t^2 / (2\rho)$ | Tube-side pressure drop | Pa |
| 11 | $\Delta P_s = f_s (D_s)(N_b + 1) G_s^2 / (2 \rho D_e)$ | Shell-side pressure drop | Pa |
| 12 | $t_s = PR/(SE - 0.6P) + C$ | Shell thickness (ASME) | m |
| 13 | $D_e = 4(P_t^2 - \pi D_o^2/4)/(\pi D_o)$ | Equivalent shell diameter, square pitch | m |
| 14 | $A = N_t \, \pi \, D_o \, L$ | Total heat-transfer area | m² |
| 15 | $\dot{m} = \rho \, v \, A_c$ | Mass flow rate from velocity & cross-section | kg/s |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

In an **Indian dairy plant**, the STHE serving a pasteuriser routinely operates at:
- Tube-side: raw milk at **4 °C → 72 °C**, $h_i \approx 2500 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$.
- Shell-side: hot water at **80 °C → 75 °C**, $h_o \approx 4500 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$.
- Overall U ≈ **1800 W/m²K** before fouling, dropping to ~**1000 W/m²K** after one shift due to milk-protein deposit — hence **CIP every 8 h** of operation is standard practice.

A **juice concentrate evaporator** running at **55 °Brix tomato paste** uses a multi-pass STHE with **316L tubes** at **vacuum shell-side (-0.85 bar g)** to keep residence time short and preserve flavour volatiles.

# MODULE 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LMTD for Pure Counterflow

**Step 0 — Problem Set-up.** Consider a differential element of exchanger of area **dA**. Let the hot fluid drop in temperature by **dT**, and the cold fluid rise by **dt**. Both streams flow counter-current.

**Step 1 — Differential energy balance.**

$$
dQ \;=\; -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \, dT \;=\; \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \, dt
$$

The negative sign on the hot side accounts for $dT < 0$. This rearranges to:

$$
dT \;=\; -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}}, \qquad dt \;=\; \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}
$$

**Step 2 — Local temperature difference.** Along the element,

$$
d(\Delta T) \;=\; d(T - t) \;=\; dT - dt \;=\; -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} \;-\; \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}
$$

Group the mass-flow–cp terms into a single factor:

$$
\frac{1}{U} \;=\; \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} \;+\; \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}
$$

(This is the *NTU* analog under constant U assumption.) Substituting $dQ = U \, dA \, \Delta T$:

$$
d(\Delta T) \;=\; -U \, \Delta T \, dA \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Separation of variables.**

$$
\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} \;=\; -U \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} \right) dA
$$

**Step 4 — Integration from $\Delta T_1$ (area = 0) to $\Delta T_2$ (area = A).**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) \;=\; -U \left( \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} \right) A
$$

**Step 5 — Express $\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}$ and $\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}$ in terms of known ΔT's.** By overall energy balance:

$$
\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \;=\; \frac{Q}{T_1 - T_2}, \qquad \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \;=\; \frac{Q}{t_2 - t_1}, \qquad Q \;=\; U A \Delta T_m
$$

Substituting these into the integrated equation and simplifying:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) \;=\; -\frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\Delta T_m}
$$

**Step 6 — Invert and solve for $\Delta T_m$.**

$$
\Delta T_m \;=\; \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}
$$

This is the celebrated **LMTD**. The mathematical requirement that the temperature differences at both ends must have the *same sign* is satisfied when neither stream undergoes phase change within the exchanger — a frequently tested KTU condition.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Overall Heat-Transfer Coefficient (U) for a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Tube

**Step 1 — Decompose the resistance network.** Consider heat flowing radially outward from the bulk tube-side fluid to the bulk shell-side fluid. Five sub-resistances in series apply.

**Step 2 — Each sub-resistance expressed per unit length:**

$$
R''_i \;=\; \frac{1}{h_i \pi D_i} \quad \text{(tube-side convection)}
$$

$$
R''_{f,i} \;=\; \frac{R_{f,i}}{\pi D_i} \quad \text{(tube-side fouling)}
$$

$$
R''_{wall} \;=\; \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k_w} \quad \text{(wall conduction, cylindrical)}
$$

$$
R''_{f,o} \;=\; \frac{R_{f,o}}{\pi D_o} \quad \text{(shell-side fouling)}
$$

$$
R''_o \;=\; \frac{1}{h_o \pi D_o} \quad \text{(shell-side convection)}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the resistances in series:**

$$
\frac{1}{U A} \;=\; \sum R'' \cdot \frac{1}{L} \;=\; \frac{1}{L} \left[ \frac{1}{h_i \pi D_i} + \frac{R_{f,i}}{\pi D_i} + \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{2 \pi k_w} + \frac{R_{f,o}}{\pi D_o} + \frac{1}{h_o \pi D_o} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Choose a reference area** (conventionally the **outer tube area**, $A_o = \pi D_o L$). Multiplying both sides by $A_o$:

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} \;=\; \frac{D_o}{D_i \, h_i} \;+\; \frac{D_o \, R_{f,i}}{D_i} \;+\; \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{k_w} \;+\; R_{f,o} \;+\; \frac{1}{h_o}
$$

This is the canonical form appearing on **every** KTU heat-exchanger design sheet. The variant $1/U_i$ uses inner diameter as reference and is obtained by multiplying through by $D_i/D_o$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem Statement:** A counter-current shell-and-tube heat exchanger preheats **20 000 kg/h** of whole milk from **4 °C → 72 °C** using hot water entering at **90 °C** and leaving at **77 °C**. Milk flows inside 1″-OD (25.4 mm) × 1.65 mm-wall 316L tubes ($k_w = 16 \, \text{W/mK}$) at an average velocity of **1.2 m/s**. The tube-side fouling resistance is **0.0004 m²K/W**; shell-side fouling is **0.0002 m²K/W**. Take $c_{p,milk} = 3.9 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $c_{p,water} = 4.18 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $\rho_{milk} = 1030 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $k_{milk} = 0.55 \, \text{W/mK}$, $\mu_{milk} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa·s}$. Estimate **(a)** the hot-water mass flow rate, **(b)** the LMTD, **(c)** the tube-side $h_i$ via Dittus–Boelter, **(d)** the overall U, and **(e)** the required heat-transfer area.

### (a) Hot-water mass flow rate
**Energy balance on the milk (tube side):**

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \dot{m}_m \, c_{p,m} \, (T_{m,out} - T_{m,in})
$$

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \left(\frac{20\,000}{3600}\right) \times 3.9 \times (72 - 4)
$$

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; 5.556 \;\times\; 3.9 \;\times\; 68
$$

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; 5.556 \;\times\; 265.2 \;\approx\; 1473.5 \;\text{kW}
$$

**Energy balance on the water (shell side):**

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \dot{m}_w \, c_{p,w} \, (T_{w,in} - T_{w,out})
$$

$$
1473.5 \;\times\; 10^3 \;=\; \dot{m}_w \;\times\; 4180 \;\times\; (90 - 77)
$$

$$
\dot{m}_w \;=\; \frac{1473.5 \times 10^3}{4180 \times 13} \;\approx\; \frac{1\,473\,500}{54\,340}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_w \;\approx\; 27.12 \;\text{kg/s} \;\approx\; 97\,635 \;\text{kg/h}
$$

### (b) LMTD (counter-flow orientation)
With milk cold (4→72) versus water hot (77→90) flowing counter-current:

**Hot-end ΔT:** water inlet (90 °C) vs milk outlet (72 °C):

$$
\Delta T_1 \;=\; 90 \;-\; 72 \;=\; 18 \;°\text{C}
$$

**Cold-end ΔT:** water outlet (77 °C) vs milk inlet (4 °C):

$$
\Delta T_2 \;=\; 77 \;-\; 4 \;=\; 73 \;°\text{C}
$$

**LMTD:**

$$
\Delta T_{LM} \;=\; \frac{73 - 18}{\ln(73/18)} \;=\; \frac{55}{\ln(4.0556)} \;=\; \frac{55}{1.4006}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{LM} \;\approx\; 39.27 \;°\text{C}
$$

### (c) Tube-side $h_i$ — Dittus–Boelter (heating of milk, n = 0.4)
**Inside diameter:**

$$
D_i \;=\; 25.4 \;-\; 2 \times 1.65 \;=\; 22.1 \;\text{mm} \;=\; 0.0221 \;\text{m}
$$

**Reynolds number:**

$$
Re \;=\; \frac{\rho v D_i}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{1030 \times 1.2 \times 0.0221}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
Re \;=\; \frac{27.32}{10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 27\,318 \;\; (\text{turbulent} > 10\,000 \; \checkmark)
$$

**Prandtl number:**

$$
Pr \;=\; \frac{c_p \mu}{k} \;=\; \frac{3900 \times 10^{-3}}{0.55}
$$

$$
Pr \;=\; \frac{3.9}{0.55} \;\approx\; 7.09
$$

**Dittus–Boelter:**

$$
Nu \;=\; 0.023 \times (27\,318)^{0.8} \times (7.09)^{0.4}
$$

Intermediate:

$$
(27\,318)^{0.8} \;\approx\; 27\,318^{0.8} \;\approx\; 4.95 \times 10^3 \quad (\text{calc below})
$$

Compute by logs: $\log(27318) \approx 4.437$; $0.8 \times 4.437 = 3.549$; $10^{3.549} \approx 3540$.

$$
(7.09)^{0.4} \;\approx\; e^{0.4 \ln 7.09} \;=\; e^{0.4 \times 1.960} \;=\; e^{0.784} \;\approx\; 2.19
$$

Therefore:

$$
Nu \;=\; 0.023 \times 3540 \times 2.19 \;\approx\; 178.3
$$

**Heat-transfer coefficient:**

$$
h_i \;=\; \frac{Nu \, k}{D_i} \;=\; \frac{178.3 \times 0.55}{0.0221}
$$

$$
h_i \;\approx\; 4\,438 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

### (d) Overall U (assume shell-side $h_o \approx 4500 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ for water)

**Diameter ratio:**

$$
\frac{D_i}{D_o} \;=\; \frac{22.1}{25.4} \;=\; 0.870
$$

**Wall conduction term:**

$$
\frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{k_w} \;=\; \frac{\ln(25.4/22.1)}{16} \;=\; \frac{\ln(1.1493)}{16} \;=\; \frac{0.1392}{16} \;=\; 8.70 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Sum the resistances:**

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} \;=\; \frac{0.870}{4438} \;+\; \frac{0.870 \times 0.0004}{1} \;+\; 0.00870 \;+\; 0.0002 \;+\; \frac{1}{4500}
$$

Compute term-by-term:

$$
\frac{0.870}{4438} \;\approx\; 1.961 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
0.870 \times 0.0004 \;=\; 3.48 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{4500} \;\approx\; 2.222 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Sum:**

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} \;=\; (1.961 + 3.480 + 8.700 + 2.000 + 2.222) \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} \;=\; 18.36 \times 10^{-4} \;\approx\; 1.836 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

$$
U_o \;\approx\; \frac{1}{1.836 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 545 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

### (e) Required heat-transfer area

$$
A \;=\; \frac{\dot{Q}}{U_o \cdot \Delta T_{LM}}
$$

$$
A \;=\; \frac{1\,473\,500}{545 \times 39.27}
$$

$$
A \;\approx\; \frac{1\,473\,500}{21\,402}
$$

$$
A \;\approx\; 68.85 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**Tube length calculation:** With tubes of $D_o = 0.0254$ m,

$$
L_{total} \;=\; \frac{A}{\pi D_o} \;=\; \frac{68.85}{\pi \times 0.0254} \;\approx\; \frac{68.85}{0.07980} \;\approx\; 863 \;\text{m of tube}
$$

With **2 passes**, **4 m-long tubes**, the number of tubes required is:

$$
N_t \;=\; \frac{863}{2 \times 4} \;\approx\; 108 \;\text{tubes}
$$

This is internally consistent with a small-to-medium pasteuriser duty.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Tube-Side Fluid

| Criterion | Tube-Side Fluid (Yes/No) | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Higher pressure | **YES** | Tubes are designed to withstand ΔP via thin wall; shells use thicker plate |
| Higher fouling tendency | **YES** | Allows mechanical cleaning of I.D. with brush |
| More corrosive | **YES** | Tube replacement cheaper than shell replacement |
| Lower flow rate | **YES** | Higher velocity achievable with smaller area → better $h_i$ |
| Higher viscosity | **YES** | Multi-pass to boost velocity and turbulence |
| Toxic / hazardous | **YES** | Leakage contained in shell rather than discharged |
| Viscous laminar food product (cream, honey) | **YES** | Use scrapped-surface STHE variant |

# MODULE 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]
**State any three distinguishing features between a fixed-tube-sheet (BEM) and a U-tube (CFU) shell-and-tube heat exchanger used in food plants.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **BEM** uses straight tubes rolled into a stationary tube sheet; **CFU** uses U-bent tubes whose free ends are also rolled into a single tube sheet, enabling differential thermal expansion to be absorbed by tube flexure rather than by expansion joints.
2. **BEM** permits only mechanical cleaning of straight tubes; **CFU** permits mechanical cleaning of the tube inner surface via a brush but **not** of the U-bend itself, so **CFU** is restricted to clean service or fully CIP-able fluids.
3. **BEM** is the lowest-cost configuration for moderate duties (e.g., milk pre-heaters); **CFU** carries the highest cost but accommodates the largest thermal-expansion mismatch, making it standard for **UHT steriliser heaters** in dairy plants.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]
**Define the equivalent shell-side diameter $D_e$ for a square-pitched tube bundle of outer diameter $D_o$ and pitch $P_t$, and explain why it is used in the Kern correlation instead of $D_o$.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
D_e \;=\; \frac{4 \cdot (P_t^2 \;-\; \pi D_o^2 / 4)}{\pi D_o}
$$

$D_e$ represents the **hydraulic diameter of the flow cross-section between adjacent tubes** in the bundle. It is preferred over $D_o$ in the Kern shell-side correlation because the heat-transfer coefficient is governed by the **flow area available to the shell fluid**, not by the bare tube outside diameter. Using $D_o$ alone would underestimate the wetted perimeter and therefore overestimate $Nu$ and $h_o$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Design Problem
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

A plate-type pre-heater is being replaced with a shell-and-tube exchanger in a fruit-juice concentrate plant. Juice flows **inside** the tubes at **0.8 kg/s**, $c_p = 3.85 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$, entering at **25 °C** and leaving at **85 °C**. Hot water at **95 °C** flows shell-side and leaves at **80 °C**. Take overall $U = 1200 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, and assume **single-pass counterflow** operation.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the LMTD, the heat duty, and the required heat-transfer area.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the exchanger is redesigned as a **2-pass-2-pass** configuration with the **same hot and cold inlet/outlet temperatures**, determine the correction factor $F$ and the new area required.

#### Model Solution

**Step 1 — Energy balance on juice.**

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; \dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_{out} - T_{in})
$$

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; 0.8 \times 3.85 \times (85 - 25) \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{Q} \;=\; 0.8 \times 3.85 \times 60 \;=\; 184.8 \;\text{kW} \;=\; 184\,800 \;\text{W}
$$

**Step 2 — Verify outlet temperature of water (already given).** Water inlet = 95 °C, outlet = 80 °C.

**Step 3 — LMTD for counterflow.**

Hot-end ΔT = 95 − 85 = **10 °C**.
Cold-end ΔT = 80 − 25 = **55 °C**.

$$
\Delta T_{LM} \;=\; \frac{55 - 10}{\ln(55/10)} \;=\; \frac{45}{\ln(5.5)} \;=\; \frac{45}{1.7047}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{LM} \;\approx\; 26.40 \;°\text{C}
$$

**Step 4 — Required area.**

$$
A \;=\; \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \, \Delta T_{LM}} \;=\; \frac{184\,800}{1200 \times 26.40}
$$

$$
A \;=\; \frac{184\,800}{31\,680} \;\approx\; 5.83 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**(a) Answer:** $\Delta T_{LM} = 26.4 \;°\text{C}$, $\dot{Q} = 184.8 \; \text{kW}$, $A = 5.83 \; \text{m}^2$.

#### Part (b) — Correction Factor F

**Step 5 — Calculate P and R.**

$$
P \;=\; \frac{t_2 - t_1}{T_1 - t_1} \;=\; \frac{85 - 25}{95 - 25} \;=\; \frac{60}{70} \;=\; 0.857
$$

$$
R \;=\; \frac{T_1 - T_2}{t_2 - t_1} \;=\; \frac{95 - 80}{85 - 25} \;=\; \frac{15}{60} \;=\; 0.250
$$

**Step 6 — Read F from the standard 2-pass-2-pass shell-and-tube F-chart.** For shell passes = 1, tube passes = 2 (i.e., the "1-2" curve), with P = 0.857 and R = 0.25, the chart gives **F ≈ 0.88** (within acceptable design range).

**Step 7 — Corrected ΔT and area.**

$$
\Delta T_m \;=\; F \times \Delta T_{LM} \;=\; 0.88 \times 26.40 \;=\; 23.23 \;°\text{C}
$$

$$
A_{new} \;=\; \frac{184\,800}{1200 \times 23.23} \;=\; \frac{184\,800}{27\,876}
$$

$$
A_{new} \;\approx\; 6.63 \;\text{m}^2
$$

**(b) Answer:** $F \approx 0.88$, $A_{new} \approx 6.63 \; \text{m}^2$, i.e., a **~14 % larger** heat-transfer area than the ideal counterflow case.

---

### Question B — Theoretical + Numerical
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a clean, labelled diagram, derive the **overall heat-transfer coefficient** $U_o$ for a shell-and-tube exchanger, referring to the tube-side and shell-side convective film coefficients, fouling resistances, and the cylindrical wall conduction term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 1-2 STHE uses 1″-OD (25.4 mm) × 1.65 mm-wall stainless-steel tubes ($k_w = 16 \, \text{W/mK}$). The tube-side fluid has $h_i = 2500 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ and the shell-side fluid has $h_o = 4200 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$. Tube-side and shell-side fouling resistances are $R_{f,i} = 0.0003 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$ and $R_{f,o} = 0.0002 \, \text{m}^2\text{K/W}$ respectively. Compute $U_o$.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation** — already established in §3.2 of these notes; key points to reproduce on the answer sheet:

- Five sub-resistances in series.
- Tube-side convection: $1/(h_i \pi D_i L)$.
- Tube-side fouling: $R_{f,i}/(\pi D_i L)$.
- Wall conduction: $\ln(D_o/D_i)/(2 \pi k_w L)$.
- Shell-side fouling: $R_{f,o}/(\pi D_o L)$.
- Shell-side convection: $1/(h_o \pi D_o L)$.
- Sum and simplify with $A_o = \pi D_o L$:

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} \;=\; \frac{D_o}{D_i h_i} \;+\; \frac{D_o R_{f,i}}{D_i} \;+\; \frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{k_w} \;+\; R_{f,o} \;+\; \frac{1}{h_o}
$$

**(b) Numerical Computation.**

**Step 1 — Inner diameter.**

$$
D_i \;=\; 25.4 - 2(1.65) \;=\; 22.1 \;\text{mm} \;=\; 0.0221 \;\text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Diameter ratio.**

$$
\frac{D_o}{D_i} \;=\; \frac{25.4}{22.1} \;=\; 1.1493
$$

**Step 3 — Each resistance term.**

$$
\frac{D_o}{D_i h_i} \;=\; \frac{1.1493}{2500} \;=\; 4.597 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\frac{D_o R_{f,i}}{D_i} \;=\; \frac{1.1493 \times 0.0003}{1} \;=\; 3.448 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\frac{\ln(D_o/D_i)}{k_w} \;=\; \frac{0.1392}{16} \;=\; 8.700 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
R_{f,o} \;=\; 0.0002
$$

$$
\frac{1}{h_o} \;=\; \frac{1}{4200} \;=\; 2.381 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum.**

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} \;=\; (4.597 + 3.448 + 87.00 + 2.000 + 2.381) \times 10^{-4}
$$

Wait — convert all terms to the same exponent: $8.700 \times 10^{-3} = 87.00 \times 10^{-4}$.

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} \;=\; (4.597 + 3.448 + 87.00 + 2.000 + 2.381) \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{U_o} \;=\; 99.43 \times 10^{-4} \;=\; 9.943 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}^2\text{K/W}
$$

**Step 5 — Invert.**

$$
U_o \;=\; \frac{1}{9.943 \times 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 100.6 \;\text{W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**(b) Answer:** $U_o \approx 100.6 \; \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$.

This very low U value indicates **wall conduction dominates** — typical of poorly-conducting or thick-walled tubes. A food engineer would respond by selecting thinner-wall tubes or a higher-conductivity alloy.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
