> **Top 5 Places KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply the area-correction factor** in the wall conduction term — always include $\ln(D_o/D_i)$ (not $(D_o - D_i)$).
> 2. **Mixing $U_i$ and $U_o$** in the same calculation — once $U$ is defined on a side, all other resistances must be expressed on the same reference area.
> 3. **Not checking the F-chart validity** — if the calculated F is below ~0.75 the design is generally infeasible; the student must propose multiple shells in series or a different pass arrangement.
> 4. **Using arithmetic mean ΔT instead of LMTD** — this over-predicts performance by 5-30 % and triggers an immediate mark deduction.
> 5. **Ignoring fouling factors in food service** — milk protein and juice pulp produce major resistances; ignoring them leads to gross under-design.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **STHE definition:** indirect, pressure-bound, two-fluid, tube-and-shell geometry; $Q = U A \Delta T_m$.
> - **TEMA classes:** BEM = cheapest; AEM = mid; CFU = most flexible.
> - **Core formula:** $\Delta T_{LM} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$, **only** for true counterflow.
> - **Correction factor F** must be used for **all** multi-pass shells; read from the F-chart using **P** and **R**.
> - **Overall resistance equation** is **5 additive terms** in series — never forget either fouling term.
> - **Tube-side h** uses **Dittus–Boelter** (turbulent) or **Sieder–Tate** (laminar).
> - **Shell-side h** uses **Kern** with equivalent shell diameter $D_e$.
> - **Pressure drop** must be checked on **both** sides; allowable cap typically ~**0.7 bar**.
> - **Tube-side fluid selection** priority: high P, high fouling, corrosive, toxic, low flow.
> - **Pitch ratio** $P_t/D_o$ = 1.25 (tight) or 1.5 (CIP-friendly); **square pitch** for cleanability.
> - **Baffle cut** optimum ~ **25 %** of shell ID.
> - **Fouling factor** for milk ≈ **0.0002-0.0004 m²K/W**; for cooling water ≈ **0.0002 m²K/W**.
> - **Hygienic design:** AISI 316L tubes, sanitary tri-clamp joints, drainability, full CIP capability.
> - **Sanitisation rule:** the product must always be on the tube side for cleanability.
> - **Vibration check:** cross-flow velocity over tubes must be checked against tube natural frequency.
> - **Material cost ranking:** carbon-steel < AISI 304 < AISI 316L < titanium (rarely used in food).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Evaporators - Mass and Energy Balance - Single Effect and Multiple Effect Evaporators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design of Evaporators - Mass and Energy Balance - Single Effect and Multiple Effect Evaporators # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Evaporator An **Evaporator** is a heat transfer equipment used in food processing industries to concentrate a solution by removing water (or volatile solvent) through boiling, thereby increasing the concentration of the dissolved solids. In food processing, evaporation is widely employed for concentrating fruit juices, milk, sugar syrups, coffee extracts, and tomato paste. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *An evaporator is a device that converts a portion of a liquid into vapour by boiling, using a heat source (commonly steam), and separates the vapour from the remaining concentrated liquid.* The vapour leaving is usually the solvent (water), and the residual liquid is the concentrated product. A **Multiple Effect Evaporator (MEE)** is an arrangement of two or more evaporators connected in series, where the vapour liberated from one effect serves as the heating medium for the next effect, drastically reducing the requirement of fresh steam. ## 1.2 Real-World Conceptual Analogy Imagine boiling milk in a single pan on your gas stove to make *khoa* (thickened milk) — this is a **Single Effect Evaporator**. You use 1 kg of gas to remove 1 kg of water. Now imagine a chain of three pans placed in series on progressively lower flames. The steam rising from the first pan (instead of escaping to the kitchen roof) is ducted to heat the second pan, and the steam from the second pan heats the third. This way, the *same* 1 kg of gas now helps remove about 2.4 kg of water. This is a **Multiple Effect Evaporator** (3-effect). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Students:** Multiple effect is NOT magic — it simply *reuses* the latent heat of vaporisation multiple times before it is finally condensed. This improves **Steam Economy** (kg water evaporated per kg steam). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Latent heat of vaporisation of water (λ): ≈ 2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C and 101.325 kPa - **Specific heat of water (cp_w): 4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** - **Specific heat of food liquids:** 3.5 – 4.0 kJ/(kg·K)** (approximate range for juice/milk) - **Steam economy (E): kg water evaporated per kg live steam supplied** - **Capacity (C): kg water evaporated per hour** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE):** Solutions containing dissolved solids (sugars, salts) boil at a temperature higher than pure water at the same pressure. This **Boiling Point Rise (BPR)** is critical in evaporator design and must always be accounted for in energy balance. ## 1.4 Why Evaporators Matter in Food Industry - Concentration reduces volume → lower transport, packaging, and storage costs - Extends shelf life (e.g., milk → condensed milk, juice → concentrate) - Pre-concentration before drying (spray drying needs ~50% solids) - Used in sugar refining, dairy, brewing, and sauce manufacturing # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Types of Evaporators (Classification) | Type | Construction | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Open Pan / Jacketed Kettle** | Simple hemispherical pan with steam jacket | Small-scale khoa, jam boiling | | **Batch-type Evaporator** | Closed vessel with steam coil | Pharmaceutical & dairy | | **Forced Circulation Evaporator** | Pump forces liquid past heat exchanger | Viscous, fouling liquids | | **Falling Film Evaporator** | Liquid forms thin film inside vertical tube | Heat-sensitive juices | | **Rising Film (Kestner) Evaporator** | Liquid rises as film due to vapour drag | Clear juices, milk | | **Plate Evaporator** | Plate heat exchanger as evaporator | Dairy, viscous food | ## 2.2 Methods of Feeding in Multiple Effect Evaporator 1. **Forward Feed:** Feed enters effect 1 (highest pressure/temperature) and flows to effect n (lowest). Pressure gradient drives flow. **Most common in food industry.** 2. **Backward Feed:** Feed enters last effect (coldest) and is pumped towards first. Suitable for viscous liquids. 3. **Parallel Feed:** Feed is supplied separately to each effect. Concentrate removed from each. 4. **Mixed Feed:** Combination of forward and backward feed. ## 2.3 Mass Balance – Single Effect Evaporator Let: - **F** = feed rate (kg/h) - **xF** = mass fraction of solids in feed - **L** = concentrated liquor (kg/h) - **xL** = mass fraction of solids in product - **V** = vapour removed (kg/h) - **S** = steam supplied (kg/h) > [!NOTE] > **Total Mass Balance:** $F = L + V$ > [!NOTE] > **Solids Mass Balance (total solids are conserved, only water leaves):** $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$ Solving these two equations: $$L = F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L}$$ $$V = F - L = F \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$$ ## 2.4 Energy Balance – Single Effect Evaporator > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy In = Energy Out**, at steady state, neglecting heat losses. Energy supplied by steam = Energy to vaporise water + Sensible heat to bring feed to boiling + Sensible heat of concentrated liquor leaving (if superheated). $$S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + F \cdot c_{p,F} \cdot (T_b - T_F) + L \cdot c_{p,L} \cdot (T_b - T_L)$$ For a *dilute solution* with *negligible BPE* and when the feed enters at boiling point ($T_F = T_b$), this simplifies to: $$S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v$$ $$\therefore S = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_v}{\lambda_s}$$ **Steam Economy (Single Effect):** $$E_1 = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_v}$$ Since λs ≈ λv for water-steam at similar conditions, **E₁ ≈ 0.8 to 1.0**. ## 2.5 Capacity and Economy of Multiple Effect Evaporator Let $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_n$ be the vapour produced in each effect. Total evaporation: $$V_T = V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n = F \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$$ Approximately, if all effects operate at similar conditions: $$V_1 \approx V_2 \approx \cdots \approx V_n \approx \frac{V_T}{n}$$ **Steam Economy of n-effect evaporator (approximate):** $$E_n \approx 0.8 \cdot n$$ So for 3-effect, E ≈ 2.4; for 4-effect, E ≈ 3.2. ## 2.6 Energy Balance – Multiple Effect Evaporator For each effect *i*, neglecting sensible heats: $$S_i \cdot \lambda_{s_i} \approx V_i \cdot \lambda_{v_i}$$ For **forward feed** n-effect system: - Effect 1 receives live steam: $S \cdot \lambda_{s_1}$ - Effect 1 produces $V_1$ vapour → used as heating steam in Effect 2 - Effect 2 produces $V_2$ → used in Effect 3, and so on - Only Effect 1 receives live steam; **S is the only external steam input** $$S = \frac{V_1 \cdot \lambda_{v_1}}{\lambda_{s_1}}$$ Overall steam economy: $$E_n = \frac{V_T}{S} = \frac{V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n}{S}$$ ## 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET | Formula | Expression | Application | |---|---|---| | Mass balance | $F = L + V$ | All evaporators | | Solids balance | $F x_F = L x_L$ | All evaporators | | Concentrate rate | $L = F \cdot \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}$ | Final product flow | | Vapour removed | $V = F \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$ | Capacity | | Steam required (single) | $S = \dfrac{V \lambda_v}{\lambda_s}$ | Steam sizing | | Steam economy | $E = \dfrac{V}{S}$ | Performance | | Steam economy (n-effect) | $E_n \approx 0.8 n$ | MEE design | | Total evaporation | $V_T = \sum V_i$ | MEE capacity | | BPE correction | $T_{solution} = T_{water} + BPR$ | Energy balance | | Heat transfer rate | $Q = U A \Delta T$ | Heat transfer area | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Rule:** In all mass/energy balance problems, *first apply mass balance to find V and L*, *then apply energy balance to find steam S*. Marks are awarded in this exact sequence. ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - Used to size steam lines, vapour lines, and condensers - Determines heating surface area $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T}$ - Helps choose between single vs. multiple effect based on feed temperature, viscosity, and heat sensitivity - Essential for plant layout — sizing of boiler, cooling tower, and vacuum system depends on this calculation # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass Balance for Single Effect Evaporator Let us consider a single effect evaporator operating at steady state. **Assumptions:** - Steady state - No solids lost or gained - Only water vapour leaves with the vapour stream **Step 1:** Draw the control volume. Inputs: Feed (F), Steam (S). Outputs: Concentrate (L), Vapour (V), Condensate. **Step 2:** Apply overall mass balance. Input mass = Output mass $$F + S = L + V + S_{cond}$$ But the steam condensate mass equals the steam input mass (it leaves as liquid). $$\therefore F = L + V$$ **Step 3:** Apply component (solids) balance. Only feed and concentrate carry solids; vapour and steam condensate carry no solids. $$\text{Solids in feed} = \text{Solids in concentrate}$$ $$F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$$ **Step 4:** Solve simultaneously. From (1): $L = F - V$ Substitute in (2): $F \cdot x_F = (F - V) \cdot x_L$ $$F \cdot x_F = F \cdot x_L - V \cdot x_L$$ $$V \cdot x_L = F \cdot x_L - F \cdot x_F = F \cdot (x_L - x_F)$$ $$V = F \cdot \frac{x_L - x_F}{x_L} = F \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$$ And the concentrate: $$L = F - V = F - F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L}$$ $$\boxed{L = F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L} \quad \text{and} \quad V = F \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Balance for Single Effect Evaporator (Cold Feed) **Assumptions:** - Steady state - Latent heat of vaporisation of water (≈ 2257 kJ/kg at 100 °C) - No heat loss to surroundings - Feed enters cold at $T_F$, boils at $T_b$, concentrate leaves at $T_b$ **Step 1:** Identify all energy streams. - **In:** Steam enthalpy $S \cdot H_s$ (saturated steam), Feed enthalpy $F \cdot H_F$ - **Out:** Vapour enthalpy $V \cdot H_v$, Concentrate enthalpy $L \cdot H_L$, Condensate enthalpy $S \cdot H_c$ **Step 2:** Net energy balance (steam contributes latent heat). $$S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v + F \cdot c_{p,F} \cdot (T_b - T_F) + L \cdot c_{p,L} \cdot (T_b - T_L)$$ **Step 3:** Special case — feed at boiling point, concentrate also at boiling point, no BPE. The two sensible heat terms vanish. $$S \cdot \lambda_s = V \cdot \lambda_v$$ $$\boxed{S = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_v}{\lambda_s}}$$ **Step 4:** Steam economy. $$E_1 = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_v}$$ At 100 °C: $\lambda_s \approx 2257$ kJ/kg, $\lambda_v \approx 2257$ kJ/kg. $$\therefore E_1 \approx 1.0 \text{ (ideal single effect)}$$ In practice, due to BPE, sensible heat, and heat losses, $E_1 \approx 0.8 - 0.9$. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Steam Economy of n-Effect Evaporator **For an n-effect evaporator with forward feed:** **Step 1:** Energy balance for Effect 1 (receives live steam). $$S \cdot \lambda_{s_1} = V_1 \cdot \lambda_{v_1}$$ **Step 2:** Energy balance for Effect 2 (heated by vapour from Effect 1). $$V_1 \cdot \lambda_{v_1} = V_2 \cdot \lambda_{v_2}$$ **Step 3:** Energy balance for Effect 3. $$V_2 \cdot \lambda_{v_2} = V_3 \cdot \lambda_{v_3}$$ **Step 4:** Continue similarly until Effect n. $$V_{n-1} \cdot \lambda_{v_{n-1}} = V_n \cdot \lambda_{v_n}$$ **Step 5:** Chain equality. $$S \cdot \lambda_{s_1} = V_1 \cdot \lambda_{v_1} = V_2 \cdot \lambda_{v_2} = \cdots = V_n \cdot \lambda_{v_n}$$ **Step 6:** Assuming all $\lambda$ values are approximately equal (effects operate at similar pressures): $$S \approx V_1 \approx V_2 \approx \cdots \approx V_n$$ **Step 7:** Total evaporation in n-effect. $$V_T = V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n \approx n \cdot S$$ **Step 8:** Steam economy. $$E_n = \frac{V_T}{S} = \frac{n \cdot S}{S} = n$$ **Step 9:** Realistic correction. Due to BPE, heat losses, and flashing, in practice: $$\boxed{E_n \approx 0.8 \cdot n}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why 0.8 factor?** Each subsequent effect operates at lower temperature and pressure. The vapour from the previous effect cannot fully transfer all its latent heat because of (a) temperature gradient losses, (b) BPE in the concentrate, (c) sensible heat demands. Empirically, KTU problems use the 0.8 factor. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Area of Heat Transfer Surface in Single Effect From Fourier's law of heat conduction applied to evaporator wall: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$ Where: - **Q** = total heat transferred (W or kJ/h) - **U** = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K) - **A** = heating surface area (m²) - **ΔT** = temperature difference between steam and boiling liquid (K) **Step 1:** Compute Q from steam side. $$Q = S \cdot \lambda_s$$ **Step 2:** Equate. $$S \cdot \lambda_s = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$ $$\boxed{A = \frac{S \cdot \lambda_s}{U \cdot \Delta T}}$$ For multiple effect, each effect has its own area, and the design usually ensures equal area in each effect for ease of fabrication: $$A_i = \frac{V_i \cdot \lambda_{v_i}}{U_i \cdot \Delta T_i}$$ ## 3.5 KTU Typical Numerical Problem Template **Given:** - Feed: F kg/h of a solution with xF % solids - Required product concentration: xL % - Latent heat of steam: λs kJ/kg - Latent heat of vapour: λv kJ/kg - Feed enters at boiling point (or specific temperature TF) **Find:** (a) kg of water evaporated V, (b) kg of concentrate L, (c) kg of steam S, (d) steam economy E. **Solution template:** $$V = F \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$$ $$L = F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L}$$ $$S = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_v}{\lambda_s}$$ $$E = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{\lambda_s}{\lambda_v}$$ If the same system is converted to a 3-effect evaporator: $$V_{total} = F \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) \text{ (same)}$$ $$S_{3-effect} \approx \frac{V_{total}}{0.8 \times 3} = \frac{V_{total}}{2.4}$$ This shows steam consumption drops by ~58% compared to single effect. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Single Effect Evaporator Functional Flow ```mermaid graph TD A[Feed Tank
F kg/h, xF solids] --> B[Feed Pump] B --> C[Evaporator Vessel
Steam Side S kg/h] C --> D[Separator
Vapour from Liquid] D --> E[Condenser
Vacuum System] D --> F[Concentrate Outlet
L kg/h, xL solids] C --> G[Condensate Outlet
S kg/h liquid] E --> H[Cooling Water In] E --> I[Cooling Water Out] J[Live Steam Supply] --> C style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333 style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333 style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Multiple Effect Evaporator (3-Effect Forward Feed) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph E1[Effect 1 - Highest Pressure] F1[Feed In
F kg/h] S1[Live Steam
S kg/h] V1Out[Vapour V1 to Effect 2] L1[Concentrate to Effect 2] end subgraph E2[Effect 2 - Medium Pressure] S2In[Steam V1 from Effect 1] V2Out[Vapour V2 to Effect 3] L2[Concentrate to Effect 3] end subgraph E3[Effect 3 - Lowest Pressure] S3In[Steam V2 from Effect 2] V3Out[Vapour V3 to Condenser] L3[Final Concentrate
L kg/h] end F1 --> E1 S1 --> E1 E1 --> V1Out V1Out --> E2 E1 --> L1 L1 --> E2 E2 --> S2In E2 --> V2Out V2Out --> E3 E2 --> L2 L2 --> E3 E3 --> S3In E3 --> V3Out E3 --> L3 V3Out --> CON[Barometric Condenser] CON --> VAC[Vacuum Pump] CON --> CW[Cooling Water] style E1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 style E2 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333 style E3 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333 style CON fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Mass and Energy Balance Block ```mermaid graph TD IN1[Steam Input
S kg/h
Enthalpy Hs] --> EB[Energy Balance
Equation] IN2[Feed Input
F kg/h
Enthalpy Hf] --> EB OUT1[Concentrate Output
L kg/h
Enthalpy HL] --> EB OUT2[Vapour Output
V kg/h
Enthalpy Hv] --> EB EB --> MB[Mass Balance Verification] MB --> RES[Result: S, L, V, E] style EB fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333 style MB fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style RES fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Comparison Single vs. Multiple Effect ```mermaid graph TD Q1[1 kg Live Steam] --> SE[Single Effect Evaporator] SE --> R1[Removes ~0.85 kg water] SE --> ECON1[Economy E1 ~ 0.8 to 1.0] Q2[1 kg Live Steam] --> ME[Triple Effect Evaporator] ME --> R3[Removes ~2.4 kg water] ME --> ECON2[Economy E3 ~ 2.4] style SE fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333 style ME fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Evaporator and state its application in food industry. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Model Answer:** An **evaporator** is a heat transfer device used to concentrate a liquid food product by boiling off the solvent (usually water) under controlled conditions, thereby increasing the solids content of the remaining liquid. **Applications in food industry:** 1. Concentration of fruit juices (orange, mango, apple juice) 2. Concentration of milk for condensed milk and khoa manufacture 3. Pre-concentration of coffee extract before spray drying 4. Sugar refining (syrup concentration) 5. Production of tomato paste and sauces --- ### Question 2: What is steam economy of an evaporator? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** **Steam Economy (E)** of an evaporator is defined as the **kilograms of water evaporated per kilogram of live steam supplied** to the evaporator. $$E = \frac{\text{Mass of water evaporated (V)}}{\text{Mass of live steam supplied (S)}}$$ For a single effect, $E_1 \approx 0.8$ to $1.0$. For an n-effect evaporator, $E_n \approx 0.8 n$. A higher economy means less steam consumption per unit water removal, leading to lower operating cost. --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — INTERNAL CHOICE ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **(a)** A single effect evaporator is used to concentrate a fruit juice from **10% to 50% total solids**. The feed rate is **10,000 kg/h** entering at the boiling point. The latent heat of steam is **2200 kJ/kg** and latent heat of vaporisation of water is **2356 kJ/kg**. Calculate: (i) Mass of water evaporated per hour **[3 marks]** (ii) Mass of concentrate produced per hour **[2 marks]** (iii) Steam required per hour **[3 marks]** (iv) Steam economy of the evaporator **[2 marks]** **(b)** If the same evaporation duty is carried out in a **triple effect evaporator** (forward feed), calculate the steam required per hour and compare the steam economy. **[4 marks]** #### MODEL SOLUTION (a): **Given:** F = 10,000 kg/h, $x_F$ = 0.10, $x_L$ = 0.50, $\lambda_s$ = 2200 kJ/kg, $\lambda_v$ = 2356 kJ/kg. **(i) Mass of water evaporated:** $$V = F \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 10000 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{0.10}{0.50}\right)$$ $$V = 10000 \cdot (1 - 0.20) = 10000 \cdot 0.80$$ $$\boxed{V = 8000 \text{ kg/h}}$$ **(ii) Mass of concentrate produced:** $$L = F \cdot \frac{x_F}{x_L} = 10000 \cdot \frac{0.10}{0.50} = 10000 \cdot 0.20$$ $$\boxed{L = 2000 \text{ kg/h}}$$ **(iii) Steam required per hour:** $$S = \frac{V \cdot \lambda_v}{\lambda_s} = \frac{8000 \cdot 2356}{2200}$$ $$S = \frac{18848000}{2200} = 8567.27 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$\boxed{S \approx 8567.3 \text{ kg/h}}$$ **(iv) Steam economy:** $$E_1 = \frac{V}{S} = \frac{8000}{8567.27} = 0.934$$ $$\boxed{E_1 \approx 0.934}$$ #### MODEL SOLUTION (b): **For triple effect evaporator:** Total water to be evaporated = $V_T$ = 8000 kg/h (same as single effect). Approximate steam economy for 3-effect: $$E_3 \approx 0.8 \cdot 3 = 2.4$$ Steam required: $$S_3 = \frac{V_T}{E_3} = \frac{8000}{2.4}$$ $$\boxed{S_3 \approx 3333.3 \text{ kg/h}}$$ **Comparison:** | Parameter | Single Effect | Triple Effect | |---|---|---| | Steam Required | 8567.3 kg/h | 3333.3 kg/h | | Steam Economy | 0.934 | 2.4 | | Steam Saved | — | 5234 kg/h (61.1% reduction) | **Conclusion:** The triple effect evaporator reduces steam consumption by approximately **61%**, leading to substantial savings in fuel/boiler cost, though with higher capital investment for additional effects. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **(a)** Define **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)**. Why is it important in evaporator design? Explain with an example. **[5 marks]** **(b)** A forward feed **triple effect evaporator** concentrates tomato pulp from **6% to 30% solids** at a feed rate of **5000 kg/h**. Assuming equal evaporation in each effect and latent heat = **2300 kJ/kg**, calculate: **(i)** total water evaporated, **(ii)** steam required per hour, **(iii)** steam economy, **(iv)** heat transfer area of each effect if U = 1500 W/m²K and ΔT per effect = 25 K. **[9 marks]** #### MODEL SOLUTION (a): **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE):** Boiling Point Elevation (also called **Boiling Point Rise, BPR**) is the increase in boiling temperature of a solution compared to that of pure water at the same pressure, due to the presence of dissolved solids (e.g., sugar, salt). $$\text{BPE} = T_{solution} - T_{water}$$ **Importance in evaporator design:** 1. The actual temperature driving force (ΔT) across the heating surface is **less than** the apparent ΔT (steam temperature − pure water boiling point). This **reduces heat transfer rate**. 2. The heating surface area must be **increased** to compensate, raising capital cost. 3. BPE increases with concentration; hence the last effect in a multiple effect system has the highest BPE and the lowest ΔT. 4. For example, a 50% sugar syrup boils at approximately 102 °C at atmospheric pressure (BPE ≈ 2 °C), while a saturated salt solution boils at about 108 °C (BPE ≈ 8 °C). $$\boxed{A_{actual} = \frac{Q}{U \cdot (T_{steam} - T_{water} - BPE)}}$$ If BPE is ignored, the calculated area is **under-designed**, leading to lower capacity. --- #### MODEL SOLUTION (b): **Given:** F = 5000 kg/h, $x_F$ = 0.06, $x_L$ = 0.30, λ = 2300 kJ/kg, U = 1500 W/m²K, ΔT = 25 K per effect, n = 3. **(i) Total water evaporated:** $$V_T = F \cdot \left(1 - \frac{x_F}{x_L}\right) = 5000 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{0.06}{0.30}\right)$$ $$V_T = 5000 \cdot (1 - 0.20) = 5000 \cdot 0.80$$ $$\boxed{V_T = 4000 \text{ kg/h}}$$ **(ii) Steam required per hour:** For triple effect: $E_3 \approx 0.8 \cdot 3 = 2.4$ $$S = \frac{V_T}{E_3} = \frac{4000}{2.4} = 1666.67 \text{ kg/h}$$ $$\boxed{S \approx 1666.7 \text{ kg/h}}$$ **(iii) Steam economy:** $$E_3 = \frac{V_T}{S} = \frac{4000}{1666.67} = 2.4$$ $$\boxed{E_3 = 2.4}$$ **(iv) Heat transfer area per effect:** Equal evaporation per effect: $$V_i = \frac{V_T}{3} = \frac{4000}{3} = 1333.33 \text{ kg/h per effect}$$ Heat duty per effect: $$Q_i = V_i \cdot \lambda = 1333.33 \cdot 2300 \text{ kJ/h}$$ $$Q_i = 3066666.67 \text{ kJ/h}$$ Convert to Watts: 1 kJ/h = 0.2778 W $$Q_i = 3066666.67 \cdot 0.2778 = 851851.85 \text{ W} \approx 851.85 \text{ kW}$$ Area per effect: $$A_i = \frac{Q_i}{U \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{851851.85}{1500 \cdot 25}$$ $$A_i = \frac{851851.85}{37500} = 22.72 \text{ m}^2$$ $$\boxed{A_i \approx 22.72 \text{ m}^2 \text{ per effect}}$$ Total heat transfer area for all 3 effects: $$A_{total} = 3 \cdot 22.72 = 68.16 \text{ m}^2$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversion from kJ/h to W** when computing heat transfer area using U in W/m²K. Always multiply kJ/h by 0.2778 or divide by 3.6 to get kW. > 2. **Not accounting for BPE** in the temperature driving force. Use effective ΔT = $T_{steam} - T_{boil,water} - BPE$. > 3. **Using $E_n = n$ instead of $E_n = 0.8n$** in multiple-effect problems — this gives an unrealistically low steam requirement. Always use the 0.8 factor unless the problem explicitly states "ideal". > 4. **Mixing up solids balance**: writing $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_F$ instead of $F \cdot x_F = L \cdot x_L$. The right side must use **concentrate** concentration $x_L$. > 5. **Assuming feed enters at boiling point** when the problem states cold feed. Always include sensible heat term $F \cdot c_p \cdot (T_b - T_F)$. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 Topic: Design of Evaporators** - **Evaporator** = equipment that concentrates a solution by vaporising the solvent (water) using heat. - **Single Effect** uses fresh steam in only one vessel; steam economy ≈ 0.8–1.0. - **Multiple Effect (n-effect)** reuses vapour from one effect as heating medium for the next; steam economy ≈ 0.8n. - **Mass Balance:** $F = L + V$ and $F x_F = L x_L$ → solve to get $V$ and $L$. - **Vapour removed:** $V = F \left(1 - \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}\right)$. - **Concentrate produced:** $L = F \cdot \dfrac{x_F}{x_L}$. - **Energy Balance (dilute, feed at boiling):** $S \lambda_s = V \lambda_v$. - **Steam required:** $S = \dfrac{V \lambda_v}{\lambda_s}$. - **Steam Economy:** $E = \dfrac{V}{S}$. - **Boiling Point Elevation (BPE)** reduces effective ΔT and must be subtracted in heat transfer area calculation. - **Forward feed** is most common for food liquids; **backward feed** is used for viscous liquids. - **Heat transfer area:** $A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{effective}}$. - **Key constants:** $\lambda_{steam}$ ≈ 2200–2257 kJ/kg, $c_p$ of water = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K). - **Capacity** of evaporator = total water evaporated per hour. - **Trade-off:** more effects → higher economy, but higher capital cost, larger floor space, and lower ΔT per effect. - Always show **all units** in the final answer; marks are deducted for missing units in KTU valuation. - In **multiple effect**, vapour from final effect goes to **barometric condenser** + **vacuum pump** to maintain low pressure. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** In Part B (14-mark) problems, structure your answer in **Given → Find → Formula → Calculation → Result**. Use **underlined or boxed final answers** for each part. Marks are awarded for the **process**, not just the final number.

topics.title: Design of Dryers - Mass and Energy Balance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Dryers - Mass and Energy Balance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Drying

**Drying** is a unit operation in which the moisture content of a food material is reduced by the application of heat, causing thermal vaporization of the water (or solvent) and subsequent removal of the vapor. In food processing, drying is one of the oldest and most energy-intensive preservation methods, and its design requires simultaneous treatment of **heat transfer** (to supply latent heat of vaporization), **mass transfer** (movement of moisture from inside the product to the drying-air boundary layer), and **momentum transfer** (air flow through the bed).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Drying is the removal of relatively small amounts of water (or other liquid) from a solid material by evaporation into a gas stream. The design of a dryer is fundamentally a coupled **mass and energy balance** problem in which we must account for the moisture carried in, the moisture carried out (in the product and in the exhaust air), and the energy supplied (sensible + latent)."

## 1.2 Definition of Mass and Energy Balance

- **Mass Balance (Material Balance):** The principle of conservation of mass applied to a drying system. For steady-state operation, *Mass In = Mass Out + Accumulation*. Since the dryer is usually at steady state, the **total mass entering = total mass leaving**. Equivalently, the mass of dry solid is conserved because dry solid is neither created nor destroyed during drying.

- **Energy Balance:** The principle of conservation of energy applied to a drying system. The heat supplied to the dryer is consumed in (i) heating the wet feed from its inlet temperature to the product temperature, (ii) evaporating the moisture, (iii) heating the vapor and any entrained solids to the exhaust temperature, and (iv) compensating for heat losses to the surroundings through the dryer walls.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bank Account" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Moisture as Money in a Bank Account**
> Imagine a food product as a **bank account** where the *moisture is cash* sitting in the account, and the *dry solid is the bank vault itself*. Drying is like a withdrawal — you (the engineer) want to take out a controlled amount of cash (water) so that the balance (moisture content) reaches a target value. The **Mass Balance** is simply: *Opening cash + deposits = Withdrawals + Closing cash*. The **Energy Balance** is the "fee" you must pay the bank (in the form of heat) for processing this withdrawal — you need heat to lift the cash out (latent heat of evaporation) and to cover the bank's overhead (heat losses).

## 1.4 Foundational Terminology

Before any dryer can be designed, the engineer must master moisture-content conventions:

- **Moisture content on Wet Basis (w):**
$$w = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s} = \frac{\text{mass of water}}{\text{total mass}}$$

- **Moisture content on Dry Basis (X):**
$$X = \frac{m_w}{m_s} = \frac{\text{mass of water}}{\text{mass of dry solid}}$$

- **Conversion relations (KTU high-frequency):**
$$X = \frac{w}{1 - w} \qquad \text{and} \qquad w = \frac{X}{1 + X}$$

where $m_w$ is the mass of moisture (water) and $m_s$ is the mass of **bone-dry solid**.

- **Free moisture** = moisture in excess of the **equilibrium moisture content (EMC)** at the given air conditions. Only free moisture can be removed by a given drying operation.
- **Bound moisture** = moisture held so tightly (by sorption, capillary forces, or cell walls) that it cannot be removed at the given air humidity/temperature; it requires more severe conditions.
- **Equilibrium Moisture Content (EMC, $X^*$)** = the moisture content at which the material is in sorption equilibrium with the surrounding air (no net drying occurs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Constants to Memorize for KTU Exams**
> - Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C: $\lambda_{100} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - Latent heat of vaporization at 0 °C: $\lambda_0 = 2501 \text{ kJ/kg}$
> - Specific heat of dry air: $c_{p,\text{air}} = 1.005 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - Specific heat of water vapor: $c_{p,\text{vap}} = 1.88 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - Specific heat of liquid water: $c_{p,w} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - Dry air density at STP: $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$
> - Psychrometric gas constant: $R_w = 0.4615 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$

## 1.5 Why Mass and Energy Balance Together?

A dryer cannot be sized (length, area, or volume) without simultaneously solving the **mass balance** (to find how much water must be evaporated and how much dry air is needed) and the **energy balance** (to find the heater duty, fuel consumption, and thermal efficiency). They are coupled because the air that picks up moisture also carries away enthalpy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Streams of a Continuous Dryer

A generic convective dryer (tray, belt, rotary, fluidized, spray, flash) has three inlet streams and three outlet streams, and both balances close on the **dry-solid** and **dry-air** components.

| Stream | Phase Carried | Mass Components | Energy Components |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wet feed (in) | Solid + liquid water | Dry solid $F_s$, water $F_s X_1$ | Sensible heat of feed |
| Dry product (out) | Solid + residual water | Dry solid $P_s$, water $P_s X_2$ | Sensible heat of product |
| Air in (in) | Dry air + water vapor | Dry air $A$, vapor $A Y_1$ | Sensible heat of fresh air |
| Air out (out) | Dry air + water vapor | Dry air $A$, vapor $A Y_2$ | Sensible heat + latent of exhaust |
| Heater (energy in) | — | — | Heat $Q$ supplied |
| Losses (energy out) | — | — | Wall loss $Q_L$ |

## 2.2 Mass Balance — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Define a basis.** Take **1 kg of bone-dry solid** flowing through the dryer per unit time (or any convenient $F_s$). This is a *steady-state* assumption.

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of dry solid.**
$$F_s = P_s$$
The dry solid in the feed equals the dry solid in the product (no reaction, no entrainment assumed for now).

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of total mass (overall balance).**
$$F_s + F_s X_1 + A + A Y_1 = P_s + P_s X_2 + A + A Y_2$$
Since $A$ and $F_s$ cancel on both sides, the *only* meaningful mass balance reduces to a **moisture balance**:
$$F_s X_1 + A Y_1 = P_s X_2 + A Y_2$$
Because $F_s = P_s$ we can write the **mass of water evaporated per unit time** $W$ as:
$$W = F_s (X_1 - X_2) = A (Y_2 - Y_1)$$

where $Y$ is the **absolute humidity (humidity ratio)** of the air — kg water vapor per kg dry air.

**Step 4 — Solve for the required dry-air mass flow rate $A$.**
$$A = \frac{F_s (X_1 - X_2)}{Y_2 - Y_1}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the volumetric air flow rate** (needed for fan/blower sizing):
$$\dot{V}_{\text{air}} = \frac{A}{\rho_{\text{air}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is dry solid conserved?** Because drying is a *physical* separation — no water is created from dry matter (unlike combustion). The dry solid is the "inert carrier" that simply gets lighter as water leaves.

## 2.3 Energy Balance — Step-by-Step Logic

For a steady-state, adiabatic (well-insulated) dryer, the **First Law of Thermodynamics** says:

$$Q + H_{\text{feed}} + H_{\text{air,in}} = H_{\text{product}} + H_{\text{air,out}} + Q_L$$

where each enthalpy $H$ is referenced to a common datum (commonly 0 °C for water and dry air).

### 2.3.1 Enthalpy of the air stream per kg of dry air

The **psychrometric enthalpy** of moist air per kg dry air is:
$$H_a = c_{p,\text{air}} (T - T_0) + Y \left[ \lambda_0 + c_{p,\text{vap}} (T - T_0) \right]$$

In KTU exams, the simpler form is often acceptable:
$$H_a \approx 1.005\, T + Y (2501 + 1.88\, T) \quad \text{[kJ/kg dry air, } T \text{ in °C]}$$

### 2.3.2 Enthalpy of the wet solid

Per kg of dry solid, treating the food as a mix of dry solid ($c_{ps}$) and water ($c_{p,w}$):
$$H_s = (c_{ps} + X \, c_{p,w}) (T - T_0)$$

For a typical food, $c_{ps} \approx 1.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$; for water inside the food, use $c_{p,w} = 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.

### 2.3.3 Final energy-balance equation (per kg dry solid)

$$Q = A (H_{a2} - H_{a1}) + P_s (c_{ps} + X_2 c_{p,w})(T_{p2} - T_{p1}) + F_s (c_{ps} + X_1 c_{p,w})(T_{p1} - T_{\text{ref}}) - F_s (c_{ps} + X_1 c_{p,w})(T_{p1} - T_{\text{ref}})$$

The last two feed terms usually cancel (feed enters at $T_{p1}$, product leaves at $T_{p2}$). The simplified working form is:

$$Q = A (H_{a2} - H_{a1}) + P_s \left[ (c_{ps} + X_2 c_{p,w})(T_{p2} - T_{\text{ref}}) - (c_{ps} + X_1 c_{p,w})(T_{p1} - T_{\text{ref}}) \right] + Q_L$$

If we take the reference at the inlet conditions so that feed enthalpy = 0, and neglect $Q_L$ for an idealized adiabatic dryer, then:

$$\boxed{Q = A (H_{a2} - H_{a1}) = W \, \lambda_{\text{eff}}}$$

where $\lambda_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective latent heat at the exhaust-air wet-bulb temperature.

## 2.4 Thermal Efficiency of a Dryer

A frequently-asked KTU question: **what fraction of supplied heat is actually used for evaporation?**

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\text{Heat used for evaporation}}{\text{Total heat supplied}} = \frac{W \, \lambda_{\text{eff}}}{Q} \times 100\%$$

For non-adiabatic operation, $Q$ must include wall losses. A well-designed spray dryer achieves 30–60 % thermal efficiency; a steam-heated tray dryer typically 20–40 %.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X = w/(1-w)$ | Wet → dry basis conversion | Always at the start of a problem |
| 2 | $w = X/(1+X)$ | Dry → wet basis conversion | Asked directly in Part A |
| 3 | $F_s = F(1-w_1) = P(1-w_2)$ | Dry-solid inventory | Find mass of dry solid |
| 4 | $W = F_s(X_1 - X_2)$ | Water evaporated (kg/s) | Central mass-balance result |
| 5 | $A = W/(Y_2 - Y_1)$ | Dry air required (kg/s) | Fan/blower sizing |
| 6 | $H_a = 1.005\,T + Y(2501 + 1.88\,T)$ | Air enthalpy (kJ/kg dry air) | Energy balance |
| 7 | $Q = A(H_{a2} - H_{a1})$ | Heat duty (kW or kJ/s) | Heater/fuel sizing |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{th}} = W\lambda/Q$ | Thermal efficiency | Performance evaluation |
| 9 | $N_A = k_Y(Y_s - Y)$ | Mass-transfer rate (kg/m²·s) | Tray/belt dryer area |
| 10 | $Q = UA \Delta T_{\text{lm}}$ | Heat transfer (conduction) | Indirect-contact dryers |
| 11 | $\lambda_T = 2501 - 2.381\,T$ | Latent heat vs. T (°C) | Temperature-corrected $\lambda$ |
| 12 | $t_F = (m_s / A_c) \int_{X_2}^{X_1} dX / N$ | Falling-rate drying time | Drum/spray holding time |
| 13 | $P_s = H_s (Y_2 - Y_1) / (X_1 - X_2)$ | Cross-relation (eliminate $W$) | Algebra shortcut |
| 14 | $\omega = 0.622\,p_v / (P - p_v)$ | Humidity ratio from partial pressure | Psychrometric chart use |
| 15 | $T_{\text{wb}}$ from psychrometric chart | Wet-bulb temperature | Determines $\lambda_{\text{eff}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick — Watch the pipe character in tables.** All absolute values and divisions in the cheat sheet use the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` form, never raw `\vert`, so that the markdown table structure is never broken.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Spray dryer design** (milk, coffee, detergent): mass balance sets the air-to-feed ratio; energy balance sets the gas-heater capacity and the feed-atomization energy.
- **Paddy parboiling & drying plants:** mass balance determines the throughput of the LSU (Liquid-State-of-the-art) LSU dryer, energy balance sets biomass-furnace size.
- **Lyophilizer (freeze-dryer) energy audit:** the same framework, with $\lambda_{\text{sublimation}} \approx 2838 \text{ kJ/kg}$ replacing $\lambda_{\text{vap}}$.
- **Spice and herb drying (fluidized bed):** mass balance gives the bed residence time, energy balance gives the steam-coil area $UA$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion Between Wet and Dry Basis

Starting from the definition of moisture content on wet basis:
$$w = \frac{m_w}{m_w + m_s}$$

We want to express $X = m_w/m_s$ in terms of $w$. Divide numerator and denominator of the right-hand side by $m_s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
w &= \frac{m_w/m_s}{(m_w/m_s) + 1} = \frac{X}{X + 1}
\end{aligned}$$

Inverting this relation:
$$X = \frac{w}{1 - w} \quad \blacksquare$$

This relation appears in **almost every** KTU numerical on drying — make it second nature.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass of Water Evaporated

**Given:** Wet feed enters at rate $F$ kg/h with wet-basis moisture $w_1$. Dried product leaves at $P$ kg/h with moisture $w_2$. Both streams contain the same dry solid.

**Step 1 — Compute the dry solid in the feed.**
$$F_s = F(1 - w_1) \quad \text{(kg dry solid/h)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dry solid in the product.**
$$P_s = P(1 - w_2) \quad \text{(kg dry solid/h)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply conservation of dry solid** (steady state, no losses):
$$F_s = P_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad F(1 - w_1) = P(1 - w_2)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the water present initially in the feed.**
$$m_{w,1} = F w_1$$

**Step 5 — Compute the water present finally in the product.**
$$m_{w,2} = P w_2$$

**Step 6 — Subtract to obtain the water evaporated.**
$$\begin{aligned}
W &= m_{w,1} - m_{w,2} \\
  &= F w_1 - P w_2
\end{aligned}$$

Using $P = F(1-w_1)/(1-w_2)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
W &= F w_1 - \frac{F(1-w_1)}{(1-w_2)} w_2 \\
  &= F \left[ w_1 - \frac{w_2(1-w_1)}{1-w_2} \right] \\
  &= F \left[ \frac{w_1(1-w_2) - w_2(1-w_1)}{1-w_2} \right] \\
  &= F \left[ \frac{w_1 - w_1 w_2 - w_2 + w_1 w_2}{1-w_2} \right] \\
  &= F \cdot \frac{w_1 - w_2}{1 - w_2} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Express in dry-basis form.** Since $F_s = F(1-w_1)$:
$$W = F_s \left(\frac{w_1 - w_2}{(1-w_1)(1-w_2)}\right)$$

Now substitute $X_1 = w_1/(1-w_1)$ and $X_2 = w_2/(1-w_2)$:
$$\begin{aligned}
W &= F_s (X_1 - X_2) \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}$$

This compact form is what KTU expects in the final answer.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Air Requirement from Humidity Balance

The dry air $A$ (kg dry air/h) is the carrier of moisture out of the dryer. Let $Y_1$ and $Y_2$ be the absolute humidity (kg water/kg dry air) of the air entering and leaving the dryer.

**Moisture carried in by air:** $A Y_1$
**Moisture carried out by air:** $A Y_2$

**Moisture balance on the air side:**
$$A Y_2 - A Y_1 = W \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{A = \frac{W}{Y_2 - Y_1}}$$

This is the most fundamental result for sizing fans and ducts.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Balance (Adiabatic Dryer)

Let us take the **feed inlet temperature as the reference** ($T_0 = T_{p1}$). The air enters at $T_{a1}$ (after the heater, i.e., the *hot* air) and leaves at $T_{a2}$ (the exhaust).

**Enthalpy of inlet air (per kg dry air):**
$$H_{a1} = 1.005\,(T_{a1} - T_{p1}) + Y_1 [2501 + 1.88\,(T_{a1} - T_{p1})]$$

**Enthalpy of exhaust air (per kg dry air):**
$$H_{a2} = 1.005\,(T_{a2} - T_{p1}) + Y_2 [2501 + 1.88\,(T_{a2} - T_{p1})]$$

**Enthalpy of wet feed (per kg dry solid):**
$$H_{s1} = (c_{ps} + X_1 c_{p,w})(T_{p1} - T_{p1}) = 0$$

**Enthalpy of product (per kg dry solid):**
$$H_{s2} = (c_{ps} + X_2 c_{p,w})(T_{p2} - T_{p1})$$

**First Law for the dryer control volume (per unit time):**
$$Q_{\text{in}} + A H_{a1} + F_s H_{s1} = A H_{a2} + P_s H_{s2}$$

Since $H_{s1} = 0$ and $F_s = P_s$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{in}} &= A (H_{a2} - H_{a1}) + F_s (c_{ps} + X_2 c_{p,w})(T_{p2} - T_{p1})
\end{aligned}$$

The first term $A(H_{a2}-H_{a1})$ is the **sensible heat picked up by the moist air** (which includes the latent heat of the evaporated water). The second term is the **sensible heat gained by the dry solid + residual moisture**. If the product leaves at the inlet temperature ($T_{p2} = T_{p1}$, e.g., a continuously-stirred dryer at wet-bulb), the second term vanishes and:

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{in}} = A (H_{a2} - H_{a1})}$$

This is the textbook **adiabatic drying line** on the psychrometric chart — the air state moves along a constant-enthalpy line from the heater outlet to the exhaust, picking up moisture as it cools.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical (KTU 14-Mark Style)

### Problem Statement
A continuous belt dryer is used to dry **500 kg/h** of wet food from an initial moisture content of **80 % (wet basis)** to a final moisture of **10 % (wet basis)**. The feed enters at **25 °C** and leaves at **35 °C**. The drying air enters the heater at **25 °C, 60 % RH** and leaves the heater at **85 °C**. It exhausts from the dryer at **40 °C, 80 % RH**. The specific heat of the dry food solid is $c_{ps} = 1.5 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$. Compute:
(a) Mass of water evaporated and mass of dried product.
(b) Humidity ratios of inlet and exhaust air.
(c) Dry-air flow rate required.
(d) Total heat to be supplied by the heater (neglecting wall losses).
(e) Thermal efficiency of the dryer.

### Part (a) — Mass of Water Evaporated and Dried Product

**Step 1.** Convert to dry basis:
$$X_1 = \frac{0.80}{1 - 0.80} = 4.0 \quad \text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$
$$X_2 = \frac{0.10}{1 - 0.10} = 0.1111 \quad \text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2.** Mass of dry solid:
$$F_s = 500 (1 - 0.80) = 100 \text{ kg dry solid/h}$$

**Step 3.** Water evaporated:
$$W = F_s (X_1 - X_2) = 100 (4.0 - 0.1111) = 388.89 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4.** Mass of dried product:
$$P = F_s (1 + X_2) = 100 (1 + 0.1111) = 111.11 \text{ kg/h}$$

> Cross-check: $500 - 388.89 = 111.11$ kg ✓

### Part (b) — Humidity Ratios

We need saturation pressures at the relevant temperatures. Use steam-table values (KTU allows approximate values from a psychrometric chart).

At **25 °C**: $p_{s,25} = 3.169 \text{ kPa}$, at 60 % RH: $p_v = 0.60 \times 3.169 = 1.901 \text{ kPa}$
$$Y_1 = 0.622 \times \frac{p_v}{P - p_v} = 0.622 \times \frac{1.901}{101.325 - 1.901} = 0.0119 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$

At **40 °C**: $p_{s,40} = 7.381 \text{ kPa}$, at 80 % RH: $p_v = 0.80 \times 7.381 = 5.905 \text{ kPa}$
$$Y_2 = 0.622 \times \frac{5.905}{101.325 - 5.905} = 0.0385 \text{ kg/kg dry air}$$

### Part (c) — Dry-Air Flow Rate

$$A = \frac{W}{Y_2 - Y_1} = \frac{388.89}{0.0385 - 0.0119} = \frac{388.89}{0.0266} = 14\,620 \text{ kg dry air/h}$$

Converting to volumetric flow (assume hot air at 85 °C, ~1 atm):
$$\rho_{\text{air}} \approx \frac{P}{R T} = \frac{101.325}{0.287 \times 358.15} = 0.987 \text{ kg/m}^3$$
$$\dot{V} = \frac{14\,620}{0.987} = 14\,814 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \approx 4.12 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

### Part (d) — Heat Duty

**Enthalpy of air entering heater** (state 1: 25 °C, $Y_1$):
$$H_1 = 1.005 \times 25 + 0.0119 (2501 + 1.88 \times 25) = 25.125 + 0.0119 \times 2548 = 25.125 + 30.32 = 55.45 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

**Enthalpy of air leaving heater** (state 2: 85 °C, same $Y_1$ since heating is sensible):
$$H_2 = 1.005 \times 85 + 0.0119 (2501 + 1.88 \times 85) = 85.43 + 0.0119 \times 2660.8 = 85.43 + 31.66 = 117.09 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

**Enthalpy of air leaving dryer** (state 3: 40 °C, $Y_2$):
$$H_3 = 1.005 \times 40 + 0.0385 (2501 + 1.88 \times 40) = 40.20 + 0.0385 \times 2576.2 = 40.20 + 99.18 = 139.38 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}$$

**Heat supplied by the heater** (per unit time):
$$Q_{\text{heater}} = A (H_2 - H_1) = 14\,620 (117.09 - 55.45) = 14\,620 \times 61.64 = 901\,177 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 250.3 \text{ kW}$$

**Heat picked up by the air inside the dryer** (no heater inside):
$$Q_{\text{dryer,air}} = A (H_3 - H_2) = 14\,620 (139.38 - 117.09) = 14\,620 \times 22.29 = 325\,880 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 90.5 \text{ kW}$$

**Total heat to be supplied to the system** (heater + air-side evaporation energy is supplied by the heater):
$$Q_{\text{total}} = Q_{\text{heater}} = 901\,177 \text{ kJ/h} = 250.3 \text{ kW}$$

### Part (e) — Thermal Efficiency

Effective latent heat at the wet-bulb of exhaust air (≈ 35 °C for state 3):
$$\lambda_{35} = 2501 - 2.381 \times 35 = 2501 - 83.34 = 2417.66 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Heat actually used for evaporation:
$$Q_{\text{evap}} = W \cdot \lambda_{35} = 388.89 \times 2417.66 = 940\,200 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{940\,200}{901\,177} \times 100\% \approx 104\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The > 100 % result means the *air* itself supplied additional energy to the dryer as it cooled from the heater outlet (85 °C) to the exhaust (40 °C). Thermal efficiency for a continuous dryer is sometimes defined against the **total heat picked up by the air** ($A(H_3-H_2)$), giving $\eta_{\text{th}} = 325\,880 / 901\,177 = 36.2\%$, which is the realistic value.

### Final Tabulated Answers

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Dry solid flow | $F_s$ | **100 kg/h** |
| Water evaporated | $W$ | **388.89 kg/h** |
| Dried product | $P$ | **111.11 kg/h** |
| Inlet humidity ratio | $Y_1$ | **0.0119 kg/kg** |
| Exhaust humidity ratio | $Y_2$ | **0.0385 kg/kg** |
| Dry-air flow | $A$ | **14 620 kg/h** |
| Volumetric air flow | $\dot V$ | **~4.12 m³/s** |
| Heater duty | $Q_{\text{heater}}$ | **~250 kW** |
| Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{\text{th}}$ | **~36 %** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Convective Dryer

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **mass and energy flow** between the principal sub-units of a generic convective dryer (belt, tray, rotary, or fluidized). It is the type of figure KTU expects in design questions, drawn schematically rather than as a literal photograph.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wet Feed F, w1, Tp1] --> M1[Mixing/Conveying Unit]
    M1 --> D[Drying Chamber]
    HTR[Heater Q_in] -- Hot Air T_a1 --> D
    D --> P[Dried Product P, w2, Tp2]
    D -- Moist Exhaust T_a2, Y2 --> EXH[Exhaust Blower + Stack]
    EXH -. Part of Hot Air Recycled .-> HTR
    ENV[Surroundings] -. Heat Loss Q_L .-> D
    C1[Mass Balance: Fs = Ps] -.-> D
    C2[Moisture Balance: W = A Y2 - A Y1] -.-> D
    C3[Energy Balance: Q + FeedH = ProdH + AirH_out + QL] -.-> D
    C4[Psychrometric Line: T drops as Y rises] -.-> D
    D --> CT[Controller: T, RH, X sensors]
    CT -- Feedback --> HTR
    CT -- Feedback --> M1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Mass and Energy Flow Sankey-Like View

This second diagram emphasizes *what is conserved* in the dryer (dry solid and dry air) and *what is transferred* (water from solid to air, heat from heater to air and to product).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INLET["INLET STREAMS"]
        F1[Feed: Fs + Fs X1 kg/h, Enthalpy Hs1]
        A1[Fresh Air: A + A Y1 kg/h, Enthalpy Ha1]
        Q1[Heater: Q kJ/h]
    end
    subgraph DRYER["DRYER CONTROL VOLUME"]
        CV[Steady-State Mass + Energy Balance]
    end
    subgraph OUTLET["OUTLET STREAMS"]
        F2[Product: Fs + Fs X2 kg/h, Enthalpy Hs2]
        A2[Exhaust Air: A + A Y2 kg/h, Enthalpy Ha2]
        QL[Wall Loss: QL kJ/h]
    end
    F1 --> CV
    A1 --> CV
    Q1 --> CV
    CV --> F2
    CV --> A2
    CV --> QL
    CV -. Moisture Transfer: W = Fs X1 - Fs X2 .-> A2
    CV -. Enthalpy Transfer: Q + Ha1 + Hs1 - Ha2 - Hs2 - QL = 0 .-> F2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Decision Tree for Selecting a Dryer Type

The choice of dryer is governed by the **physical form of the feed** and the **drying characteristics**. This decision tree is the standard KTU 7-mark answer for "Suggest a suitable dryer for X".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{Feed physical form?}
    Q -->|Liquid / slurry| R1[Liquid feed]
    Q -->|Paste| R2[Thick paste]
    Q -->|Free-flowing granules| R3[Granular solid]
    Q -->|Sheet / film| R4[Sheet]
    R1 --> S1[Spray Dryer]
    R1 --> S2[Drum Dryer]
    R2 --> S2
    R2 --> S3[Flash / Pneumatic Dryer]
    R3 --> S4[Fluidized-Bed Dryer]
    R3 --> S5[Rotary Dryer]
    R3 --> S6[Spouted-Bed Dryer]
    R4 --> S7[Belt / Tunnel Dryer]
    R4 --> S8[Tray Dryer]
    S1 --> N1[Atomizer + hot air co-current]
    S2 --> N2[Heated drum, thin film]
    S3 --> N3[High-velocity hot air, short residence]
    S4 --> N4[Air-fluidized bed, good mixing]
    S5 --> N5[Rotating shell, cascading]
    S6 --> N6[Spout of high-velocity air]
    S7 --> N7[Perforated belt, multi-zone]
    S8 --> N8[Static trays, batch or tunnel]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the diagrams in an exam** — The arrows in the first two diagrams represent *flow direction* of mass and energy. The dashed arrows represent **balance equations** governing the dryer. The third diagram is a *classification* aid.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** Define the terms **(i)** moisture content on dry basis, **(ii)** equilibrium moisture content, and **(iii)** free moisture. How are (i) and (ii) related to the concept of bound moisture?

**Model Answer:**
(i) **Moisture content on dry basis (X)** is the mass of water per unit mass of bone-dry solid, i.e. $X = m_w / m_s$. It is preferred in dryer design because the denominator (dry solid mass) does not change during drying.
(ii) **Equilibrium moisture content (EMC, $X^*$)** is the moisture content of the material when it is in thermodynamic sorption equilibrium with the surrounding air at given T and RH. Beyond $X^*$, the material cannot be dried by that air.
(iii) **Free moisture** = $X - X^*$, the portion that *can* be removed by the given drying conditions.

**Bound moisture** is the portion of moisture that is held so strongly (by sorption in capillaries, cell walls, or as chemically bound water) that it cannot be removed at the given air condition. Numerically, *bound moisture = total moisture − free moisture at the given $T$ and RH*. Bound moisture cannot exceed the EMC at 100 % RH.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the **psychrometric enthalpy** of moist air? Write the equation used in dryer energy balance and state the reference state assumed.

**Model Answer:**
**Psychrometric enthalpy** is the total enthalpy of a moist-air mixture expressed *per unit mass of dry air*. It includes the sensible heat of the dry air and the water vapor, plus the latent heat of vaporization of the vapor. The working form is:

$$H_a = 1.005\,T + Y\,(2501 + 1.88\,T) \quad \text{[kJ per kg dry air]}$$

where $T$ is in °C and $Y$ is the absolute humidity (kg water / kg dry air). The **reference state** is liquid water at 0 °C (so that the latent-heat term 2501 kJ/kg appears explicitly). In KTU problems, it is common to take the feed-inlet temperature as reference to simplify the solid-side enthalpy to zero.

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Convention:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer *either* Question A *or* Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]** A continuous-tray dryer handles **800 kg/h** of diced fruit containing **75 % moisture (wet basis)** down to **12 % moisture (wet basis)**. The fruit enters at **28 °C** and leaves at **45 °C**. Air at **20 °C, 50 % RH** is heated to **90 °C** in the heater and leaves the dryer at **38 °C, 70 % RH**. The specific heat of the dry fruit solid is **2.0 kJ/(kg·K)**. Compute:

**(a)** Mass of dry solid, mass of water evaporated, mass of dried product, and dry-air flow rate. (7 marks)
**(b)** Heat to be supplied by the heater, the heat actually used for evaporation, and the thermal efficiency of the dryer. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Convert to dry basis.**
$$X_1 = \frac{0.75}{1-0.75} = 3.000 \quad ; \quad X_2 = \frac{0.12}{1-0.12} = 0.1364 \quad \text{kg water/kg dry solid}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of dry solid.**
$$F_s = 800(1 - 0.75) = 200 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of water evaporated.**
$$W = F_s(X_1 - X_2) = 200(3.000 - 0.1364) = 572.73 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Mass of dried product.**
$$P = F_s(1 + X_2) = 200(1.1364) = 227.27 \text{ kg/h}$$

Cross-check: $800 - 572.73 = 227.27$ ✓

**Step 5 — Humidity ratios (using approximate saturation pressures).**

At 20 °C: $p_{s,20} = 2.339$ kPa; $p_v = 0.50 \times 2.339 = 1.170$ kPa
$$Y_1 = 0.622 \times \frac{1.170}{101.325 - 1.170} = 0.00726 \text{ kg/kg}$$

At 38 °C: $p_{s,38} = 6.629$ kPa; $p_v = 0.70 \times 6.629 = 4.640$ kPa
$$Y_2 = 0.622 \times \frac{4.640}{101.325 - 4.640} = 0.02981 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Step 6 — Dry-air flow rate.**
$$A = \frac{W}{Y_2 - Y_1} = \frac{572.73}{0.02981 - 0.00726} = \frac{572.73}{0.02255} = 25\,399 \text{ kg dry air/h}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 7 — Enthalpies of the three air states.**

State 1 (inlet to heater, 20 °C, $Y_1$):
$$H_1 = 1.005 \times 20 + 0.00726 (2501 + 1.88 \times 20) = 20.10 + 0.00726 \times 2538.6 = 20.10 + 18.43 = 38.53 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

State 2 (inlet to dryer, 90 °C, $Y_1$):
$$H_2 = 1.005 \times 90 + 0.00726 (2501 + 1.88 \times 90) = 90.45 + 0.00726 \times 2670.2 = 90.45 + 19.38 = 109.83 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

State 3 (exhaust, 38 °C, $Y_2$):
$$H_3 = 1.005 \times 38 + 0.02981 (2501 + 1.88 \times 38) = 38.19 + 0.02981 \times 2572.4 = 38.19 + 76.68 = 114.87 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 8 — Heater duty.**
$$Q_{\text{heater}} = A(H_2 - H_1) = 25\,399 (109.83 - 38.53) = 25\,399 \times 71.30 = 1\,810\,949 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 503 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 9 — Heat used for evaporation** (effective latent heat at ~33 °C wet-bulb of exhaust):
$$\lambda_{33} = 2501 - 2.381 \times 33 = 2422.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$Q_{\text{evap}} = W \cdot \lambda_{33} = 572.73 \times 2422.4 = 1\,387\,264 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 10 — Thermal efficiency** (against heat picked up by the air inside the dryer, $A(H_3-H_2)$):
$$A(H_3 - H_2) = 25\,399(114.87 - 109.83) = 25\,399 \times 5.04 = 128\,011 \text{ kJ/h}$$

This is small because the air has nearly reached equilibrium with the wet bulb. The thermodynamic thermal efficiency based on adiabatic assumption is:
$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{Q_{\text{evap}}}{Q_{\text{heater}}} \times 100 = \frac{1\,387\,264}{1\,810\,949} \times 100 = 76.6\%$$

> Note: The "missing" energy is the sensible heat required to raise the product from 28 °C to 45 °C — a perfectly acceptable use of supplied heat.

**Product sensible heat:**
$$Q_{\text{prod}} = 200 \left[ (2.0 + 0.1364 \times 4.186)(45 - 28) \right] = 200(2.571)(17) = 8741 \text{ kJ/h}$$

This is small relative to $Q_{\text{heater}}$ and accounts for the difference.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Explain with neat sketches:
**(a)** The general layout of a **spray dryer** and the route taken by air and feed through it. Identify the main design sub-systems. (7 marks)
**(b)** Derive the mass-balance equation for a continuous adiabatic dryer in terms of dry-solid flow and absolute humidity. Apply it to a worked example where **1000 kg/h** of milk (initial moisture 87 % w.b., final 4 % w.b.) is spray-dried, with air entering at $Y_1 = 0.010$ kg/kg and leaving at $Y_2 = 0.045$ kg/kg. Compute the air requirement and the steam (at 3 bar, latent heat 2133 kJ/kg) needed in the air heater, assuming the air enters the heater at 25 °C and leaves at 180 °C. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A spray dryer consists of:
1. **Feed tank & atomizer** (centrifugal disc, pressure nozzle, or two-fluid nozzle) — produces 10–500 µm droplets.
2. **Drying chamber** — vertical cylindrical-conical vessel where droplets meet hot air.
3. **Air heater** — indirect steam/gas/fired heater raising air to 150–220 °C.
4. **Air disperser** at the top (co-current) or bottom (counter-current) — distributes air uniformly.
5. **Product collection** — cyclone separator and/or bag filter, plus rotary airlock.
6. **Exhaust fan & stack** — maintains slight negative pressure.

**Air and feed route (co-current):** Feed → atomizer → top of chamber → falls by gravity → meets hot air entering through the top air ring → water evaporates from droplets → dry powder falls to the bottom cone → cyclone separates fines from exhaust → clean exhaust leaves via fan.

The three concurrent flow streams (feed droplets falling, air rising briefly, then both moving down) make the spray dryer essentially an **adiabatic co-current** system in which the air rapidly cools from ~180 °C to ~80–100 °C at the outlet.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation (re-stated from §3.3):**
$$F_s = P_s, \quad F_s X_1 + A Y_1 = F_s X_2 + A Y_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \frac{F_s (X_1 - X_2)}{Y_2 - Y_1}$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

**Step 1 — Convert to dry basis.**
$$X_1 = \frac{0.87}{0.13} = 6.692 \quad ; \quad X_2 = \frac{0.04}{0.96} = 0.0417$$

**Step 2 — Dry solid flow.**
$$F_s = 1000(1 - 0.87) = 130 \text{ kg dry solid/h}$$

**Step 3 — Water evaporated.**
$$W = 130 (6.692 - 0.0417) = 130 \times 6.650 = 864.5 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Air requirement.**
$$A = \frac{864.5}{0.045 - 0.010} = \frac{864.5}{0.035} = 24\,700 \text{ kg dry air/h}$$

**Step 5 — Heater duty.** Air enthalpy at 25 °C:
$$H_1 = 1.005(25) + 0.010(2501 + 1.88 \times 25) = 25.13 + 25.48 = 50.61 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Air enthalpy at 180 °C (same $Y_1$ since heater is sensible):
$$H_2 = 1.005(180) + 0.010(2501 + 1.88 \times 180) = 180.90 + 28.39 = 209.29 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$Q = 24\,700(209.29 - 50.61) = 24\,700 \times 158.68 = 3\,919\,396 \text{ kJ/h} \approx 1089 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Steam requirement** (latent heat 2133 kJ/kg):
$$m_{\text{steam}} = \frac{3\,919\,396}{2133} = 1838 \text{ kg/h} \approx 30.6 \text{ kg/min}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert wet basis to dry basis** before applying $W = F_s(X_1 - X_2)$. This is a *one-line* error that costs 7 marks.
> 2. **Mixing $Y$ (absolute humidity) with RH (relative humidity)**. RH is a fraction; $Y$ has units of kg/kg. Many students write $Y_1 = 0.60$ instead of $Y_1 \approx 0.012$.
> 3. **Using $T$ in Kelvin inside the psychrometric-enthalpy equation** (which assumes °C). Subtract 273 before using $H_a = 1.005 T + Y(2501 + 1.88 T)$.
> 4. **Not accounting for the latent-heat temperature correction** ($\lambda_T = 2501 - 2.381\,T$). Using 2501 universally is acceptable only for quick estimates; for KTU 14-mark questions, the corrected $\lambda_T$ is expected at the exhaust wet-bulb.
> 5. **Reporting thermal efficiency > 100 %** and not understanding the role of the *air-side* energy transfer inside the dryer. State your reference (heater duty vs. total air enthalpy change) explicitly.
> 6. **Omitting the dry-solid conservation** statement $F_s = P_s$ in the answer narrative. Examiners reward 1 mark for the explicit sentence.
> 7. **Using the wrong sign** in $A(H_2 - H_1)$. If the second term is negative, the heater is delivering *less* energy than the air gained inside the dryer — physically impossible unless there is an external heat source (e.g., an in-dryer steam coil), which is rarely the case.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definitions that earn 1-mark each:** dry basis $X$, wet basis $w$, EMC $X^*$, free moisture, bound moisture, absolute humidity $Y$, wet-bulb temperature, psychrometric enthalpy.
- **Two must-know conversions:** $X = w/(1-w)$ and $w = X/(1+X)$.
- **Three conservation laws for a steady-state dryer:**
  1. Dry solid: $F(1-w_1) = P(1-w_2)$.
  2. Moisture: $F w_1 + A Y_1 = P w_2 + A Y_2$.
  3. Energy: $Q + H_{\text{feed}} + H_{\text{air,in}} = H_{\text{product}} + H_{\text{air,out}} + Q_L$.
- **Worked flow of a KTU numerical:**
  1. Convert $w$ to $X$.
  2. Compute $F_s = F(1-w_1)$.
  3. Compute $W = F_s(X_1-X_2)$.
  4. Compute $P = F_s(1+X_2)$.
  5. From given RH and $T$, read $Y_1$ and $Y_2$ from a psychrometric chart or compute via $Y = 0.622\,p_v/(P-p_v)$.
  6. Compute $A = W/(Y_2 - Y_1)$.
  7. Compute $H_1, H_2, H_3$ using $H_a = 1.005 T + Y(2501 + 1.88 T)$.
  8. Compute $Q_{\text{heater}} = A(H_2 - H_1)$.
  9. Compute $\eta_{\text{th}} = W \lambda_{\text{eff}} / Q_{\text{heater}}$.
- **Latent-heat correction:** $\lambda_T = 2501 - 2.381\,T$ (with $T$ in °C). Use at the wet-bulb of the exhaust.
- **Thermal efficiency for an adiabatic co-current dryer** is typically **30–70 %** depending on whether sensible heating of product and wall losses are charged to "useful" heat.
- **Energy to evaporate 1 kg of water at 80–100 °C** ≈ **2300 kJ** (use this for quick estimates).
- **Energy to heat 1 kg of air by 1 °C** ≈ **1.005 kJ** (use this for quick estimates).
- **Volume of air to evaporate 1 kg of water** at typical conditions (ΔY ≈ 0.025 kg/kg) ≈ **35 m³** of dry air at STP — a useful sanity check.
- **Common dryer types and their governing equation:**
  - Tray/belt → $t = (m_s/A_c) \int dX / N$
  - Rotary → $\theta = L/(v \cdot f(\text{holdup}))$
  - Spray → atomization + co-current air; sizing from $Q$ and $A$.
  - Fluidized → $u_{\text{mf}}$ and bed height; $Q$ from $UA\Delta T$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychrometrics - Drying Time Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Psychrometrics - Drying Time Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Psychrometrics — Formal KTU Definition

**Psychrometrics** is the branch of engineering thermodynamics that deals with the determination of the physical and thermodynamic properties of **gas-vapour mixtures**, with particular emphasis on the **dry air – water vapour system** because atmospheric air almost universally serves as the drying medium in food process engineering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, PCFTT601 / Module 2):**
> Psychrometric properties and the psychrometric chart form the *foundation* for the design of tray dryers, fluidized bed dryers, spray dryers, belt dryers, and batch dryers — because every one of these equipment types uses *heated, humid air* to remove moisture from food.

The governing parameters of a moist-air system are:
- **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT, T)** — the true thermodynamic temperature of the air-water vapour mixture measured by an ordinary thermometer. **Units: °C or K.**
- **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT, T_w)** — the adiabatic saturation temperature approached by a liquid surface when the surrounding unsaturated air flows past it. **Units: °C or K.**
- **Absolute / Specific Humidity (Y or ω)** — the mass of water vapour carried per unit mass of **bone-dry air**. **Units: kg water / kg dry air.**
- **Relative Humidity (RH, φ)** — the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapour in the mixture to its saturation pressure at the same DBT. **Dimensionless (%).**
- **Dew Point Temperature (DPT, T_dew)** — the temperature at which the water vapour in the air becomes saturated (100% RH) when cooled at constant pressure.
- **Enthalpy (H)** — total heat content per kg of dry air (sensible + latent). **Units: kJ / kg dry air.**
- **Specific Volume (v)** — volume occupied by 1 kg of dry air together with its associated vapour. **Units: m³ / kg dry air.**
- **Humid Heat (c_s)** — heat capacity of moist air per kg of dry air. **Units: kJ / (kg dry air · K).**

---

## 1.2 The Analogy — "How Hot Does It Feel?"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you are standing on a beach. Two people report "different" hotness even though the actual air temperature is 35 °C. One feels sticky and suffocating (high relative humidity, no sweat evaporation), while the other feels bearable (low RH, sweat evaporates freely). Psychrometrics is the *science* of quantifying exactly that "feel" — by tracking not just temperature but also how much water the air is already carrying, how much more it *can* carry, and how much heat energy is locked inside as latent heat of condensation.

A **psychrometric chart** (Mollier chart or Carrier chart) is the *map* engineers use to read all these properties at once, just like you would read coordinates off a map.

## 1.3 Drying — The Twin Process

**Drying** is a simultaneous **heat- and mass-transfer operation** in which moisture is removed from a food material by thermal evaporation into an unsaturated gas stream (almost always air). Two sub-processes occur:
1. **Heat transfer** — from the heated air *into* the wet food (drives the evaporation).
2. **Mass transfer** — of water vapour *from* the food surface *into* the bulk air stream.

The **drying rate** is governed by which of these resistances dominates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Insight for KTU Examinations:**
> The drying process exhibits two distinct regimes — the **Constant Rate Period (CRP)** and the **Falling Rate Period (FRP)** — separated by the **Critical Moisture Content (X_c)**. Almost every numerical problem on this topic asks the student to compute the *total drying time* by integrating the rate over both periods.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Psychrometric Property Equations

The **Daltonian partial-pressure relation** for an ideal gas mixture gives the absolute humidity:

$$Y \;=\; 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_w}{P - P_w}$$

where **P** is the total pressure (usually **101.325 kPa**) and **P_w** is the partial pressure of water vapour.

The **Relative Humidity** is defined as the partial pressure ratio:

$$\phi \;=\; \frac{P_w}{P_{ws}(T)} \;=\; \frac{\text{actual vapour pressure}}{\text{saturation vapour pressure at DBT}}$$

> P_ws is read from steam tables for the given DBT.

The **Humid Heat (c_s)** is the heat required to raise the temperature of 1 kg of dry air **plus** its associated vapour by 1 °C:

$$c_s \;=\; c_{p,a} \;+\; Y \cdot c_{p,v} \;\;\approx\;\; 1.005 \;+\; 1.88\,Y \;\;\text{kJ/(kg dry air·K)}$$

The **Saturated Humid Volume** at any DBT (m³ / kg dry air):

$$v_H \;=\; \frac{R_a \, T}{P - P_{ws}} \;=\; 22.4 \cdot \frac{T}{273 \cdot (P - P_{ws})} \quad \text{(per kg dry air, with P in atm)}$$

The **Adiabatic Saturation (≈ Wet Bulb) Relation** — the *master equation* tying DBT, WBT and humidity:

$$Y_{ws} - Y \;=\; \frac{c_s}{\lambda_{w}} \cdot (T - T_w)$$

where **λ_w** is the latent heat of vaporization evaluated at the wet-bulb temperature (≈ 2400 – 2500 kJ/kg at 60 – 90 °C).

The **Lewis Number Relation** — links the convective heat- and mass-transfer coefficients:

$$\frac{h_L}{k_Y} \;=\; c_s \quad\Rightarrow\quad h_L \;=\; k_Y \, c_s$$

## 2.2 The Drying Curve — Stages of Moisture Removal

When a wet solid is exposed to a steady stream of hot, dry air, the moisture content **X(t) (kg water / kg dry solid)** and the drying rate **N = – dX/dt (kg water / m²·h)** evolve through well-defined stages:

| Stage | Mechanism | Rate behaviour |
|-------|-----------|----------------|
| **A → B (Initial transient)** | Surface warming up | Rises rapidly |
| **B → C (Constant Rate Period)** | Surface is saturated with free water; rate governed by external air-side resistance | **Constant** |
| **C → D (First Falling Rate Period)** | Wet surface starts to recede; partially dry patches appear | Linear decrease |
| **D → E (Second Falling Rate Period)** | Internal moisture movement (diffusion, capillarity) controls | Non-linear decay |
| **E → F (Equilibrium)** | Solid is in equilibrium with air (X = X*) | Rate → 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Critical Moisture Content (X_c)** marks the transition from CRP to FRP. It is *not* a material constant — it depends on the **drying rate itself, particle size, and geometry**. Thin materials have low X_c, thick slabs have high X_c.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Application |
|---|---------|---------------------------|
| 1 | $Y = 0.622\,\dfrac{P_w}{P-P_w}$ | Specific humidity from partial pressure |
| 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{P_w}{P_{ws}}$ | Relative humidity |
| 3 | $c_s = 1.005 + 1.88\,Y$ | Humid heat (kJ/kg d.a.·K) |
| 4 | $v_H = \dfrac{R_a\,T}{P-P_{ws}}$ | Humid specific volume |
| 5 | $Y_{ws}-Y = \dfrac{c_s}{\lambda_w}(T-T_w)$ | Wet-bulb / adiabatic saturation relation |
| 6 | $h_L = k_Y \, c_s$ | Lewis relation (heat ↔ mass transfer) |
| 7 | $N_c = \dfrac{h_L\,(T-T_w)}{\lambda_w} = k_Y\,(Y_{ws}-Y)$ | **Drying rate in CRP** |
| 8 | $N = -\dfrac{L_s}{A}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Mass balance rate equation |
| 9 | $t_c = \dfrac{L_s\,(X_1-X_c)}{A\,N_c}$ | **Time for CRP only** |
| 10 | $t_f = \dfrac{L_s}{N_c}\!\left[\ln\!\dfrac{X_c-X^*}{X_2-X^*}\right]$ | **Time for FRP (linear-fall assumption)** |
| 11 | $t_{\text{total}} = t_c + t_f$ | Total drying time |
| 12 | $\theta_a = \dfrac{T - T_w}{Y_{ws}-Y}$ | Adiabatic wet-bulb slope on chart |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation key used above:**
> $L_s$ = mass of bone-dry solid (kg); $A$ = exposed drying area (m²); $X_1$, $X_2$, $X_c$, $X^*$ = initial, final, critical and equilibrium moisture contents (kg water / kg dry solid); $N_c$ = constant drying rate (kg water / m²·h); $h_L$ = convective heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K); $k_Y$ = mass-transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s·ΔY); $\lambda_w$ = latent heat at WBT (kJ/kg).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why is this Critical?

In the design of industrial food dryers, the psychrometric state of the *inlet* and *outlet* air sets the *driving force* (T – T_w and Y_ws – Y) for moisture removal. The drying time calculation uses this driving force to size the dryer (area, residence time, fan power, fuel consumption). **Without a correct understanding of psychrometrics, no dryer design can be completed.**
