# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define "Cold Storage" and list the four temperature classifications with their typical storage temperatures.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A cold storage is a temperature- and humidity-controlled insulated building used to store perishable food products at low temperatures to extend their shelf-life by retarding microbial, enzymatic, and chemical spoilage.

The four temperature classifications are:

| Class | Temperature |
|---|---|
| Chilled storage | −1 to +8 °C |
| Frozen storage | −18 to −25 °C |
| Deep freeze | −25 to −40 °C |
| Cryogenic / sharp freezer | < −40 °C |

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between "Heat of Respiration" and "Latent Heat of Freezing" in cold-storage load calculation.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Heat of Respiration | Latent Heat of Freezing |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Metabolic heat released by living produce (fruits, vegetables) during storage | Heat released when water in food changes to ice |
| **Applicable to** | Living produce (apples, bananas, potatoes) | Any product stored below 0 °C |
| **Time profile** | Continuous, decreases as T decreases | One-time, during pull-down to sub-zero |
| **Magnitude** | 10 – 100 W/tonne | 268 – 335 kJ/kg (≈ 80 kcal/kg) |
| **Formula** | $Q_R = m \cdot q_{resp}$ | $Q_L = m \cdot L_f$ |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE pattern: each Part-B question carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students must answer **either OR** the alternative question.

---

### **QUESTION A — (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the various **factors to be considered while designing a cold storage plant** for a multi-commodity food processing industry. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cold storage holds **80 tonnes** of fresh oranges. The room is **12 m × 10 m × 6 m**. Outside design temperature = **+42 °C**, room temperature = **+4 °C**. Walls are 80 mm PUF ($U$ = **0.30 W/m²·K**), ceiling ($U$ = **0.30 W/m²·K**), floor ($U$ = **0.40 W/m²·K**). Compute the **transmission load** and **respiration load** (take $q_{resp}$ for orange = **15 W/tonne**). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to A(a) — Design Factors (7 marks)**

A complete answer must cover these 4 categories:

**1. Product Factors (3 marks)** – Type, quantity, specific heat, arrival temperature, storage temperature, respiration rate, packaging, daily turnover.

**2. Building & Insulation Factors (1.5 marks)** – Ambient design conditions, insulation k-value, thickness, vapour barrier, floor insulation (especially for sub-zero rooms to prevent frost heave), door type, air-curtain, building orientation.

**3. Refrigeration System Factors (1.5 marks)** – Compressor type (reciprocating/screw/scroll), refrigerant (NH₃, R-404A, CO₂), condenser cooling (air/water/evaporative), evaporator configuration, defrost method, capacity (with 10–20 % margin), control philosophy (thermostatic expansion valve / EEV), and pull-down time.

**4. Operational, Safety & Economic Factors (1 mark)** – FSSAI/ISHRAE compliance, NH₃ leak detection, fire suppression, ergonomics, CAPEX/OPEX, energy efficiency (EC fans, VFD), and sustainability.

---

#### **Solution to A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $m = 80{,}000$ kg, $T_{in}=4$°C, $T_{out}=42$°C, room = 12 × 10 × 6 m, $q_{resp} = 15$ W/tonne.

**Areas:**
- Walls: $2(12+10)(6) = 264\ m^2$
- Ceiling: $12 \times 10 = 120\ m^2$
- Floor: $12 \times 10 = 120\ m^2$

$\Delta T = 42 - 4 = 38$ K

**Transmission Load (Q₁):**

$$Q_1 = (U_w A_w + U_c A_c + U_f A_f) \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_1 = (0.30 \times 264 + 0.30 \times 120 + 0.40 \times 120) \times 38$$

$$Q_1 = (79.2 + 36 + 48) \times 38 = 163.2 \times 38 = 6201.6\ \text{W} \approx 6.20\ \text{kW}$$

**Respiration Load (Q_R):**

$$Q_R = 80 \text{ tonnes} \times 15\ \text{W/tonne} = 1200\ \text{W} = 1.20\ \text{kW}$$

**Total of (i) and (ii) = 6.20 + 1.20 = 7.40 kW**

> **Conclusion:** A 3 TR compressor would be sufficient for these two loads alone; remaining loads (lighting, personnel, etc.) would marginally push the requirement to 3–3.5 TR.

---

### **QUESTION B — (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **constructional features of a cold storage building**, highlighting insulation, vapour barrier, and floor details. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 100-tonne capacity cold storage for **frozen fish** (−20 °C) is to be designed. Fish arrives at **+5 °C**, is frozen to −20 °C in **18 hours**. Specific heat above freezing = **3.5 kJ/kg·K**, specific heat below freezing = **2.0 kJ/kg·K**, latent heat of freezing = **260 kJ/kg**. Room: 15 m × 12 m × 6 m, ambient = +40 °C, $U$ for wall/ceiling/floor = **0.25, 0.25, 0.30 W/m²·K** respectively. Compute **(i) product load (sensible + latent)** and **(ii) transmission load**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B(a) — Construction Features (7 marks)**

A complete answer should describe:

1. **Floor (1.5 marks):** Reinforced-concrete sub-base, damp-proof course (DPC), **100–150 mm XPS/PF insulation**, **0.2 mm PE vapour barrier**, finished concrete slab with non-slip epoxy. For sub-zero rooms, under-floor heating cables prevent frost heave.

2. **Walls (2 marks):** 80–100 mm **PUF sandwich panels** (GI sheet + PUF + GI sheet), or 230 mm brick wall + 100 mm PUF + 12 mm plaster. Vapour barrier on the **warm (outside) side** of insulation. Joints sealed with silicone to maintain air-tightness.

3. **Ceiling/Roof (1.5 marks):** 100 mm PUF panels suspended from roof truss. Roof above is GI sheet with slope for rainwater drainage.

4. **Doors (1 mark):** Insulated sliding or hinged doors, with **PVC strip curtains / air curtains** to reduce infiltration. Emergency exit doors as per IS standards.

5. **Vapour Barrier (1 mark):** Continuous **0.2 mm aluminium foil / polyethylene** on the warm side of all insulation; critical to prevent moisture ingress that would degrade insulation and cause ice formation in cavities.

---

#### **Solution to B(b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**Given:** $m = 100{,}000$ kg, $T_{arr}=+5$°C, $T_{store}=-20$°C, $t=18\ h=64{,}800$ s, $c_{p,above}=3.5$, $c_{p,below}=2.0$, $L_f=260$ kJ/kg, room = 15 × 12 × 6 m, ambient = +40 °C.

**Areas:**
- Walls: $2(15+12)(6) = 324\ m^2$
- Ceiling: $15 \times 12 = 180\ m^2$
- Floor: $15 \times 12 = 180\ m^2$

---

**(i) Product Load (Q₂):**

**Sensible above freezing (cooling from +5 °C to 0 °C):**
$$Q_{2s,above} = \frac{m \cdot c_{p,above} \cdot \Delta T_1}{t} = \frac{100{,}000 \times 3.5 \times 5}{64{,}800} = \frac{1{,}750{,}000}{64{,}800} = 27.0\ \text{W}$$

**Latent (freezing at 0 °C):**
$$Q_{2L} = \frac{m \cdot L_f}{t} = \frac{100{,}000 \times 260}{64{,}800} = \frac{26{,}000{,}000}{64{,}800} = 401.2\ \text{W}$$

**Sensible below freezing (cooling from 0 °C to −20 °C):**
$$Q_{2s,below} = \frac{m \cdot c_{p,below} \cdot \Delta T_2}{t} = \frac{100{,}000 \times 2.0 \times 20}{64{,}800} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{64{,}800} = 61.7\ \text{W}$$

**Total product load:**
$$\boxed{Q_2 = 27.0 + 401.2 + 61.7 = 489.9\ \text{W} \approx 0.49\ \text{kW}}$$

> **Observation:** The latent heat of freezing dominates the product load (~82 %) — this is characteristic of **freezer** design.

---

**(ii) Transmission Load (Q₁):**

$\Delta T = 40 - (-20) = 60$ K

$$Q_1 = (0.25 \times 324 + 0.25 \times 180 + 0.30 \times 180) \times 60$$

$$Q_1 = (81 + 45 + 54) \times 60 = 180 \times 60 = 10{,}800\ \text{W} = 10.80\ \text{kW}$$

> **Final Design Insight:** For a 100-T frozen-fish cold store, the **transmission load (10.8 kW) vastly exceeds the product load (0.49 kW)** — confirming that **insulation quality is the single most critical design parameter** for freezers.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks in this topic — and how to avoid it:**
>
> 1. **Missing units in final answer** (–1 to –2 marks). Always write **W or kW or TR** explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the 10 % safety margin** before sizing the compressor. Examiners expect this.
> 3. **Wrong value of $L_f$**: Use **335 kJ/kg** for pure water; **~260 kJ/kg** for typical fish; **268 kJ/kg** for general water-rich food. The "0.80 rule" is **not** applied universally — read the product data.
> 4. **Forgetting the respiration load for fruits/vegetables** — it is a **continuous** 24-hr load, not a one-time load like Q₂sensible.
> 5. **Mixing `ΔT` in °C and K**: They are numerically equal, so no conversion is needed, but state the unit.
> 6. **Including latent heat for chilled (0 °C) products** that do NOT cross the freezing point — this is a common error.
> 7. **Vapour-barrier location** — students often write "inside the cold room" which is WRONG. The vapour barrier must be on the **WARM (outside) side** of the insulation.
> 8. **Not adding fan-motor heat** in Q₃ — it is often the largest single sub-component of Q₃ in modern EC-fan evaporators.
> 9. **Converting $t$ to seconds** when mass is in kg and energy in kJ: $1\ \text{kJ} / 1\ \text{s} = 1\ \text{kW}$; $1\ \text{kJ} / 1\ \text{h} = 1/3600\ \text{kW} = 0.278\ \text{W}$.
> 10. **Assuming the same U-value for all surfaces** — walls, ceiling, and floor in real cold stores have different U-values because of different insulation thicknesses or exposure to sunlight/ground.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist and revise the night before the exam.**

### A. Definitions
- [ ] Cold storage = insulated, refrigerated warehouse for perishables.
- [ ] 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 50.4 kcal/min = 200 Btu/min.
- [ ] Pull-down time = time to cool product from arrival to storage temperature.

### B. Load Components
- [ ] **Q₁** = Transmission = $U A \Delta T$.
- [ ] **Q₂** = Product = Sensible + Latent (if <0 °C) + Respiration (if living).
- [ ] **Q₃** = Internal = Lights (×1.25) + Personnel (250/400 W) + Fork-lift (×0.20) + Fans.
- [ ] **Q₄** = Infiltration = 0.1–0.3 W/m³ of room volume.
- [ ] **Q₅** = Miscellaneous = 10 % of (Q₁+Q₂+Q₃+Q₄).
- [ ] **Q_ref = 1.10 × Q_total** → TR = Q_ref / 3.517.

### C. Insulation & Construction
- [ ] Best insulation: **PUF (k = 0.024 W/m·K)**, 80–100 mm.
- [ ] Vapour barrier on **warm (outside) side**.
- [ ] Floor insulation critical in freezers — use **XPS + vapour barrier + under-floor heating cables** to prevent frost heave.
- [ ] Air curtains / strip curtains on doors reduce Q₄ by 50–70 %.

### D. Operation
- [ ] Pre-cool produce BEFORE entering cold room to reduce Q₂sensible.
- [ ] Maintain **85–95 % RH** for fruits/vegetables; **75–85 %** for frozen.
- [ ] Defrost cycle adds ~5–10 % to daily load.
- [ ] Air circulation rate: 20–30 air changes/hour.
- [ ] Monitor NH₃ concentration if ammonia refrigerant; PESO/MoEFCC clearance mandatory.

### E. Numericals — Unit Conversion Reminders
- [ ] **1 kcal = 4.187 kJ**; **1 TR = 3.517 kW**; **1 Btu = 1.055 kJ**.
- [ ] Time conversion: **1 day = 86,400 s = 24 h**.
- [ ] $L_f$ (water) = 335 kJ/kg; food (typical) = 260–290 kJ/kg.

### F. Frequently Asked 2-Mark Questions
- [ ] "What is a TR?" — A refrigeration ton is the heat extracted to freeze 1 ton (2000 lb) of water at 0 °C in 24 h = 3.517 kW.
- [ ] "Why PUF is preferred?" — Lowest k-value (0.024), closed-cell, moisture-resistant, high strength-to-weight.
- [ ] "Why vapour barrier is on warm side?" — To prevent water-vapour diffusion into the cold insulation, which would condense and degrade k-value.
- [ ] "Differentiate between chilled and frozen storage." — Temperature (0 °C vs −18 °C), humidity (90 % vs 80 %), refrigerant (R-404A vs NH₃ / cascade), product type, energy consumption.

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — DESIGN OF COLD STORAGE**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | PCFTT601 | 2024 Scheme*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Agitation Vessels - Power Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Agitation Vessels - Power Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Agitation** in food processing refers to the forced induced movement of a liquid (Newtonian or non-Newtonian) inside a vessel by means of a rotating mechanical device called an **impeller** mounted on a shaft. The fluid motion serves to achieve mixing, blending, emulsification, homogenization, suspension of solids, heat transfer enhancement, and mass transfer (such as gas-liquid reactions common in fermenters and bioreactors used in the food industry).

The **Power Requirement (P)** of an agitation vessel is the mechanical energy per unit time that the motor must deliver to the impeller shaft to overcome fluid friction (drag) and create the desired flow pattern inside the vessel. The power drawn depends on the impeller geometry, rotational speed, impeller diameter, fluid properties (density and viscosity), and vessel geometry (presence of baffles, vessel diameter, liquid depth).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Power requirement is derived through **dimensional analysis** leading to the **Power Number (Np)** as a function of **Reynolds Number (NRe)** and **Froude Number (NFr)**. This topic carries high weightage in ESE (End Semester Examination) and is often combined with impeller design questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **stirring honey in a cup with a spoon** versus **stirring water in the same cup**:

- When you stir **water** (low viscosity), the spoon moves easily — you barely feel resistance — yet a vortex forms and the entire fluid body rotates chaotically.
- When you stir **honey** (high viscosity), the spoon feels "heavy" — strong resistance — but the honey rotates almost as a solid block around the spoon, with minimal vortex.

This sensation of "effort" you feel on the spoon is exactly what the **motor power** measures in an agitation vessel. The motor must supply enough torque to overcome this resistance.

| Real-World Observation | Engineering Parameter |
|------------------------|-----------------------|
| Effort to rotate spoon | **Power (P)** in Watts |
| Speed of stirring | **Impeller Speed (N)** in rev/s |
| Thickness of fluid | **Viscosity (μ)** in Pa·s |
| Size of spoon | **Impeller Diameter (D)** in m |
| Swirl/vortex formation | **Froude Number (NFr)** effect |
| Flow regime (smooth/turbulent) | **Reynolds Number (NRe)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - **g = 9.81 m/s²** (Acceleration due to gravity)
> - **Density of water at 25°C: ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - **Viscosity of water at 25°C: μ = 0.001 Pa·s (1 cP)**

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Variables Governing Power Consumption

Power consumption in an agitated vessel depends on **eleven variables** classified into four groups:

**Geometric Variables (dimensionless groups):**
- Impeller diameter: **D** (m)
- Vessel (tank) diameter: **T** (m)
- Liquid height: **H_L** (m)
- Impeller clearance from bottom: **C** (m)
- Number of blades: **n_b**
- Blade width: **W** (m)
- Baffle width: **B** (m)
- Number of baffles: **n_B**

**Operating Variables:**
- Impeller speed: **N** (rev/s or rpm)

**Fluid Property Variables:**
- Fluid density: **ρ** (kg/m³)
- Fluid viscosity: **μ** (Pa·s)

**Acceleration:**
- Gravitational acceleration: **g** (m/s²)

### 2.2 Dimensional Analysis (Buckingham π Theorem)

Applying **Buckingham π Theorem**, the eleven variables are reduced to **eight dimensionless groups** using D, N, ρ as repeating variables. The functional relationship is:

$$
f\left(\frac{D}{T}, \frac{H_L}{T}, \frac{C}{T}, \frac{n_b W}{D}, \frac{n_B B}{D}, \frac{\rho N D^2}{\mu}, \frac{N^2 D}{g}, \frac{P}{\rho N^3 D^5}\right) = 0
$$

For **geometrically similar systems**, all geometric ratios are held constant. The functional relationship simplifies to:

$$
N_p = \phi(N_{Re}, N_{Fr})
$$

This is the **fundamental power correlation** that appears in every agitation KTU question.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|--------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Power Number | $N_p$ | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, N^3 \, D^5}$ | dimensionless | Function of $N_{Re}$, $N_{Fr}$ |
| 2 | Reynolds Number | $N_{Re}$ | $N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, N \, D^2}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Classifies flow regime |
| 3 | Froude Number | $N_{Fr}$ | $N_{Fr} = \dfrac{N^2 \, D}{g}$ | dimensionless | Accounts for vortex (unbaffled) |
| 4 | Power | $P$ | $P = N_p \, \rho \, N^3 \, D^5$ | W (Watts) | Final design equation |
| 5 | Torque | $\tau$ | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{2 \pi N}$ | N·m | If shaft speed known |
| 6 | Power for laminar regime | $P$ | $P = K_p \, \mu \, N^2 \, D^3$ | W | $N_{Re} < 10$ |
| 7 | Power for turbulent regime | $P$ | $P = N_p \, \rho \, N^3 \, D^5$ | W | $N_{Re} > 10^4$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** When the table contains absolute-value-like expressions in formulas, use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX. Avoid the bare vertical pipe `|` inside markdown tables to prevent rendering breakage.

### 2.4 Power Curves and Flow Regime Classification

| Flow Regime | $N_{Re}$ Range | Behavior | Equation |
|-------------|----------------|----------|----------|
| **Laminar** | $N_{Re} < 10$ | Viscous forces dominate; power depends only on $N_{Re}$ | $N_p = \dfrac{K_p}{N_{Re}}$ |
| **Transition** | $10 < N_{Re} < 10^4$ | Both viscous and inertial forces matter | Read from curve |
| **Turbulent** | $N_{Re} > 10^4$ | Inertia dominates; $N_p$ becomes constant | $N_p = $ constant |

**Typical K values ($K_p$) for laminar regime** (when $N_p \, N_{Re} = K_p$):

| Impeller Type | $K_p$ Value |
|---------------|-------------|
| Paddle (2-blade) | 36.5 |
| Paddle (4-blade) | 64.0 |
| Turbine (6-blade) | 71.0 |
| Propeller (3-blade, square pitch) | 41.3 |
| Helical Ribbon | 300 – 425 |
| Anchor | 300 |

### 2.5 Baffled vs Unbaffled Vessels

| Condition | Effect on $N_p$ | Why? |
|-----------|-----------------|------|
| **Unbaffled + $N_{Re} < 300$** | $N_p$ depends on $N_{Re}$ alone | Vortex is weak, Froude effect negligible |
| **Unbaffled + $N_{Re} > 300$** | $N_p$ depends on BOTH $N_{Re}$ and $N_{Fr}$ | Vortex forms, surface deformation matters |
| **Baffled (any $N_{Re}$)** | $N_p$ depends on $N_{Re}$ only | Baffles suppress vortex; Froude effect eliminated |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **food processing industry**, agitation vessels are used for:
- **Dairy**: Mixing milk with cultures, emulsifying cream
- **Beverages**: Blending sugar, flavors, and carbonation
- **Sauces/Ketchups**: Maintaining homogeneous dispersion of solids in viscous fluids
- **Fermentation**: Bioreactors where oxygen mass transfer depends on impeller power input
- **Crystallization**: Sugar refining requires controlled mixing

The **Power number $N_p$ vs $N_{Re}$** curve (called the **power curve** or **Rushton curve**) is the **universal design chart** that lets an engineer read off the required power directly for any impeller type, fluid, and operating speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Power Number"?** Because $N_p$ is dimensionless, the power curve for a given impeller geometry is **universal** — valid regardless of fluid type, impeller size, or operating speed. This is the power of dimensional analysis.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Power Number (Dimensional Analysis)

**Step 1: List all variables affecting Power (P)**

Power P depends on the following:

$$
P = f(D, T, H_L, C, W, n_b, B, n_B, N, \rho, \mu, g)
$$

That gives us **n = 12 variables** and **r = 3 fundamental dimensions** (M, L, t).

By Buckingham π Theorem, the number of dimensionless groups is:

$$
\pi = n - r = 12 - 3 = 9
$$

**Step 2: Choose repeating variables**

Select **D, N, ρ** as repeating variables (they collectively contain M, L, t).

**Step 3: Form the first π-group containing P**

Let:

$$
\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{D^a \, N^b \, \rho^c}
$$

Solving for dimensions:

- $P$: $M L^2 t^{-3}$
- $D$: $L$
- $N$: $t^{-1}$
- $\rho$: $M L^{-3}$

For M: $\quad 1 = c \Rightarrow c = 1$

For L: $\quad 2 = a - 3c = a - 3 \Rightarrow a = 5$

For t: $\quad -3 = -b \Rightarrow b = 3$

Therefore:

$$
\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, N^3 \, D^5} = N_p \quad \text{(Power Number)}
$$

**Step 4: Form the second π-group containing μ**

Let:

$$
\pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{D^a \, N^b \, \rho^c}
$$

Solving:

- For M: $\quad 1 = c \Rightarrow c = 1$
- For L: $\quad -1 = a - 3 \Rightarrow a = 2$
- For t: $\quad -1 = -b \Rightarrow b = 1$

Therefore:

$$
\pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho \, N \, D^2} = \dfrac{1}{N_{Re}} \Rightarrow N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, N \, D^2}{\mu}
$$

**Step 5: Form the third π-group containing g**

Let:

$$
\pi_3 = \dfrac{g}{D^a \, N^b \, \rho^c}
$$

Since g has units of $L t^{-2}$ and ρ is independent of time, we must eliminate ρ:

- For M: $\quad 0 = c \Rightarrow c = 0$
- For L: $\quad 1 = a \Rightarrow a = 1$
- For t: $\quad -2 = -b \Rightarrow b = 2$

Therefore:

$$
\pi_3 = \dfrac{g}{N^2 \, D} = \dfrac{1}{N_{Fr}} \Rightarrow N_{Fr} = \dfrac{N^2 \, D}{g}
$$

**Step 6: Final functional relationship**

The complete relationship for geometrically similar systems becomes:

$$
N_p = \phi(N_{Re}, N_{Fr})
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
\boxed{P = N_p \, \rho \, N^3 \, D^5}
$$

### 3.2 Detailed Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A flat-blade turbine impeller of diameter **D = 0.5 m** rotates at **N = 90 rpm** in a baffled tank containing a liquid food product with **ρ = 1050 kg/m³** and **μ = 0.05 Pa·s**. Determine the power required and verify the flow regime. (Use $K_p = 71$ for 6-blade turbine if laminar applies; otherwise assume $N_p = 6.3$ for turbulent regime.)

**Step 1: Convert rotational speed to consistent units**

$$
N = \dfrac{90}{60} = 1.5 \text{ rev/s}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Reynolds Number**

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, N \, D^2}{\mu}
$$

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{1050 \times 1.5 \times (0.5)^2}{0.05}
$$

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{1050 \times 1.5 \times 0.25}{0.05}
$$

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{393.75}{0.05}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{Re} = 7875}
$$

**Step 3: Classify flow regime**

Since $10 < N_{Re} = 7875 < 10^4$, the flow is in the **transition regime**.

**Step 4: Determine Power Number**

For the transition regime, the Power Number must be read from the standard power curve. For a 6-blade flat turbine with D/T = 0.5, in a baffled vessel:

$$
N_p \approx 5.0 \text{ (typical value from Rushton curve at } N_{Re} \approx 7875\text{)}
$$

**Step 5: Calculate Power**

$$
P = N_p \, \rho \, N^3 \, D^5
$$

$$
P = 5.0 \times 1050 \times (1.5)^3 \times (0.5)^5
$$

Calculating each term:

- $N^3 = (1.5)^3 = 3.375$
- $D^5 = (0.5)^5 = 0.03125$
- $\rho \times N^3 \times D^5 = 1050 \times 3.375 \times 0.03125 = 110.74$

$$
P = 5.0 \times 110.74
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 553.7 \text{ W} \approx 0.554 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 6: Motor Selection with Safety Factor**

Industrial practice uses a **service factor (SF) of 1.2 to 1.5** to account for startup transients, fluid density variations, and partial filling conditions:

$$
P_{motor} = SF \times P_{calculated} = 1.25 \times 553.7 \approx 692 \text{ W}
$$

Select a standard motor: **0.75 kW (1 HP) motor**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Impeller Power Performance

| Impeller Type | Typical $K_p$ | $N_p$ (Turbulent) | Suitable Viscosity | Power Efficiency | Best Application |
|---------------|--------------|-------------------|--------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Propeller** | 41.3 | 0.3 – 0.6 | Low ($\mu < 5$ Pa·s) | High | Blending low-viscosity fluids |
| **Paddle (2-blade)** | 36.5 | 1.7 – 2.0 | Medium | Moderate | Gentle mixing |
| **Paddle (4-blade)** | 64.0 | 2.5 – 3.0 | Medium | Moderate | Suspension of solids |
| **Flat-Blade Turbine** | 71.0 | 5.0 – 6.3 | Medium-High | High | General purpose (most common) |
| **Anchor** | 300 | – | Very High | Low for low-$\mu$ | Viscous pastes, scraping |
| **Helical Ribbon** | 425 | – | Very High | High | Viscous non-Newtonian fluids |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** For the **same D, N, ρ**, a turbine consumes ~3× more power than a propeller in the turbulent regime. This is why impeller selection is critical for energy-efficient food plant design.

### 3.4 Effect of Baffles on Power

For a **flat-blade turbine (D/T = 0.5)**:

| Configuration | $N_p$ at $N_{Re} > 10^4$ | Power Ratio (vs Unbaffled) |
|---------------|---------------------------|----------------------------|
| Unbaffled (low N) | 1.4 | 1.0 |
| 2 Baffles (B = T/12) | 4.5 | ~3.2× |
| 4 Baffles (B = T/12) | 6.3 | ~4.5× |
| 4 Baffles (B = T/10) | 7.1 | ~5.1× |

**Engineering takeaway:** Baffles can increase power consumption by **3 to 5 times**. But they prevent vortex formation, improve mixing uniformity, and eliminate air entrainment — essential for anaerobic food fermentations.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define Power Number ($N_p$) and Reynolds Number ($N_{Re}$) for an agitation vessel. State their physical significance.

**Model Answer:**

**Power Number ($N_p$):** It is a dimensionless number that represents the ratio of the resistance force (drag) to the inertia force acting on the impeller. It is defined as:

$$
N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, N^3 \, D^5}
$$

where P is power (W), ρ is density (kg/m³), N is impeller speed (rev/s), D is impeller diameter (m).

**Reynolds Number ($N_{Re}$):** It is the ratio of inertia forces to viscous forces in the agitated fluid:

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, N \, D^2}{\mu}
$$

**Significance:**
- $N_p$ quantifies the power consumed by the impeller relative to inertial forces; it is **constant** for geometrically similar systems at a given $N_{Re}$.
- $N_{Re}$ determines the **flow regime** — laminar, transition, or turbulent — and thereby the equation used to calculate power.

*(Marks: 1.5 for each definition + significance)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Why is it necessary to provide baffles inside an agitation vessel? What happens if baffles are absent and $N_{Re} > 300$?

**Model Answer:**

**Need for Baffles:**
- Baffles (typically 4 vertical flat strips, width = T/12) prevent the formation of a central vortex and solid-body rotation.
- They convert swirling motion into axial/radial mixing, improving bulk fluid motion and uniformity.
- In food processing (e.g., fermentation), baffles prevent air entrainment that could cause oxidation of sensitive products.

**Effect of Absent Baffles when $N_{Re} > 300$:**
- A deep vortex forms at the liquid surface.
- The vortex creates an effective change in hydrostatic head that the impeller must "lift" — this introduces **gravity dependence**.
- Consequently, $N_p$ becomes a function of **both $N_{Re}$ and $N_{Fr}$**: $N_p = \phi(N_{Re}, N_{Fr})$.
- Power consumption is significantly **lower** than in baffled vessels (vortex short-circuits useful mixing).

*(Marks: 2 for baffle necessity + 1 for unbaffled behavior)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** A six-blade flat-blade turbine impeller with diameter 0.6 m is used in a baffled tank. The impeller rotates at 120 rpm. The liquid has density 1100 kg/m³ and viscosity 0.02 Pa·s. Determine:
1. Reynolds Number
2. Power Number (use $K_p = 71$ if laminar; $N_p = 6.3$ if turbulent)
3. Power consumed by the impeller

**(b)** [7 Marks] Briefly explain the effect of fluid viscosity, impeller diameter, and baffles on the power consumption of an agitation vessel. Support your answer with relevant dimensionless relations.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1: Convert speed to rev/s**

$$
N = \dfrac{120}{60} = 2.0 \text{ rev/s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Reynolds Number**

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, N \, D^2}{\mu} = \dfrac{1100 \times 2.0 \times (0.6)^2}{0.02}
$$

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{1100 \times 2.0 \times 0.36}{0.02} = \dfrac{792}{0.02}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{Re} = 39600}
$$

**Step 3: Determine Power Number**

Since $N_{Re} = 39600 > 10^4$, the flow is **turbulent**. For a 6-blade flat-blade turbine in a baffled vessel:

$$
N_p = 6.3
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Power**

$$
P = N_p \, \rho \, N^3 \, D^5 = 6.3 \times 1100 \times (2.0)^3 \times (0.6)^5
$$

Computing stepwise:
- $N^3 = (2.0)^3 = 8.0$
- $D^5 = (0.6)^5 = 0.07776$
- $1100 \times 8.0 \times 0.07776 = 684.288$

$$
P = 6.3 \times 684.288 \approx 4311 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 4.31 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Recommended motor rating:** $P_{motor} = 1.25 \times 4.31 \approx 5.4$ kW → Select standard **5.5 kW motor**.

---

**Part (b): Effects on Power Consumption**

**Effect of Fluid Viscosity (μ):**

In **laminar regime** ($N_{Re} < 10$), power is proportional to viscosity:

$$
P = K_p \, \mu \, N^2 \, D^3
$$

Hence $P \propto \mu$. Doubling viscosity doubles the power. In **turbulent regime**, viscosity has negligible effect since $N_p$ is constant and $P \propto \rho$ only.

**Effect of Impeller Diameter (D):**

In turbulent regime:

$$
P \propto D^5
$$

Doubling impeller diameter increases power by a factor of **32**. This is the **most dominant geometric parameter** for power.

**Effect of Baffles:**

In a baffled vessel, vortex is suppressed and $N_p$ (turbulent) for a 6-blade turbine ≈ 6.3. In an unbaffled vessel, the same impeller may have $N_p \approx 1.4$. Hence baffles **increase power** by a factor of ~4.5×, but vastly improve mixing quality.

**Key Relation Recap:**

$$
P = N_p(N_{Re}, N_{Fr}) \, \rho \, N^3 \, D^5
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** [7 Marks] **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** With the help of dimensional analysis, derive the relationship between Power Number ($N_p$), Reynolds Number ($N_{Re}$), and Froude Number ($N_{Fr}$) for geometrically similar agitation systems. List the variables and repeating variables used.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A four-blade paddle impeller (D = 0.4 m) operates at 60 rpm in a tank containing an oil with ρ = 950 kg/m³ and μ = 0.5 Pa·s. The vessel is unbaffled. Determine:
1. The flow regime
2. Power required ($K_p = 64$ for 4-blade paddle)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Dimensional Analysis Derivation**

**Variables affecting Power:** P, D, N, ρ, μ, g (six variables once geometry is fixed for similar systems).

Dimensions: [M L t] basis.

Number of dimensionless groups:

$$
\pi = n - r = 6 - 3 = 3
$$

**Repeating variables:** D, N, ρ (form an independent set covering M, L, t).

**π₁ (containing P):** Already derived:

$$
\pi_1 = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, N^3 \, D^5} = N_p
$$

**π₂ (containing μ):**

$$
\pi_2 = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho \, N \, D^2} = \dfrac{1}{N_{Re}} \Rightarrow N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho N D^2}{\mu}
$$

**π₃ (containing g):**

$$
\pi_3 = \dfrac{g}{N^2 \, D} = \dfrac{1}{N_{Fr}} \Rightarrow N_{Fr} = \dfrac{N^2 D}{g}
$$

**Final Correlation:**

$$
N_p = \phi(N_{Re}, N_{Fr})
$$

For **baffled** vessels, Froude Number effect is suppressed:

$$
N_p = \phi(N_{Re}) \text{ only}
$$

For **laminar** regime (independent of inertia):

$$
N_p \, N_{Re} = K_p = \text{constant}
$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1: Convert N to rev/s**

$$
N = \dfrac{60}{60} = 1.0 \text{ rev/s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Reynolds Number**

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{\rho \, N \, D^2}{\mu} = \dfrac{950 \times 1.0 \times (0.4)^2}{0.5}
$$

$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{950 \times 0.16}{0.5} = \dfrac{152}{0.5}
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{Re} = 304}
$$

**Step 3: Flow regime**

Since $10 < N_{Re} = 304 < 10^4$, the flow is in the **transition regime**. However, the problem statement specifies to use $K_p$ for laminar estimation (common KTU simplification).

Using the laminar relationship for an approximate answer:

$$
P = K_p \, \mu \, N^2 \, D^3 = 64 \times 0.5 \times (1.0)^2 \times (0.4)^3
$$

Computing:

- $D^3 = (0.4)^3 = 0.064$
- $P = 64 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 0.064 = 32 \times 0.064$

$$
\boxed{P = 2.048 \text{ W}}
$$

**Step 4: Baffled vs Unbaffled note**

Since the vessel is unbaffled, the actual power may be slightly lower due to vortex formation. For more accurate answer in transition regime, read $N_p$ from standard power curve at $N_{Re} = 304$.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students forget to convert rpm → rev/s before using the $N_{Re}$ formula. KTU examiners **strictly deduct 1–2 marks** for unit errors.
>
> 2. **Confusing $K_p$ with $N_p$:** $K_p$ is used **only in the laminar regime** as $N_p \cdot N_{Re} = K_p$. Using $K_p$ directly as $N_p$ in turbulent flow is a **fatal error** resulting in 3-mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Skipping Reynolds Number Calculation:** Many students jump straight to power equation without checking the flow regime. This violates the logical flow and **loses 2 marks** for "missing design justification".
>
> 4. **Forgetting Motor Safety Factor:** Always multiply calculated power by 1.2–1.5 before specifying motor rating. Omitting this loses 1 mark in the "design recommendation" section.
>
> 5. **Missing Baffle Effect Justification:** When the question mentions "baffled" or "unbaffled", students must explicitly state whether Froude Number applies. This is a **3-mark differentiator** between Pass and Distinction.
>
> 6. **Wrong Dimensional Groupings:** In the dimensional analysis derivation, students often pick wrong repeating variables. KTU strictly checks that the chosen repeating set is dimensionally independent (contains M, L, t collectively).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Power Requirements in Agitation Vessels**

- **Three primary dimensionless numbers:** $N_p$ (Power), $N_{Re}$ (Reynolds), $N_{Fr}$ (Froude).
- **Master formula:** $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$ — must be memorized.
- **Flow regime classification:** Laminar ($N_{Re} < 10$), Transition ($10 < N_{Re} < 10^4$), Turbulent ($N_{Re} > 10^4$).
- **Laminar formula:** $P = K_p \cdot \mu \cdot N^2 \cdot D^3$ where $K_p$ depends on impeller type (Paddle: 36.5–64; Turbine: 71; Propeller: 41.3; Anchor: 300).
- **Turbulent formula:** $P = N_p \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D^5$ where $N_p$ is read from the standard power curve (6-blade turbine baffled ≈ 6.3).
- **Baffles eliminate Froude effect:** In baffled vessels, $N_p = \phi(N_{Re})$ only; in unbaffled vessels with $N_{Re} > 300$, both $N_{Re}$ and $N_{Fr}$ matter.
- **Power scales strongly with diameter:** $P \propto D^5$ in turbulent regime — impeller diameter is the most influential design variable.
- **Power scales with cube of speed:** $P \propto N^3$ — doubling speed increases power 8×.
- **Unit conversions are mandatory:** Always convert rpm to rev/s; diameters to meters; viscosity to Pa·s.
- **Motor selection:** Apply 1.2–1.5 safety factor on calculated power to handle startup and fluid property variations.
- **Dimensional analysis foundation:** Buckingham π Theorem converts 6 variables (P, D, N, ρ, μ, g) into 3 dimensionless groups ($N_p$, $N_{Re}$, $N_{Fr}$).
- **Repeating variables in dimensional analysis:** D, N, ρ — must form a dimensionally independent set covering M, L, t.
- **Geometric similarity requirement:** All power curves are valid only when geometric ratios (D/T, C/T, H_L/T, etc.) are constant — typical KTU standard: D/T = 1/3, C/T = 1/3, H_L/T = 1, 4 baffles with B/T = 1/12.
- **Industrial application:** Higher power means better mixing but higher energy cost — design optimization balances mixing time vs power input.
- **Food industry relevance:** Viscosity is the dominant property — for honey, jam, ketchup pastes, anchor/ribbon impellers with laminar power equations apply.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Centrifugal Separator - Rate of Separation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Centrifugal Separator - Rate of Separation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Centrifugal Separation** is a mechanical separation technique that exploits the density differential between a dispersed phase (droplet or solid particle) and a continuous phase (liquid medium) by imposing a high angular velocity, thereby generating a centrifugal acceleration field many times greater than the gravitational field, which dramatically accelerates the radial migration of the dispersed phase.

The **Rate of Separation (Q)** of a centrifugal separator is defined as the maximum volumetric throughput of the feed slurry or emulsion at which a particle of critical diameter $d_c$ (the smallest particle that can be removed with 100 % efficiency) is just able to migrate from the inner boundary radius $r_i$ to the outer boundary radius $r_o$ of the separation zone during the residence time of the fluid inside the bowl. It is the central design parameter that links bowl geometry, rotational speed, fluid properties, and product specifications.

**Equivalent Area Concept (Sigma Factor, Σ):** Introduced by Ambler (1952), the *sigma factor* expresses the performance of any centrifugal separator as the cross-sectional area of a **hypothetical gravity settler** that would achieve the same separation at the same flow rate. This abstraction allows direct comparison between centrifuges of different geometries (tubular, disc, scroll-type) and against gravity settling tanks.

Mathematically, the **sigma concept** reduces to:

$$Q \;=\; \frac{\Sigma \cdot d^2 \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot g}{18 \, \mu}$$

where $d$ is the critical droplet/particle diameter, $\Delta\rho = \vert \rho_p - \rho_f \vert$ is the density difference, $g$ is gravitational acceleration, and $\mu$ is the continuous-phase viscosity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 3):** Students must derive the rate-of-separation expression for both the **tubular-bowl** and **disc-bowl** configurations and must be able to compute the sigma factor for any given geometry.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you have a glass of **chocolate milk** left to stand on a table. The chocolate particles slowly drift to the bottom — this is **gravity settling**, and it is painfully slow because gravity is weak ($g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$).

Now imagine the same glass is strapped inside a **washing machine during the spin cycle**. The drum rotates at, say, **1200 rpm**, generating an acceleration field of about **400 g** at the bowl wall. Suddenly, the chocolate particles are flung outward **400 times faster** than they would have settled under gravity, and a **clear cream-colored layer** forms at the centre in seconds.

**Key intuitions for the student:**

- The **centrifugal acceleration** $a_c = \omega^2 r$ replaces $g$ in every Stokes-type equation. The ratio $a_c / g$ is called the **separation factor (Z)** — the higher the Z, the more powerful the centrifuge.

- The **separator bowl is a "pump" that buys you time** for particles to migrate radially. The liquid travels axially from inlet to outlet; the particles must cross radially during that residence time.

- The **Sigma Factor** is the "areal rating" of a centrifuge, just as a heat exchanger has an area rating in $\text{m}^2$. Bigger $\Sigma$ means more capacity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world KTU exam cue:** Whenever a problem says *"compare the performance of two centrifuges"*, the answer is invariably *"compare their Σ values at the operating rpm"* — not their diameters, not their bowl volumes, but their equivalent areas.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following constants and metrics are mandatory citations in any derivation:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | **9.81 m/s²** |
| $\rho_p$ | Dispersed-phase density | 800 – 1500 kg/m³ |
| $\rho_f$ | Continuous-phase density | 800 – 1200 kg/m³ |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity of continuous phase | 10⁻³ – 10⁻² Pa·s |
| $\omega$ | Angular velocity | rad/s |
| $N$ | Rotational speed | **rpm** (most common in industry) |
| $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ | Conversion from rpm to rad/s | — |
| $Z = \omega^2 r/g$ | Separation factor | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> Always convert **N (rpm)** to **ω (rad/s)** using $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ **before** substituting into any formula. Marks are routinely lost in KTU valuations when this conversion is skipped or done incorrectly.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The derivation of the rate of separation proceeds through **four sequential logical steps**:

1. **Establish the radial (terminal) velocity** of a single particle in a centrifugal field via a force balance on a sphere.

2. **Integrate the radial migration time** from the inner to the outer radius of the separation zone, accounting for the fact that radial velocity itself depends on radius.

3. **Equate the particle-migration time to the fluid residence time** inside the bowl (this sets the throughput limit for 100 % removal of particles of diameter $d$).

4. **Compare the result with Stokes' law in a vertical gravity settler** to extract the geometric term — the **Sigma factor Σ**.

### Step 1 — Radial Terminal Velocity in a Centrifugal Field

A spherical particle of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_p$ immersed in a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$, rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ at radius $r$, experiences three forces in the radial direction:

- **Centrifugal force** (outward): $F_c = (\pi/6)\,d^3\,\rho_p\,\omega^2 r$
- **Buoyancy force** (inward): $F_b = (\pi/6)\,d^3\,\rho_f\,\omega^2 r$
- **Stokes drag force** (inward, opposing outward motion): $F_D = 3\pi\mu d\, v_r$

At terminal velocity, $F_c - F_b = F_D$:

$$(\pi/6)\,d^3\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r \;=\; 3\pi\mu d\, v_r$$

Solving for the radial terminal velocity:

$$v_r \;=\; \frac{d^2\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r}{18 \, \mu}$$

> This is the **Stokes terminal velocity** with $g$ replaced by $\omega^2 r$.

### Step 2 — Migration Time from $r_i$ to $r_o$

Because $v_r$ itself depends on $r$, the time is found by integration:

$$\mathrm{d}t \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{d}r}{v_r} \;=\; \frac{18\,\mu\,\mathrm{d}r}{d^2\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2\,r}$$

$$t_{\text{mig}} \;=\; \frac{18\,\mu}{d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2}\;\int_{r_i}^{r_o}\frac{\mathrm{d}r}{r} \;=\; \frac{18\,\mu}{d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2}\;\ln\!\left(\frac{r_o}{r_i}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Equate to Fluid Residence Time

The fluid residence time in a bowl of annular volume $V$ at volumetric flow $Q$ is $\tau = V/Q$. For **complete separation** of diameter-$d$ particles:

$$\tau \;\geq\; t_{\text{mig}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{V}{Q} \;\geq\; \frac{18\,\mu}{d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{r_o}{r_i}\right)$$

Therefore the **maximum (100 %-efficient) throughput** is:

$$\boxed{\;Q_{\max} \;=\; \frac{V\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2}{18\,\mu\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}\;}$$

### Step 4 — Sigma Factor Extraction

Compare with gravity settling in a vertical tank of cross-section $A$:

$$Q_{\text{grav}} \;=\; \frac{A\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,g}{18\,\mu}$$

By definition $\Sigma \equiv A_{\text{equiv}}$. Equating $Q_{\max} = Q_{\text{grav}}$ and cancelling the common $d^2\Delta\rho/18\mu$:

$$\Sigma \;=\; \frac{V\,\omega^2}{g\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}$$

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **board-exam gold**. Memorise the Σ expressions for both geometries and the **operating equation**. Notice that **vertical bars** in tables have been replaced by `\vert` to protect the markdown parser.

| # | Geometry | Annular Volume $V$ | Sigma Factor $\Sigma$ | Operating Equation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tubular bowl** | $\pi L(r_o^2 - r_i^2)$ | $\dfrac{\pi\,L\,\omega^2\,(r_o^2 - r_i^2)}{g\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}$ | $Q = \dfrac{\Sigma\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,g}{18\mu}$ |
| 2 | **Disc bowl** | Negligible (thin discs) | $\dfrac{2\pi\,\omega^2\,(r_o^3 - r_i^3)}{3\,g}\,\cot\theta$ | $Q = \dfrac{\Sigma\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,g}{18\mu}$ |
| 3 | **General bowl** | $V$ | $\dfrac{V\,\omega^2}{g\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}$ | $Q = \dfrac{\Sigma\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,g}{18\mu}$ |
| 4 | **Vertical gravity settler (reference)** | — | $A$ (cross-section) | $Q = \dfrac{A\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,g}{18\mu}$ |

| Auxiliary Relationship | Formula |
|---|---|
| Separation factor at radius $r$ | $Z(r) = \omega^2 r / g$ |
| Conversion rpm → rad/s | $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$ |
| Critical (cut) diameter | $d_c = \sqrt{18\mu Q/(\Sigma \Delta\rho g)}$ |
| Reynolds check (must be < 1 for Stokes validity) | $\text{Re} = \rho_f\,v_r\,d/\mu$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stokes-law validity:** All the above presumes laminar flow around the particle, i.e. particle Reynolds number $< 1$. For dairy fat globules (5 – 10 μm) at typical centrifugal conditions, this assumption holds comfortably. For larger mineral particles, a slip-Reynolds correction may be needed.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the food industry the rate-of-separation equation governs the **sizing and selection** of:

- **Cream separators** in dairies (milk fat 3.5 % → cream 40 % fat) — typical $Z \approx 5000$ – $10000$.
- **Oil refining centrifuges** for palm-oil clarification, olive-oil purification, and vegetable-oil degumming — $Z \approx 3000$ – $8000$.
- **Juice clarifiers** in apple, citrus, and grape processing for haze removal.
- **Brewery / winery centrifuges** for yeast and protein removal post-fermentation.
- **Edible-oil refining** for soapstock and gums separation.

In plant layout (the parent course theme), knowing $\Sigma$ per machine and required $Q$ enables calculation of the **number of parallel separators** $n = Q_{\text{plant}}/Q_{\text{machine}}$ and therefore the floor space, piping, and downstream holding-tank sizing.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation — Tubular-Bowl Centrifuge

Consider a **vertical tubular bowl** of inner radius $r_i$, outer radius $r_o$, and length $L$, rotating at angular velocity $\omega$. The feed enters from the bottom, travels upward as a thin liquid annulus between $r_i$ and $r_o$, and exits at the top.

### 3.1.1 Annular Volume of Liquid

The liquid occupies a cylindrical shell between radii $r_i$ and $r_o$ over the axial length $L$:

$$V \;=\; \pi (r_o^2 - r_i^2) L$$

### 3.1.2 Radial Settling Velocity of a Single Particle

Force balance on a sphere of diameter $d$ at radius $r$:

$$\underbrace{\frac{\pi}{6}\,d^3\,\rho_p\,\omega^2 r}_{\text{Centrifugal}} \;-\; \underbrace{\frac{\pi}{6}\,d^3\,\rho_f\,\omega^2 r}_{\text{Buoyancy}} \;-\; \underbrace{3\pi\mu d\,v_r}_{\text{Stokes drag}} \;=\; 0$$

The left-hand side simplifies as follows:

$$\frac{\pi}{6}\,d^3\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r \;=\; 3\pi\mu d\,v_r$$

Cancel a factor of $\pi d$ from both sides:

$$\frac{d^2\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r}{6} \;=\; 3\mu\,v_r$$

Solving explicitly for $v_r$:

$$v_r \;=\; \frac{d^2\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r}{18\,\mu}$$

### 3.1.3 Time for a Particle to Traverse from $r_i$ to $r_o$

The element of time for the particle to move a radial increment $\mathrm{d}r$ is $\mathrm{d}t = \mathrm{d}r/v_r$. Therefore:

$$\mathrm{d}t \;=\; \frac{\mathrm{d}r}{v_r} \;=\; \frac{18\,\mu\,\mathrm{d}r}{d^2\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2\,r}$$

Integrate from $r_i$ to $r_o$:

$$t_{\text{mig}} \;=\; \frac{18\,\mu}{d^2\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2}\int_{r_i}^{r_o}\frac{\mathrm{d}r}{r}$$

The integral evaluates to a natural logarithm:

$$\int_{r_i}^{r_o}\frac{\mathrm{d}r}{r} \;=\; \ln(r_o) - \ln(r_i) \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{r_o}{r_i}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$t_{\text{mig}} \;=\; \frac{18\,\mu\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}{d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2}$$

### 3.1.4 Fluid Residence Time Inside the Bowl

If the volumetric feed rate is $Q$ (m³/s), the average axial velocity of the fluid is $u_{\text{ax}} = Q/A_{\text{ann}}$, and the residence time is:

$$\tau \;=\; \frac{L}{u_{\text{ax}}} \;=\; \frac{L\,A_{\text{ann}}}{Q} \;=\; \frac{V}{Q}$$

### 3.1.5 Setting the 100 %-Separation Criterion

For every particle of diameter $d$ to be captured before the fluid exits, the radial migration must complete within the residence time:

$$\tau \;\geq\; t_{\text{mig}} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{V}{Q} \;\geq\; \frac{18\,\mu\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}{d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2}$$

Rearrange to extract $Q$:

$$Q \;\leq\; \frac{V\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2}{18\,\mu\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}$$

The right-hand side is the **maximum (limit) throughput**, denoted $Q_{\max}$:

$$\boxed{\;Q_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\pi\,L\,(r_o^2 - r_i^2)\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2}{18\,\mu\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}\;}$$

### 3.1.6 Sigma Factor for Tubular Geometry

Substituting $V = \pi L(r_o^2 - r_i^2)$ and comparing with $Q = A d^2 \Delta\rho g/(18\mu)$:

$$\Sigma_{\text{tubular}} \;=\; \frac{V\,\omega^2}{g\,\ln(r_o/r_i)} \;=\; \frac{\pi\,L\,\omega^2\,(r_o^2 - r_i^2)}{g\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}$$

In terms of rpm (more practical for industry):

$$\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi N}{60} \;\Longrightarrow\; \omega^2 \;=\; \frac{4\pi^2 N^2}{3600} \;=\; \frac{\pi^2 N^2}{900}$$

Therefore:

$$\Sigma_{\text{tubular}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^3\,L\,N^2\,(r_o^2 - r_i^2)}{900\,g\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}$$

---

## 3.2 Full Derivation — Disc-Bowl Centrifuge

A disc-stack bowl contains $n$ conical discs separated by a thin gap $b$, each inclined at half-angle $\theta$ to the vertical axis. The discs dramatically shorten the radial settling distance.

### 3.2.1 Settling Path Along the Disc Surface

A droplet sandwiched between two discs at radius $r$ experiences a centrifugal component **along** the disc face (not purely radial). The component of $\omega^2 r$ directed along the disc is:

$$a_{\text{disc}} \;=\; \omega^2 r \,\sin\theta$$

The perpendicular (normal) component does not contribute to sliding because the disc walls block it.

### 3.2.2 Migration Time Across a Single Gap

Following the same force-balance procedure as for the tubular case but with $a_{\text{disc}}$ instead of $\omega^2 r$:

$$t_{\text{mig,single}} \;=\; \frac{18\,\mu\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}{d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2\,\sin\theta} \;=\; \frac{18\,\mu\,\cot\theta\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}{d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2\,\cos\theta\,\cdot\sin\theta/\sin\theta}$$

It is conventional to express the result using $\cot\theta$ and the disc thickness / spacing. After integrating over the entire disc-stack length:

$$Q_{\max} \;=\; \frac{2\pi\,n\,\omega^2\,(r_o^3 - r_i^3)\,\cot\theta\,\cdot d^2\,\Delta\rho}{3 \cdot 18\,\mu}$$

### 3.2.3 Sigma Factor for Disc Geometry

Comparing with the gravity reference gives:

$$\Sigma_{\text{disc}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi\,n\,\omega^2\,(r_o^3 - r_i^3)\,\cot\theta}{3\,g}$$

If the stack has a single representative disc-pair (common simplification):

$$\boxed{\;\Sigma_{\text{disc}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi\,\omega^2\,(r_o^3 - r_i^3)\,\cot\theta}{3\,g}\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why the cube of radius?** Because the discs partition the bowl into many thin *radial* layers, and the active volume integral yields $\int r\,\mathrm{d}r = (r^2/2)$ per gap, but the geometry of conical spacing introduces an extra factor $r$ — hence $r^3$.

---

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **[Worked Example]** A tubular-bowl centrifuge has $r_i = 0.05\ \text{m}$, $r_o = 0.10\ \text{m}$, $L = 0.50\ \text{m}$, and is operated at $N = 30000\ \text{rpm}$. It is used to separate milk-fat globules of $d = 5\ \mu\text{m}$ and density $\rho_p = 900\ \text{kg/m}^3$ from skim milk of density $\rho_f = 1030\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and viscosity $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$. Calculate:
>
> **(a)** Separation factor at the outer wall.
>
> **(b)** Sigma factor for the bowl.
>
> **(c)** Maximum volumetric feed rate (rate of separation).
>
> **(d)** Time a globule takes to traverse from $r_i$ to $r_o$.

### Step (a) — Separation factor at $r_o$

Convert rpm to rad/s:

$$\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi\,N}{60} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \cdot 30000}{60} \;=\; 1000\pi \;\approx\; 3141.59\ \text{rad/s}$$

At $r_o = 0.10\ \text{m}$:

$$Z(r_o) \;=\; \frac{\omega^2\,r_o}{g} \;=\; \frac{(3141.59)^2 \cdot 0.10}{9.81}$$

$$\omega^2 \;=\; 9.8696 \times 10^{6}\ \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$Z \;=\; \frac{9.8696\times 10^{6} \cdot 0.10}{9.81} \;=\; \frac{9.8696\times 10^{5}}{9.81} \;\approx\; 1.006\times 10^{5}$$

The separation factor is therefore **$Z \approx 1.01 \times 10^5$**, i.e. about **100,000 g** — a typical order of magnitude for a laboratory/industrial tubular cream separator.

### Step (b) — Sigma factor

$$\Sigma \;=\; \frac{\pi\,L\,\omega^2\,(r_o^2 - r_i^2)}{g\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}$$

Compute each term:

$$r_o^2 - r_i^2 \;=\; (0.10)^2 - (0.05)^2 \;=\; 0.0100 - 0.0025 \;=\; 0.0075\ \text{m}^2$$

$$\ln(r_o/r_i) \;=\; \ln(0.10/0.05) \;=\; \ln 2 \;=\; 0.6931$$

$$\Sigma \;=\; \frac{\pi \cdot 0.50 \cdot 9.8696\times 10^6 \cdot 0.0075}{9.81 \cdot 0.6931}$$

Numerator:

$$\pi \cdot 0.50 \cdot 9.8696\times 10^6 \cdot 0.0075 \;=\; 1.5708 \cdot 9.8696\times 10^6 \cdot 0.0075$$

$$1.5708 \cdot 9.8696\times 10^6 \;=\; 1.5502\times 10^7$$

$$1.5502\times 10^7 \cdot 0.0075 \;=\; 1.1627\times 10^5$$

Denominator:

$$9.81 \cdot 0.6931 \;=\; 6.7994$$

Therefore:

$$\Sigma \;=\; \frac{1.1627\times 10^5}{6.7994} \;\approx\; 1.710\times 10^{4}\ \text{m}^2 \;=\; 17100\ \text{m}^2$$

### Step (c) — Rate of separation

$$Q_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\Sigma\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,g}{18\,\mu}$$

Plug numbers:

- $d^2 = (5\times 10^{-6})^2 = 2.5\times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^2$
- $\Delta\rho = \vert 900 - 1030 \vert = 130\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$
- $\mu = 1.0\times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$

Numerator:

$$\Sigma\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,g \;=\; 1.710\times 10^4 \cdot 2.5\times 10^{-11} \cdot 130 \cdot 9.81$$

Step by step:

$$1.710\times 10^4 \cdot 2.5\times 10^{-11} \;=\; 4.275\times 10^{-7}$$

$$4.275\times 10^{-7} \cdot 130 \;=\; 5.5575\times 10^{-5}$$

$$5.5575\times 10^{-5} \cdot 9.81 \;=\; 5.452\times 10^{-4}$$

Denominator:

$$18\mu \;=\; 18 \cdot 1.0\times 10^{-3} \;=\; 1.8\times 10^{-2}$$

Therefore:

$$Q_{\max} \;=\; \frac{5.452\times 10^{-4}}{1.8\times 10^{-2}} \;=\; 3.03\times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to more intuitive units:

$$Q_{\max} \;\approx\; 30.3\ \text{L/s} \;\approx\; 1.82\ \text{m}^3/\text{min} \;\approx\; 109\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

### Step (d) — Migration time

$$t_{\text{mig}} \;=\; \frac{18\,\mu\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}{d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2}$$

Numerator:

$$18\,\mu\,\ln(r_o/r_i) \;=\; 18 \cdot 1.0\times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.6931 \;=\; 1.2476\times 10^{-2}$$

Denominator:

$$d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2 \;=\; 2.5\times 10^{-11} \cdot 130 \cdot 9.8696\times 10^6$$

$$2.5\times 10^{-11} \cdot 130 \;=\; 3.25\times 10^{-9}$$

$$3.25\times 10^{-9} \cdot 9.8696\times 10^6 \;=\; 3.2076\times 10^{-2}$$

Therefore:

$$t_{\text{mig}} \;=\; \frac{1.2476\times 10^{-2}}{3.2076\times 10^{-2}} \;\approx\; 0.389\ \text{s}$$

A fat globule takes about **0.39 seconds** to traverse the annular gap — confirming that the bowl provides ample residence time at the design flow rate.

---

## 3.4 Critical-Diameter (Cut-Size) Form

Often the design problem is *inverted*: given $Q$ and geometry, find the smallest diameter completely removed. Simply rearrange the operating equation:

$$\boxed{\;d_c \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{18\,\mu\,Q}{\Sigma\,\Delta\rho\,g}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **cut-size diameter** (sometimes called $d_{50}$ when the efficiency is 50 %). For KTU 14-mark problems the statement *"find the smallest particle completely separated"* almost always requires this inverted formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Centrifuge Selection Logic — Block-Level Architecture Flow

The decision sequence for sizing a centrifugal separator using the rate-of-separation framework is depicted below. Notice the use of purely **alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeB`) and **raw uppercase labels** (no bold/italic/HTML inside the double quotes) to satisfy the Mermaid parser.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START FEED SPECS Q D DP RHOF MU"] --> nodeB["CHOOSE GEOMETRY TUBULAR OR DISC"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["INPUT BOWL DIMENSIONS L RI RO THETA"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["INPUT OPERATING SPEED N RPM"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["COMPUTE OMEGA AND OMEGA SQUARED"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["COMPUTE SIGMA FACTOR USING GEOMETRY FORMULA"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["COMPUTE DESIRED CUT SIZE DC REQUIRED"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["VERIFY STOKES REGIME RE LESS THAN ONE"]
    nodeH --> nodeI{"DC MEETS SPEC"}
    nodeI -->|YES| nodeJ["OUTPUT BOWL SPECS TO LAYOUT"]
    nodeI -->|NO| nodeK["ADJUST N OR GEOMETRY AND REITERATE"]
    nodeK --> nodeE
```

---

## 4.2 Function-Block Schematic — Tubular-Bowl Centrifuge with Sigma Labelling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInlet["FEED INLET"] --> nodeBowl["ROTATING TUBULAR BOWL LENGTH L"]
    nodeBowl --> nodeOuter["OUTER RADIUS RO"]
    nodeBowl --> nodeInner["INNER RIADIUS RI"]
    nodeOuter --> nodeSigma["SIGMA FACTOR EQUIVALENT AREA"]
    nodeInner --> nodeSigma
    nodeSpeed["ANGULAR VELOCITY OMEGA"] --> nodeSigma
    nodeVisc["VISCOSITY MU"] --> nodeQ["RATE OF SEPARATION Q"]
    nodeSigma --> nodeQ
    nodeDens["DENSITY DIFFERENCE DRHO"] --> nodeQ
    nodeD["PARTICLE DIAMETER D"] --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeOutlet["PURIFIED OUTLET"]
    nodeBowl --> nodeSolids["SOLIDS COLLECTED AT WALL"]
```

---

## 4.3 Comparative Geometry Schematic — Tubular vs Disc

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeTub["TUBULAR BOWL GEOMETRY"]
        nodeTub1["INNER RADIUS RI"] --- nodeTub2["OUTER RADIUS RO"]
        nodeTub2 --- nodeTub3["LENGTH L"]
    end
    subgraph nodeDisc["DISC BOWL GEOMETRY"]
        nodeDisc1["INNER DISC RADIUS RI"] --- nodeDisc2["OUTER DISC RADIUS RO"]
        nodeDisc2 --- nodeDisc3["DISC HALF ANGLE THETA"]
        nodeDisc2 --- nodeDisc4["NUMBER OF DISCS N"]
    end
    nodeTub --> nodeEqT["SIGMA TUBULAR PI L OMEGA SQ RO SQ MINUS RI SQ OVER G LN RO OVER RI"]
    nodeDisc --> nodeEqD["SIGMA DISC 2PI N OMEGA SQ RO CUBED MINUS RI CUBED COT THETA OVER 3G"]
    nodeEqT --> nodeFinal["BOTH COMPUTE Q EQUAL SIGMA D SQ DRHO G OVER 18 MU"]
    nodeEqD --> nodeFinal
```

> [!TIP]
> When students are asked *"which centrifuge geometry is better?"*, the answer requires comparing Σ values numerically for the given rpm and dimensions. The disc geometry is generally **far more efficient** for liquid-liquid separations because the short radial path between adjacent discs allows higher throughputs.

---

## 4.4 Process-Flow Layout — Centrifuge in a Dairy Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeRaw["RAW MILK TANK"] --> nodePump["FEED PUMP"]
    nodePump --> nodeHeater["PLATE HEAT EXCHANGER"]
    nodeHeater --> nodeSep["TUBULAR CREAM SEPARATOR"]
    nodeSep --> nodeCream["CREAM OUTLET 40 PERCENT FAT"]
    nodeSep --> nodeSkim["SKIM MILK OUTLET 0 POINT 1 PERCENT FAT"]
    nodeCream --> nodePasteur["CREAM PASTEURIZER"]
    nodeSkim --> nodePasteur2["SKIM MILK PASTEURIZER"]
    nodePasteur --> nodeStorage1["CREAM STORAGE"]
    nodePasteur2 --> nodeStorage2["SKIM MILK STORAGE"]
```

> This block-level architecture flow emphasises how the **rate of separation** computed in earlier sections directly determines the **feed-pump capacity**, the **heat-exchanger sizing**, and the **storage-tank volumes** — all of which feed back into the plant-layout phase of the parent course.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Conceptual

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define the terms (i) Sigma factor and (ii) Separation factor as applied to centrifugal separators. State their significance in design.**

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Sigma factor (Σ):** The sigma factor of a centrifuge is the **equivalent cross-sectional area of a hypothetical gravity settling tank** that would deliver the same separation performance as the centrifuge at the same volumetric throughput. It has units of $\text{m}^2$ and is computed from the bowl geometry and rotational speed.

- **(ii) Separation factor (Z):** Defined as $Z = \omega^2 r / g$, it is the ratio of **centrifugal acceleration** at radius $r$ to **gravitational acceleration**. A larger $Z$ indicates more effective separation and is the single most useful index of centrifuge power.

- **Significance in design:** The two quantities allow the engineer to (a) compare different centrifuge geometries on a common basis, (b) predict throughput via $Q = \Sigma d^2 \Delta\rho g/(18\mu)$, and (c) scale-up or scale-down laboratory data to industrial machines.

---

### Question A2 — Definitional

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Distinguish clearly between "rate of separation" and "critical (cut) diameter" in a disc-bowl centrifuge.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Rate of Separation $Q$ | Critical Diameter $d_c$ |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Maximum volumetric feed rate at which all particles of a *given* diameter are removed | Smallest particle diameter completely removed at a *given* flow rate |
| Depends on | Geometry, rpm, fluid properties, *and* the chosen particle size | Geometry, rpm, fluid properties, *and* the chosen flow rate |
| Mathematical role | Numerator of $Q = \Sigma d^2 \Delta\rho g / 18\mu$ | $d_c = \sqrt{18\mu Q/(\Sigma \Delta\rho g)}$ |
| Design use | Determines pump & piping capacity | Determines product clarity |

The two are reciprocally related: fixing one determines the other.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### Question B-A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** Derive, from first principles, the expression for the **maximum rate of separation** of a **tubular-bowl centrifuge** for the removal of spherical particles from a liquid. Clearly state all assumptions used in the derivation. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A tubular-bowl centrifuge has an inner radius of $0.04\ \text{m}$, an outer radius of $0.08\ \text{m}$, and a bowl length of $0.40\ \text{m}$. It is rotated at $25000\ \text{rpm}$ and is fed with a slurry containing particles of $d = 4\ \mu\text{m}$, $\rho_p = 1050\ \text{kg/m}^3$, in a liquid of $\rho_f = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.2\times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$.
>
> Calculate:
>
> (i) The **separation factor** at the outer wall.
>
> (ii) The **sigma factor** of the bowl.
>
> (iii) The **maximum rate of separation** in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ and $\text{L/h}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Full Model Solution

### Part (a) — Derivation

**Assumptions:**

1. Spherical, rigid particles of uniform diameter $d$.
2. **Laminar flow** around the particle — Stokes' law applies ($Re_p < 1$).
3. Particle concentration is **dilute** — particle-particle interactions are negligible.
4. The flow inside the bowl is **plug-flow** in the axial direction; radial mixing is neglected.
5. **Steady-state**, **isothermal** operation.
6. The bowl wall is the only collection surface; all particles that reach $r_o$ are captured.
7. The fluid annular volume is constant: $V = \pi L (r_o^2 - r_i^2)$.

**Derivation:**

Force balance on a particle at radius $r$ in the centrifugal field:

$$\frac{\pi}{6}\,d^3\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r \;=\; 3\pi\mu d\,v_r$$

Radial terminal velocity:

$$v_r \;=\; \frac{d^2\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r}{18\,\mu}$$

Migration time from $r_i$ to $r_o$:

$$t_{\text{mig}} \;=\; \int_{r_i}^{r_o}\frac{\mathrm{d}r}{v_r} \;=\; \frac{18\,\mu\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}{d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2}$$

Fluid residence time in bowl: $\tau = V/Q$.

Setting $\tau = t_{\text{mig}}$ for the limiting (maximum) throughput:

$$Q_{\max} \;=\; \frac{V\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2}{18\,\mu\,\ln(r_o/r_i)} \;=\; \frac{\pi L (r_o^2 - r_i^2)\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2}{18\,\mu\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}$$

> All assumptions and the integral step must appear in a 7-mark answer. Skipping the integral is the single biggest mark loss in this derivation.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Separation factor at $r_o$:**

Convert $N = 25000\ \text{rpm}$ to rad/s:

$$\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi \cdot 25000}{60} \;=\; \frac{50000\pi}{60} \;\approx\; 2617.99\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega^2 \;=\; 6.854\times 10^{6}\ \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

At $r_o = 0.08\ \text{m}$:

$$Z(r_o) \;=\; \frac{\omega^2 r_o}{g} \;=\; \frac{6.854\times 10^6 \cdot 0.08}{9.81} \;=\; \frac{5.483\times 10^5}{9.81} \;\approx\; 5.59\times 10^4$$

**$Z \approx 5.59 \times 10^4$** (about 55,900 g).

**(ii) Sigma factor:**

$$r_o^2 - r_i^2 \;=\; 0.08^2 - 0.04^2 \;=\; 0.0064 - 0.0016 \;=\; 0.0048\ \text{m}^2$$

$$\ln(r_o/r_i) \;=\; \ln(0.08/0.04) \;=\; \ln 2 \;=\; 0.6931$$

$$\Sigma \;=\; \frac{\pi \cdot 0.40 \cdot 6.854\times 10^6 \cdot 0.0048}{9.81 \cdot 0.6931}$$

Numerator:

$$\pi \cdot 0.40 \cdot 6.854\times 10^6 \cdot 0.0048 \;=\; 1.2566 \cdot 6.854\times 10^6 \cdot 0.0048$$

$$1.2566 \cdot 6.854\times 10^6 \;=\; 8.613\times 10^6$$

$$8.613\times 10^6 \cdot 0.0048 \;=\; 4.134\times 10^4$$

Denominator:

$$9.81 \cdot 0.6931 \;=\; 6.799$$

$$\Sigma \;=\; \frac{4.134\times 10^4}{6.799} \;\approx\; 6.08\times 10^{3}\ \text{m}^2$$

**$\Sigma \approx 6080\ \text{m}^2$**.

**(iii) Maximum rate of separation:**

$$Q_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\Sigma\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,g}{18\,\mu}$$

Plug values:

- $d^2 = (4\times 10^{-6})^2 = 1.6\times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^2$
- $\Delta\rho = 1050 - 1000 = 50\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81$
- $\mu = 1.2\times 10^{-3}$

Numerator:

$$6.08\times 10^3 \cdot 1.6\times 10^{-11} \cdot 50 \cdot 9.81$$

$$6.08\times 10^3 \cdot 1.6\times 10^{-11} \;=\; 9.728\times 10^{-8}$$

$$9.728\times 10^{-8} \cdot 50 \;=\; 4.864\times 10^{-6}$$

$$4.864\times 10^{-6} \cdot 9.81 \;=\; 4.772\times 10^{-5}$$

Denominator:

$$18 \cdot 1.2\times 10^{-3} \;=\; 2.16\times 10^{-2}$$

$$Q_{\max} \;=\; \frac{4.772\times 10^{-5}}{2.16\times 10^{-2}} \;\approx\; 2.21\times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to L/h:

$$Q_{\max} \;=\; 2.21\times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \cdot \frac{1000\ \text{L}}{1\ \text{m}^3} \cdot \frac{3600\ \text{s}}{1\ \text{h}} \;=\; 2.21\times 10^{-3} \cdot 3.6\times 10^{6}\ \text{L/h}$$

$$Q_{\max} \;\approx\; 7.96\times 10^{3}\ \text{L/h} \;\approx\; 7960\ \text{L/h}$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_{\max} \approx 2.21 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ **or** **$\approx 7960\ \text{L/h}$**.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3, Alternate]**
>
> **(a)** With the aid of a clear derivation, develop the expression for the **Sigma factor of a disc-bowl centrifuge** and discuss why disc-bowl machines give much higher throughputs than tubular-bowl machines of the same diameter and speed. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A disc-bowl centrifuge has $r_i = 0.05\ \text{m}$, $r_o = 0.15\ \text{m}$, disc half-angle $\theta = 35°$, number of discs $n = 60$, and runs at $N = 8000\ \text{rpm}$. The feed contains oil droplets of $d = 10\ \mu\text{m}$, $\rho_p = 920\ \text{kg/m}^3$, dispersed in water of $\rho_f = 998\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.0\times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa·s}$.
>
> Calculate:
>
> (i) The **omega** value and the **omega-squared** term.
>
> (ii) The **sigma factor** of the disc stack.
>
> (iii) The **maximum rate of separation** in $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Full Model Solution

### Part (a) — Derivation and Discussion

**Derivation:**

In a disc-bowl centrifuge, conical discs of half-angle $\theta$ are stacked, dividing the bowl into many thin wedge-shaped gaps. A droplet between two discs experiences a centrifugal component **along** the disc face equal to $\omega^2 r \sin\theta$.

Force balance on a droplet sliding along the disc:

$$\frac{\pi}{6}\,d^3\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r \sin\theta \;=\; 3\pi\mu d\,v_{\text{slide}}$$

$$v_{\text{slide}} \;=\; \frac{d^2\,(\rho_p - \rho_f)\,\omega^2 r \sin\theta}{18\,\mu}$$

Migration time along the disc from $r_i$ to $r_o$:

$$t_{\text{slide}} \;=\; \int_{r_i}^{r_o}\frac{\mathrm{d}r}{v_{\text{slide}}} \;=\; \frac{18\,\mu\,\ln(r_o/r_i)}{d^2\,\Delta\rho\,\omega^2\sin\theta}$$

Total radial distance a droplet must traverse in the bowl is now $r_o - r_i$, but the *equivalent* number of "effective gaps" scales with $n$. After integration over the disc-stack volume and comparing with the gravity reference:

$$\Sigma_{\text{disc}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi\,n\,\omega^2\,(r_o^3 - r_i^3)\,\cot\theta}{3\,g}$$

**Discussion — Why disc-bowl machines give higher throughputs:**

- The discs **partition the radial distance** $r_o - r_i$ into many short segments — droplets travel only a small radial distance per disc.
- The **inclined-disc geometry** converts radial centrifugal force into tangential motion along the disc surface, ensuring droplets glide rather than having to fight radial drag alone.
- The **larger radial zone** ($r_o^3 - r_i^3$ factor) traps droplets efficiently even when $r_i$ is a sizeable fraction of $r_o$.
- For the **same diameter and rpm**, $\Sigma_{\text{disc}}$ is typically an order of magnitude larger than $\Sigma_{\text{tubular}}$.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Angular velocity and omega-squared:**

$$\omega \;=\; \frac{2\pi \cdot 8000}{60} \;=\; \frac{16000\pi}{60} \;\approx\; 837.76\ \text{rad/s}$$

$$\omega^2 \;=\; (837.76)^2 \;\approx\; 7.018\times 10^{5}\ \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**(ii) Sigma factor:**

Compute each term:

- $r_o^3 - r_i^3 = 0.15^3 - 0.05^3 = 0.003375 - 0.000125 = 0.003250\ \text{m}^3$
- $\cot\theta = \cot 35° = 1 / \tan 35° = 1 / 0.7002 = 1.4281$
- $n = 60$

$$\Sigma_{\text{disc}} \;=\; \frac{2\pi \cdot 60 \cdot 7.018\times 10^5 \cdot 0.003250 \cdot 1.4281}{3 \cdot 9.81}$$

Numerator:

$$2\pi \cdot 60 \;=\; 376.99$$

$$376.99 \cdot 7.018\times 10^5 \;=\; 2.6456\times 10^8$$

$$2.6456\times 10^8 \cdot 0.003250 \;=\; 8.598\times 10^5$$

$$8.598\times 10^5 \cdot 1.4281 \;=\; 1.2280\times 10^6$$

Denominator:

$$3 \cdot 9.81 \;=\; 29.43$$

$$\Sigma_{\text{disc}} \;=\; \frac{1.2280\times 10^6}{29.43} \;\approx\; 4.17\times 10^{4}\ \text{m}^2$$

**$\Sigma_{\text{disc}} \approx 4.17 \times 10^4\ \text{m}^2$** (about **41,700 m²**).

**(iii) Maximum rate of separation:**

$$Q_{\max} \;=\; \frac{\Sigma\,d^2\,\Delta\rho\,g}{18\,\mu}$$

- $d^2 = (10\times 10^{-6})^2 = 1.0\times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^2$
- $\Delta\rho = 998 - 920 = 78\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\mu = 1.0\times 10^{-3}$

Numerator:

$$4.17\times 10^4 \cdot 1.0\times 10^{-10} \cdot 78 \cdot 9.81$$

$$4.17\times 10^4 \cdot 1.0\times 10^{-10} \;=\; 4.17\times 10^{-6}$$

$$4.17\times 10^{-6} \cdot 78 \;=\; 3.253\times 10^{-4}$$

$$3.253\times 10^{-4} \cdot 9.81 \;=\; 3.191\times 10^{-3}$$

Denominator:

$$18 \cdot 1.0\times 10^{-3} \;=\; 1.8\times 10^{-2}$$

$$Q_{\max} \;=\; \frac{3.191\times 10^{-3}}{1.8\times 10^{-2}} \;\approx\; 1.77\times 10^{-1}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to m³/h:

$$Q_{\max} \;=\; 1.77\times 10^{-1} \cdot 3600 \;\approx\; 638\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Final Answer:** $Q_{\max} \approx 638\ \text{m}^3/\text{h}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (per KTU 2024 valuation key):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm to rad/s.** Substituting $N$ in rpm directly into $\omega^2$ is the single most common error — it inflates $\Sigma$ by a factor of ~3600 and gives wildly unrealistic answers.
>
> 2. **Missing the logarithmic term $\ln(r_o/r_i)$ in the tubular derivation.** Students often write $r_o - r_i$ or $r_o/r_i$ without the log. KTU examiners specifically look for the natural-logarithm integral step; missing it costs 2 marks in a 7-mark derivation.
>
> 3. **Writing $r_o^3$ instead of $r_o^3 - r_i^3$** in the disc formula.
>
> 4. **Mixing up $\Delta\rho$ direction.** Always use the absolute value $\vert \rho_p - \rho_f \vert$; signing causes silent sign errors.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the $n$ (number of discs) in the disc-bowl Σ.** Many students use a "single-disc" formula when the question clearly states 60 discs.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Stokes-regime check.** A 1-line statement verifying $\text{Re}_p < 1$ earns a bonus mark for "engineering judgement" and protects the rest of the solution if Stokes happens to be borderline.
>
> 7. **Unit-mismatch in final answer.** Always state $Q$ in both $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ *and* a practical unit ($\text{L/h}$ or $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Centrifugal Separator Rate of Separation**

- [ ] **Centrifugal acceleration:** $a_c = \omega^2 r$ — replaces $g$ in Stokes' law.
- [ ] **Separation factor:** $Z = \omega^2 r / g$ (dimensionless) — measure of machine "power".
- [ ] **Stokes radial velocity:** $v_r = d^2 \Delta\rho\, \omega^2 r / (18\mu)$.
- [ ] **Migration time:** $t_{\text{mig}} = 18\mu \ln(r_o/r_i) / (d^2 \Delta\rho \omega^2)$.
- [ ] **Residence time:** $\tau = V/Q$.
- [ ] **Max rate (tubular):** $Q_{\max} = \pi L (r_o^2 - r_i^2)\, d^2 \Delta\rho \omega^2 / [18\mu \ln(r_o/r_i)]$.
- [ ] **Max rate (disc):** $Q_{\max} = 2\pi n (r_o^3 - r_i^3) \cot\theta\, d^2 \Delta\rho \omega^2 / (54\mu g)$.
- [ ] **Sigma factor (tubular):** $\Sigma = \pi L \omega^2 (r_o^2 - r_i^2) / [g \ln(r_o/r_i)]$.
- [ ] **Sigma factor (disc):** $\Sigma = 2\pi n \omega^2 (r_o^3 - r_i^3) \cot\theta / (3g)$.
- [ ] **Operating equation (universal):** $Q = \Sigma\, d^2 \Delta\rho\, g / (18\mu)$.
- [ ] **Cut-size (inverted form):** $d_c = \sqrt{18\mu Q / (\Sigma \Delta\rho g)}$.
- [ ] **Sigma unit:** $\text{m}^2$ — equivalent area of a gravity settler.
- [ ] **Always:** convert $N$ (rpm) → $\omega$ (rad/s) via $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$.
- [ ] **Always:** check Stokes validity ($Re_p < 1$).
- [ ] **Always:** use $\vert \rho_p - \rho_f \vert$ for $\Delta\rho$.
- [ ] **Remember:** higher $N$, higher $r$, higher $\Delta\rho$, larger $L$ (tubular), more discs $n$ (disc) → larger $\Sigma$ → larger $Q_{\max}$.
- [ ] **Remember:** disc bowls are superior for liquid-liquid separations due to short radial path and inclined surfaces.
- [ ] **Industrial anchor values:** milk fat separator $Z \sim 10^4$ – $10^5$; yeast separator $Z \sim 5000$ – $10^4$; oil purifier $Z \sim 3000$ – $8000$.
- [ ] **Plant-layout implication:** $n_{\text{machines}} = Q_{\text{plant}} / Q_{\text{machine}}$, governs floor space and parallel-piping design.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Considerations of Cleaners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Considerations of Cleaners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cleaners** in food processing engineering are unit operations equipment designed to **remove extraneous matter** (soil, dirt, microbial load, pesticide residues, field debris, insects, and chemical contaminants) from the **surface of raw food materials** prior to subsequent processing operations such as peeling, sorting, grading, or thermal treatment.

A cleaner is essentially the **first critical hygienic barrier** in any food processing line. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCFTT601, Module 3), the *design of cleaners* encompasses the engineering specification of mechanical, hydraulic, and pneumatic parameters that ensure **effective contaminant removal** while preserving the **physical integrity** and **nutritional quality** of the food product.

### 1.2 Classification of Cleaners (KTU Syllabus Framework)

Cleaners are classified along three orthogonal axes used in the 2024 scheme:

| Classification Axis | Categories | Example Equipment |
|---|---|---|
| **Cleaning Medium** | Wet, Dry, Abrasive | Flotation washer, Air-blast cleaner, Brush polisher |
| **Mechanism of Action** | Soaking, Spraying, Abrasion, Friction, Flotation, Ultrasonication | Soak tank, Spray washer, Drum washer, Flotation tank |
| **Mode of Operation** | Batch, Continuous | Batch soak tank, Continuous rotary drum |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Washing a Car**
> Think of cleaning food the same way you wash a car. You do not just dump water on it — you (1) **pre-soak** to loosen the dried mud, (2) **spray pressurized water** to dislodge grit, (3) **rub with a brush** for stubborn dirt, and finally (4) **rinse with clean water**. A food cleaner follows exactly the same logic, but it must do this *continuously, at industrial scale, without bruising the product*.
>
> A *mushroom* is like a soft sponge (gentle, soak only), while a *coconut* is like a stone (aggressive abrasion permitted). The designer must therefore match the **cleaning intensity** to the **mechanical fragility** of the food.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following standard constants are routinely used in cleaner design:

- **Water density (ρ_w) = 1000 kg/m³**
- **Dynamic viscosity of water at 20 °C (μ) = 1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
- **Gravitational acceleration (g) = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Surface tension of water (σ) = 0.0728 N/m**
- **Standard water consumption in vegetable cleaning: 2–5 m³ per ton of product**
- **Typical residence time range: 30 s to 10 min** depending on soil load

---
