5. **Bearing Reaction:** The end bearings carry radial + axial loads. Hanger bearings (intermediate) support long screws but interrupt material flow — design trade-off exists.

**The "Why" — Why does the material move forward and not just rotate?**

Because the helix is *inclined* relative to the axis. The angle of the flight (helix angle) determines the axial vs. tangential force ratio. A standard pitch (s = D) gives a helix angle of ≈ 14.04° for a single flight, which is the sweet spot for efficient conveyance without material tumbling.

### 2.2 Types of Screw Conveyors (Classification)

**A. By Orientation:**
- **Horizontal** — most common; capacity highest.
- **Inclined** — capacity reduces with angle (correction factor C).
- **Vertical** — uses special tapered flight; low capacity.

**B. By Flight Geometry:**
- **Standard Pitch** (s = D) — general purpose.
- **Short Pitch** (s = 2D/3) — for inclined conveyors and sticky materials.
- **Long Pitch** (s = 1.5D to 2D) — for free-flowing, non-abrasive materials at high rates.
- **Variable Pitch** — tapered to control feed rate vs. discharge.
- **Cut & Folded Flight** — for mixing, de-lumping, or agitator use.
- **Ribbon Flight** — used for sticky/wet food (e.g., wet dough, pastes).
- **Paddle Flight** — for cooling/heating duties (heat-transfer screws).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Part-B (14 mark) design problem.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric Capacity | $Q = 47 \times D^2 \times s \times n \times \Phi$ | m³/h | CEMA metric formula; s = pitch, n = rpm |
| 2 | Mass Capacity (W) | $W = Q \times \gamma$ | t/h | γ = bulk density (t/m³) |
| 3 | Capacity (standard pitch, s = D) | $Q = 47 \times D^3 \times n \times \Phi$ | m³/h | Simpler form when s = D |
| 4 | Inclined Capacity | $Q_{incl} = Q_{horiz} \times C$ | m³/h | C from inclination table |
| 5 | Power at Drive Shaft | $P = \dfrac{W \times L \times F_d}{367}$ | kW | W in t/h, L in m, Fd = material factor |
| 6 | Total Power (inclined) | $P_{tot} = P_{horiz} + P_{lift}$ | kW | $P_{lift} = \dfrac{W \times H}{367}$ where H = vertical lift |
| 7 | Motor Power | $P_m = \dfrac{P_{tot}}{\eta}$ | kW | η = drive efficiency (0.85–0.95) |
| 8 | Torque at Shaft | $T = \dfrac{9550 \times P}{n}$ | N·m | P in kW, n in rpm |
| 9 | Recommended Max Speed | $n_{max} = \dfrac{42}{\sqrt{D}}$ (D in m) | rpm | Empirical limit to prevent throw-out |
| 10 | Shaft Diameter (empirical) | $d_s = (0.4 \text{ to } 0.5) \times D$ | mm | For design of shaft rigidity |

### 2.4 Inclination Correction Factor (C) — Must Memorize

| Inclination Angle θ | 0° | 5° | 10° | 15° | 20° | 25° | 30° | 35° | 40° |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Factor C | 1.00 | 0.97 | 0.94 | 0.90 | 0.85 | 0.80 | 0.75 | 0.70 | 0.65 |

> Above 40°, use a vertical screw conveyor (separate design) — horizontal formula is no longer valid.

### 2.5 Material Factor (Fd) — Typical Values for Food Materials

| Food Material | Bulk Density γ (kg/m³) | Material Factor Fd |
|---|---|---|
| Wheat | 750 | 1.5 |
| Rice (milled) | 770 | 1.4 |
| Corn (shelled) | 720 | 1.5 |
| Flour | 590 | 1.0 |
| Sugar (granular) | 800 | 1.0 |
| Salt (coarse) | 1200 | 1.6 |
| Cocoa powder | 500 | 1.4 |
| Groundnut (shelled) | 600 | 1.2 |
| Animal feed (pellets) | 650 | 1.2 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Screw conveyors are the *most common* means of bulk-material transport in food plants because they are: (1) **enclosed** → dust-free & hygienic, (2) **cheap** compared to belt/pneumatic, (3) **versatile** — can feed, mix, cool, and discharge, (4) **simple** to maintain. Limitation: not suitable for fragile food (e.g., chips, breakfast cereals) due to breakage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Capacity Formula (CEMA Metric)

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area filled by material (in the trough):**

When the screw rotates, it carries a "slug" of material between successive flights. The cross-section of this slug is approximately a circle of diameter D minus the inner shaft diameter $d_s$. For simplification (and as used in CEMA), the area is taken as a fraction of the full disc area.

**Step 2 — Volume conveyed per revolution:**

The material advances one full pitch (s) per revolution. Hence, volume per revolution is:

$$V_{rev} = A_{mat} \times s$$

where $A_{mat}$ = effective material cross-section, and $s$ = screw pitch.

**Step 3 — Accounting for incomplete filling:**

Not all of the cross-section is filled (Φ < 1.0). The empirical CEMA fill factor Φ corrects this. The 47 in the formula is a unit-conversion constant combining geometric (π/4) and unit-normalization (1 hr / 60 min) factors.

**Step 4 — Final assembled equation:**

$$Q = 47 \times D^2 \times s \times n \times \Phi \quad \text{[m}^3\text{/h]}$$

**Step 5 — Mass flow rate:**

$$W = Q \times \gamma$$

where γ is the bulk density in t/m³ (note: 1 t/m³ = 1000 kg/m³).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Power Formula

**Step 1 — Power to overcome friction (horizontal motion):**

The material sliding along the trough generates friction. CEMA's empirical result for frictional power:

$$P_{fric} = \frac{W \times L \times F_d}{367}$$

The 367 in the denominator is a unit-conversion constant: 102 (kg→N) × 3600 (s/h) / 1000 (kW).

**Step 2 — Power to lift material (inclined case only):**

If the conveyor lifts the material to a vertical height H (m), additional power is needed:

$$P_{lift} = \frac{W \times H}{367}$$

**Step 3 — Total drive-shaft power:**

$$P_{tot} = P_{fric} + P_{lift} = \frac{W \times (L \cdot F_d + H)}{367}$$

**Step 4 — Motor power (after drive losses):**

$$P_m = \frac{P_{tot}}{\eta_m}$$

where η_m = combined efficiency of gearbox + coupling (typically 0.85–0.90).

### 3.3 Complete Analytical Worked Example (14-Mark Style)

> **PROBLEM:** Design a horizontal screw conveyor to convey **50 tonnes per hour** of wheat over a horizontal distance of **30 m**. Wheat bulk density γ = 750 kg/m³. Material factor Fd = 1.5. Drive efficiency η = 0.90.

---

#### Solution — Step 1: Convert mass flow to volumetric flow

$$Q_v = \frac{W}{\gamma} = \frac{50 \text{ t/h}}{0.75 \text{ t/m}^3} = 66.67 \text{ m}^3\text{/h}$$

**Logic:** We need the volumetric rate first because the CEMA capacity formula gives $Q$ in m³/h.

---

#### Solution — Step 2: Select screw diameter and recommended speed

Using empirical rule $n_{max} \approx 42 / \sqrt{D}$ (D in m):

Try **D = 0.4 m (400 mm)**, which is a standard size.

$$n_{max} = \frac{42}{\sqrt{0.4}} = \frac{42}{0.6325} \approx 66.4 \text{ rpm}$$

For safe operation, select **n = 60 rpm**. Also adopt **standard pitch s = D = 0.4 m**, and assume filling factor **Φ = 0.30** (typical for grain at standard pitch).

---

#### Solution — Step 3: Verify capacity at chosen parameters

$$Q = 47 \times D^2 \times s \times n \times \Phi$$

Substituting D = 0.4 m, s = 0.4 m, n = 60 rpm, Φ = 0.30:

$$Q = 47 \times (0.4)^2 \times 0.4 \times 60 \times 0.30$$

$$Q = 47 \times 0.16 \times 0.4 \times 60 \times 0.30$$

$$Q = 47 \times 0.16 \times 0.4 \times 18 = 54.14 \text{ m}^3\text{/h}$$

In t/h: $W = 54.14 \times 0.75 = 40.6$ t/h.

> ❌ This is **less than the required 50 t/h**. Increase Φ or n.

---

#### Solution — Step 4: Increase n to 80 rpm and Φ to 0.35

$$Q = 47 \times 0.16 \times 0.4 \times 80 \times 0.35$$

$$Q = 47 \times 0.16 \times 0.4 \times 28 = 84.22 \text{ m}^3\text{/h}$$

In t/h: $W = 84.22 \times 0.75 = 63.16$ t/h.

> ✅ **63.16 t/h > 50 t/h — design is acceptable with margin.** (Note: n = 80 < n_max = 66.4 — wait, this exceeds the empirical limit! Reduce.)

---

#### Solution — Step 5: Correct to n = 60 rpm with Φ = 0.45 (max for food)

$$Q = 47 \times 0.16 \times 0.4 \times 60 \times 0.45 = 81.22 \text{ m}^3\text{/h}$$

In t/h: $W = 81.22 \times 0.75 = 60.9$ t/h.

> ✅ **Final design selection: D = 400 mm, s = 400 mm, n = 60 rpm, Φ = 0.45, W_actual ≈ 60.9 t/h.**

---

#### Solution — Step 6: Power at drive shaft

$$P = \frac{W \times L \times F_d}{367} = \frac{50 \times 30 \times 1.5}{367} = \frac{2250}{367} = 6.13 \text{ kW}$$

**Logic:** Use the *required* W (50 t/h) for power sizing, not the actual delivered W.

---

#### Solution — Step 7: Motor power (accounting for drive efficiency)

$$P_m = \frac{P}{\eta} = \frac{6.13}{0.90} = 6.81 \text{ kW}$$

**Standard motor selection:** Next standard size up = **7.5 kW (10 HP)** squirrel-cage induction motor at 1440 rpm with a reduction gearbox (60:1).

---

#### Solution — Step 8: Shaft diameter (rule-of-thumb)

$$d_s = 0.45 \times D = 0.45 \times 400 = 180 \text{ mm}$$

(The shaft must be checked for torsional stress using ASME code, but rule-of-thumb suffices for KTU design problems.)

---

#### Solution — Step 9: Final Design Summary

| Parameter | Final Value |
|---|---|
| Screw Diameter (D) | 400 mm |
| Pitch (s) | 400 mm (standard) |
| Speed (n) | 60 rpm |
| Filling factor (Φ) | 0.45 |
| Power at shaft | 6.13 kW |
| Motor power | 7.5 kW (10 HP) |
| Shaft diameter | 180 mm |
| Material of construction | SS 304 (food grade) |
| Trough type | U-trough with end flanges |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip from KTU Examiners:** Always state your *assumed* values for Φ, Fd, and η. Examiners give step marks only when assumptions are explicitly written. Don't just plug numbers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Anatomy of a Screw Conveyor — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional architecture of a screw conveyor, illustrating the flow of material, energy, and structural relationships between components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FEED HOPPER] --> B[INLET BOOT SECTION]
    B --> C[HELICAL FLIGHT ASSEMBLY]
    C --> D[INLET HANGER BEARING]
    C --> E[INTERMEDIATE HANGER BEARINGS]
    C --> F[DISCHARGE END BEARING]
    F --> G[DISCHARGE CHUTE]
    H[ELECTRIC MOTOR] --> I[GEAR REDUCER]
    I --> J[COUPLING]
    J --> K[DRIVE SHAFT]
    K --> C
    L[TROUGH U SECTION] --- C
    M[END COVERS WITH SEAL] --- B
    M --- G
    N[INSPECTION COVERS] --- L
    O[SUPPORT FRAME] --- L
    P[FEED CONTROL GATE] --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **material flow path** (top): Hopper → Boot → Flight → Discharge Chute.
- The **power transmission path** (right): Motor → Reducer → Coupling → Drive Shaft → Flight.
- The **structural support** (bottom): Frame, trough, covers, and inspection ports.

### 4.2 Flight Geometry — Detailed Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HELICAL FLIGHT TYPES] --> B[STANDARD PITCH]
    A --> C[SHORT PITCH 2D by 3]
    A --> D[LONG PITCH 1.5D to 2D]
    A --> E[RIBBON FLIGHT]
    A --> F[PADDLE FLIGHT]
    A --> G[CUT AND FOLDED]
    B --> B1[USE: GENERAL PURPOSE GRAIN FLOUR]
    C --> C1[USE: INCLINED STICKY FOODS]
    D --> D1[USE: FREE FLOWING HIGH CAPACITY]
    E --> E1[USE: WET PASTY STICKY MASS]
    F --> F1[USE: COOLING DRYING DUTIES]
    G --> G1[USE: MIXING DE LUMPING]
```

### 4.3 Material & Energy Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[BULK FOOD MATERIAL] --> INLET[INLET: GRAVITY FEED]
    INLET --> TR[THRUST FROM FLIGHT]
    TR --> AX[AXIAL DISPLACEMENT]
    TR --> FR[FRICTION WITH TROUGH]
    AX --> DIS[DISCHARGE]
    FR --> HEAT[HEAT GENERATION: FRICTION LOSS]
    HEAT --> AMB[DISSIPATED TO AMBIENT]
    MOTOR[ELECTRICAL ENERGY INPUT] --> GB[GEARBOX LOSS]
    GB --> SHAFT[SHAFT POWER]
    SHAFT --> USEFUL[USEFUL CONVEYANCE WORK]
    SHAFT --> LOSS[FRICTION POWER LOSS]
    USEFUL --> DIS
    LOSS --> HEAT
```

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Selecting Screw Conveyor Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[START: IDENTIFY MATERIAL] --> Q1{IS MATERIAL FREE FLOWING?}
    Q1 -->|YES| Q2{IS PARTICLE LESS THAN D BY 10?}
    Q1 -->|NO| R1[USE RIBBON OR SHORT PITCH FLIGHT]
    Q2 -->|YES| Q3{HORIZONTAL OR LOW INCLINATION?}
    Q2 -->|NO| R2[USE LARGER DIAMETER OR SPECIAL FEEDER]
    Q3 -->|YES| R3[STANDARD PITCH HORIZONTAL SCREW]
    Q3 -->|NO| Q4{INCLINATION LESS THAN 20 DEG?}
    Q4 -->|YES| R4[STANDARD PITCH INCLINED C LESS THAN 1]
    Q4 -->|NO| R5[VERTICAL SCREW CONVEYOR OR ALTERNATIVE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> These Mermaid diagrams are **KTU board-compatible** — they can be redrawn by hand during exams. Focus on memorising the *flow logic* and *component list* rather than the exact arrow layout.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a screw conveyor. List any four types of screw flights used in food processing industries.*

**Model Answer:**

A **screw conveyor** is a bulk material handling device that uses a rotating helical screw blade (called a "flight") mounted on a central shaft inside a trough to transport powdered, granular, or semi-fluid materials axially from one point to another by the thrust generated by the rotating flight.

**Four types of screw flights:**

1. **Standard Pitch Flight** — pitch equals screw diameter (s = D); general purpose.
2. **Short Pitch Flight** — pitch = 2D/3; for inclined conveyors and sticky materials.
3. **Ribbon Flight** — used for wet, sticky, or pasty foods (e.g., wet dough, slurries).
4. **Paddle Flight** — flat blades instead of helix; for cooling, drying, or mixing duties.

*(Any 4 of: standard, short, long, cut & folded, ribbon, paddle — full 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the role of the "filling factor" (Φ) in screw conveyor design. What is its typical range?*

**Model Answer:**

The **filling factor (Φ)** is the ratio of the actual cross-sectional area of material carried by the screw to the theoretical full cross-sectional area of the screw disc.

- **Range:** 0.15 to 0.45 (typically 0.30 for horizontal conveyors at standard pitch).
- **Role:**
  - Lower Φ → less material per revolution, but allows more complete filling near trough walls and reduces power per ton conveyed.
  - Higher Φ → higher capacity, but risks spillage at the feed inlet, increased torque, and material degradation.
- For **food materials** like grains and flour, Φ = 0.30–0.40 is common to avoid crushing.
- For **inclined conveyors**, Φ is reduced along with the inclination factor C.

*(3 marks for definition, range, and role.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks (CHOICE 1)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Design a horizontal screw conveyor to convey **40 tonnes per hour** of **rice (milled)** over a length of **25 m**. The bulk density of rice is 770 kg/m³ and the material factor Fd = 1.4. Assume a drive efficiency of 0.90. Determine the **screw diameter, recommended speed, capacity check, drive power, and motor power.** (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **selection of material of construction and sanitary design features** required for screw conveyors used in food processing plants. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Convert mass flow to volume flow.**

$$Q_v = \frac{W}{\gamma} = \frac{40}{0.77} = 51.95 \text{ m}^3\text{/h}$$

**Step 2 — Select screw diameter.**

Try **D = 0.35 m (350 mm)**, standard size. Standard pitch s = D = 0.35 m.

**Step 3 — Calculate recommended maximum speed.**

$$n_{max} = \frac{42}{\sqrt{0.35}} = \frac{42}{0.5916} \approx 71.0 \text{ rpm}$$

Adopt **n = 65 rpm** (safe operating value). Assume **Φ = 0.35** (typical for rice).

**Step 4 — Calculate actual capacity at chosen values.**

$$Q = 47 \times D^2 \times s \times n \times \Phi = 47 \times (0.35)^2 \times 0.35 \times 65 \times 0.35$$

$$Q = 47 \times 0.1225 \times 0.35 \times 65 \times 0.35 = 47 \times 0.1225 \times 0.35 \times 22.75$$

$$Q = 47 \times 0.1225 \times 7.9625 = 45.83 \text{ m}^3\text{/h}$$

In t/h: $W_{act} = 45.83 \times 0.77 = 35.29$ t/h. ❌ *Insufficient* (less than 40 t/h required).

**Step 5 — Increase filling factor to Φ = 0.40.**

$$Q = 47 \times 0.1225 \times 0.35 \times 65 \times 0.40 = 52.38 \text{ m}^3\text{/h}$$

In t/h: $W_{act} = 52.38 \times 0.77 = 40.33$ t/h. ✅ *Sufficient.*

**Step 6 — Calculate drive power at shaft.**

$$P = \frac{W \times L \times F_d}{367} = \frac{40 \times 25 \times 1.4}{367} = \frac{1400}{367} = 3.81 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 — Calculate motor power.**

$$P_m = \frac{P}{\eta} = \frac{3.81}{0.90} = 4.24 \text{ kW}$$

Standard motor: **5.0 kW (6.7 HP)** with reduction gearbox.

**Step 8 — Summary table (essential for full marks):**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Screw diameter (D) | 350 mm |
| Pitch (s) | 350 mm |
| Rotational speed (n) | 65 rpm |
| Filling factor (Φ) | 0.40 |
| Capacity delivered | 40.33 t/h |
| Drive shaft power | 3.81 kW |
| Motor power | 5.0 kW |

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Material of Construction for Food-Grade Screw Conveyors:**

1. **Stainless Steel SS 304** — Used for most dry food applications (grains, flour, sugar). Cost-effective, corrosion-resistant, easy to clean.

2. **Stainless Steel SS 316** — Used for corrosive foods (salty, acidic, vinegar-based) due to its superior chloride resistance (molybdenum content).

3. **Carbon Steel (with epoxy/food-grade coating)** — Used for non-contact or low-hygiene zones; NOT recommended for direct food contact.

4. **Polymer / UHMWPE Liners** — Used as wear liners in troughs to prevent metal-to-metal abrasion and reduce noise.

**Sanitary Design Features:**

1. **Surface Finish:** All product-contact surfaces must have **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm** (mirror finish) to prevent bacterial adhesion and enable CIP (Clean-in-Place).

2. **No Dead Spots:** All corners must have **≥ 3 mm radius** fillets; no crevices where food can accumulate.

3. **Hygienic Bearings:** Use **sealed, externally-located bearings** so that lubricants do not contaminate food.

4. **Quick-Release Covers:** Inspection doors with silicone gaskets for fast disassembly and washdown.

5. **CIP Compatibility:** Conveyor must be designed to allow cleaning solutions (typically 1–2% NaOH at 80°C) to flow through without disassembly.

6. **Drainability:** Trough must slope towards drain ports to prevent pooling.

7. **FDA-Compliant Seals:** Use EPDM or silicone rubber gaskets meeting FDA 21 CFR §177.2600.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (CHOICE 2 — ALTERNATIVE)**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A screw conveyor of diameter **D = 300 mm** and pitch **s = 240 mm** rotates at **80 rpm** with a filling factor of **0.30**. The bulk density of the material (refined sugar) is **800 kg/m³** and the material factor is **1.2**. The conveyor length is **20 m** horizontally. Calculate: (i) the **volumetric and mass capacity**, and (ii) the **drive shaft power and required motor power** (assume η = 0.85). (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the **advantages and limitations** of screw conveyors with **belt conveyors** for food material handling. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Given:** D = 0.30 m, s = 0.24 m, n = 80 rpm, Φ = 0.30, γ = 0.80 t/m³, Fd = 1.2, L = 20 m, η = 0.85.

**Part (i) — Volumetric and mass capacity:**

$$Q = 47 \times D^2 \times s \times n \times \Phi = 47 \times (0.30)^2 \times 0.24 \times 80 \times 0.30$$

$$Q = 47 \times 0.09 \times 0.24 \times 80 \times 0.30 = 47 \times 0.09 \times 0.24 \times 24$$

$$Q = 47 \times 0.5184 = 24.36 \text{ m}^3\text{/h}$$

$$W = Q \times \gamma = 24.36 \times 0.80 = 19.49 \text{ t/h}$$

**Part (ii) — Drive shaft power and motor power:**

$$P = \frac{W \times L \times F_d}{367} = \frac{19.49 \times 20 \times 1.2}{367} = \frac{467.76}{367} = 1.275 \text{ kW}$$

$$P_m = \frac{P}{\eta} = \frac{1.275}{0.85} = 1.50 \text{ kW}$$

Standard motor: **1.5 kW (2 HP)** at 1440 rpm with reduction gearbox (18:1 to give ~80 rpm).

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

| Aspect | Screw Conveyor | Belt Conveyor |
|---|---|---|
| Cost | Lower initial cost | Higher initial cost |
| Footprint | Compact | Requires more space |
| Capacity | 5 – 150 t/h (typical) | Up to 1000+ t/h |
| Material suitability | Powders, granules, sticky foods | Mostly dry, free-flowing, lumpy |
| Inclination | Up to ~40° efficiently | Up to ~20° (with cleats, more) |
| Enclosure | Easily enclosed; hygienic | Open or enclosed (less common) |
| Maintenance | Simple, low cost | Belt replacement costly |
| Product damage | Higher (crushing risk) | Lower (gentle) |
| Cleaning | Excellent (CIP-friendly) | Difficult (porous belt) |
| Length | Up to ~30 m practical | Up to several hundred metres |
| Power consumption | Higher per ton | Lower per ton for long distances |

**Conclusion:** For **food plants handling powders and small granules over short distances (≤ 30 m)** with strict hygiene requirements, screw conveyors are preferred. For **long-distance, high-capacity, gentle handling of fragile food items** (e.g., snack pellets, fruits), belt conveyors are better.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — Using W in kg/h instead of t/h in the power formula is the #1 reason for wrong answers. The 367 constant requires **t/h**.
>
> 2. **Not checking $n \le n_{max}$** — Students often pick arbitrary speeds (like 100 rpm for a 400 mm screw) without verifying the empirical speed limit. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
>
> 3. **Confusing pitch s with diameter D** — Standard pitch is *equal* to diameter, but students sometimes use s = D/2 or s = 2D. Always **state your assumed pitch explicitly.**
>
> 4. **Using actual delivered W in power formula** — Power should be calculated using the *required* design W, not the actual W that the screw happens to deliver.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to divide by efficiency** — Motor power must be *higher* than shaft power. Skipping the η division loses 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Not stating assumptions for Φ and Fd** — These must be justified or quoted from a table.
>
> 7. **Ignoring inclination correction** — If the conveyor is inclined at θ°, capacity must be multiplied by factor C. Students often forget this entirely.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Screw Conveyor (Module 3, PCFTT601)**
>
> - [ ] A screw conveyor uses a **rotating helical flight inside a trough** to move bulk food materials.
> - [ ] Capacity formula: **$Q = 47 \times D^2 \times s \times n \times \Phi$** (in m³/h).
> - [ ] Mass flow: $W = Q \times \gamma$ (in t/h).
> - [ ] Power formula: **$P = W \times L \times F_d / 367$** (kW) — with W in t/h, L in m.
> - [ ] Motor power: $P_m = P / \eta$ where η ≈ 0.85–0.90.
> - [ ] Empirical max speed: **$n_{max} = 42 / \sqrt{D}$** (D in metres).
> - [ ] Filling factor Φ: typically **0.30** for horizontal; reduce for inclined.
> - [ ] Inclination correction: multiply Q by factor C (0.65 at 40° to 1.00 at 0°).
> - [ ] Standard pitch s = D is the most common configuration.
> - [ ] For food-grade conveyors: **SS 304 / SS 316**, surface finish **Ra ≤ 0.8 μm**, **CIP-compatible**, **hygienic bearings**.
> - [ ] Flight types: standard, short, long, ribbon, paddle, cut & folded.
> - [ ] Limitations: not suitable for **fragile foods** (chips, breakfast cereals) due to breakage; max practical length ~ 30 m.
> - [ ] Material factors (Fd): wheat 1.5, flour 1.0, sugar 1.0, salt 1.6, cocoa 1.4.
> - [ ] Always **state assumptions** for Φ, Fd, η, and s in your solution.
> - [ ] Final design step is to **select a standard motor size** (next size up from calculated kW).
> - [ ] Power transmission path: **Motor → Gear Reducer → Coupling → Drive Shaft → Screw Flight**.
> - [ ] Trough shapes: **U-trough (open)** for easy cleaning; **pipe-enclosed** for dust control.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chain Conveyor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chain Conveyor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Chain Conveyor** is a continuous material-handling device in which one or more endless chains run over terminal sprockets (or drums) and carry **flights, scrapers, buckets, or slats** attached at regular intervals to transport bulk granular, lumpy, or packaged food materials horizontally, inclined, or vertically between processing stations.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCFTT601, Module 3)**, the chain conveyor is treated as a **positive-displacement conveying system** — meaning the material is *pushed/pulled* by the chain-attached elements rather than dragged by gravity or air. This makes it ideal for handling **abrasive, hot, oily, or sticky food materials** such as grain, sugar, flour, oilseeds, bagasse, and animal feed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Chain conveyors are studied as a sub-class of *Mechanical Conveyors* with focus on **capacity estimation, power calculation, chain selection, and flight/sprocket design** for food plant layouts.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pedal-to-Paddle Machine"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a bicycle chain… but instead of pedaling the wheel, the chain is fitted with small metal paddles (flights) that scoop up grain and drag it along a trough.**

Imagine a pair of rotating sprockets connected by an endless loop (the chain). Bolted perpendicular to this loop are flat metal plates — the **flights**. As the chain turns, the flights move through a stationary **U-trough**, scooping material in front of them and dragging it forward — exactly like a **cookie dough scoop moving through a tray**, or a **snowplow pushing snow off a road**. The trough contains the material; the flights do the work.

This is why chain conveyors are sometimes called **"En-Masse Conveyors"** (French: *in mass*) — because the material is conveyed as a *full, compact mass* within the trough, not in a thin stream.

### 1.3 Classification of Chain Conveyors

| Type | Carrying Element | Typical Food Industry Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Scraper (Drag) Conveyor** | Flat/paddle flights between two chains | Grain, sugar, flour, bagasse |
| **En-Masse Conveyor** | Skirted flights (close-fitting) | Fine powders, meals, dusty materials |
| **Apron Conveyor** | Hinged overlapping plates (no trough) | Cans, bottles, packaged goods |
| **Slat Conveyor** | Single/double strands with flat slats | Bottles, trays, cartons |
| **Bucket Chain (Elevator)** | Buckets bolted to chain | Vertical lifting of grains, seeds |

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Bulk density of common food materials:** Wheat ≈ **770 kg/m³**, Rice ≈ **750 kg/m³**, Sugar (granular) ≈ **800 kg/m³**, Flour ≈ **600 kg/m³**.
- **Standard chain speeds (v):** **0.25 to 0.6 m/s** for food-grade materials.
- **Standard chain pitches:** **63 mm, 80 mm, 100 mm, 125 mm, 160 mm, 200 mm** (ISO 1977).
- **Drive sprocket speeds:** Typically **15 to 60 rpm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The working of a chain conveyor can be broken down into **five sequential physical actions**:

1. **Power Transmission:** An electric motor drives a reduction gearbox → output shaft carries the **head (drive) sprocket**.
2. **Chain Engagement:** The drive sprocket teeth mesh with chain rollers, converting rotary motion into linear motion of the chain.
3. **Material Engagement:** Flights attached to the chain enter the **loading zone** (boot/trough head) and scoop bulk material.
4. **Conveyance:** The material is dragged horizontally (or pushed up an incline) inside the enclosed trough.
5. **Discharge:** At the **head end**, flights clear the trough and material is thrown forward by centrifugal action onto a chute or next equipment.

### 2.2 Key Design Variables

The "Why and How" behind each design variable:

- **Chain velocity (v):** Governs capacity. Limited to **0.6 m/s** for fragile/abrasive food products to minimize degradation.
- **Flight pitch (s):** Distance between two consecutive flights. Smaller pitch → higher capacity, higher chain load.
- **Flight width (W):** Equals trough width. Wider troughs handle more material but require stronger chains.
- **Trough cross-section (A):** Usually rectangular; cross-sectional area of material bed ≈ **0.85 × W × h** (h = depth of fill).
- **Sprocket pitch diameter (D):** Determines chain velocity for a given rpm.
- **Material bulk density (ρ):** Critical input — varies with moisture content.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chain velocity | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ | m/s | $D$ = pitch dia (m), $N$ = sprocket rpm |
| 2 | Volumetric capacity | $Q_v = 3600 \cdot \dfrac{W \cdot h \cdot v}{1}$ | m³/h | $h$ = depth of fill (m) |
| 3 | Mass flow rate | $Q = 3600 \cdot W \cdot h \cdot v \cdot \rho$ | kg/h | $\rho$ = bulk density (kg/m³) |
| 4 | Capacity (flight-based) | $Q = 3600 \cdot \dfrac{W \cdot v \cdot \rho}{s}$ | kg/h | $s$ = flight pitch (m) |
| 5 | Horizontal power (friction) | $P_h = \dfrac{f \cdot W_{load} \cdot L \cdot v}{1000}$ | kW | $f$ = friction coeff. (0.5–0.8) |
| 6 | Vertical lift power | $P_v = \dfrac{Q \cdot H}{3600 \cdot 1000}$ | kW | $H$ = lift height (m) |
| 7 | Total shaft power | $P = \dfrac{P_h + P_v}{\eta}$ | kW | $\eta$ = drive efficiency (0.7–0.85) |
| 8 | Chain breaking load (design) | $F_{design} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{FoS}$ | N | $FoS$ = Factor of Safety (8–10 for chains) |
| 9 | Maximum chain tension | $F_{max} = F_{effective} + F_{lift}$ | N | Sum of all tension contributions |
| 10 | Sprocket teeth (minimum) | $N_t = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D}{p}$ | — | $p$ = chain pitch (m) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember:** In KTU problems, the *flight-based capacity formula* (Eq. 4) is the most-tested one. Use $W$ in metres, $v$ in m/s, $\rho$ in kg/m³, and $s$ in metres.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Chain conveyors are the **workhorse of food processing plants** because they:

- Handle **hot materials** (up to 200°C) like cooked mash, sugar slurry solids, oilseed meal post-expulsion.
- Operate in **inclined orientations** (up to 45° with skirts) — replacing the need for separate elevators.
- Are **enclosed and dust-tight**, satisfying **FSSAI hygiene norms** for flour, spices, milk powder.
- Allow **intermediate discharge** through bottom gates along the trough length.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Chain Velocity from Sprocket Geometry

The chain moves at the same tangential speed as the sprocket pitch circle (no slip, positive engagement).

**Step 1 — Circumference of sprocket pitch circle:**
The pitch circle is the imaginary circle on which the chain rollers roll. Its circumference equals the distance the chain travels per revolution.

$$C = \pi \cdot D$$

where $D$ is the pitch diameter in metres.

**Step 2 — Distance covered per minute:**
If the sprocket rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute (rpm), the chain covers $\pi \cdot D$ metres per revolution, hence:

$$\text{Distance per minute} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \quad \text{(m/min)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to distance per second (chain velocity):**

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{(m/s)}$$

This is the fundamental kinematic relation. For $D = 0.4$ m and $N = 30$ rpm:

$$v = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.4 \cdot 30}{60} = \frac{37.70}{60} = 0.628 \text{ m/s}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mass Flow Rate (Capacity)

**Step 1 — Volume of material carried per flight:**
Each flight scoops a *slice* of material having cross-section $A$ and length equal to one flight pitch $s$ (the distance the chain advances between successive flights).

$$V_{per\ flight} = A \cdot s = (W \cdot h) \cdot s$$

**Step 2 — Number of flights passing a section per second:**
If the chain moves at velocity $v$, then in one second, a length $v$ metres of chain passes a point. The number of flights in this length is:

$$n_{flights} = \frac{v}{s}$$

**Step 3 — Volume discharged per second:**

$$V_{per\ sec} = n_{flights} \cdot V_{per\ flight} = \frac{v}{s} \cdot W \cdot h \cdot s = W \cdot h \cdot v$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by bulk density to get mass per second:**

$$Q_{sec} = W \cdot h \cdot v \cdot \rho \quad \text{(kg/s)}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kg/h (multiply by 3600):**

$$Q = 3600 \cdot W \cdot h \cdot v \cdot \rho \quad \text{(kg/h)}$$

**Step 6 — Alternative form (eliminating $h$):**
If the trough is filled to the brim of the flights (depth $h = $ flight height), we may write $W \cdot h = W \cdot (\text{constant})$, leading to the *flight-pitch form*:

$$Q = 3600 \cdot \frac{W \cdot v \cdot \rho}{s} \quad \text{(kg/h)}$$

provided $h = 1/s$ in normalised units. The KTU-recommended form for exam problems is:

$$\boxed{\;Q = 3600 \cdot \frac{W \cdot v \cdot \rho}{s}\; \text{ kg/h}\;}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Required at the Drive Shaft

The conveyor must overcome two resistances:

**A) Frictional resistance (horizontal motion):**
The total weight on the chain is the weight of the chain itself plus the weight of the material being conveyed.

$$W_{load} = (m_{chain} + m_{material}) \cdot g$$

The frictional force between flights and trough, plus rolling resistance of the chain on the trough bottom, gives an effective coefficient $f$ (typically 0.5 to 0.8 for steel-on-steel, lubricated):

$$F_{friction} = f \cdot W_{load} \quad \text{(N)}$$

Power to overcome friction:

$$P_{friction} = F_{friction} \cdot v = f \cdot W_{load} \cdot v \quad \text{(W)}$$

**B) Lift power (inclined/vertical motion):**
If the conveyor lifts the material through a height $H$ at mass rate $Q$ kg/s:

$$P_{lift} = Q \cdot g \cdot H \quad \text{(W)}$$

**C) Total power at the drive shaft:**

$$\boxed{\;P_{shaft} = \frac{f \cdot W_{load} \cdot v \cdot L + Q \cdot g \cdot H}{1000 \cdot \eta}\; \text{ kW}\;}$$

where $L$ = horizontal length, $H$ = vertical lift, and $\eta$ = overall drive efficiency (gearbox + bearings ≈ **0.7 to 0.85**).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A chain conveyor is required to transport **wheat** ($\rho = 770$ kg/m³) at a rate of **40 tonnes/h** over a horizontal distance of **30 m**. Flight width $W = 0.4$ m, flight pitch $s = 0.2$ m, chain speed $v = 0.5$ m/s, and overall efficiency $\eta = 0.8$. Calculate: (a) actual capacity of the conveyor, and (b) the drive power if the friction coefficient $f = 0.6$ and total moving load is 8 kN.

**Solution — Part (a):**

$$Q = 3600 \cdot \frac{W \cdot v \cdot \rho}{s} = 3600 \cdot \frac{0.4 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 770}{0.2}$$

$$Q = 3600 \cdot \frac{154}{0.2} = 3600 \cdot 770 = 2{,}772{,}000 \text{ kg/h} = 2772 \text{ tonnes/h}$$

**Interpretation:** The designed capacity (2772 t/h) far exceeds the required 40 t/h — the conveyor is **massively over-designed**. The student should re-check inputs; for a 40 t/h requirement, the chain speed should be much lower.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Plugging $W$ in **mm** instead of **m** into the capacity formula. Always convert to SI units *before* substitution.

**Solution — Part (b):**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{f \cdot W_{load} \cdot v \cdot L}{1000 \cdot \eta} = \frac{0.6 \cdot 8000 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 30}{1000 \cdot 0.8}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{72{,}000}{800} = 90 \text{ kW}$$

(Assuming horizontal, $H = 0$, hence no lift term.)

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Scraper Conveyor | En-Masse Conveyor | Bucket Elevator |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacity range | 10–500 t/h | 5–150 t/h | 5–200 t/h |
| Typical speed | 0.3–0.6 m/s | 0.25–0.4 m/s | 0.5–1.5 m/s |
| Inclination limit | 30° | 45° (skirted) | 90° (vertical) |
| Material degradation | Moderate | Low | Low |
| Best for | Coarse grain | Fine powder | Vertical lift |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Chain Conveyor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Motor AC 3 Phase] --> B[Gearbox Reducer]
    B --> C[Drive Shaft Coupling]
    C --> D[Head Sprocket Drive]
    D --> E[Endless Chain with Flights]
    E --> F[Trough U Section]
    G[Tail Sprocket Idler] --> E
    F --> H[Discharge Chute]
    F --> I[Intermediate Discharge Gates]
    J[Boot Section Hopper] --> F
    E --> K[Tail Section Take Up]
    L[Take Up Tensioner] --> K
    M[Material Infeed] --> J
```

### 4.2 Component-Level Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PowerTrain["Power Train"]
        P1[Electric Motor] --> P2[V Belt Drive]
        P2 --> P3[Helical Gearbox]
        P3 --> P4[Drive Coupling]
    end
    subgraph ConveyorHead["Conveyor Head Section"]
        H1[Head Sprocket Shaft] --> H2[Head Sprocket]
        H2 --> H3[Chain Engagement]
    end
    subgraph ConveyorBody["Conveyor Body"]
        B1[Trough Section 1] --> B2[Trough Section 2]
        B2 --> B3[Trough Section N]
    end
    subgraph ConveyorTail["Conveyor Tail Section"]
        T1[Tail Sprocket Idler] --> T2[Take Up Screws]
        T2 --> T3[Boot Hopper]
    end
    P4 --> H1
    H3 --> B1
    B3 --> T1
    T3 --> M1[Material Discharge]
```

### 4.3 Load & Tension Distribution Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drive Sprocket Max Tension F1] --> B[Carrying Strand]
    B --> C[Tail Sprocket F2]
    C --> D[Return Strand]
    D --> A
    E[F1 Max at Drive] --> F[F2 Min at Tail]
    G[Material Load on Carrying Side] --> B
    H[Empty Return Side] --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> The maximum chain tension always occurs on the **carrying (loaded) side at the drive sprocket**. This is the critical point where chain breaking strength must be evaluated.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a chain conveyor. List any four applications of chain conveyors in food processing industries.**

**Model Answer:**
A chain conveyor is a positive-displacement material handling equipment that uses an endless chain with attached flights, buckets, or slats to transport bulk or packaged food materials along a defined path.

**Four applications:**
1. Horizontal transport of wheat, rice, and maize in rice/flour mills.
2. Inclined transport of oilseed meal from solvent extraction units.
3. Vertical lifting of grains and pulses using bucket chain elevators.
4. En-masse conveying of sticky/semi-dry materials like jaggery, khandsari, and animal feed mash.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is meant by 'en-masse' conveying? How does it differ from a scraper chain conveyor?**

**Model Answer:**
**En-masse conveying** is a chain conveying technique in which the material is transported as a complete, compact mass within a closely-fitted U-trough, with the flights (skirts) almost touching the trough walls and bottom. The material fills the entire cross-section between successive flights, behaving like a moving "plug".

**Differences from Scraper Conveyor:**

| Feature | En-Masse Conveyor | Scraper Conveyor |
|---|---|---|
| Flight fit | Close fit (skirts on sides) | Loose fit, open flights |
| Material state | Compacted mass | Loose dragged layer |
| Trough cross-section | Fully filled | Partially filled |
| Inclination capability | Up to 45° | Limited to ~15–20° |
| Material degradation | Minimal | Moderate |
| Power per tonne | Lower | Higher |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a flight chain conveyor** used in a flour mill. List its main components. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A flight chain conveyor is used to transport **sugar (bulk density 800 kg/m³)** at a rate of **60 tonnes/h** over a length of **25 m**. The trough width is **0.5 m**, flight pitch is **0.25 m**, and chain velocity is **0.4 m/s**. Calculate: (i) the **mass flow rate** the conveyor is actually capable of, and (ii) the **shaft power** required, assuming $f = 0.55$, total moving load = 10 kN, and $\eta = 0.8$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working:**

A flight chain conveyor consists of the following components:

1. **Endless chain** (single or double strand) — runs over head and tail sprockets.
2. **Flights** — flat metal plates bolted to chain links at regular pitch.
3. **Head (drive) sprocket** — driven by motor-gearbox, provides pulling force.
4. **Tail (idler) sprocket** — supports return strand; mounted on take-up frame.
5. **Trough** — U-shaped or rectangular casing, often sectional.
6. **Take-up assembly** — maintains chain tension (screw or spring type).
7. **Drive unit** — motor + gearbox + coupling.
8. **Loading boot** — feed inlet at the tail.
9. **Discharge spout** — at the head end.

**Working:** Material is fed into the boot section. As the chain with flights moves forward, the flights scoop and drag the material through the trough. At the head sprocket, the flights clear the trough and centrifugal force discharges the material into a chute.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Actual mass flow rate:**

$$Q = 3600 \cdot \frac{W \cdot v \cdot \rho}{s} = 3600 \cdot \frac{0.5 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 800}{0.25}$$

$$Q = 3600 \cdot \frac{160}{0.25} = 3600 \cdot 640 = 2{,}304{,}000 \text{ kg/h} = 2304 \text{ t/h}$$

The conveyor's *actual* capacity (2304 t/h) is much higher than the required 60 t/h — indicating the design is conservatively rated.

**(ii) Shaft power (horizontal, so H = 0):**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{f \cdot W_{load} \cdot v \cdot L}{1000 \cdot \eta} = \frac{0.55 \cdot 10000 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 25}{1000 \cdot 0.8}$$

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{55000}{800} = 68.75 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer:** Actual capacity = **2304 t/h**; Shaft power = **68.75 kW**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the **factors affecting the selection of a chain conveyor** for a given food processing application. Discuss the role of **chain speed, flight pitch, and material bulk density**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A chain conveyor with an **inclination of 20°** transports **rice ($\rho = 750$ kg/m³)** at **30 t/h**. The horizontal length is **20 m**, vertical lift is **7.28 m**, chain velocity is **0.35 m/s**, trough width is **0.4 m**, and flight pitch is **0.2 m**. The friction coefficient $f = 0.6$, total moving load = 6 kN, and $\eta = 0.75$. Calculate the **shaft power required**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Selection Factors:**

The selection of a chain conveyor depends on:

1. **Material characteristics:** Bulk density, moisture, abrasiveness, particle size, stickiness, temperature.
2. **Chain speed (v):** Affects capacity and product damage. For fragile food (flakes, pellets), $v$ is limited to 0.3 m/s; for grains, up to 0.6 m/s.
3. **Flight pitch (s):** Smaller pitch → higher capacity but higher chain pull and power.
4. **Capacity (Q):** Determined by required throughput in t/h.
5. **Distance and lift:** Horizontal length + vertical lift determine power.
6. **Trough/flight material:** SS-304 for food-grade, mild steel for non-contact.
7. **Hygiene and cleaning:** Removable trough sections, drainable bottoms, FDA-approved food-grade lubricants.
8. **Inclination angle:** En-masse up to 45°, scraper up to 20°.
9. **Drive and controls:** VFD for variable speed, overload protection.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Verify capacity:**

$$Q_{actual} = 3600 \cdot \frac{W \cdot v \cdot \rho}{s} = 3600 \cdot \frac{0.4 \cdot 0.35 \cdot 750}{0.2}$$

$$Q_{actual} = 3600 \cdot 525 = 1{,}890{,}000 \text{ kg/h} = 1890 \text{ t/h}$$

The conveyor can deliver up to 1890 t/h, far exceeding the required 30 t/h. This is acceptable (over-design margin).

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate (kg/s) for lift power calculation:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{30{,}000}{3600} = 8.33 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Lift power:**

$$P_{lift} = \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot H = 8.33 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 7.28 = 595.0 \text{ W} = 0.595 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Friction power (horizontal length L = 20 m):**

$$P_{friction} = \frac{f \cdot W_{load} \cdot v \cdot L}{1000} = \frac{0.6 \cdot 6000 \cdot 0.35 \cdot 20}{1000} = \frac{25{,}200}{1000} = 25.2 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Total shaft power:**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{friction} + P_{lift}}{\eta} = \frac{25.2 + 0.595}{0.75} = \frac{25.795}{0.75} = 34.39 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer:** Shaft power required = **34.39 kW** (≈ 35 kW motor rating).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting $g = 9.81$ m/s²** in the lift power term. Some students use $H$ alone without gravity.
> 2. **Mixing up $W_{load}$ (moving weight in N) with $W$ (trough width in m).** These are *two different W's* — use different symbols in your answer sheet.
> 3. **Not converting rpm to m/s** when given sprocket speed directly.
> 4. **Neglecting efficiency $\eta$** — KTU always awards marks for the $\eta$ term.
> 5. **Using horizontal length $L$ when inclined length is needed**, and vice versa. Read the problem carefully.
> 6. **Wrong unit conversion:** $W$ in mm gives capacity 1000× too large.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Chain conveyor = positive-displacement bulk conveyor** using flights, scrapers, or buckets on an endless chain.
- **Capacity formula (most tested):** $Q = 3600 \cdot \dfrac{W \cdot v \cdot \rho}{s}$ kg/h.
- **Chain velocity:** $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ m/s.
- **Total shaft power:** $P = \dfrac{f \cdot W_{load} \cdot v \cdot L + \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot H}{1000 \cdot \eta}$ kW.
- **Types to remember:** Scraper, En-masse, Apron, Slat, Bucket (elevator).
- **Design Factor of Safety for chains:** typically **8 to 10** (chains wear out — high FoS is mandatory).
- **Standard food industry speeds:** 0.25–0.6 m/s (slower = less product damage).
- **Maximum inclination:** Scraper ≤ 20°, En-masse ≤ 45°, Bucket elevator = 90°.
- **Always convert** all dimensions to **SI units (m, kg, s)** before substitution.
- **Always include efficiency** $\eta$ (0.7–0.85) when computing shaft power.
- **Critical design point:** Maximum chain tension occurs on the **loaded strand at the drive sprocket**.
- **Hygiene note:** Use **SS-304 troughs**, food-grade lubricants, and **removable covers** for CIP (Clean-in-Place) compatibility in food plants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pneumatic Conveyor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pneumatic Conveyor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pneumatic Conveying** is a bulk solids transportation technique in which particulate food materials (powders, granules, flakes, or pellets) are suspended or pushed through an enclosed pipeline using a **gaseous carrier medium** (typically air or an inert gas) generated by a positive-pressure blower, fan, compressor, or a negative-pressure vacuum source.

In the context of food processing equipment design (KTU PCFTT601), pneumatic conveyors are classified as **continuous solids-handling systems** used for transporting dry, free-flowing, low-to-moderate bulk-density food products such as **flour, sugar, milk powder, spices, salt, grain, rice, and starch**.

> [!NOTE]
> The system relies on the principle of **fluidization**, where a controlled airstream provides sufficient drag and lift to overcome gravitational settling and inter-particle friction, transforming bulk solids into a flowing suspension.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible River of Air"

Imagine blowing through a **straw into a pile of chalk dust**. The dust *puffs up and floats along* with your exhaled breath. That, in essence, is pneumatic conveying.

- **You** → The blower / compressor (energy source).
- **Your breath** → The conveying air (carrier medium).
- **The chalk dust** → Food powder / grain (bulk solid).
- **The straw** → The pipeline (conveying duct).
- **Direction of breath** → Pressure gradient (positive or vacuum).

If you blow *gently* (low velocity), the dust settles and clogs. If you blow *too hard*, you waste energy and damage particles. There exists a **sweet spot — the pickup velocity** — at which the food particles are suspended and transported efficiently without degradation.

## 1.3 Classification of Pneumatic Conveyors

Pneumatic conveyors are classified along **two primary axes** as mandated by KTU 2024 Module 3 design content:

### (A) By Phase Density (Solids-to-Air Ratio)

| Mode | Solids Loading Ratio (μ) | Air Velocity (m/s) | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dilute Phase** | 1 – 15 | 15 – 25 | Most common for food powders; gentle handling |
| **Dense Phase** | 30 – 400+ | 5 – 12 | High product quality; minimum breakage |

### (B) By Pressure Mode

1. **Positive Pressure System** — Blower pushes material downstream; used for single-source-to-multi-destination layouts.
2. **Negative Pressure / Vacuum System** — Suction pulls material; ideal for multi-source-to-single-destination (e.g., ship unloading to silos).
3. **Combined Pressure-Vacuum System** — Hybrid system for complex plant layouts.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values for Food Pneumatic Conveying Design (at 20 °C, 1 atm):**
> - Air density (ρₐ) = **1.20 kg/m³**
> - Dynamic viscosity of air (μₐ) = **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s**
> - Kinematic viscosity (ν) = **1.51 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**
> - Acceleration due to gravity (g) = **9.81 m/s²**
> - Typical conveying velocity for food powders = **18 – 22 m/s**
> - Pickup velocity ≈ **1.5 × Terminal velocity** of the particle

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Why Pneumatic Conveying Works

For a food particle to remain **suspended** in a horizontal airstream, the following force balance must be satisfied at the incipient conveying condition:

1. **Drag Force (F_D)** generated by the relative velocity between air and particle must overcome the **Gravitational Force (F_G)** component.
2. The air velocity must exceed the **Terminal Velocity (v_t)** of the particle — otherwise, particles drop out of suspension (saltation).
3. The system must maintain a **Pressure Gradient (ΔP/L)** sufficient to overcome wall friction, bend losses, and acceleration losses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Functional Zones** must be designed into every pneumatic conveyor:
> - **Zone 1 — Initiation Zone:** Particle pickup and acceleration (highest local velocity).
> - **Zone 2 — Conveying Zone:** Steady-state suspension flow (constant velocity).
> - **Zone 3 — Termination Zone:** Particle deceleration and separation (lowest local velocity).

## 2.2 Core Theoretical Components

### A. Particle Dynamics — Terminal Velocity

The terminal velocity is the steady-state falling velocity of a particle under gravity in still air. It is **the single most important design parameter** in pneumatic conveying.

For a spherical particle in a vertical airstream, force balance at terminal velocity:

$$F_D = F_G$$

$$\tfrac{1}{2} C_D \, \rho_a \, A_p \, v_t^{2} = m_p \, g$$

where:
- $C_D$ = Drag coefficient (dimensionless)
- $\rho_a$ = Air density (kg/m³)
- $A_p$ = Projected area of particle = $\pi d_p^{2}/4$ (m²)
- $v_t$ = Terminal velocity (m/s)
- $m_p$ = Particle mass = $\pi d_p^{3} \rho_p / 6$ (kg)
- $g$ = Gravitational acceleration = 9.81 m/s²

### B. Drag Coefficient Regimes

The drag coefficient $C_D$ depends on the **Reynolds number (Re_p)** of the particle:

$$Re_p = \frac{\rho_a \, v_t \, d_p}{\mu_a}$$

| Regime | Reynolds Number Range | $C_D$ Expression |
|---|---|---|
| **Stokes (Laminar)** | $Re_p < 0.2$ | $C_D = 24 / Re_p$ |
| **Intermediate (Transition)** | $0.2 < Re_p < 1000$ | $C_D = 18.5 / Re_p^{0.6}$ |
| **Newton (Turbulent)** | $Re_p > 1000$ | $C_D \approx 0.44$ |

### C. Conveying Air Flow Rate

The volumetric flow rate of conveying air is determined by the **solid loading ratio (μ)**:

$$\mu = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{\dot{m}_a}$$

Rearranging:

$$Q_a = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{\mu \, \rho_a}$$

where $\dot{m}_s$ = mass flow rate of solid (kg/s), $\dot{m}_a$ = mass flow rate of air (kg/s), $Q_a$ = volumetric air flow (m³/s).

### D. Total System Pressure Drop

The total pressure drop across a pneumatic conveyor is the **sum of all line losses**:

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_{straight} + \Delta P_{acceleration} + \Delta P_{bends} + \Delta P_{lift} + \Delta P_{feeder/discharger}$$

For dilute-phase conveying, a useful empirical shortcut (Zenz / Klinzing):

$$\frac{\Delta P_{total}}{L} \propto \mu \cdot \rho_a \cdot v_a^{2} \cdot f_f$$

where $f_f$ is a dimensionless friction factor and $L$ is the equivalent pipe length.

### E. Power Consumption

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{Q_a \cdot \Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{blower}}$$

where $\eta_{blower}$ is the mechanical efficiency of the air mover (typically **0.65 – 0.85**).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solids loading ratio | $\mu = \dot{m}_s / \dot{m}_a$ | dimensionless | Defines conveying mode |
| 2 | Volumetric air flow | $Q_a = \dot{m}_s / (\mu \cdot \rho_a)$ | m³/s | Blower sizing |
| 3 | Particle Reynolds number | $Re_p = \rho_a v_t d_p / \mu_a$ | dimensionless | Drag regime selection |
| 4 | Terminal velocity (Stokes) | $v_t = g \, d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_a) / (18 \mu_a)$ | m/s | Dilute-phase design |
| 5 | Terminal velocity (Newton) | $v_t = \sqrt{4 g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_a) / (3 C_D \rho_a)}$ | m/s | Coarse-particle design |
| 6 | Pickup velocity | $v_{pickup} \approx 1.5 \, v_t$ | m/s | Minimum design velocity |
| 7 | Conveying velocity | $v_a = 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \, v_t$ | m/s | Pipeline design |
| 8 | Blower power | $P = Q_a \, \Delta P_{total} / \eta$ | W | Energy audit |
| 9 | Pipe diameter (empirical) | $D = \sqrt{Q_a / (v_a \cdot \pi/4)}$ | m | Pipe sizing |
| 10 | Specific power consumption | $E_s = P / \dot{m}_s$ | kJ/kg | Energy benchmark |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - In a typical **flour mill plant** (Kerala small-scale food industry), specific power consumption of 8 – 12 kJ/kg is acceptable.
> - Above **15 kJ/kg**, the design is flagged as energy-inefficient in the plant layout economics module.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Terminal Velocity of a Spherical Food Particle (Stokes Regime)

> **Objective:** Derive the terminal velocity of a small food particle (e.g., fine milk powder, $d_p < 100 \, \mu m$) moving in still air, where viscous forces dominate.

### Step 1 — Write the Force Balance

At terminal (steady) velocity, the net force on the particle is zero. The drag force equals the net gravitational force (gravity minus buoyancy):

$$F_D = F_{G,net}$$

### Step 2 — Expand Each Force Term

The drag force for a sphere:

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} C_D \, \rho_a \, A_p \, v_t^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_D \, \rho_a \left(\tfrac{\pi d_p^{2}}{4}\right) v_t^{2}$$

The net gravitational (effective weight) force:

$$F_{G,net} = \tfrac{\pi d_p^{3}}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_a) \, g$$

where $\rho_p$ is particle density and $\rho_a$ is air density.

### Step 3 — Substitute Stokes Drag Coefficient

In the **Stokes regime**, $C_D = 24 / Re_p$. First, express Re_p in terms of $v_t$:

$$Re_p = \frac{\rho_a \, v_t \, d_p}{\mu_a}$$

So:

$$C_D = \frac{24 \, \mu_a}{\rho_a \, v_t \, d_p}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $C_D$ into the Force Balance

$$\tfrac{1}{2} \left(\frac{24 \, \mu_a}{\rho_a \, v_t \, d_p}\right) \rho_a \left(\tfrac{\pi d_p^{2}}{4}\right) v_t^{2} = \tfrac{\pi d_p^{3}}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_a) \, g$$

### Step 5 — Simplify the Left-Hand Side

The $\rho_a$ cancels. The $v_t$ cancels one power. Grouping:

$$\tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 24 \cdot \mu_a \cdot \tfrac{\pi d_p^{2}}{4} \cdot \frac{v_t}{1} = \tfrac{\pi d_p^{3}}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_a) \, g$$

$$3 \pi \mu_a d_p \, v_t = \tfrac{\pi d_p^{3}}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_a) \, g$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $v_t$

Divide both sides by $\pi d_p$:

$$3 \mu_a \, v_t = \tfrac{d_p^{2}}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_a) \, g$$

$$\boxed{\,v_t = \frac{g \, d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_a)}{18 \, \mu_a}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Stokes Terminal Velocity Equation** — a high-yield KTU formula. Note that for dilute-phase conveying of fine food powders, $v_t$ scales with the **square of the particle diameter**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Pipeline Diameter from Volumetric Air Flow

> **Objective:** Derive the conveying pipe diameter given the air volumetric flow rate and the chosen conveying velocity.

### Step 1 — Continuity Equation for Air

The volumetric flow rate through a circular pipe of diameter $D$ at mean velocity $v_a$:

$$Q_a = v_a \cdot A_c = v_a \cdot \tfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $D$

$$D^{2} = \frac{4 Q_a}{\pi \, v_a}$$

$$\boxed{\,D = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q_a}{\pi \, v_a}}\,}$$

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A pneumatic conveyor must transport wheat flour at a mass flow rate of $\dot{m}_s = 2.0$ kg/s using a solid loading ratio of $\mu = 8$ (dilute phase). Air density is $\rho_a = 1.20$ kg/m³. The design conveying velocity is $v_a = 20$ m/s. Calculate the required pipe diameter.

**Solution:**

**Step 3.1** — Compute mass flow rate of air:

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{\mu} = \frac{2.0}{8} = 0.25 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 3.2** — Compute volumetric air flow rate:

$$Q_a = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a} = \frac{0.25}{1.20} = 0.2083 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

**Step 3.3** — Apply continuity to obtain pipe diameter:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.2083}{\pi \times 20}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.8333}{62.832}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{0.01326} = 0.1152 \text{ m} \approx 115 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Design Decision:** The next standard pipe size above 115 mm (typically DN125 or 5-inch NB) should be selected to ensure the conveying velocity remains above the pickup velocity for all particle sizes in the distribution.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Blower Power for a Pneumatic Conveyor

### Step 1 — General Power Equation

For an incompressible fluid (air at low pressure drop), the shaft power required by the blower:

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{Q_a \cdot \Delta P_{total}}{\eta_{blower}}$$

### Step 2 — Express in Terms of Solids Flow Rate

Substituting $Q_a = \dot{m}_s / (\mu \rho_a)$:

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{\dot{m}_s \cdot \Delta P_{total}}{\mu \, \rho_a \, \eta_{blower}}$$

### Step 3 — Define Specific Power Consumption (SPC)

This is the **energy audit metric** for the plant:

$$E_s = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\dot{m}_s} = \frac{\Delta P_{total}}{\mu \, \rho_a \, \eta_{blower}}$$

Units: J/kg of solid conveyed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Design Benchmark (Food Industry):**
> - **Dilute-phase flour conveying:** 8 – 12 kJ/kg
> - **Dilute-phase sugar conveying:** 6 – 10 kJ/kg
> - **Dense-phase fine powder:** 4 – 7 kJ/kg

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Food Material Suitability for Pneumatic Conveying

| Food Material | Particle Size | Bulk Density (kg/m³) | Conveying Mode | KTU Design Caution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wheat flour | 50 – 200 μm | 450 – 600 | Dilute phase | Hygroscopic; moisture < 14% mandatory |
| Milk powder | 30 – 150 μm | 500 – 650 | Dilute phase | Abrasive; SS316L pipe required |
| Refined sugar | 200 – 800 μm | 800 – 900 | Dilute / Dense | Hygroscopic; avoid humid air |
| Crushed spices | 500 – 2000 μm | 400 – 700 | Dilute phase | Abrasive; bend erosion risk |
| Salt (granular) | 300 – 1500 μm | 1100 – 1300 | Dilute phase | Corrosive; use coated steel |
| Paddy / rice | 6 – 8 mm | 550 – 650 | Dilute / Dense | High breakage; dense-phase preferred |
| Starch | 10 – 80 μm | 500 – 700 | Dilute phase | Explosive dust; ATEX blower required |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Positive-Pressure Dilute-Phase Pneumatic Conveyor

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **complete functional flow** of a typical food-industry pneumatic conveyor used in a Kerala small-scale spice or flour plant:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HOPPER_A["HOPPER A"] --> FEEDER["ROTARY VALVE FEEDER"]
    HOPPER_B["HOPPER B"] --> FEEDER
    FEEDER --> BLOWER_IN["BLOWER INLET"]
    FILTER_A["AIR FILTER UNIT"] --> BLOWER_IN
    BLOWER_IN --> BLOWER["POSITIVE DISPLACEMENT BLOWER"]
    BLOWER --> CONVEY_PIPE["CONVEYING PIPELINE DN125"]
    CONVEY_PIPE --> BEND1["90 DEGREE BEND R1.5D"]
    BEND1 --> CONVEY_PIPE2["STRAIGHT PIPELINE"]
    CONVEY_PIPE2 --> BEND2["45 DEGREE BEND"]
    BEND2 --> DISCH["DISCHARGE CYCLONE SEPARATOR"]
    DISCH --> BAGF["BAG FILTER DUST COLLECTOR"]
    DISCH --> SILO["RECEIVING SILO"]
    BAGF --> EXHAUST["CLEAN AIR EXHAUST TO ATMOSPHERE"]
    CONTROL["PLC CONTROL PANEL"] -.->|Monitors| BLOWER
    CONTROL -.->|Modulates| FEEDER
    CONTROL -.->|Triggers| DISCH
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Solid lines** = Material (powder + air) flow path.
- **Dotted lines** = Control signal paths (sensor feedback to PLC).
- The **Blower** provides the energy; the **Rotary Valve Feeder** meters solid mass flow; the **Cyclone + Bag Filter** separates product from air.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Vacuum (Suction) Pneumatic Conveyor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC1["SOURCE PICKUP POINT 1"] --> SUC_PIPE["SUCTION PIPELINE"]
    SRC2["SOURCE PICKUP POINT 2"] --> SUC_PIPE
    SRC3["SOURCE PICKUP POINT 3"] --> SUC_PIPE
    SUC_PIPE --> SEP_V["VACUUM CYCLONE SEPARATOR"]
    SEP_V --> ROT_V["ROTARY AIRLOCK VALVE"]
    ROT_V --> RCVR["RECEIVER HOPPER"]
    SEP_V --> BAG_V["BAG FILTER"]
    BAG_V --> VAC_PUMP["VACUUM PUMP EXHAUSTER"]
    VAC_PUMP --> SILENCER["MUFFLER SILENCER"]
    SILENCER --> EXH_V["EXHAUST AIR TO ATMOSPHERE"]
    PLC_V["PLC CONTROL UNIT"] -.->|VFD Control| VAC_PUMP
    PLC_V -.->|Feedback| SEP_V
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Difference:** The vacuum system pulls material from multiple pickup points to a **single central receiver**, whereas the pressure system pushes material from a **single source to multiple destinations**. The choice depends on plant layout geometry.

## 4.3 Conveying Mode Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Start: Material Selection"] --> Q1{"Is material fine powder less than 1 mm?"}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{"Is product quality critical low breakage required?"}
    Q1 -->|No| DENSE["Use DENSE PHASE low velocity conveying"]
    Q2 -->|Yes| DENSE2["Use DENSE PHASE plug flow conveying"]
    Q2 -->|No| DIL["Use DILUTE PHASE 18 to 22 m/s"]
    DENSE --> CALC["Compute air mass flow Qa"]
    DENSE2 --> CALC
    DIL --> CALC
    CALC --> DPIPE["Compute pipe diameter D"]
    DPIPE --> DELP["Compute total pressure drop DeltaP"]
    DELP --> POW["Compute blower power P"]
    POW --> OUT["Plant Layout Integration Done"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
> **Define pneumatic conveying. State any two advantages and two disadvantages of pneumatic conveyors used in food processing industries.**

**Model Answer:**

Pneumatic conveying is the transportation of dry bulk particulate food materials through an enclosed pipeline using air as the carrier medium under positive or negative pressure.

**Advantages:**
1. Fully enclosed system — no dust emission, hygienic, prevents contamination (critical for food-grade materials like milk powder and spices).
2. Layout flexibility — pipelines can route around obstacles, vertically, and horizontally; ideal for compact plant layouts.

**Disadvantages:**
1. High specific power consumption (8 – 15 kJ/kg) compared to mechanical conveyors (belt conveyors ~ 1 – 3 kJ/kg).
2. Particle degradation / breakage risk for fragile products (flakes, puffed cereals) due to high-velocity particle impacts at bends.

> **[Marks Distribution:** Definition 1M + Advantages 1M + Disadvantages 1M**]**

---

> **Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
> **Differentiate between dilute-phase and dense-phase pneumatic conveying. Mention the typical solids loading ratio (μ) for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Dilute Phase | Dense Phase |
|---|---|---|
| Solids loading ratio (μ) | 1 – 15 | 30 – 400+ |
| Air velocity (m/s) | 15 – 25 | 5 – 12 |
| Pressure requirement | Low – Moderate | High |
| Particle degradation | Higher | Minimal |
| Energy consumption | Higher | Lower (per kg) |
| Typical food use | Flour, sugar, milk powder | Fragile / quality-critical powders |

**Key distinction:** Dilute phase suspends particles individually (high velocity); dense phase moves particles as plugs, dunes, or slugs at low velocity with high solids-to-air mass ratio.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> Students commonly lose marks by: **(1)** forgetting to state assumptions explicitly; **(2)** mixing up the Stokes and Newton regimes in terminal velocity calculations; **(3)** using the *mass* flow ratio instead of the *volumetric* flow ratio; **(4)** not converting units consistently (e.g., using cm instead of m for particle diameter); **(5)** omitting the blower efficiency term in power calculations. Always write **assumptions → formula → substitution → numerical answer with units** to score full marks.

---

### Question B — Option A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main Paper]**
> **(a)** A dilute-phase pneumatic conveyor is to be designed to transport wheat flour at 3.0 kg/s. The solid loading ratio is to be $\mu = 10$, conveying velocity $v_a = 20$ m/s, air density $\rho_a = 1.20$ kg/m³, particle diameter $d_p = 150 \, \mu m$, particle density $\rho_p = 1450$ kg/m³, and air viscosity $\mu_a = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s. Calculate:
> (i) The volumetric air flow rate. (ii) The pipe diameter. (iii) The terminal velocity using Stokes equation. (iv) Verify whether conveying velocity exceeds pickup velocity. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram the components of a positive-pressure pneumatic conveying system used in a food processing plant. Discuss the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Option A

#### Part (a) — Numerical Design (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $\dot{m}_s = 3.0$ kg/s
- $\mu = 10$
- $v_a = 20$ m/s
- $\rho_a = 1.20$ kg/m³
- $d_p = 150 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\rho_p = 1450$ kg/m³
- $\mu_a = 1.81 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s

**(i) Volumetric Air Flow Rate $Q_a$ — (2 Marks)**

Mass flow rate of air:

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{\dot{m}_s}{\mu} = \frac{3.0}{10} = 0.30 \text{ kg/s}$$

Volumetric air flow rate:

$$Q_a = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a} = \frac{0.30}{1.20} = 0.25 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

**(ii) Pipe Diameter $D$ — (2 Marks)**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q_a}{\pi \, v_a}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.25}{\pi \times 20}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0}{62.832}}$$

$$D = \sqrt{0.01592} = 0.1262 \text{ m} \approx 126 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Terminal Velocity using Stokes Equation — (2 Marks)**

$$v_t = \frac{g \, d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_a)}{18 \, \mu_a}$$

$$v_t = \frac{9.81 \times (150 \times 10^{-6})^{2} \times (1450 - 1.20)}{18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5}}$$

Numerator:

$$9.81 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-8} \times 1448.8 = 3.197 \times 10^{-4}$$

Denominator:

$$18 \times 1.81 \times 10^{-5} = 3.258 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$v_t = \frac{3.197 \times 10^{-4}}{3.258 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.981 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iv) Pickup Velocity Verification — (1 Mark)**

$$v_{pickup} \approx 1.5 \, v_t = 1.5 \times 0.981 = 1.47 \text{ m/s}$$

Since $v_a = 20$ m/s $\gg$ 1.47 m/s, the conveying velocity is **far above** the pickup velocity. **Design is safe.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The selected pipe of standard size **DN150 (≈ 150 mm nominal)** should be chosen to provide a safety margin (slightly lower velocity, still safe) and accommodate the full particle size distribution.

---

#### Part (b) — Block Diagram & Components (7 Marks)

A positive-pressure pneumatic conveying system consists of the following functional blocks:

1. **Feed Hopper (1M)** — Storage of bulk food material; equipped with level sensors and vibratory discharger to ensure steady gravity feed.

2. **Rotary Valve Feeder (1.5M)** — An airlock device with rotating vanes that meters the solid into the conveying line while preventing air backflow. Rotational speed (RPM) directly controls the mass flow rate.

3. **Air Filter (0.5M)** — Pre-filters ambient intake air to remove dust and contaminants; critical for food-grade hygiene.

4. **Positive Displacement Blower (1.5M)** — The prime energy source; provides air at 0.5 – 1.0 bar gauge pressure. Common types: **Roots blower** (for high volume, low pressure) or **twin-screw compressor** (for dense-phase).

5. **Conveying Pipeline (1M)** — Mild steel or SS316 pipe (food-grade) of DN100 – DN200 with bolted flanges and bend radius $\geq 1.5 D$ to minimize erosion and pressure drop.

6. **Discharge Cyclone Separator (1M)** — Tangential inlet creates swirl; centrifugal force separates product from air. Product drops to receiver silo; air exits to filter.

7. **Bag Filter Dust Collector (0.5M)** — Final-stage air cleaning; protects environment and recovers fines. Pulse-jet cleaning system.

> **Marks Summary:** Diagram 2M + Functional description of each block 5M.

---

### Question B — Option B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Define terminal velocity and pickup velocity in pneumatic conveying. Derive the Stokes terminal velocity expression for a spherical food particle. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** A food plant uses a pneumatic conveyor to handle 5.0 kg/s of rice (bulk density 600 kg/m³, particle diameter 4 mm). The system operates at $\mu = 5$ with $v_a = 22$ m/s. Calculate (i) the air volumetric flow rate, (ii) the pipe diameter, and (iii) discuss whether the design is acceptable from a particle breakage standpoint. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Option B

#### Part (a) — Theory & Derivation (7 Marks)

**Definitions (2 Marks):**
- **Terminal Velocity ($v_t$):** The steady-state velocity at which a particle falling under gravity in still air experiences zero net force — drag equals net weight.
- **Pickup Velocity ($v_{pickup}$):** The minimum air velocity required to entrain and transport a stationary particle from rest. Empirically, $v_{pickup} \approx 1.5 \, v_t$ for horizontal conveying.

**Derivation (5 Marks):** Follows Section 3.1 of this document.

Step 1 — Force balance: $F_D = F_{G,net}$.
Step 2 — Expand: $\tfrac{1}{2} C_D \rho_a (\pi d_p^{2}/4) v_t^{2} = \tfrac{\pi d_p^{3}}{6} (\rho_p - \rho_a) g$.
Step 3 — Substitute Stokes $C_D = 24 \mu_a / (\rho_a v_t d_p)$.
Step 4 — Cancel and simplify.
Step 5 — Final boxed equation: $v_t = g d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_a) / (18 \mu_a)$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**(i) Air Volumetric Flow Rate (2 Marks):**

$$\dot{m}_a = \frac{5.0}{5} = 1.0 \text{ kg/s}, \quad Q_a = \frac{1.0}{1.20} = 0.833 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$$

**(ii) Pipe Diameter (2 Marks):**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.833}{\pi \times 22}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.333}{69.115}} = \sqrt{0.04823}$$

$$D = 0.2196 \text{ m} \approx 220 \text{ mm}$$

Select standard **DN250 (≈ 250 mm)** for safe operation.

**(iii) Particle Breakage Discussion (3 Marks):**

For **rice grains (4 mm)**, dilute-phase pneumatic conveying at 22 m/s presents significant risks:
- **Mechanical impact** at the rotary valve feeder and pipeline bends causes **grain breakage** and **dust generation**.
- **Recommendation:** For rice and similar granular food products, **dense-phase conveying** ($\mu = 50$ – 100, $v_a = 6$ – 10 m/s) is strongly preferred to minimize breakage and preserve product quality.
- **Alternate recommendation:** Use **belt or bucket conveyors** for whole-grain products, reserving pneumatic systems for broken rice, flour, or rice bran.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Trap:** Students often answer "the design is acceptable because pipe diameter is calculated." This is **incorrect** — at 22 m/s, rice breakage will be 8 – 15%, which is unacceptable in a food plant. Always cross-check particle sensitivity with the conveying mode selected.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Pneumatic Conveyor (Module 3)**

- ✅ Pneumatic conveying = transport of **bulk solids by air/gas** in an enclosed pipe.
- ✅ Two major classifications: **Dilute Phase** ($\mu = 1$ – 15, high velocity) and **Dense Phase** ($\mu = 30$ – 400+, low velocity).
- ✅ Two operating modes: **Positive pressure** (push) and **Negative pressure / Vacuum** (pull).
- ✅ **Pickup velocity** ≈ **1.5 × Terminal velocity** — minimum design criterion.
- ✅ **Stokes Terminal Velocity:** $v_t = g d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_a) / (18 \mu_a)$ — valid only when $Re_p < 0.2$.
- ✅ **Newton Terminal Velocity:** $v_t = \sqrt{4 g d_p (\rho_p - \rho_a) / (3 C_D \rho_a)}$ — valid when $Re_p > 1000$.
- ✅ **Solids Loading Ratio:** $\mu = \dot{m}_s / \dot{m}_a$ — dimensionless.
- ✅ **Pipe diameter** from continuity: $D = \sqrt{4 Q_a / (\pi v_a)}$.
- ✅ **Blower power:** $P = Q_a \, \Delta P_{total} / \eta_{blower}$; include efficiency (typically 0.65 – 0.85).
- ✅ **Specific energy benchmark** for food powders = **6 – 12 kJ/kg**.
- ✅ Standard pipeline material: **SS316L** (food-grade) or **MS with epoxy lining**.
- ✅ Critical components: **Hopper → Rotary Valve → Blower → Pipeline → Cyclone → Bag Filter → Silo**.
- ✅ For **hygroscopic food** (sugar, milk powder), moisture content must be **< 14%** to prevent pipe clogging.
- ✅ For **fine combustible dust** (starch, flour), **ATEX-certified** blower and grounding are mandatory.
- ✅ **Bend radius** must be **$\geq 1.5 D$** to minimize particle erosion and pressure drop.
- ✅ Vacuum systems for **multi-source → single destination**; pressure systems for **single source → multi-destination**.
- ✅ Always state **assumptions**, **units**, and **regime validation** ($Re_p$) in numerical answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Concepts of Plant Layout and Design with Special Reference to Food Process Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCFTT601 - DESIGN OF FOOD PROCESSING EQUIPMENT & PLANT LAYOUT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Concepts of Plant Layout and Design with Special Reference to Food Process Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Plant Layout** is the physical arrangement of industrial equipment, machinery, workstations, storage facilities, material handling systems, service utilities (steam, water, electricity, refrigeration), and personnel pathways within the four walls of a production facility, optimized to achieve maximum productivity, minimum product-handling cost, optimal material flow, and adherence to hygienic and safety standards prescribed for the specific industry.

In the context of **Food Process Industries**, plant layout transcends conventional industrial layout engineering because the "product" being handled is consumed by humans. Therefore, the layout must simultaneously satisfy:

1. **Process efficiency** (minimum distance, minimum delay)
2. **Microbiological safety** (HACCP compliance, hygienic zoning)
3. **Regulatory compliance** (FSSAI, BIS, USFDA, EU Regulations)
4. **Energy economy** (heat recovery, refrigeration integration)
5. **Worker ergonomics** and **operator safety**

### Definition — KTU Board-Ready Statement

> **Plant Layout (Definitive):** A pre-engineered, blueprint-level plan that establishes the spatial coordinates, operational sequence, and inter-connectivity of every physical asset in a manufacturing plant, with the objective of transforming raw materials into finished goods via the shortest, safest, cleanest, and most economical route.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **plant layout as the floor plan of a large commercial kitchen in a five-star hotel**.

In a kitchen, the chef does not place the refrigerator next to the dishwasher, nor the rice-cooker next to the salad-preparation counter. Each station is placed with a deliberate logic:

- **Cold storage (raw materials)** is closest to the **washing/prep station**.
- The **cooking station** is next to the **prep station**, because food moves in one direction: *raw → prep → cook → plate → serve*.
- The **dishwashing area** is isolated from the cooking area to avoid cross-contamination.
- The **cold room** for desserts is placed far from the hot ovens, to minimize energy waste.

A food processing plant works on **exactly the same logic**, only at industrial scale.

| Kitchen Analogy | Industrial Food Plant Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Refrigerator | Raw material cold storage / warehouse |
| Prep counter | Sorting, grading, washing line |
| Cooking range | Blancher / Pasteurizer / Retort |
| Plate-up station | Packing line |
| Serving hatch | Dispatch bay / cold chain dispatch |
| Exhaust hood | HVAC system with HEPA filtration |

The only difference is that in an industrial plant, the "chef" is replaced by **process engineers**, **CIP systems**, **PLC-controlled conveyors**, and **computerized batch tracking**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test whether the student understands that *food plant layout ≠ general manufacturing plant layout*. The non-negotiable differentiators are: **Hygienic Design, Sanitary Zoning, Cross-Contamination Prevention, and CIP Integration**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following industry-standard metrics are universally used in food plant layout engineering and must be memorized by every KTU 2024 Scheme student:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Floor load bearing capacity | $\sigma_{floor}$ | **7.5 to 15 kN/m²** (1500–3000 kgf/m²) | Heavy equipment / multi-floor |
| Ceiling height (food plants) | $H$ | **4.5 m to 6.0 m minimum** | To allow overhead piping & ventilation |
| Aisle width (forklift) | $w_{aisle}$ | **2.4 m minimum** | Two-way forklift traffic |
| Aisle width (personnel only) | $w_{aisle}$ | **0.9 m minimum** | Single-person movement |
| Floor slope for drainage | $\theta_{slope}$ | **1 in 60 to 1 in 100** | Wash-down floor drainage |
| Wall-to-floor coving radius | $R_{coving}$ | **25 mm to 50 mm** | Eliminates dirt-trap corners |
| Operating temperature (cold zone) | $T_{cold}$ | **4 °C ± 2 °C** | Chilled processing rooms |
| Operating temperature (frozen zone) | $T_{frozen}$ | **–18 °C ± 2 °C** | Frozen storage |
| Relative humidity (dry processing) | $RH$ | **45% to 60%** | To prevent condensation & microbial growth |
| Air changes per hour (ACH) | $n_{ACH}$ | **15 to 20** (process rooms) | HVAC for hygiene |
| Lighting intensity | $E$ | **200 to 500 lux** | Process areas (BIS standards) |

> [!NOTE]
